Her beauty, grace, and natural charm made her a figure of fascination, capable of evoking deep emotions and reflecting the multifaceted nature of womanhood and the human experience.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had left; Madame Firmiani found him still seated before her as the clock struck two in the morning.“Madame,” he began as she rose, signaling he should go, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”Madame Firmiani sat back down, visibly affected. The old man, Monsieur de Bourbonne, could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure—provincial men often misread women's restrained emotions. She gave him a sharp look that left him perplexed.“Someone in my province tells me my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth,” he said bluntly. “Forgive my rustic honesty; you should know these rumors.”“Stop, monsieur,” she cut him off. “I know of them. You are too polite to press further and too gallant to think you may question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe me when I say I scorn money; I married without fortune. Your nephew’s wealth or poverty matters not; I welcome him as a friend. All my friends respect one another; I cannot accept those I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip and dishonesty.”“Madame,” Monsieur de Bourbonne said, trembling, “I am old, almost a father to Octave. Pardon me for asking one thing. I swear as a gentleman your answer shall remain here.” He placed his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”“To any other man I would answer with a look,” she replied. “To you I ask what you would think of a woman who answered such a question. To confess love is the lover’s reward. To tell another—” She did not finish, rose, bowed, and withdrew to her private rooms.He left thinking her either sly or angelic.The next morning, around eight, Monsieur de Bourbonne found Octave in his garret. The old man berated him for squandering the advantages of a comfortable uncle who meant to leave him an estate in Touraine. “Have you been gambling? Lost at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie, I’ll return to my youthful ways.”“I saw your Madame Firmiani yesterday,” he went on, approvingly. “Have you ruined yourself for her?”“Yes, uncle,” Octave admitted.“I guessed as much. In my day women were cleverer at ruining men—this one is a piece of the last century,” the old man said.“You are mistaken, uncle. Madame Firmiani deserves esteem and adoration,” Octave replied. He produced a letter from her, given in a portfolio she had handed him. “Read this; it explains almost everything.”Octave began to read aloud: “My beloved—”“Then you are still intimate with her?” his uncle interrupted.“Of course,” Octave said. “We are married.”“Heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Then why do you live in a garret?”“Let me continue.” Octave read, sometimes pausing with feeling: “My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; a wife should be gay and caressing. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is in the man she loves. Esteem, consideration, honor—must they not belong to him who receives our all? Well, my angel has fallen. The tale you told me has tarnished my past joys. I feel humiliated in you—you whom I thought the most honorable of men. I must have deep confidence to make this avowal, which costs me much. How can you, knowing your father unjustly deprived others of their property, keep it? You told me of this in the room where our love began; you are a gentleman, you think yourself noble, and I am yours. I find some excuses in your youth and thoughtlessness, but I cannot respect a man who knowingly is smirched for a sum of money. Five francs stolen at play or five times a hundred thousand won by a legal trick equally dishonor. Love, to a woman, means boundless confidence and reverence. If you come to me poor, my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. I do not wish you to make restitution because I urge it; consult your conscience. A man who loves a woman sincerely respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself. I am your wife, not your mistress. I blame myself for preaching, but scold me if you must—only a little. Dear, between us the power is yours—you alone must perceive your faults.”There was silence. Octave wiped his eyes. “There’s more,” he said, but the rest he smiled away as a lover’s secret.“You teach mathematics, and you have your uncle’s capital?” Monsieur de Bourbonne asked. Octave explained that the letter had sparked a revolution in him. Haunted by conscience—every pleasure felt like theft—he rushed to Madame Firmiani. Together they calculated what was due to the Bourgneufs, whom Octave’s father had wronged. “I condemned myself to pay three percent interest,” he said, though his means did not cover the full amount. Madame Firmiani, whose position had made her careful, offered her savings and he accepted.“You mean that adorable woman actually saves money?” the uncle exclaimed.“Do not laugh at her,” Octave said. “Her husband went to Greece in 1820 and died three years later. Legal proof of his death and his will, leaving everything to her, were lost or stolen during the troubles there. Unsure whether she might lose her fortune, she lived prudently. I wanted to acquire property of my own to provide for her if she lost hers.” He added that he gave lessons to avoid living at anyone’s expense and that the act of restitution had brought him joys beyond millions. He found the Bourgneufs living miserably: an old father agent, daughters keeping books and house, a sick mother. To bring them relief felt like being Providence; words could not convey the happiness of making amends. He called Madame Firmiani “my dear conscience”—she inspired a delicacy of feeling he thought he had lacked—and he hoped to become rich by his own industry.Touched, the old man said, “Ah, my boy, you have your mother’s soul.” Then, from the street, the sound of a carriage announced Madame Firmiani. She entered, slightly annoyed to find Monsieur de Bourbonne there but smiling. “I came to humbly entreat my husband to accept my fortune,” she said. “The Austrian Embassy has sent me a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet kept safe for me. Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here”—she laid her hand on his heart—“to which none but God can add.” She then laid her head on his breast in unrestrained happiness.Monsieur de Bourbonne concluded, “In my day we made love; in yours, you love. Women are all that is best in humanity; they are not even guilty of their faults, for they come through us.”MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once a dark, winding lane near the Hôtel de Ville, ended at the Rue Martroi and was marked by a turnstile until 1823. Less than six feet wide at its broadest, it often pooled gutter water and remained muddy; sunlight came only briefly in summer, and dampness lingered.Residents lit lamps at five in June and kept them burning through winter. Approaching the Marais felt like walking through cellars, as many old Paris streets were. The corner house at Rue du Tourniquet and Rue de la Tixeranderie showed its age: iron rings remained from chains once used for public safety.Built to combat humidity, the house had cellars raised above ground and an entrance reached by three steps. The door had a closed arch with a keystone carved as a female head. Three high windows, barred with iron, lit a small dwelling. Inside, two beds draped in green serge were visible, and an old woman tended a brazier while preparing a porter’s-wife stew. The kitchen-dining room was sparsely furnished but kept with evident cleanliness and thrift.The old woman’s pale, wrinkled face seemed part of the house. She sat by the fireplace with an expression of mischief mixed with resignation while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. With slow fingers and antiquated spectacles, the mother occasionally glanced from her work out the window; the daughter bent over her needle with focused modesty.Their modest home reflected the lives of working-class Parisian women. Despite poverty, the girl retained youthful charm—rosy cheeks and bright eyes suggesting a spirit not wholly dimmed by hardship. The old woman speculated about passersby and hoped for a better future for her daughter; the girl, absorbed in embroidery, usually kept her gaze down.One August day in 1815 the mother noticed a new face among the regular passersby: a tall, thin man in black whose solemn bearing intrigued her. She fancied he was a clerk from the Préfet’s office, and her excitement was plain. Caroline, roused from indifference by her mother’s remarks, glimpsed the stranger as he passed.The old woman eagerly awaited his return, convinced he would come by again. When he did, Caroline caught another glimpse and felt an unfamiliar sympathy, noting traces of suffering on his face. This was the first passerby to stir such feeling in her; she typically regarded people with indifference, focused on work and routine. For the first time she felt compassion for someone beyond her own troubles.As she resumed her embroidery, Caroline found herself looking forward to another sight of him, intrigued by the possibility that this new acquaintance might alter the course of her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved erratically, no longer passing the Rue du Tourniquet at the same time. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted manner—often lost in thought and rarely acknowledging her or her daughter, Caroline. One mid-September morning Caroline’s lively presence caught his eye; he watched the animated scene at the window, and a silent connection of fleeting glances suggested a deeper understanding.Weeks passed and the Gentleman began to pass twice daily; their brief encounters became a routine they both anticipated. Their silent exchanges conveyed mutual concern: Caroline sensed when he was troubled, and he could tell when she was anxious about her work. Both remained cautious, fearing that greater intimacy might bring complications or heartache.Madame Crochard, irritated by her daughter’s indecision, turned her displeasure toward the Gentleman, who had once been the object of her mild approval. As winter neared, life’s hardships weighed on Caroline, and the Gentleman noticed how they affected her spirit. One night he overheard a mother-and-daughter conversation that made their financial struggles plain. Touched, he secretly left money for them; Caroline found it the next day, and a moment of silent gratitude passed between them.In early May Caroline proposed a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—unexpectedly joined them. During the journey Madame Crochard pretended to sleep, giving Caroline and the Gentleman a chance for light-hearted talk that revealed their growing affection. The countryside’s beauty and the warmth of their exchange lifted his spirits; he began to see Caroline differently.Their bond deepened as they shared personal stories. Caroline spoke of her family’s hardships and her mother’s sacrifices. The Gentleman, now called Roger, was drawn to her honesty and resilience. Their day in Montmorency brimmed with laughter and joy, a sharp contrast to their previously somber meetings.As evening approached, Caroline voiced her newfound happiness, and Roger reflected on the possibility of joy beyond that day. Their shared understanding hinted at a deeper bond. Preparing to leave, they playfully held hands, solidifying the connection. Madame Crochard pretended not to notice, but the gesture marked a meaningful step—an unspoken promise and the hope of a brighter future together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The adventures of Roger and Caroline continue in modern Paris, where newly built apartments suit honeymooners. In a pristine building on Rue Taitbout, Caroline has lived for over a month in a charming second-floor apartment, furnished with care and style. The rooms, freshly painted and elegantly appointed, reflect current trends influenced by English design.Caroline's bedroom has gray hangings trimmed with green silk, comfortable chairs, and a writing table for love letters. A bronze clock showing Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-patterned carpet complete the cozy setting. As she prepares for Roger's visit, her hairdresser flatters her, reminding her of his admiration.On a chilly September day in 1816, Caroline wears a green grenadine gown trimmed with chinchilla. Eagerly she steps onto the balcony to watch for Roger; her heart races as she anticipates his arrival. After a quarter-hour of waiting, impatience replaces excitement, but she brightens when she finally sees him approaching.Roger arrives, and they embrace passionately, rejoicing in their reunion after three days apart. He then tells her he must attend a meeting with the Attorney-General, which disappoints Caroline. She playfully objects to the idea of his enjoying the theatre without her, declaring her deep affection.Despite the setback, Caroline quickly returns to the joy of being with Roger. They share tender moments, and she expresses her wish to take his name—Caroline de Bellefeuille—as a sign of commitment. Perched on his lap, she speaks of using her earnings to help the poor, underscoring her gratitude for their life together.Her excitement grows while she plays a sonata, but Roger, mindful of obligations, prepares to leave. They exchange a playful farewell, and Caroline watches him drive away, savoring the moment and longing for his return.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years after Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille settled in her Rue Taitbout home, she sits serenely with her four-year-old son, Charles, who plays with a rocking horse. Caroline, now twenty-four, shows a beauty formed by happiness and fulfillment. She has embraced motherhood, devoted to nurturing her children and cultivating their appreciation for goodness and beauty. Content with these simple pleasures, she remains indifferent to societal expectations, never craving luxury or status.As she rocks her infant daughter, Eugenie, Caroline recalls joyful visits to Bellefeuille with Roger. Their love has flourished in the simplicity of shared life, free from social complication. Caroline wonders quietly why Roger seeks happiness in secrecy, curious about the nature of their arrangement.On May 6, 1822, the anniversary of a pivotal day for them, Caroline prepares for dinner, anticipating Roger's arrival. He brings a document outlining Eugenie’s marriage portion, prompting discussion about different provisions for their children. Roger explains he believes Charles, as a boy, should learn self-sufficiency, while Eugenie, as a girl, deserves a fortune.Their evening is warm and lively, ending with a magical lantern show for Charles. But as night deepens, Roger must leave, and Caroline feels the familiar pang of separation. He reassures her he has three days off, yet duty hangs over him.Soon after this anniversary, Caroline hastens to her mother, Madame Crochard, who is gravely ill. The old widow’s acquaintances, unaware Caroline is her daughter, have summoned a priest to offer last rites. Caroline arrives amid confusion and deceit: the old women hope to secure benefit from Madame Crochard’s wealth. Despite their intentions, Caroline’s love for her mother shows as she cares for her.In her final moments, Madame Crochard expresses regret over their estrangement and reveals that the priest has made her confess about Caroline’s benefactor—an admission that hints at a complex web of relationships and secrets to follow.To understand those connections, the narrative backtracks to events before Madame Crochard’s death. In November 1805, a young barrister, Monsieur de Granville, finds himself in a precarious position after leaving a gathering at the High Chancellor’s residence and is offered a chance to advance his career through his uncle’s influence.Granville receives a letter from his father urging a swift return: a possible marriage to Mademoiselle Angelique Bontems could secure his future. The letter details the financial benefits of the match, despite the social stigma attached to the Bontems family. Torn between ambition and feeling, Granville recalls childhood affection for Angelique, whom he has not seen in years.The next day he travels to Bayeux, greeted by his father and taken to the Bontems’ home, where the atmosphere is stifling under the widow’s strict religious observances. Granville dreads the match, yet his father’s insistence weighs heavily.During a church service Granville glimpses Angelique, whose beauty captivates him anew. Their brief interaction rekindles his feelings, though he is acutely aware of family constraints. As the service ends, his impatience leads him to approach Angelique, setting the stage for a reunion that could alter their lives.The intertwining stories of Caroline and Granville reveal complexities of love, duty, and social expectation, as both navigate personal desires against familial obligations and ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he visited her regularly in her dreary drawing-room. During these visits he watched Angelique marking her trousseau; she never initiated religious talk. When Granville playfully handled her rosary or mocked practices, she quietly returned it to its bag with a serene smile. She firmly accepted the Church’s teachings, insisting a mother should have a religion and questioning how one could judge between disbelievers and God.Granville, captivated by her conviction, sometimes mistook his desire for her as love. Angelique reconciled feeling and duty easily, leaving Granville unsure of his own emotions. He was grateful to Madame Bontems for instilling religious principles in Angelique, thinking they would help her face life’s challenges.On the day of their marriage contract, Madame Bontems required Granville to promise to respect Angelique’s religious practices and let her freedom of conscience. Angelique’s innocent gaze convinced him; the Abbe Fontanon, who guided their consciences, smiled approvingly. Shortly after their wedding the couple moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor.Angelique persuaded him to choose a large ground-floor apartment in the Marais, near a church and chapel, arguing it would benefit their future children. Granville, who had preferred a more fashionable quarter, yielded. He set up his study and left the home decoration to Angelique, who delighted in furnishing their new home. But Granville was dismayed by the cold, solemn atmosphere she created, the reflection of her austere upbringing.Despite trying to understand her choices, Granville grew increasingly frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm. He attributed the decor to Angelique’s Bayeux background, where taste seemed less refined, and tried to comfort her by insisting happiness came from her love and gentleness rather than material things.From April through winter the couple appeared harmonious; Granville stayed busy with work and remained largely unaware of Angelique’s strict religious observances—Lenten meals she disguised as carelessness. He accompanied her to church on Sundays but did not grasp her fervor until a significant event disrupted their lives.In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, who had guided Angelique, arrived in Paris alarmed by what he saw as her moral decline and sought to reclaim her from a lax lifestyle. Angelique adopted stricter practices, which caused misunderstandings, particularly over social obligations. Granville grew irritated by her frequent excuses to avoid events, especially balls.After a tense evening he confronted her about her demeanor and attire, urging her to embrace her social role. Arguments escalated: Angelique cited her director’s prohibitions against attending balls; Granville insisted her priest was overstepping. The dispute turned theological and culminated in Angelique writing to the Pope for guidance.The Pope’s reply affirmed Granville’s position, advising that a wife should accompany her husband even if it meant compromising her virtue. Rather than resolve matters, this deepened the divide; Angelique and her director dismissed the Pope’s advice. Frustrated by her strict observances, Granville began to assert his preferences more forcefully, ordering meat daily despite her objections.As the home atmosphere grew oppressive, Granville withdrew from social life, weighed down by Angelique’s bigotry. The household became stifling and gloom-filled, affecting everyone including the servants. He recognized that their home had been transformed into a place devoid of joy, marked by rigid piety.Granville’s discontent deepened as Angelique’s beauty was overshadowed by austerity. He felt trapped in a marriage where duty had replaced love, and her devotion left little room for affection. Their children, raised under her strict rules, suffered from the oppressive climate; Granville sent his sons to school early and resolved to marry off his daughters as soon as they came of age.His isolation increased as he struggled with his marriage’s reality. He sought solace in work, attempting to write a book on law, but Angelique’s attempts to reconvert him only added to his frustration. The relationship deteriorated: Granville felt increasingly alienated from his wife and their home, which had become a place of coldness and rigidity rather than warmth and companionship.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The history of the de Granville household between 1806 and 1825 was largely uneventful. Madame de Granville, having lost her husband's affection, continued to pay for Masses hoping to discover her faults and restore their marriage. Granville, now a prominent judge, lived in the entresol of their home to avoid her. Each morning a housekeeper relayed messages; he habitually declined breakfast invitations, citing pressing court business.At thirty-five and looking older, Madame de Granville awaited his return, eager to engage him with her narrow remarks. When Granville refused the presidency of a provincial court, her friends and confessor suspected an illicit affair; they took his refusal as a sign of liaison, which deeply distressed her, for she believed him incapable of such sin.During Lent in 1822 her health declined from stress and a low fever, worsened by Granville's coldness. Attempts at affection were often undone by her recurring bigotry. By late May, as she recovered, she sought guidance from the Abbe Fontanon, who delivered devastating news: Granville had lived in sin with a concubine for seven years, fathering two children and squandering their fortune.In disbelief, Madame de Granville struggled with the revelation, torn between faith and betrayal. The Abbe urged forgiveness and patience for Granville's eventual enlightenment, but she was consumed by turmoil. After a tumultuous day she left home, determined to confront him.Meanwhile Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille were in a tense exchange: she declared hatred for his wife, whom she blamed for their unhappiness. Roger reassured her of hopes for a shared future, but their conversation was cut short by Madame de Granville's sudden fainting. On regaining consciousness she confronted Granville, questioning his love and fidelity. He accused her of sacrificing his happiness to piety, insisting she had failed to fulfill her role as a loving wife.Their exchange exposed deep faults. Granville argued true love requires sacrifice and understanding; Madame de Granville clung to moral righteousness. He explained that while he had sought happiness elsewhere, she had neglected their relationship through religious devotion. As the conversation escalated, her jealousy and anger flared, producing a painful recognition of their irreconcilable differences. Granville's indifference and admission of affection for another left her devastated. Despite tears and appeals for forgiveness, he remained resolute, saying he could not rekindle love faded by years of neglect.The dialogue ended in a stark acknowledgment of their failed marriage: love could not be commanded or revived at will. Madame de Granville was left to confront the reality of his emotional detachment and to grapple with the consequences of their choices and the uncertain future of their relationship.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in December 1833 a white-haired man, his features more worn by grief than years, walked the Rue Gaillon at midnight and paused before a modest two-story house with a dim attic light. A young man emerged; recognizing him, he approached.“What, is it you, Monsieur le Président? Alone at this hour, so far from the Rue Saint-Lazare. Allow me to give you my arm. The pavement is greasy this morning.”The Comte de Granville answered, “I am no more than fifty-five, unfortunately for me. At that age a man is still hale and strong.”“Then you are in waiting on a lady, I suppose,” said Horace Bianchon. “You do not go about Paris on foot when you keep such fine horses—”“Sometimes I return from the Courts or the club on foot,” Granville said.“And with large sums of money perhaps!” the doctor exclaimed. “It’s an invitation to the assassin’s knife.”“I am not afraid,” Granville replied, with melancholy indifference.“Then don’t stand about,” Bianchon urged, leading him toward the boulevard. “A little more and I shall believe you are bent on robbing me of your last illness—dying in some other hand than mine.”“You caught me playing the spy,” Granville admitted. “For some time I have observed at a third-floor window the shadow of a person who seems to work with heroic constancy.”He paused, seeming in pain. “I take as great an interest in that garret as a citizen of Paris must feel in the finishing of the Palais Royal.”“Well,” Bianchon said eagerly, “I can tell you—”“Tell me nothing,” Granville interrupted. “I would not give a centime to know whether the shadow is a man or a woman, nor whether the inhabitant is happy or miserable. I was surprised not to see anyone at work this evening. For nine years I have ceased to wonder that old men amuse themselves with growing flowers and planting trees; the events of life have taught them disbelief in all human affection. I grew old within a few days. I will no longer attach myself to any creature but unreasoning animals, plants, or superficial things. I think more of Taglioni’s grace than of all human feeling. I abhor life and the world in which I live alone. Nothing, nothing can move or interest me.”“But you have children?” Bianchon asked.“My children!” Granville repeated bitterly. “My eldest daughter is the Comtesse de Vandenesse; the other will make a good match through her sister. My sons have succeeded in their careers—the Viscount was public prosecutor at Limoges and is now President of the Court at Orléans; the younger is public prosecutor in Paris. They have their own cares. If one of those hearts had been devoted to me, if one had tried to fill this void”—he struck his breast—“that one would have failed in life, sacrificing it to me. And why should they? To bring sunshine into my few remaining years—and would they have succeeded? Might I not have accepted such generosity as a debt? But it is not for nothing that we teach them arithmetic and how to count. At this moment perhaps they are waiting for my money.”“O Monsieur le Comte, how could such an idea enter your head—you who are kind, friendly, and humane!” Bianchon protested. “If I were not living proof of the benevolence you exercise so liberally—”“To please myself,” Granville replied. “I pay for a sensation, as I would pay a pile of gold to recover the most childish illusion that would make my heart glow. I help my fellow-creatures for my own sake, just as I gamble; I look for gratitude from none. I should see you die without blinking; and I beg you to feel the same with regard to me. Life has swept over my heart like the lavas of Vesuvius over Herculaneum. The town is there—dead.”“Those who have brought a soul as warm and living as yours to such indifference are indeed guilty!” Bianchon exclaimed.“Say no more,” Granville said, shuddering.“You have a malady which you ought to allow me to treat,” Bianchon insisted.“What, do you know of a cure for death?” Granville cried irritably.“I undertake, Monsieur le Comte, to revive the heart you believe frozen.”“Are you a match for Talma, then?” Granville asked satirically.“No. But Nature is as far above Talma as Talma is superior to me. Listen: the garret you watch is inhabited by a woman of about thirty whose love is fanatic. The object of her adoration is a young man of pleasing appearance but endowed with every conceivable vice. He is a gambler, addicted to wine and women, and guilty of acts punishable by law. To him this unhappy woman sacrificed a life of ease, a man who worshiped her, and the father of her children.”“What is wrong, Monsieur le Comte?” Granville asked.“Nothing. Go on,” he replied.“She has allowed him to squander a fortune; she would give him the world if she had it. She works night and day; many times she has seen him rob her even of the money saved for her children’s clothes and food. Only three days ago she sold her hair, the finest I ever saw; he came in, and she could not hide the gold quickly enough. For a smile, for a kiss, she gave up the price of a fortnight’s life and peace. It is dreadful and yet sublime. Work is wearing her cheeks hollow. Her children’s crying has broken her heart; she is ill and now on her wretched bed. This evening they had nothing to eat; the children lacked strength to cry; they were silent when I went up.”Bianchon stood still. Against his will, Granville put his hand into his waistcoat.“I guess how she is still alive if you attend her,” Granville murmured.“O poor soul!” Bianchon cried. “Who could refuse to help her? I only wish I were richer; I hope to cure her of her passion.”“How can you expect me to pity a misery whose joys would seem cheaply bought with my whole fortune!” Granville exclaimed, withdrawing his hand empty of the notes Bianchon assumed he had. “That woman feels; she is alive! Would not Louis XV have given his kingdom to rise from the grave and have three days of youth and life? Is not that the history of thousands—dead men, sick men, old men?”“Poor Caroline!” Bianchon lamented.At the name, Granville shuddered and gripped the doctor’s arm tightly.“Her name is Caroline Crochard?” he asked, voice breaking.“Then you know her?” Bianchon said, astonished.“And the wretch’s name is Solvet. Ay, you have kept your word!” Granville exclaimed. “You have roused my heart to the most terrible pain it can suffer until it is dust. That emotion, too, is a gift from hell, and I always know how to pay those debts.”They reached the corner of the Rue de la Chaussée d’Antin. A night scavenger with a shriveled face stood by the curb.“Do you ever pick up a thousand-franc note?” Granville asked.“Now and then, master,” the scavenger replied.“And you restore them?”“It depends on the reward.”“You’re the man for me,” Granville said, handing him a thousand-franc note. “Take this, but remember: spend it at the wineshop, get drunk, fight, beat your wife, black your friends’ eyes. That will give work to the watch, the surgeon, the druggist—perhaps to the police, the public prosecutor, the judge, and the prison warders. Don’t do anything else, or the devil will be revenged on you sooner or later.”Granville pointed out the scavenger’s grotesque face to Bianchon, who stood stupefied. “Now I have squared accounts with hell and had some pleasure for my money,” he said. “As for Caroline Crochard—she may die of hunger and thirst, hearing the heartrending cries of her starving children, convinced of the baseness of the man she loves. I will not give a sou to rescue her; and because you have helped her, I will see you no more—”He left Bianchon like a statue and walked briskly to the Rue Saint-Lazare, where a carriage waited at his door.“Monsieur, your son, the attorney-general, came about an hour ago,” the servant said. “He is waiting for you in your bedroom.”Granville dismissed the servant and entered. “What motive can be strong enough to require you to infringe the order I have given my children never to come to me unless I send for them?” he asked his son.“Father,” the younger man replied tremulously, “I hope you will forgive me when you have heard me.”“Your reply is proper,” Granville said. “Sit down.” After a pause: “But whether I walk up and down or take a seat, speak without heeding me.”“Father,” the son continued, “this afternoon, at four o’clock, a very young man arrested in the house of a friend of mine, whom he had robbed, appealed to you. He says he is your son.”“His name?” Granville asked hoarsely.“Charles Crochard.”“That will do,” Granville said, waving his hand imperiously.He paced in silence; his son waited.“My son,” he began, voice mild and fatherly—causing the young lawyer to start. “Charles Crochard spoke the truth. I am glad you came to me tonight, my good Eugène.” He handed him a bundle of banknotes. “Here is a considerable sum of money; you can make any use of it in this matter. I trust you implicitly and approve whatever arrangements you may make, now or in the future. Eugène, my dear son, kiss me. We part perhaps for the last time. I shall tomorrow crave my dismissal from the King, and I am going to Italy.“Though a father owes no account of his life to his children, he is bound to bequeath to them the experience Fate sells him dearly—is it not part of their inheritance? When you marry,” he added, shuddering involuntarily, “do not undertake it lightly; that act is the most important society requires of us. Study the character of the woman who is to be your partner; consult me too, I will judge her myself. A lack of union between husband and wife leads to terrible misfortune; sooner or later we are punished for contravening the social law. I will write to you on this from Florence. A father who presides over a supreme court of justice must not have to blush in the presence of his son. Good-bye.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The incident in this sketch occurred in late November 1809, during the height of Napoleon’s empire. The echoes of the Battle of Wagram still resonated in Austria as peace was negotiated between France and the Coalition. Kings and princes orbited Napoleon, who reveled in his power and staged Parisian entertainments around his marriage to an Austrian archduchess. The opulence of the French aristocracy—diamonds and gold on women and military uniforms—contrasted starkly with the Republic’s earlier austerity. Military leaders indulged in wealth amassed through the sacrifices of countless soldiers.Many women adopted a carefree attitude reminiscent of the Louis XV era. Whether influenced by the fallen monarchy or the Imperial family, both men and women embraced a hedonistic lifestyle that seemed to foreshadow disaster. The allure of military men became a frenzy aligned with the Emperor’s interests, since frequent calls to arms made every peace treaty temporary. Women’s affections shifted rapidly; many moved from mistress to widow in weeks.The appeal of soldiers sprang from prospects of early widowhood, the lure of historical legacy, or the romantic notion of courage. Women were drawn to the idea that their passions would remain secret, buried on the battlefield. This fascination with military men, seen as embodiments of honor and adventure, produced a culture where bravery was prized. The period was marked by a mania for glittering possessions: diamonds and fireworks were in vogue, and men wore jewels following extravagant examples like Murat.Comte de Gondreville, formerly Citizen Malin, had risen to prominence and postponed a grand entertainment to outshine other courtiers and impress Napoleon. His drawing-rooms filled with ambassadors, influential figures, and a few princes, all expecting the Emperor. But a private dispute between Napoleon and Josephine, hinting at divorce, kept him from the festivities, though the guests remained unaware of the tension.The most beautiful women in Paris flocked to Gondreville’s event, embodying luxury and elegance. The financial elite, proud of their wealth, sought to rival the military’s splendor, using grand balls to introduce daughters to Napoleon’s inner circle and hoping to trade fortunes for favor. Women confident in their beauty attended to test their allure, masking true intentions behind smiles and friendly facades. Amidst the gaiety, many distrusted friends more than enemies, as the pursuit of wealth and power underpinned social interactions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents surrounding the little imbroglio that is the subject of this study, reflecting the tone prevalent in Paris drawing-rooms at the time.“Look toward the pedestal supporting that candelabrum—do you see the young lady with her hair a la Chinoise? She’s in the corner to the left, with bluebells in her chestnut curls. She’s so pale you’d think she’s ill—delicate and very small. Now she’s turning her head; those almond-shaped blue eyes look as if they were made for tears. She’s leaning forward to see Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd.”“I see her now. You only had to mention her lovely complexion; I would have recognized her. She has the whitest skin here, but she’s either a prude or a coquette; her bodice barely hints at her beauty. What shoulders! What lily-whiteness!”“Who is she?” asked the first speaker.“I don’t know.”“An aristocrat! Do you want to keep them all to yourself, Montcornet?”“You of all people to tease me!” Montcornet smiled. “Do you think you can insult a poor general like me because you rival Soulanges? Or because I’ve only just arrived in this Promised Land? Come, Monsieur le Maitre des Requetes, let us glean in the field you can only hold precariously. We have a right to live! If you knew the German women, you might help me with the Parisian you love best.”“Well, General, since you’ve noticed that lady, have you seen her dance?”“Where have you been? There’s a triple rank of the most undaunted coquettes of Paris between her and the swarm of dancing men. It’s a miracle anyone could spot her in that corner, buried in gloom despite the candles. Between her and us is a sparkle of diamonds and glances, so many floating plumes and fluttering lace that it’s impossible for any dancer to detect her among those stars. How could you think she’s the wife of some sous-préfet from Lippe or Dyle?”“Oh, he will be!” exclaimed the Master of Appeals.“I doubt it,” the Colonel of Cuirassiers laughed. “She seems as raw in intrigue as you are in diplomacy. I bet you don’t know how she got there.”The lawyer looked at the Colonel with a mix of contempt and curiosity.“She probably arrived punctually at nine, the first of the company, and embarrassed the Comtesse de Gondreville, who can’t think straight. Repulsed by the mistress of the house and driven into the corner by jealous ladies, she became a victim of their coalition against her. Each of those sweet faces would have conspired to keep the men from taking her out. That’s how those tender-looking women would have formed a rampart against the stranger without saying a word beyond, ‘Do you know that little woman in blue?’ If you care to run the gauntlet of flattering glances and inviting questions, try to get through the rampart defending that Queen of Dyle, Lippe, or Charente. You’ll see how even the dullest woman will invent a wile to keep the plaintive stranger in the shadows. Doesn’t she look like an elegy?”“Do you think so, Montcornet? Then she must be married?”“Why not a widow?”“She would be less passive,” the lawyer laughed.“She might be the widow of a gambler,” the handsome Colonel suggested.“Of course; since the peace, there are many widows of that sort!” said Martial. “But my dear Montcornet, we are simpletons. That face is too innocent; there’s too much youth and freshness for her to be married. What splendid flesh-tints! Nothing has sunk in the modeling of her nose. Her lips and chin are as fresh as a white rosebud, though clouds of sadness veil her expression. Who could make that young creature weep?”“Women cry for little,” said the Colonel.“I don’t know,” replied Martial; “but she’s not crying because she’s left without a partner; her grief is not new. It’s clear she beautified herself for this evening with intention. I would wager she’s in love already.”“Bah! She’s perhaps the daughter of some German princeling; no one talks to her,” said Montcornet.“How unhappy a poor child may be! Can anything be more graceful and refined than our little stranger? Yet not one of those furies around her, who think they can feel, will say a word to her. If she would only speak, we’d see if she has fine teeth.”“You boil over like milk at the least increase of temperature!” cried the Colonel, nettled at finding a rival in his friend.“What!” exclaimed the lawyer, ignoring the Colonel’s question. “Can nobody here tell us the name of this exotic flower?”“Some lady companion!” said Montcornet.“A companion! Wearing sapphires fit for a queen and Malines lace? Tell that to the marines, General. You wouldn’t shine in diplomacy if you jump from a German princess to a lady companion.”Montcornet stopped a fat little man with iron-gray hair and clever eyes, mingling unceremoniously with various groups.“Gondreville, my friend,” said Montcornet, “who is that charming little woman sitting under that huge candelabrum?”“The candelabrum? Ravrio’s work; Isabey made the design.”“Oh, I recognized your lavishness and taste; but the lady?”“Ah! I don’t know. Some friend of my wife’s, no doubt.”“Or your mistress, you old rascal.”“No, on my honor. The Comtesse de Gondreville is the only one capable of inviting people no one knows.”Despite this acerbic comment, the fat little man smilingly retained the Colonel’s jest. Montcornet returned to the lawyer, who had rejoined a neighboring group, intent on asking, but in vain, for information about the fair unknown. He grasped Martial’s arm and said in his ear:“Mind what you’re about. Madame de Vaudremont has been watching you for some time with ominous attentiveness; she can guess by the movement of your lips what you say to me. Our eyes have already told her too much; she’s thinking even more than we are about the little blue lady.”“That’s too old a trick in warfare, my dear Montcornet! However, what do I care? Like the Emperor, when I make a conquest, I keep it.”“Martial, your fatuity cries out for a lesson. You, a civilian, engaged to Madame de Vaudremont—a widow of two-and-twenty with four thousand napoleons a year—who slips such a diamond on your finger,” he added, taking the lawyer’s left hand, which the young man complacently allowed; “and you affect the Lovelace, as if you were a colonel obliged to maintain the military reputation! Fie! Just think of all you may lose.”“At any rate, I shall not lose my liberty,” replied Martial with a forced laugh.He cast a passionate glance at Madame de Vaudremont, who responded only with an uneasy smile, having seen the Colonel examining the lawyer’s ring.“Listen to me, Martial. If you flutter around my young stranger, I shall set to work to win Madame de Vaudremont.”“You have my full permission, my dear Cuirassier, but you will not gain this much,” the young Maitre des Requetes put his polished thumb-nail under an upper tooth with a mocking click.“Remember that I am unmarried,” said the Colonel; “that my sword is my whole fortune; and that such a challenge is setting Tantalus down to a banquet he cannot devour.”“Prrr.”This defiant roll of consonants was the only reply to the Colonel’s declaration, as Martial looked him over before turning away.Fashion required men to wear white kerseymere breeches and silk stockings at a ball. This costume showcased Montcornet’s fine shape. At thirty-five, he attracted attention with his stalwart height, emphasized by the handsome uniform of the Cuirassiers of the Imperial Guard. A black moustache highlighted his soldierly countenance, with a broad forehead, aquiline nose, and bright red lips. Montcornet’s manner, marked by a certain superiority from his command experience, might please a woman sensible enough not to aim at making a slave of her husband. The Colonel smiled as he looked at the lawyer, one of his favorite college friends, whose small figure made Montcornet look down slightly as he answered the raillery with a friendly glance.Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon was a young Provencal favored by Napoleon; his fate might lead to a splendid embassy. He had won the Emperor with his Italian suppleness, a talent for intrigue, and a knowledge of manners, good substitutes for higher qualities. Though young and eager, his face had acquired the rigid brilliancy of tinned iron, a trait of diplomatists that conceals emotions. The heart of a diplomat may be an insoluble problem, as the most illustrious ambassadors of the time were distinguished by enduring hatreds and romantic attachments.Martial, however, was one of those men capable of reckoning on the future amid their enjoyment; he had learned to judge the world and hid his ambition under the guise of a lady-killer, cloaking his talent under the commonplace of mediocrity as he watched the rapid advancement of those who offended their master less.The two friends parted with a cordial handshake. The introductory tune, signaling the ladies to form squares for a fresh quadrille, cleared the men from the space they had filled while talking in Gondreville’s large drawing-room. Their whispered conversation had taken place during the usual interval between dances. The chandeliers and flambeaux illuminated their faces, failing to conceal the faint expressions of their feelings from the keen-sighted countess or the artless stranger. This espionage of thoughts is perhaps one of society’s idle pleasures; many disappointed people are bored without daring to admit it.To appreciate this conversation’s interest, it’s necessary to recount an incident that would serve as an invisible bond drawing together the actors now scattered through the rooms.At about eleven, just as the dancers returned to their seats, the company observed the entrance of the handsomest woman in Paris, the queen of fashion, the only person missing from the brilliant assembly. She made it a rule never to appear until a party reached a pitch of excitement that doesn’t allow women to preserve the freshness of their faces or dresses. This brief hour is the springtime of a ball. An hour later, when pleasure falls flat and fatigue sets in, everything is spoiled. Madame de Vaudremont never committed the blunder of remaining at a party to be seen with drooping flowers, hair out of curl, tumbled frills, and a face courting sleep. She took care not to let her beauty be seen drowsy, as her rivals did; she cleverly maintained her reputation by always leaving a ballroom in brilliant order. Women whispered with envy that she planned and wore as many different dresses as parties she attended in one evening.On this occasion Madame de Vaudremont was not destined to leave when she wished. Pausing on the threshold, she shot swift, observant glances at the women present, hastily scrutinizing their dresses to assure herself that her own eclipsed them all.She presented herself at the same moment as one of the bravest colonels of the Guards’ Artillery and the Emperor’s favorite, the Comte de Soulanges. The transient association of these two had an air of mystery. Upon hearing their names announced, a few women rose, and men hurried from side-rooms to the principal doorway. One of the jesters remarked that “women have as much curiosity about seeing a man faithful to his passion as men do in studying a woman difficult to enthrall.”The Comte de Soulanges, about thirty-two, had a nervous temperament that hinted at fine qualities. His slender build and pale complexion were not initially attractive, but his black eyes revealed great vivacity. He was taciturn in company, and nothing in his appearance hinted at the oratory he would later show in the legislative assembly under the Restoration.The Comtesse de Vaudremont, tall and somewhat plump, with dazzlingly white skin and a small head carried well, inspired love through her gracious manner. She was one of those beings who keep all the promise of their beauty.The pair briefly became the center of observation, but did not allow curiosity long to exercise itself. The Colonel and the Countess seemed to understand that accident had placed them in an awkward position. Martial, as they approached, hastened to join a group by the fireplace, watching Madame de Vaudremont with the jealous anxiety of a first flame of passion from behind a barrier of heads. A secret voice warned him that the success he prided himself on might be precarious. However, the coldly polite smile with which the Countess thanked Monsieur de Soulanges and her little bow of dismissal as she sat down by Madame de Gondreville relaxed the rigidity jealousy had made his face. Seeing Soulanges still standing near the sofa where Madame de Vaudremont sat, apparently unaware of the glance that conveyed they were both playing a ridiculous part, Martial knitted his brows and smoothed his curls to maintain composure, watching the faces of the Countess and Soulanges while still chatting with his neighbors. He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their acquaintance, but he listened without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.Soulanges gazed calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep around the immense ballroom, admiring the display of diamonds, rubies, gold, and shining hair, which almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cut glass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s indifference unsettled the lawyer. Unable to control his impatience, Martial approached Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. Upon seeing the Provencal, Soulanges cast him a covert glance and impertinently turned away his head. A solemn silence reigned, curiosity peaking. Faces wore strange mixed expressions; everyone anticipated an outbreak that men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly, the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor to hide his agitation. Spotting the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and the company thought Soulanges had publicly surrendered his position out of fear of the ridicule that follows a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened so inattentively that he missed her words behind her fan:“Martial, please don’t wear that ring you took from me. I have my reasons and will explain them when we leave. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. It was then that the little blue lady became the object of curiosity that agitated the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.When the friends parted after their challenge, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the intoxication dancing produces in a woman and the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress that enhances their attractions as much as it does women’s, Martial thought he might yield to the charm that drew his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he managed to hide his first glances toward the lady in blue from the Countess’s watchful eyes, he was soon caught; and while he excused himself once for his absent-mindedness, he could not justify the unseemly silence that followed the most insinuating question a woman can ask:“Do you like me very much this evening?”The more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.While Martial danced, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting even the most indifferent, he resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed at liberty to ask her the stranger’s name. He noticed a little space cleared between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several chairs vacant, forming rows of fortifications held by mothers and middle-aged women; the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade of shawls. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and from one to another, through compliments, he finally reached the empty space next to the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dripping wax, to Martial’s annoyance.Too tactful to speak suddenly to his right, he began by addressing a plain woman seated on the left: “This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! Every woman here is pretty! You’re not dancing—because you don’t care for it, no doubt.”This vapid conversation aimed to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; however, she, silent and absent-minded, paid no attention. He had prepared several phrases leading to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped for great things. But he was surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing at Madame de Vaudremont.“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked hesitantly.“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.“And your husband is here, of course?”“Yes, monsieur.”“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”The mournful lady smiled sadly.“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will ensure not to bring you back here. When so many are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball that your beauty may claim.”“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”The curt tone of her replies was discouraging; the Colonel withdrew. Martial, guessing the officer’s request and refusal, smiled, stroking his chin and making the diamond on his finger sparkle.“What are you laughing at?” asked the Comtesse de Vaudremont.“At the poor Colonel’s failure, who just put his foot in it—”“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.“I did not hear you.”“If you can hear nothing this evening, at least you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, vexed.“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”“I thank you for telling me his name,” she said; “he seems an agreeable man.”“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” asked the lady, looking inquiringly at the Colonel.“On the very best.”The unknown turned pale.“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”“I fancied Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering from the grief that had clouded her beauty.“For a week past, the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he’s still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone of dismissal.At that moment, the quadrille was ending. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, telling himself, “She is married.”“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe garden air, “how are you getting on?”“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”“What does that matter?”“Oh, deuce take it! I’m a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no intention of courting a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me she did not intend to dance.”“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile, I’m going to look for Soulanges; he might know the lady, as she seems interested in him.”“You’ve lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I’m an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”With these words the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges at a bouillotte table. Though no deep friendship linked the two soldiers beyond comradeship, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was alarmed to see the Colonel of Artillery, usually prudent, playing a game that could lead to ruin. Heaps of gold and notes showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood around the players. Occasionally a few words—pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken—were spoken, but the five motionless men seemed to converse only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, approached, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table, cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he didn’t even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, defying fortune rather than thanking her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” He thought to help by pulling him from the card-table. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”“What is that?” asked Soulanges.“That you will answer a question.”The Comte de Soulanges rose abruptly, gathering his winnings into his handkerchief; his fierce look discouraged protest—players seemed relieved to see him leave.“Those soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” remarked a diplomat who took his place. A single tired face turned to the newcomer and said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”“My dear fellow,” Montcornet said to Soulanges, leading him aside, “the Emperor praised you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”“The Master does not love the Artillery.”“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said that men who married in Paris during the campaign are not in disgrace. Well?”Soulanges looked bewildered.“And now I hope you will tell me if you know a charming woman sitting under a huge candelabrum----”At this the Count’s face brightened; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” he said, voice changed, “if anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull with this gold. Leave me, I beg you. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than— I hate everything I see. I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”“My poor friend!” Montcornet replied gently, squeezing his hand. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you that Martial thinks so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is quite smitten with that little lady?”“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling with rage, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!”He sank into an easy chair. Montcornet slowly left, seeing Soulanges too furious for consolation.Back in the ballroom, Montcornet noticed Madame de Vaudremont, agitated, and sat beside her.“I wager something has vexed you?” he said.“A mere trifle, General. I want to go to the Grand Duchess of Berg’s ball, but first to the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon amuses himself by flirting with the dowagers.”“That’s not the whole secret. I bet a hundred louis you stay here all evening.”“Impertinent man!”“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet—”“Of whom?” she exclaimed feigning surprise.“Of that candelabrum,” he replied, glancing at the fair stranger and scrutinizing the Countess.“You have guessed it,” she said, hiding behind her fan. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as an old monkey, told me Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is flirting with that stranger, who sits here like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face—so cruelly beautiful and pale. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac, who only goes to a ball to watch while pretending to sleep, has made me miserable. Martial shall pay dearly. Urge him, since he is your friend, not to make me unhappy.”“I just left a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that lady. And he is a man to keep his word. But I know Martial; such threats encourage him. Besides, we have wagered—” The Colonel lowered his voice.“Can it be true?” asked the Countess.“On my word of honor.”“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” Madame de Vaudremont replied, her glance inviting.“Will you do me the honor of dancing with me?”“Yes; but the next quadrille. During this one, I want to see what will come of this intrigue and learn who the little blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”Seeing the Countess wished to be alone, the Colonel retired, pleased to have made a good start.At such entertainments, women like Madame de Lansac gather like old sailors watching younger mariners. She easily guessed the Countess’s agitation. Madame de Vaudremont might fan and smile, hide her emotion, but the Duchess, a sharp-eyed, mischievous figure, read her heart. She detected the slightest movements that revealed feeling. From her large armchair, the coquette of a bygone generation admired the younger woman’s attempts to disguise annoyance and grief. The Countess had believed Martial a man of talent who could raise her to power; now she realized her error—an instant passion that wounded her pride as if an unblemished limb were amputated.The Duchess, eager to speak to the Countess, dismissed her Ambassador; in a lover’s quarrel every other interest paled. Madame de Lansac shot a sardonic glance at the younger woman, making her fear the dowager’s influence. There are looks between women that strike like torches in a drama; none who did not know the Duchess could appreciate the terror her gaze fired in the Countess.Madame de Lansac was tall; people said, “That must have been a handsome woman!” She rouged her cheeks, wore diamonds, and dressed with taste. Her sharp nose promised epigrams; her polite manners softened mischief, making direct spite unlikely. Her eyes lit triumphantly; a smile seemed to say, “I promised you as much!” A blush of hope crossed the pale cheeks of the young woman under the chandelier. The alliance between Lansac and the stranger did not escape Madame de Vaudremont, who sensed a mystery and sought to uncover it.At this moment the Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned the dowagers in vain about the blue lady’s name, turned in despair to the Comtesse de Gondreville, who answered curtly, “A lady whom the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”By chance the Baron met the Duchess’s intelligent glance toward the stranger. Despite bad terms between them, he decided to speak. The Duchess, seeing him approach, smiled with sardonic irony and looked at Madame de Vaudremont, causing Montcornet to laugh.“If the old witch pretends to be friendly,” thought the Baron, “she will play me some spiteful trick.” He said, “Madame, I am told you have undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”“Do you take me for a dragon?” the old lady returned. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added sweetly, reviving Martial’s hopes.“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, whom you men do not know; she goes nowhere.”“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature. May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you will tell me all I want to know, I promise to urge a petition for the restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands on the Emperor.”The younger branch of Navarreins bears the arms of Lansac; the Duchess’s liaison with Louis XV had secured her husband a dukedom. The Baron's suggestion to petition for an estate belonging to the elder branch was transparent trickery.“Monsieur,” the old woman said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room shares your curiosity. All eyes involuntarily turn to the corner where my protégée sits modestly; she reaps the homage the women wished to deprive her of. Happy the man she chooses!” She paused, fixing Madame de Vaudremont with a look that plainly meant, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from your handsome Countess than from me.”There was such marked defiance that Madame de Vaudremont rose, approached, and took the chair Martial placed for her. Without acknowledging him, she said, “I can guess, madame, that you are talking of me; but I admit my want of perspicacity; I do not know whether it is for good or evil.”Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and replied in a low, compassionate tone, “Poor child!”They exchanged glances. Madame de Vaudremont understood Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious “Leave us.”The Baron, displeased to see the Countess in the sibyl’s thrall, cast one of those looks that might sway a blinded heart but seemed ridiculous to a woman beginning to suspect the man who attracted her.“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” said Madame de Vaudremont, turning to give an ironical sidelong glance at the lawyer.Martial, a man of the world with wit and acumen, would not risk breaking with a woman favored at Court, whom the Emperor wished to see married. He relied on the jealousy he intended to provoke to learn the reason for her coolness and withdrew willingly as a new quadrille began.Making room for dancing, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two women talk. He followed their glances to the stranger. Comparing the Countess to this new beauty, whose mystery enhanced her charm, he succumbed to the common self-interest of adventurous seducers—torn between a fortune within reach and indulging caprice. Light highlighted his anxious, sullen face against white silk hangings, making him seem an evil genius. Observers likely thought, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”Meanwhile the Colonel, leaning between the ballroom and the cardroom, laughed undetected under his moustache. He enjoyed the dance’s turmoil, reading agitation in the Countess and Martial, pondering Soulanges’ gloom and the fair unknown’s expression, which shifted between hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the feast’s king, receiving smiles from many women; as a Colonel of the Imperial Guard he was one of the best matches in the army.It was nearly midnight. Conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, rivalries, and scheming had reached fervor; a young man exclaimed, “A fine ball!”“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you are enduring a thousand deaths, I thought I might give charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty spoils your future; it is like tearing the gown you must wear. Later we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, making clever enemies and foolish friends, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly asked.“My child,” the Duchess replied, “at your age you must choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not wise enough to be a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to squander your fortune. That would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left. In three years he will be a wreck. Then he will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed—I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, with gossip and presumption but no real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he computes you as an investment. If you must marry, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught twice in the same trap? A new alliance should be a good speculation; in marrying again you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Maréchale!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As she spoke, both women naturally looked at Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “then you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passions. I came here to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve turned into a lovesick fool for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married for two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has experienced remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come and see the result of your actions.”The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.“There,” said Madame de Lansac, indicating a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child gives her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”The Countess looked away, lost in sad thoughts.The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. Players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amid festivity, struck the Countess with foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not completely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”“But she loves her husband.”“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”“But she is married.”“A whimsical objection!”“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”“You deserve that I should send you to her.”“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”This thought stirred her pride more than curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control. The Colonel, misreading her embarrassment, became more ardent. Old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues. The passions of the two couples were evident in the crowded rooms, reflected in the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of impotence.Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty up close, captivated by her fresh neck and graceful figure. At that time, women wore sashes tied under the bosom, a style that flattered those with perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.“Yes, very much, monsieur.”Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred something in him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”Artless confusion colored her cheeks.“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”“Precisely.”“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”“Yes, monsieur.”Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through their physical grace.
