Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the most brilliant women prized dancing above all. As the orchestra played, the Baron felt immense pride surveying the ladies forming the square. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled even Madame de Vaudremont’s, who stood opposite him and the lady in blue. All eyes briefly turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur showed she was the topic among the men. The admiration and envy made the young woman blush, enhancing her charm. When she raised her eyes, she looked at her partner as if wishing to transfer the glory of admiration to him, showing she valued his opinion above all others. She blended innocence and vanity, displaying the budding affection youthful hearts possess. Though modest and inexperienced in ballroom arts, she knew how to glance at him at just the right moments.When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial dismissed the concern: “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”After the quadrille, whispers circulated. The less attractive women made moral comments about Martial and the Comtesse, while the more beautiful marveled at her apparent ease in surrendering to him. The men were baffled by the Baron’s luck, not finding him particularly charming. A few sympathetic women warned against judging the Countess harshly, suggesting young wives would be ruined if a mere glance or graceful dance could compromise a reputation.Only Martial understood his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the moulinet, he clasped the Countess’s hand, feeling her response through the thin glove. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only benefit you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”Madame de Soulanges yielded to his charm, and they walked through the crowded rooms. She hesitated at each door, peering in before entering, only reassured when he said, “He is not here.” They reached a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper approached, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, draped with flowers and blue hangings. The noise faded. Initially startled, the Countess resisted following him but eventually sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they could be seen.“This room is charming,” she remarked, admiring the decor. “All here is love and delight!” Martial, deeply moved, noticed her face in the dim light: a mix of unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, which seemed to quell her internal struggle. Flirtatiously she took his left hand and removed the ring he wore, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”Martial, intoxicated by her touch, looked at her with eyes as bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He trailed off, captivated by her rapture. She asked, “You give it to me?” with surprise. “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured her.She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her, she suddenly stood and said clearly, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.”“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied with a smile. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring, revealing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair, then fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. He noticed the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughing nearby, and the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial took their banter in stride, grateful for their silence regarding the evening’s events, as he exchanged his horse for a young wife.As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris to her home in Faubourg Saint-Germain, she was consumed by anxiety. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville, she searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her mind. A silent witness to her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont had ensnared him, she had hoped for his repentance. Now she feared she had made a grave mistake by agreeing to her aunt’s scheme.The evening darkened her innocent spirit. Initially alarmed by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed upon seeing her rival’s beauty, feeling the weight of societal corruption. As she crossed the Pont Royal, she discarded the hair from the diamond, a token of pure affection. Tears flowed as she recalled her long-standing grief, and she shuddered at the thought that a woman seeking peace must bury her pain without complaint.“Alas!” she pondered, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.”Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”“Oh, my dear Leon,” she coaxed, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!”Her words softened the Count’s stern demeanor, for he had been dreading her return, fearing she would learn of his infidelity. In a bid to deflect her anger, he had tried to be the first to criticize her. Delighted to see him smile and grateful for his presence in a room he had frequented less often, the Countess looked at him tenderly, blushing and lowering her eyes. Her kindness further enchanted Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully; gratitude often accompanies love.“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding, as she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In September 1835 Mademoiselle du Rouvre, only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. The Marquis had squandered much of his fortune, but Clémentine was a great heiress through her mother’s family; settlements by relatives ensured her a steady annual income upon marriage.Comte Adam, an exile, was no charge on the French government. He came from an illustrious Polish family but faced prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was poorly regarded. Initially seen as a student enjoying Paris after losing political prospects, Adam navigated a divided Polish community in France—split between republican and noble exiles—and became the butt of Parisian humor, with some questioning his wealth and status.In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he was established in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clémentine. Their marriage elevated his standing and he soon became fashionable. The couple’s home was a luxurious house blending styles and exquisite decor, reflecting the wealth and tastes of the new bourgeoisie.Clémentine, now a countess, began to notice the mysterious presence of Captain Paz, a factotum who managed their affairs yet remained largely invisible. She questioned Adam about Paz, who was a constant figure in their lives, handling everything from finances to horse care. Adam explained that Paz came from a poor branch of the Paz family, had fought bravely for Poland, and had become a close friend through shared hardships.Paz’s loyalty and dedication were evident; he ran the household with remarkable efficiency and discretion. Adam admired Paz’s character, insisting their friendship rested on mutual respect and understanding. Despite social hierarchies, Adam regarded Paz as an equal; their bond had been strengthened by shared experiences and challenges. Once Adam offered Paz a financial gift, which Paz declined—an example of the depth of their friendship and pride.As the couple prepared for a day out, Clémentine’s affection for both Adam and Paz grew, highlighting the unique dynamics among them. The narrative emphasizes loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th-century Paris, showing how personal ties could defy social norms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Paz lived a reclusive life, largely unnoticed by Parisian society, until the Comtesse Laginska was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. During that outing Clémentine insisted Thaddeus dine with them, so he dressed modestly for the occasion. At dinner he behaved like a subordinate, listening and answering minimally, which frustrated Adam, who urged him to be more lively. Though outwardly dull, Thaddeus was acutely aware of his feelings for Clémentine, feelings he tried hard to conceal.After dinner he agreed to go to the Opera but remained detached, prompting Clémentine to remark on his lack of engagement while Adam excused him as disagreeable. Thaddeus, however, was wrestling with unrequited love, a pain sharpened after her return from a trip. He took a bittersweet pride in being indispensable to their household; he knew their affairs would falter without his management.Clémentine, now more beautiful and confident in marriage, focused on her social standing in Paris and had little room for thoughts of Thaddeus. Yet one day she noticed him while driving home and, almost awkwardly, invited him to join them. The invitation flustered him; their interactions were charged with unspoken emotion. Thaddeus felt gratitude and longing but kept his distance, returning to routine and to the solace of managing their affairs while feeling increasingly overlooked.He reflected on his love’s nature—the depth, the joy in small service, and the pain of being unreciprocated. Thaddeus resumed solitary habits, content in silent devotion, admiring Clémentine from afar and cherishing glimpses of her happiness. When she hosted a reception, his absence went unnoticed; this reinforced his role as the quiet, indispensable presence in their lives. Clémentine’s Parisian existence became a constant scramble for attention and precedence; distracted by rivals and strained by the demands of society, she often neglected Adam. Through it all Thaddeus remained steadfast, quietly maintaining order while privately suffering.In May, as Clémentine prepared to leave for her uncle’s estate, she spotted Thaddeus again. Their brief exchange revived her curiosity and she insisted he join them for dinner. He hesitated but complied. During the meal he attempted more engagement, though shyness and duty limited him. As the evening progressed Clémentine’s interest in him grew; she began to see him differently. Their conversation hinted at a deeper connection and momentarily emboldened Thaddeus, yet his loyalty to Adam restrained any open pursuit.After dinner Clémentine voiced dissatisfaction with Adam’s behavior and a desire for more passion. Thaddeus, torn between love for her and friendship with Adam, felt the urge to confess but held back, aware of the situation’s moral complexity. Clémentine’s insecurities led her to question love and loyalty; Thaddeus sensed her vulnerability and felt faint hope, though he remained cautious. Their talk deepened and silences became charged with unspoken feeling.To deflect attention and avoid exposing his true feelings, Thaddeus fabricated a story about being in love with a circus performer named Malaga, describing her as captivating and agile—a contrast to Parisian women. The ruse let him speak of longing for a simpler, more genuine connection without implicating Clémentine. He visited Malaga as an escape from his unrequited love, providing for her and ensuring she lived comfortably, yet keeping an emotional distance and refusing a romantic relationship.As Malaga’s fortunes changed, she began to question Thaddeus’s intentions, causing misunderstandings and tension. Despite these challenges, he remained her committed benefactor while continuing to be haunted by feelings for Clémentine. In the end Malaga sent him a heartfelt letter expressing despair over their separation and longing for his companionship. The letter struck him, reminding him of the depth and constancy of his devotion—to Clémentine, to duty, and to the painful complexities of love, loyalty, and sacrifice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess spoke about Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus's misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his relationship with a circus performer. Clementine expressed disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga.Paz, who had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, was seen by the countess as a potential ruin for Adam. Though concerned, Adam believed Thaddeus would eventually leave Malaga if it served his interests; the countess remained skeptical.Days later Paz noticed a change in Clementine's demeanor toward him, sensing her contempt. This plunged him into despair, convinced he had caused her to despise him. Despite his heartache, he found joy in silently admiring her at social events and devoted himself to her well-being and the household.Paz's love for Clementine was profound, yet he felt unworthy of her respect. He fell ill from his emotional turmoil but later recovered. Adam then lost a significant sum gambling and asked Thaddeus to cover it by attributing the loss to Malaga. Thaddeus reluctantly agreed, feeling the weight of obligations.Clementine discovered Thaddeus's financial generosity toward Paz and voiced her frustration, questioning his loyalty. Thaddeus, in turn, felt burdened by her expectations and the need to manage her affairs. He resolved to teach her household management, believing she could handle it independently.As spring approached, Adam fell seriously ill and Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him. Clementine, aware of Adam's precarious state, sought the best medical care. Thaddeus's dedication was unwavering; through attentive care he managed to keep the count alive.After weeks of nursing Adam improved, and Thaddeus received praise for his efforts. Yet he was tormented by his feelings for Clementine and the realization he could never have her. Concluding that leaving was the only escape from unrequited love, he decided to go to the Caucasus.Before departing Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, confessing his deep feelings and the sacrifices he had made for her happiness. He acknowledged that staying in Paris would only prolong his pain.Clementine, on receiving the letter, reflected on her feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus's sacrifices, lamented the departure, believing their bond could have withstood trials.In the years that followed Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. She remained unaware of his true feelings and the depth of his devotion, while he lived in the shadows, forever changed by his love. As time passed Clementine was drawn into a new romantic entanglement, but the memory of Thaddeus lingered—a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she maintains a virtuous façade to support his political prospects. While some women reserve judgment until his elevation to the peerage, the marquise’s conduct is strictly principled.The marquis is a bland figure, known more for faults than virtues. A silent deputy who votes correctly but lacks charisma, he has earned the nickname “dull weather.” His unremarkable nature makes him a safe match for a virtuous woman, incapable of folly.Despite a reserved manner, the marquise provokes both respect and disdain from men who attempt to flirt with her, receiving only contempt in return. She converses with agreeable men without generating gossip, unlike women with ulterior motives. She does not play coy; her behavior is genuinely principled.The narrator, an admirer, sketches her appearance: neither plain nor conventionally pretty, she possesses a captivating presence. Animated eyes reveal a deeper soul, and she exudes charm without pursuing it. This quality appears in her recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man who combines modesty and vivacity.Rastignac, adept at navigating society, speaks with the marquise at a ball about topics from opera to women’s duties. Their exchange reflects mutual interest, though he abruptly leaves afterward. The next morning, still preoccupied by their talk, he writes letters in a comfortable setting and finds his thoughts increasingly absorbed by her.He imagines the pleasures of romance, and is interrupted by his friend Horace, with whom he discusses various matters, including a possible expedition to Morea.Meanwhile, the marquise receives a letter from Rastignac as she prepares for the day. It begins with affectionate terms that spark her curiosity. Initially inclined to discard it, she reads on to understand his sentiments. The letter’s conclusion leaves her fatigued as she contemplates the impertinence of his tone and the implications of his words.This moment captures the tension between her virtuous exterior and emerging emotion, foreshadowing the complexities of her character and the dynamics of her relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac, resolving to show him more than disdain because she found his insolence intolerable. She had considered keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it. "Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline told the housemaid, who was surprised.That evening Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant expecting Rastignac; by two in the morning she realized he was not coming, despite intending to confront him with coldness. Stendhal called this mental process "crystallization."Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet Joseph for misplacing letters. Joseph, distracted, recalled one was for Madame de Listomere and another for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned the letter was not for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed, recognizing his heart had strayed. His mistakes began when he tried to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the blunder and worsened by not visiting for several days, allowing her resentment to harden.When he finally called, the porter said Madame de Listomere was out; the Marquis de Listomere, unaware of the tension, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs, Rastignac noted several social blunders. On seeing him, Madame de Listomere blushed; he thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere, absorbed in his newspaper, was oblivious to the undercurrent.Despite initial embarrassment, Madame de Listomere quickly regained composure. When Eugene asked about her health she said she was well. He suggested his visit might be unwelcome because of the marquis's introduction; she replied her husband did not share all her secrets, hinting at a complex marriage. Trying to ease the mood, Eugene said silence would excuse his visit. She promised forgetfulness while preserving a dignified air. When he mentioned the letter she feigned offense but could not hide amusement.Eugene insisted no offense was intended and the letter was not meant for her. Madame de Listomere, pretending to be offended, said she could laugh at the situation but warned him against further mistakes. Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted to mention a mutual acquaintance's death, prompting discussion that exposed the marquise's irritation. Rastignac naively suggested she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only increased her curiosity.When Eugene brought up Madame de Nucingen, Madame de Listomere's demeanor shifted. She rang for wood, signaling it was time for him to leave, and coldly confronted him about the letter, asking how his pen could have written her name. Embarrassed, Eugene stumbled and departed. Days later she confirmed his claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, while her physician recognized it as a nervous condition she used to justify seclusion.The episode underscores the complexity of social interactions and misunderstandings in romantic contexts, revealing characters' emotional responses and social roles.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At Paris, balls commonly include two gatherings: the formal party of superficial pleasantries, and a later, more intimate meeting where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, preserves the old spirit of French wit and allows free-flowing ideas and camaraderie.One memorable evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon gathered notable figures, including the Minister de Marsay, in an atmosphere brimful of wit and ease characteristic of Parisian conversation. Anecdotes and confessions flowed, creating a lively tapestry of social interaction.As the night progressed, talk turned to de Marsay's recollections of early love, especially a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He recounted youthful infatuation marked by intense devotion and secrecy. Despite his fervor, he maintained a façade of indifference to shield their relationship from society.De Marsay described their love as a careful dance of appearances and feeling: he rode past her carriage, exchanged silent signals through the flowers she carried, and relished mutual admiration and the thrill of concealment, unaware of any betrayal.A turning point came when he fell ill and sent her a note to cancel their meeting. Consumed by jealousy and suspicion, he decided to visit her despite his condition. There he found her with another man, igniting a storm of emotion. Yet her calm demeanor and tender concern briefly soothed his doubts.The narrative darkened as he grappled with the realization of betrayal. He felt a profound shift—an end to innocence and the onset of a more cynical view of love. He reflected on revenge and the complexities of passion, ultimately concluding that true love must transcend the desire for vengeance.His account resonated with those present, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the social expectations shaping relationships. De Marsay's experience served as a poignant reminder of trust's fragility and love's transformative power, leaving a lasting impression on the salon.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Cured of my cold and my pure love, I began an affair with a charming heroine whose beauty sharply contrasted with that of my deceptive angel. I avoided quarrels with this clever woman, doubting whether true love could match the delights of her skilled deceit. Such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue.I sought a hair artist in the Rue Boucher to make a keepsake for my new angel. He specialized in hair mementos and showed me work surpassing fairy tales, mentioning trends like marking linen with hair. Suspicion rose when I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, confirming doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections. I left the shop believing in pleasure but skeptical about love.Two months later, in the boudoir of the ethereal being, I held her hand as we explored the depths of sentiment. In a moment of tenderness, I seized the chance to draw out her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed love, insisting I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally.The women listening to de Marsay seemed offended by his portrayal of their airs. Just as I might have believed her sweet falsehoods, I asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” The directness startled her; her reaction revealed guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him, as he was rich and devout.Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me. I urged her to be truthful, and she swore before God. I confessed doubts about her fidelity, which she quickly turned against me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape the confrontation, but curiosity compelled her to ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke several times; she dismissed it as jealousy.I explained that her marriage to the Duke would elevate her status and advised her to embrace the role, as he was virtuous and would expect propriety. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had only spoken sooner we could have been happy. I maintained it was too late and she had gone too far with the Duke.She worried about the Duke’s suspicions; I reassured her he likely had none but that she needed to prove devotion. As she paced the boudoir, she shifted demeanor, held out her hand, and declared I was noble and charming, vowing never to forget me.Her tactics were admirable, and I played the distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed I had made advances to another woman. This startled Charlotte, who accused me of being unfaithful. I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer.Charlotte expressed disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but pointed out that taking love seriously in society was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess.Her bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat.The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay maintained a slight smile. Conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley noted that women often did not see their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, saying duchesses and marquises were disappearing and the Civil Code had leveled social hierarchies.Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay observed that countesses might survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women.The Comte de Vandenesse commented on how the current structure allowed anyone to claim nobility, while Lord Dudley noted property had not been organized, accelerating the aristocracy’s decline.Joseph Bridau observed that the beauty of womanhood had diminished and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez said the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who might otherwise have shaped society.The discussion turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the era, embodying good taste and grace but lacking the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen explained that a woman’s worth was no longer tied to lineage but to character and presentation.Count Adam Laginski asked what defined a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described her as exuding elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration. He painted a vivid picture of her bearing and demeanor, emphasizing grace and poise in various social settings.The dialogue highlighted modern femininity’s complexities: the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, blending beauty and intellect to captivate those around her. Guests considered whether this new type of woman compensated for the vanished greatness of earlier aristocratic women, or merely reflected a reshaped society where merit and presentation replaced inherited splendour.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“You will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet said gravely. “A lady will conceal hers; you may only see a middle-aged man with a decoration bow and depart. She has ordered her carriage and leaves.“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed beneath a golden canopy of a delightful dream that may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates.“At home the lady admits no one before four; she keeps you waiting. Her house is tastefully arranged; her luxury is for daily use and regularly renewed. No dusty glass shades, no wrappings; the staircase is warm, flowers abound—her only accepted gift and only from chosen people, since flowers mean promise. Fashionable trinkets lie about, but not like museum pieces. She sits by the fire in a low chair, not rising to greet you. Her conversation at home differs from that at the ball; there, she was our creditor; here, she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are nuances she masters. What she seeks is a man to widen her circle, an object for her attentions. To draw you into her drawing-room she will be charming. This shows how isolated women are today and why they want a little world of their own to shine in. Conversation there often relies on generalities.“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have caught the flaw of our age. The epigram—a volume in a word—no longer strikes at people or things but at sordid events, fading quickly.”“Hence,” Blondet continued, “a lady’s intelligence, if she has any, lies in doubting everything. The townswoman is virtuous; the lady hesitates and contends with her virtue. This uncertainty is one of the last graces left her by our dreadful times. She rarely goes to church yet will discuss religion; if you express free thought, she will try to convert you, sliding into the familiar phrases of such women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society is tottering, and you deprive it of its support. Religion means you and me; it is property and the future of our children! Let us not be selfish! Individualism is the disease of the age, and religion is the only remedy; it unites families which your laws put asunder.’ She drifts into a neo-Christian, politically laced moral discourse—a muddle of modern doctrines at odds with each other.”The women laughed at Blondet’s satire.“This shows the ‘perfect lady’ embodies intellectual and political confusion, surrounded by flashy yet fleeting products of an industry that replaces its work with something new. When you leave her you think: She certainly has superior ideas! You believe it more because she has delicately probed your heart and asked your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows, even when she does. You alone feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart. Great ladies of old flaunted their affairs; today’s lady arranges her passions like a musical score, with notes, rests, pauses and sharps. A merely weak woman is anxious not to compromise her love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, position, and fortune no longer shield her; the aristocracy does not protect her. Unlike the great lady of the past, she lacks lofty antagonism; she cannot crush anything; rather, she would be crushed. Thus she masters Jesuitical compromises, moving between propriety and anonymous passion. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. This woman—so free at a ball, so charming in a walk—is a slave at home; she is never independent except in privacy or theoretically. Her task is to preserve her position as a lady.“For in our day a woman repudiated by her husband, reduced to a meager allowance, without a carriage, luxury, or opera box, is neither wife nor maid nor townswoman; she is adrift, a chattel. The Carmelites will not accept a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover still want her? That is the question. Thus, your perfect lady may give rise to calumny, but never to slander.”“It is all so horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ exists between English hypocrisy and eighteenth-century frankness—a bastard system symptomatic of an age where nothing that arises resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere and everything is a matter of degree; all great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes purely personal. I am convinced it is impossible for a woman, even if born near a throne, to acquire before twenty-five the encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the important small things, the musical tones of coloring, the angelic mischief and innocent cunning, the speech and silence, the seriousness and banter, the wit and obtuseness, the diplomacy and ignorance that make up the perfect lady.”“And where, according to your sketch,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked, “would you classify the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman comme il faut?”“When she has no genius, she is a woman comme il n’en faut pas,” Blondet replied, with a glance that might be praise for Camille Maupin. “This epigram is not mine, but Napoleon’s,” he added.“You need not hold a grudge against Napoleon for that,” Canalis interjected. “He was weak in being jealous of literary genius—he had his flaws. Who can explain Napoleon? A man with arms folded who did everything: the greatest force known, concentrated, caustic; a singular genius who spread armed civilization without fixing it anywhere; a man who could do everything because he willed everything. He conquered illness through battle, yet died of disease in bed after living amid balls and bullets; he had a code and sword in his brain; word and deed. He foresaw everything but his own fall; a capricious politician who risked men for economy yet spared three heads—Talleyrand, Pozzo de Borgo, and Metternich—whose deaths might have saved the Empire. Nature gave him, as a rare privilege, a heart in a bronze frame: mirthful and kind at midnight among women, manipulating Europe by morning. Hypocritical and generous; loving tawdriness and simplicity; devoid of taste yet protecting the arts; despite contradictions, truly great by instinct or temperament; Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty; later, a good husband and father. He improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, even romance—with more range than precision. Did he not aim to make all Europe France? After making us weigh on the earth in such a way as to change the laws of gravitation, he left us poorer; he who had taken an empire by his name lost it on the frontier of his empire in a sea of blood. A man of thought and action, who understood Desaix and Fouché.”“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.“Ah! what a pleasure it is to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.“But do you think the treat we are giving you is common?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you had to pay for the charms of conversation as you do for dancing or music, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”“And are we really so much diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked, smiling skeptically at the women. “In these days, under a regime that makes everything small, you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books—does that prove women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? In all ages passions remain the same. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—that glory that once gave luster to some women’s errors. But even without saving a King of France, one is not less an Agnès Sorel. Do you believe our Marquise d’Espard is not the equal of Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffant, in whose rooms so much evil was spoken and done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Or Malibran the equal of Saint-Huberti? Are not our poets superior to those of the eighteenth century? If, due to the Grocers who govern us, we lack a style of our own, did not the Empire have its distinguishing stamp as the age of Louis XV did, and was not its splendor fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”“I completely agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” the Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear as fine as the most beautiful women of the past? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand now in Paris who certainly write better than she did and do not publish their letters. Whether the Frenchwoman is called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.“Émile Blondet has painted the fascinations of a woman of the day; but, when needed, this creature who bristles or preens, who echoes this or that man, can be heroic. Your faults, mesdames, are all the more poetic because they are surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much of the world—perhaps too late; but where the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I have always observed the effects of what you may call Providence, inevitably overwhelming those we consider light women.”“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways——”“Oh, let the Comte de Vandenesse preach to us!” Madame de Serizy exclaimed.“With all the more reason because he has preached a great deal by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you are very fond of,” he said, glancing at Blondet—“in which the finger of God has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was almost by my act——”“Well, tell us all about it!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay replied, glancing at Lord Dudley’s daughter.“During the campaign of 1812,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may be of use to you, Doctor Bianchon,” he said to me, “since, while you devote yourself to the body, you concern yourself with the mind; it may help solve some problems of the will.“I was on my second campaign; I loved danger and laughed at everything, like the young and foolish lieutenant of artillery I was. When we reached the Beresina, the army had lost all discipline. It was a medley of men of many nations, instinctively making their way south. Soldiers would drive a ragged, barefoot general from their fire if he brought neither wood nor food. After crossing the river, disorder did not lessen. I had come alone, without food, from the marshes of Zembin, seeking shelter. Finding none or being driven away, I finally saw a wretched Polish farm, like the wooden houses of Lower Normandy or the poorest Beauce farms—one room, one end partitioned for forage.“In the twilight I saw faint smoke above the house. Hoping to find comrades more compassionate than those I had met, I approached. Inside the table was laid. Several officers, with a woman—a common sight—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh broiled over charcoal, and frozen beetroots. I recognized artillery captains from my first regiment. I was welcomed with shouts; at that moment the cold was less fierce, my comrades were warm, they had food, and the straw-strewn floor promised a night’s rest. We did not ask for much then. My comrades could be philanthropists for free—one of the commonest ways of being philanthropic. I sat to eat on a straw bundle.“At the end of the table, by the door to the hay-filled division, sat my old colonel, one of the most extraordinary men I ever met among the mixed collection of men I knew. He was Italian. Whenever human nature is truly fine in the South, it is sublime. I do not know if you have noticed the extreme fairness of Italians when fair. It is exquisite under artificial light. My colonel—tall, admirably proportioned, perhaps a little stout but prodigiously powerful, active, and clean-limbed like a greyhound—had abundant black curls, a white complexion, small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and an aquiline nose that would pinch and turn white when he was angry, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; no one could remain calm in his presence. I alone perhaps was not afraid of him; he had a singular fancy for me and thought everything I did was right. When angry his brow knit and the muscles of his forehead formed a horseshoe; his whole frame quivered, and his strength in repose became almost unbounded. He spoke with a strong guttural roll that in fury expressed enormous force. When calm his blue eyes were angelically sweet and his smooth brow charming. On parade no man could compare with him.“Everything about this exceptional man was contrast. Passion thrives on contrast. Thus you need not ask whether he exerted over women the irresistible influence to which our nature yields—yet, by singular fatality, the Colonel was neither a lady-killer nor indifferent to such successes.“To illustrate his violence: we were dragging guns up a narrow road when we met another artillery regiment. Their colonel ordered his battery forward; the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him off his horse. Our Colonel, nearby, galloped up, calling ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam rose to his lips; he roared like a lion, drew his sword, and in two seconds his opponent lay with his skull split in two. The soldiers backed away.“The captain, nearly crushed and yelping, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This aggravated his rage. He felt pledged to protect the husband, bound to defend him as he would the woman herself.“In the hovel beyond Zembin where I was so well received, this captain sat opposite me, and his wife sat facing the Colonel. This Sicilian was a little woman named Rosina, very dark, with the Southern sun’s fire in her black almond-shaped eyes. Terribly thin, her face dusted like fruit exposed on a highway, scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair disheveled under a shawl, she still had a woman’s graces—engaging movements, a small rose mouth and white teeth, outlines of features and figure that misery had not wholly erased. Rosina’s frame seemed fragile but was wiry and springy. Her husband, a Piedmontese gentleman, had an ironical simplicity betraying cruelty; a thin, restless underlip turned down at the corners hinted at that cruelty.“The conversation was not lively when I arrived. My weary comrades ate in silence, asked questions, shared misadventures, mixed reflections on the campaign, generals, Russians, and the cold. After finishing his meager meal, the colonel wiped his mustache, bade us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman and said, ‘Rosina?’ without waiting, then went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of his call was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband—yet there was a sort of presentiment in her rigid features; perhaps she foresaw her fate.“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was settled, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’ The tone was more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel. All sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too.“‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband. ‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly. We lay down, and before long all were sound asleep.“Next morning each set out again, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, most of us met again and walked on together, like geese led in flocks. The same necessity urged us all.“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse, we heard sounds like the roar of lions and a woman’s feeble scream. Mingled with this ominous roar we smelled a pungent reek. We all looked around, seized by terror; the house was in flames. The farmhouse had been barricaded and was burning. A few yards behind, the captain quietly joined our caravan; we gazed at him in silence, for no one dared question him; but he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’ We all walked on without a word.”“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.“It would be frightful to leave with this picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.“Yes,” I replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption in its last stage left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking around four, signed to me with a friendly smile to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her face was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling sprang from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.“At this moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out, took her husband’s hand and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.PARIS, June 1839-42.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy.Bianchon, Horace: appears in Father Goriot; The Atheist’s Mass; Cesar Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Bachelor’s Establishment; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty; The Country Parson. A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Cesar Birotteau; Letters of Two Brides; A Start in Life; The Marriage Settlement; The Secrets of a Princess; The Gondreville Mystery; A Daughter of Eve.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY ######################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have some appalling stories in my collection. But each has its proper hour in conversation—you know Chamfort’s jest to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” rose the cry.The obliging doctor bowed, and silence reigned.“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house with very high roofs, so isolated that there is nothing near it, not even a tannery or tavern. In front is a garden running to the river, where once-clipped box shrubs now straggle. A few willows rooted in the stream form a half-hidden fence. Weeds have clothed the bank; fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, their suckers forming a thicket. Espaliers resemble a copse. The paths, once graveled, are overgrown; in fact, there is no trace of a path.“From the hilltop where the ruins of the old castle of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, you can see that this spot must once have been the joy of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture. An arbor, or rather its wreck, with a table still standing, is visible. The aspect of this garden evokes provincial peace, like reading the epitaph of a worthy tradesman. To complete the mournful impression, one wall bears a sundial with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’“The roof is dreadfully dilapidated; the outside shutters are always closed; swallows hang their nests from the balconies; the doors are shut. Straggling grasses green the flagstones of the steps; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, and peeled off the paint. The dreary silence is broken only by birds and cats, polecats, rats, and mice scampering around. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’“If you look from the street, you will see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of holes made by children. I later learned the door had been blocked for ten years. Through these breaches you see the courtyard side in the same ruinous state as the garden side: weeds on the paving-stones; walls scored by cracks; the blackened coping laced with pellitory. Stone steps are disjointed; the bell-cord is rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven could have fallen here? By what decree has salt been sown on this dwelling? Has God been mocked, or was France betrayed? We ask ourselves. Reptiles crawl over it, but give no reply. This empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.“It was formerly a little domain, held in fief, known as La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me in charge of a rich patient, the sight of this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. Was it not better than a ruin? Certain memories cling to a ruin; but this house, still standing though slowly destroyed by an avenging hand, contained a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once in the evening I climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braved scratches to enter the ownerless garden, and lingered for hours in its disorder. I would not have traded the story that produced this strange scene for any gossip. There I wove romances and indulged in melancholy that enchanted me. Had I known the reason for the neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that intoxicated me. To me, this refuge represented phases of human life shadowed by misfortune: sometimes the peace of a graveyard without the dead, sometimes the home of lepers, sometimes the house of the Atridae; but above all provincial life, contemplative and hour‑glass in its existence. I often wept there; I never laughed.“More than once I felt involuntary terrors at the dull hum of some passing wood‑pigeon overhead. The earth is dank; you must watch for lizards, vipers, and frogs wandering with nature’s wild freedom; above all, you must have no fear of cold, for in a few moments you feel an icy cloak settle on your shoulders, like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.“One evening I shuddered; the wind had turned an old rusty weathercock, and the creaking sounded like a cry from the house, just as I was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this monumental embodiment of woe. I returned to my inn, lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper, the hostess came into my room with an air of mystery, saying, ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said, and left the room.“Suddenly a tall, slim man dressed in black appeared, hat in hand, entering like a ram ready to butt, with a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘whom have I the honor of addressing?’ He took a chair, sat before my fire, placed his hat on my table, and replied while rubbing his hands: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’“I encouraged myself: seek!“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’“‘I am delighted to hear it, monsieur,’ I exclaimed. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’“‘One moment!’ he said, holding up his hand. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed that you sometimes walk in the garden of la Grande Bretèche.’“‘Yes, monsieur.’“‘One moment!’ he repeated. ‘That constitutes a misdemeanor. Monsieur, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I come in her name to beg you to discontinue the practice. One moment! I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you may be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. Nevertheless, you must be a man of education and should know that the laws forbid, under heavy penalties, any trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be quite content to make you free to come and go in the house; but being bound to respect the will of the testatrix, I beg that you will go into the garden no more. I myself, monsieur, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which, as I informed you, is part of the estate of the late Madame de Merret. We have done nothing there but verify the number of doors and windows to assess the taxes I must pay annually out of the funds left by the late Madame de Merret. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in the town.’“The good man paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility, understanding that administering Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life, his reputation, his glory. As I was forced to bid farewell to my reveries and romances, I declined the official truth.“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘would it be indiscreet if I asked the reasons for such eccentricity?’“At these words pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He drew up his collar, took out his snuffbox, offered me a pinch; on my refusing, he took a large one. He was happy! A man without a hobby does not know the good in life. In that moment I understood Sterne’s charming passion and had the perfect idea of the delight with which my uncle Toby, encouraged by Trim, bestrode his hobby‑horse.“‘Monsieur,’ said Monsieur Regnault, ‘I was head‑clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first‑rate house—you may have heard of it? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; among others, a wealthy aunt who allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, before my marriage, I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Chateau of Merret. Her maid, now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you that Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, plunging into every dissipation. You understand?“‘On the day he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted la Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some say she burnt the furniture and hangings—in short, everything used in furnishing the premises now let by the said M.—(Dear, what am I saying? I beg your pardon, I thought I was dictating a lease.)—In short, they say she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’“‘For about three months before, the Count and Countess had lived very eccentrically; they admitted no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor, Monsieur on the first floor. When the Countess was left alone, she was seen only at church. At home she refused to see friends. She was already much altered when she left la Grande Bretèche to go to Merret. That dear lady—I say dear, for it was she who gave me this diamond, though I saw her but once—was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; many fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was excited when I heard she needed my services. Nor was I the only one interested. That very night, all the town knew I was going to Merret.“‘The waiting‑woman replied vaguely; nevertheless, she told me her mistress had received the Sacrament that day and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the chateau. I went up the great staircase. After crossing some large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors about this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I repeated them), I had imagined a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty seeing her in the great bed where she lay. To light this enormous, old‑fashioned room thick with dust, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! but you have not been to Merret. The bed is of the old world, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table by the bed held an ‘Imitation of Christ,’ which, by the way, I bought for my wife, and the lamp. There was also a deep armchair for her confidential maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, not enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.“‘My dear sir, if you had seen that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt transported into a romance. It was icy—nay, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand theatrically and paused.“‘At last I saw Madame de Merret under the lamp’s glimmer. Her face was as yellow as wax and as narrow as two folded hands. She wore a lace cap showing abundant hair, white as linen thread. She sat up in bed, keeping upright with great difficulty. Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever and half‑dead, hardly moved beneath the bony arch of her eyebrows.—There,’ he added, pointing to his brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; the veins and muscles were perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; but now it was awful—sickness had consumed her into a phantom. Her pale‑violet lips seemed not to move when she spoke.“‘Though my profession has brought me to many deaths to record last wishes, families in tears and the agonies I have seen were as nothing compared with this lonely woman in her vast chateau. I heard no sound; I perceived not the breathing that ought to move the sheets, and I stood motionless, absorbed. In fancy I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back, and she uttered words like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to speak.“‘“Madame,” I began.’ She signed to me to be silent. The old housekeeper rose and whispered, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise.”“‘I sat down. A few moments later, Madame de Merret mustered all her strength, slipped her right hand under the bolster, paused, and with a last effort withdrew a sealed paper, drops of perspiration on her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix, lifted it to her lips, and died.“‘The expression of her eyes makes me shudder. She must have suffered much! Joy was in her last glance and remained stamped on her dead eyes.“‘I brought away the will, and when opened found she had appointed me her executor. She left most of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding la Grande Bretèche: she ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from her death, in the state it was at her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if necessary to secure fulfillment. At the expiration of that term, if the will has been duly carried out, the house becomes my heirs’ property, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise, la Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs‑at‑law, but on condition of fulfilling certain terms set forth in a codicil not to be opened until the fifty years expire. The will has not been disputed, so—’ And without finishing his sentence the lanky notary looked at me with triumph; I made him happy by offering congratulations.“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘you have so vividly impressed me I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’“‘Monsieur,’ said he with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.”“However, I soon loosened the tongue of the discreet notary, who communicated, not without long digressions, the opinions of Vendôme’s deep politicians. These were so contradictory and diffuse that I nearly fell asleep despite my interest. The notary’s ponderous voice and monotonous accent, used to listen to himself, were too much for my curiosity. Happily, he went away.“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ said he on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five‑and‑forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid his first finger to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—you must not be past sixty now.’“I closed my door, having been roused by this last speech, which the notary thought very funny; then I sat down in my armchair with my feet on the fire‑dogs. I had lost myself in a romance à la Radcliffe, built on the juridical base given me by Monsieur Regnault, when the door, opened by a woman’s cautious hand, turned on the hinges. I saw my landlady enter, a buxom, florid dame, always good‑humored, who had missed her calling in life. She was a Fleming, who ought to have been born in a Teniers picture.“‘Well, monsieur,’ said she, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of la Grande Bretèche?’“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’“‘And what did he tell you?’“I repeated in a few words the creepy story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence my hostess leaned forward, looking at me with an innkeeper’s keen scrutiny, a happy compromise between the instinct of a constable, the astuteness of a spy, and the cunning of a dealer.“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ I said as I ended, ‘you seem to know more about it. If not, why have you come up to me?’“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid everything down, so as never to have difficulties with anyone. He was hot‑tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’“‘Because he was hot‑tempered?’ I asked.“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose, sir, that a man had to have something to show for a figurehead before he could marry Madame de Merret, who, without any reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet‑looking, quite a gem. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’“‘And were they happy together?’“‘Hm, hm! so‑so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail‑fellow‑well‑met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?’“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When the Emperor sent Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the charge of the Government a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub‑prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—neither more nor less. He had a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name in my books; you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, which I admired. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, and among others General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to dislike him, though he scarcely spoke; if spoken to, he often did not answer—a way, a mania perhaps they have.“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the first time, no one imagined purpose. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening shortly after he arrived, the Spaniard stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait. He lodged in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he saw the Spanish grandee swimming in the river. When he came back, I warned him about the weeds; he seemed annoyed at being seen.A few mornings later we found he hadn’t returned. Searching his things I found a note, fifty doubloons (about five thousand francs), and a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he did not come back, he left the money and diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.My husband went searching for him and returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater by la Grande Bretèche. He had gone early so no one saw him. After reading the letter he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wish, announced that the Spaniard had escaped. The sub-prefect sent the constabulary, but he was never caught. Lepas thought he had drowned; I suspected involvement in the affair of Madame de Merret. Rosalie had told me that the crucifix her mistress treasured and was buried with was ebony and silver; Monsieur Férédia had possessed a similar crucifix early in his stay, which I never saw again. “Should I feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs?” my hostess asked. “Aren’t they truly mine?”“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”I left with vague, sinister thoughts—romantic curiosity and a religious dread, like entering a dark church at night and seeing a faint glimmer under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, filled my mind. I felt compelled to explore that dwelling and uncover the heart of this solemn story, the drama that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Beneath her blooming health I sensed an inner thought—sorrow or hope—her manner suggesting a secret: the look of devout souls who pray too much or a woman haunted by a past crime. Yet she was simple and clumsy; her vacant smile betrayed no cunning; the checked kerchief in her lilac-and-white-striped gown made her seem innocent. I resolved not to leave Vendôme without learning the full story of la Grande Bretèche, and if necessary to make advances toward Rosalie.“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, then regained composure—every woman from the highest lady to an inn-servant possesses presence of mind.“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”Her answer was evasive. Rosalie seemed central to the affair; she appeared tied to its core. This was no ordinary courtship matter—she contained the final chapter of a romance. All my attention turned to her. Studying her, I found admirable qualities: she was clean, neat, undeniably attractive; soon she embodied every charm desire can bestow. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one morning I said:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and prepared to tell the tale; there is an hour and an attitude for telling stories—this was one of them. If I reproduced Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence I would hardly do it justice. Her account stands between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s version; I will summarize it concisely.Madame de Merret’s sleeping room was on the ground floor; a small four-foot-deep wall cupboard served as her wardrobe. Three months before the incident Madame de Merret had been very ill, and her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. One evening he returned two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. Distracted by the billiards losses and the evening’s talk, he assumed his wife was in bed. For some time he had relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative replies encouraged him to go to his room, trusting her implicitly. That night he decided to check on his wife to share his misfortunes and perhaps find comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner; her convalescence had enhanced her beauty—a realization husbands often make too late.Upon entering he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. He sensed something wrong and noticed an anxious expression in her eyes. Rosalie entered, which surprised him. He paced the room, arms crossed.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly as Rosalie helped undress. He did not answer.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She wanted to be alone with her husband; Rosalie left, or seemed to, lingering in the passage. Monsieur de Merret then confronted his wife, bluntly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!”She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”This “No” pierced him; he did not believe her, yet she appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet but she took his hand and stopped him, saying with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”Her extraordinary dignity filled him with deep respect and inspired an almost legendary resolve.“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words Madame de Merret looked haggard. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”She took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” he said coldly. After a pause he remarked, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony-and-silver crucifix.“I found it at Duvivier’s,” she replied; “last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited until Rosalie returned, then instructed Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to watch the household and to come down and tell him when they were all asleep—mind, asleep. Monsieur de Merret had recently had new ceilings installed in all the ground-floor reception rooms. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme; he had ordered a large quantity intending to sell any surplus. That circumstance inspired his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he replied.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason. “Gorenflot,” her husband said, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered instructions: Gorenflot would sleep in the house that night; tomorrow he would be given a passport and six thousand francs to go abroad to a town Monsieur de Merret would specify. He must live in that country for ten years, travel through Paris and wait there until the master joined him; a further six thousand francs would be paid on return provided he kept silent. Rosalie was promised ten thousand francs payable at her wedding if she married Gorenflot and remained silent; otherwise no wedding gift.“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband walked the room, watching the door, the mason, and his wife without betraying suspicion. Gorenflot made noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to whisper to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. The silence was intentional: he gave her no chance to speak ambiguously; she showed pride or prudence. When the wall was half built the mason, seeing the master’s back, broke one of the two panes at the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret and Rosalie then saw the face of a dark, gloomy man with black hair and fierce eyes. Before her husband turned, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”At four o’clock in September the work was finished. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning he said casually, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He took his hat, paused, and grabbed the crucifix.
