His wife trembled with joy, thinking he would go to Duvivier’s. As soon as he left she rang for Rosalie, crying in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”Rosalie brought a cleaver; with desperate energy the mistress began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.He had foreseen what might happen in his absence and set a trap: he had only written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, glaring at his wife. “Jean,” he added to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel husband remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. In the early days, when there was noise in the closet and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he would say without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After this story all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some shuddered at the last words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration the Archbishop of Besançon frequently attended Baronne de Watteville’s drawing-room because of her devoutness.Monsieur de Watteville, descended from the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After a quiet life in La Franche-Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family; the match was arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following her grandparents’ deaths, Madame de Watteville became more devout, adopting a pious life that influenced Besançon’s upper circles.Monsieur de Watteville, thin and unremarkable in appearance, occupied himself as a turner and collector of local geological specimens. Many noted a certain nobility of soul in him: realizing early he would not dominate his wife, he immersed himself in work and hobbies.The Rupt residence reflected both families’ standing—grand rooms, elegant furnishings, and fine cuisine—with Monsieur sometimes acting as his own butler. Madame de Watteville’s fortune was considerable while her husband’s estate was modest. Their intimacy with the Archbishop drew several notable abbés to their home.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests warmly received Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit. The Vicar-General explained the success had been unexpected given the political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, for his decisive role.Curious about Savaron, Madame de Watteville inquired into his background. The Vicar-General said Savaron lived nearby but that his origins were unknown, which piqued the guests’ curiosity. Amedée de Soulas, present at the gathering, would later prove significant to the story that followed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often skirting customs. What is "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual borrowing. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, first derided, became a dominant European fashion for decades. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, yet French styles soon shifted in response.More recently England supplied French with terms like "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset's poetry, sparked a trend of stylish young men in Paris that spread to the provinces. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt ill at ease in the dull, military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained an ostentatious appearance, keeping a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and lavished expense on his own wardrobe while subsisting on scant income.Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was adept at economizing, often dining out to save on household meals. His costly grooming and clothes were a façade aimed at impressing the local aristocracy and winning Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and Parisian knowledge helped him cultivate a reputation as a well-informed young man despite financial strain.Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy, called "the Colony," kept strict social hierarchies. Amedee’s unusual fashions—like wearing trouser-straps—set him apart and bolstered his status as a "lion." The town’s nobles wished to appear modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee enabled that image.Amedee’s chief ambition was to marry Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict, religious household with little contact with the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye and instructed more in domestic skills than social polish. Sheltered from modern influences, she lacked awareness of the social expectations surrounding courtship.Despite a passive exterior, Rosalie had a resolute spirit inherited from her lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This dynamic created tension as Amedee courted Rosalie: the Baroness’s strictness led Rosalie to suppress any feelings for him, producing a complex relationship marked by maternal jealousy and Rosalie’s uncertainty.As Amedee pursued Rosalie, he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies to secure a favorable match while protecting his reputation. The town’s elite regarded him as charming, and gossip about a possible engagement circulated among the nobility. Yet Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence complicated his efforts; she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations around her. Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus illustrates the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville’s heart amid rigid social structures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie as ‘ALBERT SAVARON’ and left his card with local officials.”“The name Savaron is well-known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville interjected, “one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms, he must add a bar-sinister. The only remaining Brabant is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”“The bar-sinister marks a bastard,” Rosalie added, “but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble.”“Enough, that will do, mademoiselle!” the Baroness interposed.“You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville remarked, “and she knows it well.”“Please continue, Monsieur de Soulas.”“In a town like Besancon, where everything is catalogued, Albert Savaron was accepted without fuss by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to achieve—he sent his card to judges instead of calling. Three days later, nobody noticed him. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron remained overlooked because no one saw him.”“Does he not go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two, works until eight, breakfasts, works again, walks in his garden, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt inquired.“I live on Rue Neuve, overlooking his house, and my servant communicates with Jerome.”“And you gossip with Babylas?”“What else is there to do while riding?”“How did you come to hire him for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar-General.“The President of the Court assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man innocent and exposed the real culprits, leading to arrests. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant then gave him a difficult case, which he won. Given our situation, Monsieur Berryer advised we employ Savaron, predicting success. Upon meeting him, I felt confident—and was right.”“Is he truly extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in an outer room lined with law books and old oak. The room was simply furnished: an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains. I did not expect my advocate to be young. His appearance matched the setting; he wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” exclaimed Madame de Watteville.“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. Black hair streaked with gray, thick and curly; a round white throat, a splendid forehead scored by deep lines of thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and fiery eyes. Despite passion, he has a calm manner and a sweet voice that can thunder when required.”“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”“The second time I visited, he received me in his shabby bedroom. The third time, he had a frugal breakfast in his library; I observed him closely. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”“After studying his head,” the Abbe replied, “and hearing him answer a prominent Parisian lawyer, I believe he will eventually make a significant impact.”“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.The conversation moved on, but the Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone exceptional, unlike the mundane Count who had courted her. Albert Savaron presented enigmas. “Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him, replaying the Abbe’s words and fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, comparing him to the men at the table.“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she replied, lost in thought about the prelate’s hands. She recalled a lighted window seen from her bed and realized it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull.“What does that matter to you?” her mother replied, sensing her daughter’s unusual interest.“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” the Abbe answered.“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit during dinner,” Madame de Watteville remarked.“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting laughter. “But Monsieur de Grancey was so engrossed that I became interested.”After dinner, Rosalie listened for any mention of Albert Savaron, but aside from congratulations to the Abbe, he was not discussed. Eager for bed, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse Albert’s windows. When the hour arrived, she felt a thrill watching candlelight through the trees. She saw him writing and imagined the color of his furniture.“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.The education of girls poses serious challenges, since the future of a nation rests on mothers. The University of France long avoided this issue: Should girls be informed about everything? Should their minds be restrained? Religion often imposes restraint. Enlightening them too much brings premature maturity, while suppressing thought can produce explosive outbursts, as in Moliere’s Agnes. Rosalie’s crisis was triggered by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.The next morning, while dressing, Rosalie looked out to see Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered.Having seen him, Rosalie resolved to enter his house, uncover his mystery, hear his voice, and glimpse his eyes. She planned to achieve this.Throughout the day she embroidered with the concentration of someone who appears idle yet is deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.She visited her father in his workshop, suggesting he turn larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. As he delighted in the idea, she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding.“Don’t worry!” he replied, sharing her frustration with his wife.Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.The eagerly awaited Sunday arrived. Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled.“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.As she crossed the courtyard, her heart raced. She had never walked the streets like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and would forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church before Mass, prayed briefly, and checked if the Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression. His solemn demeanor suggested a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. Rosalie now fully understood the Vicar-General’s words. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a bold moment she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a place to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced Monsieur Giroud’s arrival, the time in the confessional felt fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended, and Albert had gone.“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”With this new desire, Rosalie focused intently on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie eagerly supported the plan for the Belvedere.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.She approved the design, choosing a spot that could not be seen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but would allow views into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor, while Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumors spread in Besancon.“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” added an Abbe.“No,” the young man corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt remarked sourly, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the Belvedere’s success, sensing superiority over others. No one suspected that the girl thought a witless goose had devised the plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, increasing the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger in Besancon. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was also launching a new venture. Through quiet connections among local merchants, he was starting a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, convinced of the need to promote Besancon as a trade hub.To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants eager to believe in their own enterprise.Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, letting his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while letting him think he was gaining. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.After the third issue, the Review gained recognition among French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, sparking discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though higher circles of Besancon dismissed it for liberal views.“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie who had shown him so much affection, subscribed and lent her the four issues already published. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact on her cannot be judged by ordinary standards. She hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages, she became convinced it reflected him, as critics noted Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men set out from Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking views. As they passed Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay while his friend Leopold continued the tour.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together. Leopold, calm and steady, was destined to be a notary in Paris. Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more troubled background: his mother had sacrificed everything for his education, hoping to secure his future. Passionate and sensitive, Rodolphe was often driven by desire, in contrast to Leopold’s temperamental restraint.In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple who informed him the woman was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were of modest means; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.The next day Rodolphe sought permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens, who rented to the Lovelaces. After hesitation he was allowed to explore. He noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who seemed to have an unusual background. Eager to meet Fanny, he encountered resistance from the protective Bergmanns.That evening Rodolphe heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later met Fanny and Gina, but their conversation was cut short when Rodolphe was attacked and wounded by an unknown assailant. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken back to safety.While he recovered Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, her affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.Fanny later revealed her husband was not as old as he had first appeared and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his feelings and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing he had great potential.Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past, sent news and then arrived. Tito’s presence stirred rivalry and fear in Rodolphe of losing Fanny’s affection.Despite these challenges Rodolphe and Fanny’s connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances—Rodolphe’s ambition, Fanny’s loyalty to her husband, and social constraints—loomed over them. As they navigated their feelings Rodolphe grappled with the tensions between love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions even as life’s realities threatened to overshadow their dreams.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed with the Bergmanns, watching Francesca almost unconsciously. He perceived in her both youthful imprudence and resolute character: sometimes resisting love, sometimes yielding. The old man treated her like a father, and her gratitude revealed an innate nobility. Rodolphe found Francesca and the situation a compelling enigma.Those days brought secret joys and melancholy, quarrels as pleasing as harmony. He grew more captivated by her constant tenderness and jealous over trifles.One evening, when Francesca said she wished to leave Gersau because it lacked luxuries, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating love of luxury with a love of art and beauty, insisting she had never complained in hardship. She mischievously suggested ordinary people naturally aspire to greatness, revealing her charm.Francesca then insisted Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to protect her husband’s reputation. She professed love for Rodolphe but demanded it remain hidden. She kissed him and left him astonished.The next day Rodolphe learned the Bergmanns’ lodgers had departed early. Restless, he went to Vevay and by the end of October to Geneva. To avoid town discomfort he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiries were about Italian refugees, especially a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord said the recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.Rodolphe’s first walk took him to Villa Diodati, whose beauty struck him. As he approached, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady, which left him dizzy. Learning they were at the Colonna residence, he felt a veil lift, confronting the implications of their past interactions: she was of rank.He feared he had been a mere whim. He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by the false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from asking to see the Princess, returning to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety.That evening his passion grew; he felt the need to raise his social standing to match Francesca’s. When he finally saw her at the concert she was radiant, and their eyes met in an electric moment. Her beauty and grace captivated him; he felt a profound connection and realized she had remained true to their past despite her rank.After the concert Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who playfully called herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of their time in Gersau; he was overwhelmed by her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.As days passed Francesca acknowledged Rodolphe’s deep love, which felt natural to her. She said their love should be faithful and persistent, whatever distance separated them. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition—he wanted to achieve greatness so Francesca could be proud of him.Francesca, with her blend of gaiety and tenderness, was remarkable. Born destined for a cloister, family tragedies forced her into marriage with Prince Gandolphini to preserve their status. Despite noble birth she preferred independence and liberal ideas and sympathized with revolutionary sentiments.Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva, where time flew in the company of the intelligent, beautiful Francesca. Yet as spring neared he felt inner turmoil: Francesca’s calm contrasted with his unrest, and he longed for more demonstrative feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.One day on the terrace Rodolphe voiced bitterness about others’ happiness, particularly that of a noblewoman. Francesca, wounded, drew back; Rodolphe followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she rebuffed him and asserted her dignity.Francesca then ordered Rodolphe to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Though stern, he complied, recognizing the strength of her will.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Returning to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, who had refused to paint several women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade the artist, but Francesca charmed him into producing an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced it in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence—their sole indulgence.Driven by ambition and love, Rodolphe invested all his resources in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years of struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but first traveled to Naples to heal his wounds and regain courage at Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their positions. Three months there revived him, restoring calm before new trials.He then tried to rebuild his fortune; his talents were recognized and, as reward for his dedication, a high post was promised. But the storm of July 1830 again thwarted his ambitions.Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts and daring attempts of this young man—endowed with fine qualities but without fortune's favor. Sustained by love, he returned to face new challenges, guided by an ever-supportive presence and a faithful heart.Lovers! Pray for him!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie’s cheeks burned and tears fell. The novel, in the then-popular mode of a man narrating his own love, was Rosalie’s first encounter with that voice, and it stirred her profoundly. Her agitation sprang from jealousy toward Francesca Colonna, whom she believed to be Albert’s beloved.Consumed by curiosity, Rosalie felt an urgent desire to win Albert despite insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent. Fearing he would not love her, she became determined to learn the truth. That night her imagination supplied schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert’s garden, writing to him, even visiting her father’s land to contrive an encounter.From Albert’s window she saw a scene promising insight: Jerome, Albert’s servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette’s plainness but also her relative financial security, which made her appealing to Jerome. The sight taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson—that her mother’s constraints could be bypassed by servants.The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized a chance to question her about Jerome. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, unexpectedly, offered assistance in exchange for access to letters Albert sent through Jerome. Relieved by Rosalie’s discretion, Mariette agreed.Rosalie’s obsession deepened when she read a letter Albert had sent to his friend Leopold. It revealed his noble lineage, past struggles, and a portrait of ambition and constancy that enthralled her. She was absorbed by his striving for success while remaining devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca.As she read more correspondence, Rosalie grew more aware of her inadequacy and the moral cost of her actions. She struggled with guilt over invading Albert’s privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion hardened into a perilous infatuation that led to penances and self-imposed suffering as attempts to demonstrate devotion.One evening Mariette brought another letter from Albert to Francesca, sending Rosalie into a spiral of jealousy and doubt. Torn over whether to read it, she yielded to the desire to know his feelings. The letter confirmed his ongoing love for Francesca and outlined his ambitions and hopes for their future.The revelation made Rosalie’s heart ache as she absorbed the depth of Albert’s devotion to another. Though she had at first resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself torn between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a clear moment she decided to step back, recognizing both the nobility of their bond and her own shortcomings.Preparing to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her emotions and the nature of love. She felt sorrow and resignation but also a yearning for the selfless, enduring love Albert and Francesca shared, even as she faced the reality of her unrequited feelings.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil, but by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude that struggle fueled her passion and dark plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; this story is a cautionary tale for them.That winter Albert de Savarus advanced his position in Besançon, eager for the Chamber’s dissolution. He had secured support from a wealthy contractor linked to the moderate party. The Romans had long been credited with supplying town water; Besançon drew its supply from Arcier, a distant hill. Restoring an aqueduct to drink “Roman” water seemed absurd given the Doubs river, yet persuading citizens would authorize large expenditures benefiting the contractor.Savarus argued the river water was unfit and promoted Arcier water. The local Review echoed him, and townspeople across parties agreed on drinking the Romans’ water. The issue became a focal point in Besançon, driven by hidden interests. Opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town, gaining influence without popularity.Throughout the winter he won several lawsuits for local priests, and ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in personal gain masked a form of moral usury: he anticipated a much larger reward.In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also qualified him for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who saw Savarus’s designs, remarked on his remarkable nature and suggested he had taken a wrong turn in life.Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, initially content to observe him. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, produced ten thousand francs a year but was poorly managed by the Baron, who left its oversight to an old servant, Modinier. The Baron's indifference stemmed from his wife's substantial income, and they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot developed by the famous Watteville.Watteville had constructed a dam and lake, enhancing the area’s beauty, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, which had long enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation that his descendants continued to assert.Rosalie’s maneuvers were subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, captivated by the lake and associating it with love. She suggested a boat trip, during which her father confided about legal troubles with Riceys over land rights. Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate.She argued that a lawsuit would add excitement and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey. Her determination to secure her future there was clear; she envisioned creating beautiful gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to consider political advantages, especially potential votes.Rosalie, determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election, wrote to the préfet revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her. She understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.As the election approached a meeting of influential men in Besançon decided to nominate Savarus, much to his surprise. He had been unaware of the political machinations. The Abbé, observing the situation, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision, captivating his audience. However, he faced challenges as the préfet worked to undermine him, eroding his voter base. Despite his efforts, the political tides shifted against him, and he struggled to maintain his position.On the day of the first voting the results disappointed Savarus; he felt the weight of his ambitions and the election’s pressure. He returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.Just as he neared hopelessness, the Abbé arrived with news of hope. The priest had labored behind the scenes to secure support, urging Savarus to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election.The Abbé revealed that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes, and he encouraged Savarus to pursue a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to further solidify his standing. Savarus was initially resistant but recognized the potential benefits.The Abbé’s guidance proved invaluable. Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, aware that his future depended on choices he would make in the coming days.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine o’clock the next evening, Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms held Besançon’s aristocracy, gathered over the unexpected decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. It was known the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, would be presented. Madame de Chavoncourt arrived with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder sister, secure in her relationship, had not bothered — in a small town such details are noticed. The Abbe de Grancey moved through the groups, listening and offering sharp comments that steered the talk.“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” The statesman said Berryer, isolated on his bench, was uncertain and might upset the Government if he rallied support. They discussed the Duc de Fitz-James’s nomination in Toulouse and how voting for Monsieur Savarus might align Republicans with them rather than with the Moderates.By nine-thirty Albert had still not appeared, and Madame de Watteville took his tardiness as an insult. Madame de Chavoncourt advised her not to let small matters distract from serious issues, suggesting Albert might be detained by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother’s exchange with Madame de Chavoncourt, noted her leniency toward Albert and hinted at a possible match between Sidonie and him.At ten Albert remained absent and impatience grew. Some gentlemen began to play cards; the Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to the window where Rosalie had withdrawn, exclaiming, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.“Jérôme!” Rosalie called to the servant below. When he confirmed that Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe de Grancey declared, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face betrayed a sudden joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended not to, wondering about her involvement.Back in the drawing-room, Monsieur de Watteville announced the shocking news of Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven only fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amédée de Soulas, and a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election. The Abbe de Grancey stationed himself near the Baroness to observe Rosalie, whose normally pale face now showed agitation.“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. At that moment a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did, he noticed Rosalie turn pale; he recognized the handwriting. He folded the letter and discreetly pocketed it, having exchanged glances with Rosalie that hinted at her feelings.“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie stopped him. “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” When he questioned how she knew the writing, her passionate reply revealed her: “I love him! What is the matter?” He told her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Rosalie, stunned, begged him to keep the news secret, realizing that if the election ended, so would the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On her way to Mass Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette how Albert had vanished. An elderly gentleman from Paris had visited Monsieur Savaron at the Hotel National. Savaron turned pale at the card; they met over an hour, and the visitor left with a large package. Savaron, distraught, went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other work.Savaron’s abrupt departure shook Besançon; the Prefect promptly granted him a passport. The affair inflamed local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, observing how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, though the Baroness fretted over finances. A match with Monsieur de Soulas was arranged; Rosalie rejected it, insisting she was content. Conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, and they endured a tense summer at les Rouxey. After Mariette and Jerome wed, the Baron invested in the estate—moves the Baroness resented. As tensions mounted, the Vicar-General sought to mediate.One day Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had sabotaged Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters, causing their separation. Horrified at the moral gravity of her deed, he urged her to seek forgiveness and to grasp the consequences.Rosalie’s guilt deepened when a clerk from Paris, acting for Albert, came to sell his belongings and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness for Rosalie and a desire to remain in solitude.When the Vicar-General shared this, he pressed Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept Monsieur de Soulas. She remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice what she perceived as her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing she had Rosalie’s support.Now of age, Rosalie encouraged her mother to marry, securing her own financial independence. With a substantial fortune she entered Parisian society, resolved to confront the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball she publicly revealed her part in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.Rosalie returned to Besançon after exposing the truth, but the emotional cost weighed heavily. The tale of love, betrayal, and ambition’s consequences left a lasting impact on all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her les Rouxey estate, riding, hunting, and improving the land, making her notable in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, appears younger, while her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has aged considerably. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”Mademoiselle de Watteville is known for her eccentric “vagaries.” Each year she visits the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps inspired by a grand-uncle who once broke through monastery walls to gain his freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon, reportedly to marry, though the true reason remains unclear. She returned after a steamboat boiler explosion on the Loire that cost her her right arm and left leg and scarred her face. Her health deteriorated; in constant pain, she now devotes herself to religious practices at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot, The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Hannequin, Leopold: Beatrix, Cousin Betty, Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman- Rhetore, Duc Alphonse de: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus: The Quest of the Absolute- Savarus, Albert Savaron de: The Quest of the Absolute- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Tinti, Clarina: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The story's middle: a pause where tension builds and characters reassess their choices.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyLETTERS OF TWO BRIDESBy Honore de BalzacTranslated by R. S. ScottDEDICATIONTo George SandYour name, dear George, lends a reflected radiance to my book—not from self-interest or diffidence, but to celebrate our lasting friendship, which has outlived the world's malice. This bond will not change. The names attached to my works gratify me amid the annoyance of their growing number; each new book brings fresh vexation, as if my pen dared to rival the world whose types I borrow. Would that future antiquarians found only great names and generous hearts among my friends! I prize this possession more than fickle popularity. For those who know you well, it is happiness to sign myself,Your friend,  DE BALZAC.  PARIS, June 1840.LETTERS OF TWO BRIDESFIRST PARTI. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.Sweetheart, I too am free! I am the first to write, unless you have already sent something to Blois.Compose yourself, lift those great black eyes, and save your transport for the letter about my first love. By the way, why always "first"? Is there to be a second?Don't interrupt; tell me first how you escaped from the convent where you were to take your vows. My deliverance was more prosaic. The cry of conscience outweighed stern policy. After you left, a melancholy seized me; my aunt feared I would pine away, and my mother—whose cure had been a novitiate—relented.So I am in Paris, thanks to you, my love! If you had seen me when I parted from you, you would have rejoiced in the impression you left on my young heart. We had been so close, sharing dreams and flights of fancy, that our souls seemed welded together. Without you I drooped like a turtle-dove widowed of her mate. Death smiled upon me; I was, in truth, proceeding to die. To be at Blois, at the Carmelites, consumed by dread of taking vows there, a Mlle. de la Valliere without her prelude, and without you! How could I not be sick unto death?How different it had been in the convent when imagination turned the monotony into a playground. But when your companionship ceased, I became a true Carmelite, drawing an empty pitcher. My aunt understood nothing of our inner life; her nunnery was her paradise on two acres. Religious life for girls our age demands either extreme simplicity or a burning devotion—my aunt's self-sacrifice had another object: a devoted brother. She could not have sacrificed herself for an abstraction.For a fortnight I swallowed reckless words, hid reflections, and accumulated confidences for your ear alone. Without writing I would have suffocated. I imagine you have begun a journal too and that soon I'll be at home in your Gemenos valley by the tale you will send me.On a certain morning a lady companion and Philippe—my grandmother's last valet—came from Paris to carry me off. When my aunt told me, I could only gaze. She kissed my forehead and said I had the pride that could lead to heaven or hell, but she trusted my nobility not to choose the downward path. I kissed her hands, swore not to return except by a broken heart, and warned that if my royal lover deserted me, death alone would tear him away.They escorted me to the carriage. At Beaugency, night fell; I pondered what awaited me in the world for which I had hungered.At home I found no one to receive me—my mother was at the Bois de Boulogne, my father at the Council, my brother rarely home. Miss Griffith and Philippe showed me to my rooms—my grandmother's suite, left as she had kept it. I was to sleep in the bed where she died.Sitting on the sofa, I burst into tears, seeing the room as if the Princess had but just passed away. Many objects that should have been removed remained, showing the carelessness of busy people. Philippe told me the Princess's furniture had been left to me by will, and my father had left the grander suites dismantled, awaiting restitution of fugitive property when a law should pass. The king's architect had estimated repairs at three hundred thousand livres.I sank into despair. Would my father spend that sum restoring the house instead of arranging a marriage for me? At first I felt profaned by the relics of the Princess's rooms; then, memories softened me. The faint scent of her powder, the yellow damask curtains, her portrait at twenty-five—all soothed me. I set Philippe to polish and arrange the furniture, giving the dear old things an air of youth.My bedroom is white, harmonizing with faded Savonnerie tapestry the Princess received from Louis XV. A timepiece from the Marshal de Saxe, china ornaments from Marshal de Richelieu—curiosities abounded. The Princess's portrait opposite the King's hinted at her independence; once, when ill, she refused to admit the Prince before the doctor: "He can come in with the doctor and his drugs," she said.Her bed has a canopy, and the fireplace is a marvel—the hearth was the center of last-century life: a gilt grate, chased fire-irons, a fan-like screen from the Gobelins, exquisite bellows. She was a coquette to the last; even in old age she tended her appearance as if awaiting Court visitors. I had adored being with her; she had been free-thinking and witty, a perfect companion for my childhood.I remembered how abruptly she parted from me in 1816. She received me without surprise and urged me not to become a victim in the convent: "You shall be independent and able to marry whom you please." Six months later she died; her will was entrusted to Prince de Talleyrand, who saw that she forbade my taking vows. I hope to learn more from him.Thus, having no one in flesh to greet me, I took consolation in the Princess's shade and resolved to keep our pledge—to tell each other the smallest details of our lives. Send me a faithful picture of every trifle around you, even the sunset lights among the tall trees.October 19th.At half-past five Rose told me my mother had returned, so I went down. My mother lives in a suite on the ground floor opposite mine, connected by a secret staircase. My father's rooms are in the opposite wing. These ancestral mansions are so roomy that my parents still occupy ground-floor rooms and can receive guests there.I wondered how my mother, who had shown so little maternal feeling, would greet me. She met me with tact, without theatrics—neither cold nor effusive—kissing my forehead."My dear little one," she said, "if you were to die at the convent, it is much better to live with your family. You frustrate our plans, but the age of blind obedience is past. I want you to see the world. I hope to be a sister to you."She was charming. At thirty-eight she remains exquisitely beautiful—dark-blue eyes, a smooth forehead, a pink-and-white complexion, marvelous bust and shoulders, a slender waist. I repaid her with affection; her manner warmed. She suggested I should not make my first appearance in society without being in the dressmaker's hands, and I agreed.My father then appeared and warmly received me, more with the air of a lover than a father."So this is my rebel daughter!" he cried, kissing me. "The pleasure of watching your success in society will atone for our disappointment."He introduced my brother, Alphonse, the Duc de Rhetore, who greeted me with a polite kiss. They urged me not to make a sudden public appearance, and I withdrew to tidy my rooms and write to you.This is the truthful account of an eighteen-year-old returning after nine years to one of France's noblest families. Tired, I went to bed at eight, and they preserved a little Saxe service the Princess favored.II. THE SAME TO THE SAME November 25th.The next day my rooms were arranged and flowers placed by Philippe. I began to feel at home, though Rose was baffled by my convent habits. At one o'clock my father knocked and found me writing to you."You will need clothes and comforts," he said, placing a purse containing twelve thousand francs for my expenses. "Arrange with your mother for a governess if Miss Griffith displeases you. A carriage and man-servant shall be at your disposal.""Let me keep Philippe," I pleaded."So be it," he said. "You have money enough not to be a burden. Your grandmother left you five hundred thousand francs—her savings, placed in government stock, now yield about forty thousand francs a year. I had intended to make an independence for your younger brother, but you have upset my plans; we will see. Your pride marks how a Chaulieu daughter should behave. Guards are an insult in our family; a slander might cost the life of the man who utters it or of one of your brothers. Enough."His frankness pleased me. Philippe spent the morning arranging my metamorphosis. The dressmaker Victorine, a woman for underclothing, a shoemaker, a corset-maker—all arrived. Janssen, the operatic shoemaker, declared I had my mother's foot. A glovemaker measured my hand; my trousseau accumulated.At a meal my mother proposed we go to the milliner's so I could form my taste. This burst of independence dazzled me. I am like a blind man regaining sight; the world I had longed for revealed itself. Yet the family seemed to live apart. My father was preoccupied—he had been offered the Ministry and refused, preferring an embassy to public debate. He and my brother spend little time at home; my mother devotes the day to toilette and society; she receives visitors, drives, dines out, and attends entertainments—there are twelve theatres in Paris. Our family life consists of lunch and dinner; otherwise we live separately. In Paris, it seems a miracle to love those you live with, for you scarcely see them.Miss Griffith, whom my mother recommended and whom I decided to keep, is a Scotch governess, thirty-six, daughter of a clergyman, who will teach me English and act as chaperon. She is well-bred, proud, and poor; she seems kind but cautious. She opens her heart to me only slowly, warning me not to be dazzled by society and to guard against what attracts me. Her counsel is simple and persistent.I have not yet set foot beyond the garden; the Italian opera opens soon, and my mother has a box. I am thrilled to hear Italian music and see French acting. Already convent habits give way to society's. I read a great deal, though indiscriminately. Corinne and Adolphe pleased me most. When I asked to see Mme. de Stael, my family laughed—my mother gently told me Mme. de Stael was dead. When I asked Miss Griffith how a woman could be betrayed, she replied, "Why, of course, when she loves." Tell me, Renee, do you think one could be betrayed by a man?III. THE SAME TO THE SAME December.My Darling,—Here I am ready to make my bow. In preparation I have rehearsed every folly. This morning I inspected myself—stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments—discovering an air of triumph. Griffith watched my doll's play. You think you know me? You are mistaken.Here is a portrait of your sister, formerly disguised as a Carmelite, now alive as a society girl. I am one of the greatest beauties in France—Provence excepted. I have my faults, but if I were a man they would be adored; they arise from promise. My wrists are fine, my shoulders bony though the contours are bold and delicate; a flood of health and blue blood shows through transparent skin. I have the foot of a gazelle, finely turned joints, Greek-correct features. My eyes are insolent blue, lashes long, forehead bright, hair pale gold with darker center locks. The hairdresser wanted to band it in two and fasten a pearl across my brow—his Medieval fancy—but I declined.My nose is slender and imperious, the mouth a little large but expressive, the teeth bright. There is harmony in my appearance—gait, voice, the flash of dimples. I can laugh or look Madonna-melancholy, as suits my purpose. I take after my father—shrewd and subtle—and have my grandmother's manner and voice. My mother is majestic rather than playful; she wounds without repairing. We are unlike, so rivalry seems unlikely.Society has such a power that people live incessantly in its grasp from nine till two or three in the morning, spending strength and money. How those of one family can live side by side and know nothing of each other astonishes me—perhaps deliberate blindness is part of some domestic diplomacy.Tomorrow, I come out. My dress is white muslin with a garland of white roses in Greek style; I shall wear my Madonna face, play the simpleton, and win the women. My father has given me many pretty gewgaws—a dressing-case, toilet service, scent-box, fan, sunshade, prayer-book, gold chain, cashmere shawl—and promises riding lessons. I have a dancing-master each morning; I must dance in a month to go to the ball.My brother honors me with contempt; he is handsome but melancholy, jealous of my father, with no post at Court. My mother and father are absorbed in their pursuits; none of us practice self-examination. I have leisure to meditate, and in that spare time I mean to probe this family's mystery.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yesterday at two o'clock I drove through the Champs-Elysées and the Bois de Boulogne on a beautiful autumn day that reminded me of the Loire. I finally experienced Paris. The Place Louis XV is impressive, a testament to human artistry.I was impeccably dressed, pensive and poised, yet not a single smile was aimed at me. The carriage moved slowly, matching my demeanor. Only one person, a duke—my father—acknowledged me with satisfaction; my mother sent a butterfly kiss. Griffith, ever observant, scanned indiscriminately. A young woman should know where to look.I was furious. One man even inspected my carriage without seeing me—probably a carriage-maker. I had misjudged my appeal; beauty, it seems, is common in Paris. I saw hats tipped to less deserving people while my mother received admiration. I am determined to understand this.The men were generally unattractive, with a few poor imitations among them. Their fashion lacks the elegance, color, and comfort of previous generations. Hats, like truncated columns, sit awkwardly on the head. It seems easier to incite a revolution than to create a stylish hat. Despite their fickle reputation, Frenchmen cling to these ridiculous styles.Overall the men felt unappealing, their worn faces reflecting ambition thwarted and vanity bruised. A fine forehead was rare. "And these are the products of Paris!" I said to Miss Griffith. "Most cultivated and pleasant men," she replied. I was silent; a thirty-six-year-old spinster's heart is a well of tolerance.In the evening I went to a ball, staying close to my mother, who received all attention. I was merely a pretext for compliments aimed at her. She found me partners who all noted the heat and beauty of the ball, as if I were freezing and blind. Each man claimed it was his first time seeing me.My dazzling dress paled beside the extravagant attire of married women, each surrounded by admirers and watching one another closely. A few shone with actual beauty, my mother among them. At a ball a girl is merely a dancing machine, insignificant.The men impressed me no more at the ball than on the Champs-Elysées: exhausted, bland, and uniform. The proud, vigorous men of our ancestors seem vanished. One man, however, stood out for his beauty yet failed to stir the expected feeling. I did not know his works, and he lacked noble birth. No matter his genius, a commoner could not touch me. He seemed absorbed in himself, suggesting intellectuals view us as objects. When they love, they should abandon their writing; otherwise their love is not genuine. I learned he is a professor and author, driven by ambition.I resolved not to quarrel with society for failing to recognize my merits and instead embraced the simple joy of dancing, which I thoroughly enjoyed. I overheard much trivial gossip about people I did not know; perhaps my ignorance kept me from enjoying it, though many seemed to relish such talk. Society is a web of intrigue, and I am perceptive.Returning home pleasantly tired, I began sharing my experiences with my mother. She interrupted, advising me to confide only in her. "My dear child," she said, "it requires as much tact to know when to be silent as when to speak."Her advice revealed feelings that should remain concealed, even from a mother. I realized the vast realm of feminine duplicity. In our innocence we would seem astute gossipers; a mere gesture or glance can teach profound lessons. Suddenly I felt overwhelming shyness. Must I never again express my joy in dancing? What of deeper emotions?I went to bed sorrowful, reeling from the clash between my candid, joyful nature and society's harsh rules. Already the hedges of my life are flecked with white wool! Farewell, beloved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################How deeply your letter moved me, especially as I reflect on our diverging paths: you toward a brilliant world, I toward a modest, peaceful retreat.In the Castle of Maucombe my room is unchanged, now with a fine view of the Gemenos valley that I once saw without understanding. Shortly after my arrival my parents took me to dine with M. de l'Estorade, a wealthy old man who prospered by frugality.He lost his only son to Bonaparte's conscription in 1813. After evading the draft the son joined the Emperor's bodyguard but vanished after the Battle of Leipzig. The Baron clung to hope and hoarded his income for him. I eventually learned the son’s unexpected return explained my own: who could have imagined that my future husband was making his way back from Russia, Poland, and Germany? His fortune turned in Berlin, where the French Minister helped his return. The Baron, a small landowner in Provence with an income of about ten thousand livres, has not become famous enough to attract notice for his son's adventures.The combined capital from the late Mme. de l'Estorade's estate and the father's savings is around two hundred and fifty thousand livres, besides property—quite a fortune in Provence. The Baron recently bought a poorly managed estate, planning to plant ten thousand mulberry trees. Now that his son is found, his one desire is to marry him to a girl of good family.My parents consented when they heard that Renée de Maucombe could be accepted without a dowry and that her future inheritance would be secured in the marriage contract. My younger brother Jean de Maucombe signed a paper acknowledging an advance on his inheritance. This is how Provençal nobles navigate Bonaparte’s restrictive Civil Code, which often sends girls into convents rather than into marriage.The dinner was the first meeting of your sweetheart and the exile. My father-in-law-to-be has no castle, only a simple country house at the entrance of a lovely valley. It is unpretentious—four pebble walls and a red-tiled roof. The garden is typically Provençal, enclosed by low walls of rounded stones and showing neglect.Despite the simplicity, the house did its best for dinner, serving us on old silver. The exile, my dear, is like the railing—emaciated, pale, marked by suffering. At thirty-seven he looks much older, with streaks of white in formerly beautiful hair. He is a little deaf, like the Knight of the Sorrowful Countenance.Still, I have agreed to become Mme. de l'Estorade; the marriage secures our combined fortune of about two hundred and fifty thousand livres, and I’ve stipulated that I be allowed to transform the grange into a park. I have asked my father for a water grant to help this. In a month I will be Mme. de l'Estorade, after making a good impression. After Siberia, Louis de l'Estorade seems pleased to marry the fair Renée de Maucombe.While you prepare to enjoy Paris’s vivid life, I descend from our shared dreams into the humble realities of country life. I am determined to bring joy to this young man, who has known little youth, having gone straight from his mother's arms to war and the harshness of Siberia.I will seek pleasure in quiet country labors, enhancing the oasis around our home with fine trees and greenery. I envision a park on the hillside, perhaps a kiosk from which I can glimpse the Mediterranean. I will cultivate orange and lemon trees and embrace the poetry of the surrounding nature. My father-in-law and the Chevalier share my religious sentiments.My life will follow a smooth highway, free from the turmoil of another Bonaparte. If I have children, they will be mine to nurture and guide. If you remain true to your destiny, your children will have a devoted protector in me.Thus I bid farewell to the romances and adventures we once imagined. My milestones will now revolve around the care of the young Masters de l'Estorade and the mischief they bring. I will embroider their caps and be cherished by a frail man in the Gemenos valley. Perhaps one day I may visit Marseilles, but provincial life is mostly safe. We will take interest in the silkworms whose mulberry leaves we sell, and share in the ordinary ups and downs of Provence. Quarrels are unlikely, for M. de l'Estorade has promised to leave household matters to me, and I shall see that he keeps that promise.You, dear Louise, will provide the romance in my life. Tell me all your adventures, your balls and parties, your dress and the men you meet. Your other self will always be with you, listening and feeling the excitement. If only I could enjoy some Parisian fun while you managed the household at La Crampade—the name I give our grange. Poor M. de l'Estorade thinks he is marrying one woman—will he discover there are two?I am rambling, and since I can be foolish only through you, I should conclude. One kiss on each cheek—my lips remain untouched, for he has only taken my hand. Our propriety is quite amusing, I assure you. I must go now. Goodbye, dear.P.S. I have just opened your third letter. I have about one thousand livres to spend; please buy me pretty things we cannot find here or even in Marseilles. While you pursue your affairs, think of the recluse at La Crampade. Remember, neither family has anyone with taste in Paris to make purchases. I will answer your letter later.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################VI. DON FELIPE HENAREZ TO DON FERNAND PARIS, September.The heading of this letter shows the head of your house is safe. The massacre of our ancestors in the Court of Lions forced us into a double identity, but it also taught us an Arab cunning that may have saved my life.Ferdinand’s fear made his acting convincing enough for Valdez to believe him. Without my intervention the Admiral would have perished. The Liberals still fail to understand kingship. I know this Bourbon well; his repeated assurances of protection confirm my suspicions. A true Spaniard need not repeat promises; excess words often signal deceit.Valdez left on an English ship. As soon as I saw the cause of my beloved Spain falter in Andalusia, I set my escape in motion. I instructed my steward, sent coral fishers to meet me on a coast, and while Ferdinand sought to seize me, I was already in my barony of Macumer, among brigands who defy authority.The last Hispano-Moorish family of Granada has found refuge in an African desert, even a Saracen horse on an estate inherited from Saracens. The brigands, who once feared my authority, now shield me from the King of Spain's vendetta, proud to defend the Duc de Soria, a Henarez—the first to visit them since the Moors ruled.I had hoped to live off this estate’s income, but Queverdo’s reports were right. He had twenty-two lives at my disposal but not a real one; vast prairies without houses, virgin forests with no furniture. A million piastres and a resident master for fifty years would be needed to make these lands profitable. I must address this.The conquered have time to reflect. Seeing my noble country reduced to a corpse for monks to exploit moved me to tears; it foreshadows Spain’s grim future.In Marseilles I learned of Riego's fall. It struck me that my life would be a prolonged, unnoticed martyrdom. Is existence worth living when one can neither die for country nor live for a woman? To love and conquer—this was our law. Yet my sabre is broken, the palace in ruins, the living spring dried in barren sand.Here is my last will and testament.Don Fernand, you now see why I restrained your enthusiasm and urged loyalty to the rey netto. As your brother and friend I implore you to obey; as your master I command. You will approach the King and claim my dignities, property, office, and titles. He may hesitate, but tell him you are loved by Marie Heredia and she can marry none but a Duc de Soria. This will please the King: the Heredia fortune has long been a barrier to my ruin. Your proposal will seem to deprive me of a final resource, and he will gladly hand over my spoils.You will then marry Marie. Her secret love for you was no secret to me, and I have prepared the old Count for your ascension. Marie and I discharged our family duties; everything favors you. You are handsome and possess Marie's heart. I am an ill-favored grandee for whom she harbors an unconfessed aversion; any reluctance from her due to my misfortunes you will soon overcome.As Duc de Soria, you will not regret my predecessor nor lose a maravedi. I will keep my mother's diamonds for my independence; the gap they leave can be filled by Marie's jewels. Send them with my nurse, old Urraca—the only servant I shall retain. No one else makes my chocolate as she does.During our short revolution, my toil was reduced to bare necessities, covered by my salary. You will find the income from the last two years with your steward. This sum is mine, but a Duc de Soria cannot marry without expenses, so we will share it. You will not refuse this wedding gift from your brigand brother; I intend it so.The barony of Macumer, not Spanish territory, remains mine. Thus I still have a country and a name should I wish to re-enter the world.Thank Heaven, this concludes our business, and the house of Soria is saved!Just as I resign myself to being merely Baron de Macumer, the French cannon announce the arrival of the Duc d'Angouleme. You will understand why I must stop here.October.When I arrived I had not ten doubloons. A poor leader finds means to live within calamities he cannot avert: for the vanquished Moor, a horse and the desert; for the frustrated Christian, the cloister and a few gold pieces.My resignation is weariness. I am not ready to abandon all thoughts of life. Ozalga gave letters of introduction, including one to a bookseller who serves our compatriots as Galignani does the English. He found me eight pupils at three francs a lesson. I teach every other day, earning twelve francs—more than I need. When Urraca arrives I will pass on my connection to another Spanish exile.I lodge in Rue Hillerin-Bertin with a poor widow who takes boarders. My room faces south, overlooking a small garden. It is quiet; I have green trees to gaze on, and I spend one piastre a day. I am amazed at the calm pleasure of this life, reminiscent of Dionysius at Corinth. From sunrise until ten I smoke and enjoy chocolate, contemplating two Spanish plants—a broom rising from a clump of jessamine—gold on white, colors that thrill any scion of the Moors. At ten I give lessons until four, return for dinner, then read and smoke until bed.I can endure this life of work, meditation, solitude, and society for a long time. Be happy, Fernand; my abdication brings no regrets. Five days have passed since I wrote my will; they feel like five centuries. Honor, titles, wealth—as if they never existed.Now that the conventional barriers of respect are down, I open my heart to you, dear boy. Though outwardly grave, my heart is full of tenderness and devotion—never granted to any object, not even she who was to be my bride since childhood. This is the source of my political enthusiasm. Spain took the place of a mistress and received my homage. Now Spain is lost. Beggared of all, I gaze upon the ruins of my former self and ponder the mystery of my existence.Why was life given this carcass, and when will it depart? Why did a race famed for chivalry infuse all its virtues into this last offshoot only for the seed not to sprout—no stem, no flower to scatter its Eastern perfumes? What crime did I commit before birth to inspire no love? Was I destined to be stranded, a useless hulk on a barren shore? In my soul I see deserts where my ancestors roamed under a scorching sun that shrivels life. Proud remnant of a fallen race, vain force, love wasted—an old man in the prime of youth—I shall await death's last grace. Alas! under this murky sky no spark will rekindle these ashes. Thus my last words may echo Christ's: "My God, Thou hast forsaken me!" A cry of agony and terror whose depth few mortals have explored.You can now understand, Fernand, the joy I find in living through you and Marie. I will watch you with the pride of a creator satisfied with his work. Love each other well and continue to do so; any discord between you would pain me more than it would you.Our mother had a premonition that events would one day fulfill her wishes. Perhaps a mother's longing is a pact with God. Was she not one of those beings who converse with Heaven and return with visions? How often I read in her brow that she coveted for Fernand the honors and wealth of Felipe! When I mentioned this she would weep, revealing a heart that rightly belonged to both her sons, yet was given to you alone by an irresistible passion.Her spirit will hover above you as you bow at the altar. Mother, have you no caress for your Felipe now that he has yielded to your favorite even the girl you regretfully thrust into his arms? What I have done pleases our womenfolk, the dead, and the King; it is God's will. Do not resist, Fernand; obey and remain silent.P.S. Tell Urraca to call me nothing but M. Henarez. Do not mention me to Marie. You must be the sole living soul to know the secrets of the last Christianized Moor, whose veins carry the blood of a great family that began in the desert and is now about to die out in a solitary exile.Farewell.VII. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBEWhat! To be married so soon? This is unheard of. After a month you become engaged to a stranger about whom you know nothing. He may be deaf—there are many kinds of deafness! He could be sickly, tiresome, insufferable!Don’t you see, Renée, what they want from you? You’re needed to carry on the glorious stock of the l’Estorades, that is all. You will be buried in the provinces. Are these the promises we made each other? Were I you, I’d rather set off to the Hyères islands in a caique, risk capture by an Algerian corsair, and be sold to the Grand Turk. Then I could be a Sultana and make a stir in the harem while I’m young—yes, and afterwards too!You are leaving one convent for another. I know you; you are a coward, and you will submit to family life with docility. But I will guide you; you must come to Paris. There we shall drive men wild and hold court like queens. Your husband may join the Chamber in three years. I know how those members work; you will work that machine well; you can live in Paris and become, as my mother calls it, a woman of fashion. Oh! do not think I will leave you in your grange!Monday.For a fortnight I have been living the life of society: one evening at the Italiens, another at the Grand Opera, and always a ball afterward. Society is bewitching. The Opera’s music enchants me; while my soul is immersed, I am the focus of admiration and opera glasses. But a single glance makes the boldest youth drop his eyes.I’ve seen charming young men, yet none have stirred me; not one has evoked the emotion I feel when listening to García in his splendid duet with Pellegrini in Otello. How jealous Rossini must have been to express jealousy so well! What a cry in "Il mio cor si divide!" I speak Greek to you, for you’ve not heard García, but you know how jealous I am!What a wretched dramatist Shakespeare is! Othello wins battles and leaves Desdemona meekly neglected. Such a sheep deserves slaughter. Let the man I love beware of thinking of anything but loving me!I prefer the long trials of old chivalry. That lout of a young lord who took offense because his sovereign lady sent him among the lions to fetch her glove was impertinent. Doubtless the lady had some exquisite prize for him, which he lost—the puppy!But I ramble. My father is likely to represent our master the King in Madrid. I say "our" master, for I shall be part of the embassy. My mother wishes to remain; my father will take me to have a woman with him.This seems simple, but there are complications. In a fortnight I have probed the household’s secrets. My mother would accompany my father to Madrid if he took M. de Canalis as secretary. But the King appoints secretaries; the Duke cannot offend the King, who hates opposition. The cunning diplomat thinks he cut the knot by leaving the Duchess here. M. de Canalis, the great poet of the day, cultivates my mother’s society and studies diplomacy with her from three to five. Diplomacy must be a fine subject, for he keeps at it as regularly as a gambler at the Stock Exchange.The Duc de Rhetore, our elder brother, solemn and capricious, will remain in Paris. Miss Griffith has discovered Alphonse is in love with a ballet girl at the Opera. How can one fall in love with legs and pirouettes? Our brother only attends when Tullia dances; he applauds and leaves. Two ballet girls in a family are more destructive than the plague.
