My second brother is with his regiment; I have not yet seen him. Thus I must act as the Antigone of His Majesty's ambassador. Perhaps I may marry in Spain; perhaps my father imagines a marriage without a dowry. He asked if I’d accompany him and offered me the use of his Spanish master."Spain, the country for castles in the air!" I exclaimed. "Perhaps you hope it will mean marriages for me!"He only gave a knowing look. For days he has teased me at lunch; he watches me, and I dissemble, playing cruelly with him as father and ambassador in petto. He asked my opinion of some young men and girls. I answered with inane remarks about hair, faces, and figures. He seemed disappointed by my crassness and inwardly blamed himself for asking."Still, father," I added, "don’t suppose I’m saying what I really think: mother made me afraid the other day that I had spoken too frankly about my impressions.""With your family, you can speak quite freely," said my mother."Very well, then," I continued. "The young men I’ve met are too self-centered to excite interest; they’re taken up with themselves. They put on an expression to talk to us, then drop it, apparently satisfied that we don’t use our eyes. The man as he converses is the lover; silent, he is the husband. The girls are so artificial that it’s impossible to know them, except from their dancing; their figures are not a sham. But the alarming thing is society’s brutality. Small incidents when supper is announced give one an idea—comparing small things with great—of what a popular uprising might be. Courtesy is a thin veneer over general selfishness. I imagined society to be very different. Women count for little in it; perhaps this is a survival of Bonapartist ideas.""Armande is coming on extraordinarily," said my mother."Mother, did you think I would never get beyond asking to see Mme. de Staël?"My father smiled and rose.Saturday.I left out one thing. The love we pictured is extremely well hidden; I have seen no trace. True, quick exchanges of glances occur in drawing-rooms, but how colorless it all is! Love as we imagined it—a world of wonders, glorious dreams, charming realities, sorrows that awaken sympathy, smiles bringing sunshine—does not exist. The bewitching words, constant interchange of happiness, misery of absence, joy of the beloved’s presence—where are they? What soil produces these radiant flowers of the soul? Which is wrong? We or the world?I have seen hundreds of men; not one has moved me. No proof of admiration or devotion on their part, not even a sword drawn in my defense, would have touched me. Love is so rare that one might live a lifetime without meeting the being who can make one happy. The thought chills; if found too late, what then?For days I have trembled at the thought of women’s destinies and begun to understand why so many wear sad faces beneath social excitement. Marriage is mere chance. Look at yours. A storm of wild thoughts swept me. To be loved every day the same, yet with a difference, to be loved as much after ten years as on the first day—such love demands years. The lover must languish; curiosity must be piqued and satisfied, feelings stirred and reciprocated.Is there a law for the inner fruits of the heart, as for nature's visible fruits? Can joy be made lasting? In what measure should love mix tears with pleasure? The convent’s monotony seems the only real life; while wealth, splendor, tears, delights, triumph, joy, and the satisfaction of an equal, shared, sanctioned love appear idle visions.I see no room in this city for the gentle ways of love—precious walks, the full moon on water, the suitor pleading in vain. Rich, young, and beautiful, I have only to love, and love would be my life; yet in three months nothing has appealed to me among the bright, covetous eyes here. No voice has thrilled me, no glance brightened my world.Music alone has filled my soul; it has even taken the place of our friendship. At night I linger by my window, summoning the future from its mystery. Sometimes, setting out to drive, I dismount and walk the Champs-Élysées imagining the man who will awaken my slumbering soul is near. Instead I meet mountebanks, vendors, jugglers, and secret lovers. I am tempted to stop them, ask, "You who are happy, tell me what is love." But I repress the impulse and return to my carriage, swearing to die an old maid. Love is an incarnation, and how many conditions must be met before it can occur! We are not certain we shall never quarrel with ourselves; how much less certain with another? Only God can solve this.I begin to think I’ll return to the convent. If I stay in society I may do foolish things, for I cannot reconcile what I see with my sense of delicacy and principle.Ah! my mother is the happiest, adored by Canalis, her great little man. I sometimes crave to know what goes on between them. Griffith tells me she has passed through such moods; she has longed to lash out at women whose happiness is evident and blackened their characters. According to her, virtue is burying savage instincts in one’s heart. But then what becomes of the heart? It becomes a sink for the worst in us.It is humiliating that no admirer has yet appeared for me. I am marriageable, but having brothers and a family sensitive about honor may keep men away. If that is why, they are cowards.The parts of Chimène in the Cid and the Cid himself delight me. What a marvelous play! Farewell.VIII. THE SAME TO THE SAME January.Our master is a poor refugee, hiding because of his part in the revolution quelled by the Duc d’Angouleme—a triumph that has led to splendid fêtes. Though a Liberal and likely a man of the people, he has piqued my interest; I suspect he was condemned to death. I make him talk to uncover his secret, but he has a truly Castilian taciturnity, proud as if he were Gonsalvo di Cordova, yet angelic in patience and gentleness. His pride is not irritable like Miss Griffith’s; it belongs to his nature, compelling civility through perfect manners and keeping us at a distance with the respect he shows.My father says much nobility dwells in Señor Henarez, whom he humorously calls Don Henarez. A few days ago I took the liberty of thus addressing him. He raised his eyes, usually cast down, and flashed a look that abashed me; he certainly has the most beautiful eyes imaginable. I asked if I had offended, and he replied in grand, rolling Spanish:"I am here only to teach you Spanish."I blushed and felt snubbed. I nearly retorted sharply, but remembering what our dear mother used to say, I instead replied:"It would be kind to tell me if you have any complaints."A tremor passed through him; the blood rose in his olive cheeks. He answered with some emotion:"Religion must have taught you, better than I can, to respect the unhappy. Had I been a don in Spain and lost everything in Ferdinand VII.'s triumph, your witticism would be unkind; but if I am only a poor language teacher, is it not heartless satire? Neither is worthy of a young lady of rank."I took his hand, saying:"In the name of religion, I beg you to pardon me."He bowed, opened my Don Quixote, and sat.This little incident disturbed me more than a harvest of compliments and glances. During the lesson I watched him closely, which I could do safely, as he never looks at me.From my observations, the tutor, whom we assumed to be forty, is under thirty. My governess remarked on the beauty of his black hair and pearly teeth. His eyes are velvet and fire, but he is plain and insignificant. Though Spaniards are often called unclean, this man is meticulously groomed, with hands whiter than his face. He stoops slightly and has an odd, large head. Smallpox scars give his ugliness a piquancy; his prominent forehead and shaggy brows give a harsh, frowning, plaintive look, like a sickly child whose life depends on superhuman care. As my father noted, his features are a shrunken Cardinal Ximenes. The natural dignity of his manners disconcerts the dear Duke, who dislikes him and never feels at ease before superiority.Once my father knows enough Spanish we will go to Madrid. When Henarez returned two days after his reprimand, I said to show gratitude:"I have no doubt you left Spain because of political events. If my father is sent there, as seems likely, we might help you obtain a pardon, if you are under sentence.""It is impossible for anyone to help me," he replied."But," I pressed, "is that because you refuse help or because the situation is impossible?""Both," he said, bowing, in a tone that closed the matter.My father's blood boiled. I was offended by this haughty posture and promptly dropped Señor Henarez.Yet something admirable lay in his refusal of aid. "He would not accept even our friendship," I thought while conjugating a verb. Suddenly I stopped and spoke to him in Spanish. Henarez replied that equality of sentiment is necessary between friends, which did not exist here, making the matter pointless."Do you mean equality in feeling, or equality in rank?" I demanded, eager to provoke him.He raised those awe-inspiring eyes, and mine fell. Dear, this man is a hopeless enigma. He seemed to ask whether my words implied love; the mixture of joy, pride, and agonized doubt in his glance moved me. It was clear that advances, taken lightly in France, might complicate with a Spaniard, and I retreated, feeling foolish.The lesson ended; he bowed, and his eyes expressed a humble prayer: "Don't trifle with a poor wretch."This sudden variance from his usual grave dignity made a deep impression. It seems horrible to think and say, but I can’t help believing there are treasures of affection in that man.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################All is over, my dear child, and it is Mme. de l'Estorade who writes to you. Between us there is no change; it is only a girl the less.Don't be troubled; I did not give my consent recklessly. My life is now mapped out, and the certainty of my path suits me. A great moral power has removed chance from my life. We have property to develop and a home to beautify; I have a household to manage and a husband to reconcile to life. I shall likely have children to care for and educate.Everyday life cannot be cast in a heroic mould. Though there may seem no room for longing after the infinite, I can still explore that boundless sea in our familiar craft. My humble duties do allow for passion; restoring faith in happiness to those who have suffered is a noble task that will relieve the monotony of existence. I see little room for suffering and much good to be done. My love for Louis de l'Estorade does not make my heart race, but there is nothing in him that offends me.You may wonder what will become of my instincts for greatness and the mental energies that bind us. This thought has troubled me, but these faculties remain mine, even if concealed and employed in secret for the welfare of our family. The time for a woman to seek admiration is short; if my life is not grand, it will at least be calm and free from shocks.Nature has favored our sex with a choice between love and motherhood. I have made mine. My children will be my gods, and this spot of earth my Eldorado.I can say no more today. Thank you for all you have sent me. Please look at my needs on the enclosed list. I am determined to live in an atmosphere of refinement and luxury, taking from provincial life only what adds to its charm. In solitude a woman is never vulgarized—she remains herself. I count on your kindness to keep me fashionable. My father-in-law is delighted and refuses me nothing; he is turning his house upside down. We are bringing in workmen from Paris to renovate everything.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE January.Oh, Renée, you have caused me misery! Your beauty, proud features, graceful manner, rich soul, and exquisite heart are destined to be wasted in a dull marriage you will end up despising. I already loathe your future children; they will be monsters!You seem resigned, without hope or fear. But what if a glorious morning brings the man who could awaken you? The thought chills me.At least you will have a friend. You will become the guardian angel of your valley and learn its beauty until it becomes part of you. As you walk with your husband and children, I can predict your letters: the valley, hills, and meadows will mirror the monotony of your soul. I will be there, a heart untouched by social pettiness, all your own.Monday.My Spaniard is adorably melancholic, with a calm, severe dignity that intrigues me. His solemnity and silence provoke my curiosity. How odd that a language teacher captivates me more than any nobleman or officer I’ve met.His coldness provokes me. He keeps distance, wrapped in pride and mystery, while I am bold. This amuses me; it’s trivial. What is a man, a Spaniard, a teacher to me? I regard no man as significant, not even a king. I would have made a slave of Napoleon!Yesterday, I made a remark to M. Henarez that must have struck him; he did not respond, and the lesson ended with a bow implying he would not return. That suits me; I do not want a new Nouvelle Héloïse. I recently read Rousseau and found it distasteful. Passion that argues and moralizes seems detestable.Clarissa is too pleased with herself and her long letter, but my father praises Richardson as an admirable portrayal of English women. Rousseau’s reads more like a philosophical sermon in letters.Love, to me, is a subjective poem. Everything written about it is both false and true. Since you will be an authority on married love, I feel called to remain unmarried and pursue a grand passion, enriching our shared experience.Tell me every detail of your first days with that strange creature called a husband. I promise the same if I ever find love.Farewell, poor martyred darling.XI. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU La Crampade.Your Spaniard and you make me shudder, my darling. I beg you to dismiss him. Everything you say fits the dangerous adventurer who risks everything. He cannot be your husband or lover. I will write more about my married life when my heart is free from the anxiety your last letter caused.XII. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE February.This morning my father visited, more thoughtful than usual. He gestured for me to sit; I sensed he wanted something.He began by flattering me, admitting he had misjudged my intelligence and praising my clear-sightedness as the only one capable of grasping his political concerns. He explained that France is in a critical position, understood only by the King and a few others. The King is a head without arms, and the nobles lack authority over elected men who refuse to cooperate.He described the Revolution as still active, embedded in law and thought, and warned that the family has ceased to exist in France. We are left with individuals, and the State treasury has replaced the family spirit. He argued that a country cannot be stable without a foundation of paternal authority.He urged me to consider the importance of family in establishing a strong state, warning of calamities if our party fails. I interrupted, asking how I could help the State, as I felt no vocation for martyrdom.He called me a little hussy, and we laughed. He asked for my help persuading him to sacrifice my inheritance for my younger brother's estate. I questioned whether I could still enjoy life if I gave up my fortune.He insisted that my future husband would not take me without a dowry; I suggested perhaps an older peer might be an option. He laughed, and we parted on good terms.I received your letter this morning, which made me reflect on the abyss you warned about. I took precautions regarding Henarez, whose gaze unsettles me. I hesitated to tell my father I wanted no more lessons, but I find myself drawn to him.His voice is sweetly thrilling, his manners simple and genuine. When he left, he almost reached for my hand to kiss it but stopped, terrified. I smiled, understanding the courage it takes for a plebeian to love a noblewoman.My smile encouraged him, and I handed him his hat with gravity. His eyes were wet with unshed tears, and in that moment I felt a strange happiness. I cannot explain it, but he has fascinated me.Friday.He is truly handsome and speaks admirably, with remarkable intellectual power. He has revealed a new world of thought. He told a story about a valet who died for a queen's glance, and I was captivated.At a ball, M. de Vandenesse told me Henarez is of noble descent, the Duke de Soria, whose titles were confiscated by King Ferdinand. This left me in suspense until our next lesson.During our lesson, I confronted him, suspecting he was the Duke de Soria. He denied it, saying he was merely the servant of the King of Spain. His reticence provoked me, and I devised a practical test: I asked him to translate a letter I wrote describing my ideal lover.He was visibly moved by my words and asked to keep the letter as a memory. He left asking to speak with my father, and from him I learned Henarez is now the Baron de Macumer, with a property in Sardinia.It has been two weeks since I last saw him, and I wonder what he is doing. I feel he has become necessary to me, and I am torn between love and the reality of my situation.Sunday.At the Italian Opera, I felt his gaze upon me; his fiery eyes drew me. He has a genius for love.XIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU LA CRAMPADE, February.My dear Louise, I waited to write until I had learned many things for your happiness. The difference between a girl and a married woman is vast; the girl cannot comprehend it, nor can the married woman return to girlhood.I chose to marry Louis de l'Estorade rather than return to the convent. The seriousness of marriage filled me with terror, but I realized friendship might be the key to a happy marriage. I accepted his love, believing it would not be slavery but a reign.We agreed to separate marriage from marital rights, to maintain independence. I watched Louis closely, wondering if suffering had softened or hardened him. I concluded his love was a passion, and I could risk much.I proposed a friendship that would let us bear life's burdens together. I asked him to leave me free, promising to consider his feelings. He was overjoyed, and we married soon after.I am nearly happy now, but I must conceal my happiness from him, as he is sensitive. For three months we lived as before, and I studied our relationship. I saw how my presence transformed him, and I became accustomed to him.I realized I could bring new life to my husband before bearing him children. His happiness has brightened our home; even my father has become generous. I manage the household with economy, aiming for pleasure without excess.I have ambitious plans for Louis, hoping to see him in politics. He has embraced my plans, showing the depth of his affection.To conclude, here is a letter he wrote, expressing his love and admiration. He sees me as a treasure, and I feel a proud sense of our relationship.XIV. THE DUC DE SORIA TO THE BARON DE MACUMER MADRID.My dear brother, I did not make you Duc de Soria to betray the name. I cannot tolerate that you are in exile while we enjoy happiness. Marie and I will not marry until you accept the money Urraca offers.We have prayed for your happiness; I believe Heaven will send you the love you seek. I have taken Marie, and I will always be grateful to you for that.You can write to us with confidence through the Nuncio. Farewell, dear brother. Be proud of the happiness you have brought us.XV. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE March.Ah, my love, marriage is making a philosopher of you! Your views on life and duty trouble me. Do you think you can convert me to matrimony with your cautious arithmetic?We left Blois innocent, armed with the weapons of meditation, and now those weapons have turned against you! If I did not know you were pure, I would think your calculations were vice.You have made a holocaust of your youth, becoming a miser before your time. If Louis loves you, he will never realize you act from necessity.Love is the first principle of all virtues; I cannot believe you are justifying your position as a married woman. You are acting a man's part, and Louis will have to play the woman!Your letter plunged me into thought. I see now the convent cannot replace a mother. Please, do not turn away from my cries!Monday.Last night I looked at the stars and saw my Spaniard in a tree, admiring my window. I was overwhelmed with joy and fear.He blew a letter to me through the window, but I burned it without reading. It felt improper to read it.At the opera he was present, but I felt no agitation. He looked sad; I learned he had performed a noble action by giving up a rich heiress for another man.I was wretched for burning his letter. What would he have said? To be loved in secret, to know a man is devoted—how can I resist?When I returned home, I opened my window, hoping he would be there. He was, and I received another letter, which I read and cherished.XVI. THE SAME TO THE SAME March.I am dressed in white, with camellias in my hair. I have a strange longing to delay my decision and make him wait.What a vision of beauty! I drove my barouche, and when I saw him, I felt a thrill. I complimented him on his horse; he shared it was a gift from his steward.Henarez is no longer a simple man; he is becoming more distinguished.Oh, my dear, do my love affairs amuse you? I am a little off my head and must stop.XVII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE CHAULIEU April.My angel, you have grieved me. You have not realized the force of the word indissoluble in marriage.Each marriage has its own laws, shaped by place and character. A wife of a leader must resign to his guidance, while a wife of a fool must take control.You speak of vice, but passion can be admirable when it springs from the heart. Sooner or later, you will understand we are answerable only to God and ourselves for our happiness.You will find a wife’s calculation necessary to maintain happiness. I have reflected on the duties of a wife and mother, and I believe a sublime hypocrisy is required to attain the ideal of a perfect woman.I persist in my principles, believing virtue varies with surroundings. If I wished to make Louis unhappy, I would leave the helm in his hands.I may not have married a man of distinction, but I hope to help him on that path. Five years from now we will see if I was mistaken.You have taught me what a woman sacrifices in marriage. Regret is not remorse, but I feel the loss of freedom.You say marriage has made you a philosopher, but I feel far from it. I have read Bonald, who revealed the strength of the family.Farewell, my dear friend.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Well, my Renee, you are a wonderful woman, and I now agree that we can only be virtuous by cheating. Will that satisfy you? The man who loves us is our property; we can make a fool or a genius of him as we please, though the former is more common. You will make yours a genius and keep the secret—two heroic actions, if you will!Ah! If there were no afterlife, how nicely you would be sold, for this is the martyrdom you are choosing. You want to make him ambitious and keep him in love! Child, surely the latter alone is sufficient.Tell me, to what extent is calculation a virtue, or is virtue calculation? You won’t say? Well, we won’t quarrel over that, since we have Bonald to refer to. We are, and intend to remain, virtuous; yet at this moment, I believe that you, with all your little knavery, are a better woman than I.Yes, I am shockingly deceitful. I love Felipe and conceal it from him with hypocrisy. I long to see him leap from his tree to my balcony—and if he did, how I would wither him with my scorn! I am frank enough with you.What restrains me? Where is the mysterious power that prevents me from telling Felipe how supremely happy he has made me with his pure, absolute, and overflowing love?Mme. de Mirbel is painting my portrait, which I intend to give him. What surprises me more every day is the animation love brings to life. Every hour, every action, every trifle is full of interest, creating confusion between the past, present, and future. Is it still so after reaching the heights of happiness? Oh! Tell me, what is happiness? Does it soothe or excite? I am restless; I feel lost, dragged to him despite reason and propriety. At least I can better understand your feelings.Felipe's happiness lies in feeling himself mine; his aloofness and strict obedience irritate me, just as his profound respect did when he was my Spanish master. I want to cry out to him, "Fool, if you love me as a picture, what will it be when you know the real me?"Oh! Renee, you burn my letters, don’t you? I will burn yours. If anyone else were to read these thoughts, I would send Felipe to silence them, perhaps even kill the owners for security.Monday.Oh! Renee, how can one fathom the heart of man? My father should introduce me to M. Bonald, since he is so learned; I would ask him. I envy God, who can read the heart's undercurrents.Does he still worship? That is the question.If I ever detect the slightest fall in the respect he used to show me as my instructor, I feel I would have the strength to end it. "Why these fine words, these grand resolutions?" you will say. Dear, I will tell you.My charming father, who treats me with the devotion of an Italian cavaliere servente, had my portrait painted by Mme. de Mirbel. I managed to get a copy made for the Duke and sent the original to Felipe. I included these lines:"Don Felipe, your single-hearted devotion is met by a blind confidence. Time will show whether this is to treat a man as more than human."It was a big reward, suggesting both a promise and a challenge, which I intended without fully committing. If his reply includes "Dear Louise!" or even "Louise," he is done for!Tuesday.No, he is not done for. The constitutional minister is perfect as a lover. Here is his letter:"Every moment away from you has been filled with ideal pictures of you; my eyes closed to the outside world, fixed on your image. Henceforth my gaze will rest upon this wondrous ivory—this talisman—since your blue eyes sparkle with life, turning paint into flesh. If I delayed writing, it was because I could not tear myself away from your presence, which wrung from me all that I was bound to keep secret."Closeted with you all last night and today, I have, for the first time, given myself up to complete happiness. Could you see yourself where I have placed you—between the Virgin and God—you might understand the agony of my night. I would not offend you by speaking of it; one glance from your eyes, robbed of the tender sweetness which is my life, would be torture for me. Queen of my life and soul, oh that you could grant me but a fraction of the love I bear you!"This was the burden of my prayer; doubt worked havoc as I oscillated between belief and despair. A criminal awaiting his verdict is not more racked with suspense than I. The smile on your lips, which alone can calm my dread of displeasing you, haunts me. From my birth, no one, not even my mother, has smiled on me. The beautiful girl designed for me rejected my heart for my brother. In politics, all my efforts have been thwarted. In the eyes of my king, I have read only thirst for vengeance; he has been my enemy since childhood, and the vote of the Cortes that placed me in power was seen as a personal insult."Less than this might breed despondency in the stoutest heart. I have no illusions; I know the gracelessness of my person and how difficult it is to do justice to the heart within this rugged shell. To be loved had ceased to be more than a dream when I met you. Thus, when I bound myself to your service, I knew that devotion alone could excuse my passion."But as I look upon this portrait and listen to your smile that whispers of rapture, rays of hope pierce the gloom, only to be obscured by rising mists of doubt and fear of your displeasure. It is impossible you should love me yet—I feel it; but in time, as you see the strength, constancy, and depth of my affection, you may yield me some foothold in your heart. If my daring offends you, tell me without anger, and I will return to my former part. But if you consent to try and love me, be merciful and break it gently to one who has placed his happiness in the thought of serving you."My dear, as I read these last words, he seemed to rise before me, pale as the night when the camellias told their story and he knew his offering was accepted. These words, in their humility, were different from the usual flowery rhetoric of lovers, and a wave of feeling washed over me; it was the breath of happiness.The weather has been atrocious; impossible to go to the Bois without raising suspicions. Even my mother, who often goes out regardless of rain, remains at home.Wednesday evening.I have just seen him at the Opera; he is another man. He came to our box, introduced by the Sardinian ambassador.Having read in my eyes that this audacity was taken well, he seemed awkwardly conscious of his limbs and addressed the Marquise d'Espard as "mademoiselle." A light brighter than the chandeliers flashed from his eyes. He left with the air of a man unsure of his next move."The Baron de Macumer is in love!" exclaimed Mme. de Maufrigneuse."Strange, isn’t it, for a fallen minister?" replied my mother.I had enough presence of mind to regard Mmes. de Maufrigneuse and d'Espard and my mother with curiosity, as if they were speaking a foreign language, but inwardly my soul sank in waves of intoxicating joy. There is only one word to express what I felt: rapture. Such love as Felipe's makes him worthy of mine. I am the very breath of his life; my hands guide his thoughts. I have a mad longing to see him clear every obstacle and stand before me, boldly asking for my hand. Then I would know whether this storm of love would calm at a glance from me.Ah! my dear, I stopped here, and I am still trembling. As I wrote, I heard a noise outside and rose to see what it was. From my window, I saw him coming along the wall at the risk of his life. I went to the bedroom window and made him a sign; it was enough. He leaped from the wall—ten feet—and then ran along the road, showing me he was not hurt. That he thought of my fear at that moment moved me so much that I am still crying; I don’t know why. Poor ungainly man! What was he coming for? What had he to say to me?I dare not write my thoughts and will go to bed joyful, thinking of all we would say if we were together. Farewell, fair silent one. I have no time to scold you for not writing, but it has been over a month since I heard from you! Does this mean you are finally happy? Have you lost the "complete independence" you were so proud of, which nearly played me false tonight?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If love is life’s essence, why do philosophers dismiss it in marriage? Society demands women sacrifice for family, creating marital discord. That discord was anticipated, prompting men to develop defenses against women; without them, their theories might be overturned by love or aversion.Marriage, as it stands, embodies two opposing forces the law should reconcile. Your letter challenges foundations laid by the sage of Aveyron, under which I once found comfort. Laws crafted by men accept conjugal love devoid of passion, allowing women to yield without love’s sanction, reflecting Nature’s emphasis on procreation.Once a person, I now feel like property. I have swallowed many tears alone, aching for a comforting smile. Why are our fates so different? You thrive in lawful love, where virtue aligns with pleasure, while I face an uncertain future.You are loved and adored. Embrace this noble romance that has long been our dream. Your beauty, refined and spiritual, was made to enchant. Guard your heart’s secret and test Felipe’s love, but, more importantly, test your own. Novelty and the illusion of happiness easily mislead.You remain independent, dear friend; I urge you not to rush into marriage without assurance. In intimate conversation, a gesture or word can reveal hidden depths. You have the courage to explore where others falter.I watch you with anxiety from afar; my heart is in sync with yours. Write to me about everything; your letters ignite passion in my otherwise mundane life. I oscillate between despair and hope—perhaps expecting too much. In youth we often believe ideals and reality will align.Seated beneath a rock in my park, I pondered that love in marriage is a fortunate accident, not a universal rule. The philosopher rightly views the family as society’s unit, placing women in subservience as history shows. The resolution to our dilemma lies in motherhood, which I yearn for to channel my energy and heart.Louis remains cheerful; his love is active while mine is passive. He enjoys the flowers of my affection without acknowledging the toil behind them. I play the devoted mother, sacrificing for his happiness, which in turn gives me joy. I await the rewards of my sacrifices, hoping motherhood will fulfill my aspirations and compensate for what I surrender.---Dear wedded friend, your letter arrived just as I contemplated a bold step. I feel a strange yearning for the unknown, revealing a conflict between societal and natural laws within me. I want to speak with Felipe alone, under the lime trees in our garden. This suits the modern girl I’ve become, as my father recognizes.There is method in this seeming madness: I wish to reward Felipe for his nights beneath my window while testing his reaction to my boldness. If he respects my folly, he may imagine me as his wife; if not, farewell, Don Felipe.Meeting him this way seems less risky than encounters in society where prying eyes abound. I cannot express the agitation this idea brings me, nor how I longed for your company to share the anticipation of our first clandestine meeting.I sent Felipe a tracing of the garden key with a note urging him to stop his reckless behavior, warning that if he fell it would tarnish my reputation. Yesterday I took Griffith to the garden, and after a delicious moment of tension, I saw him approach like a shadow.When he reached the garden, I introduced him to Griffith, who withdrew a few steps. The night was mild and heavy with scent; I felt intoxicated by the thrill of being almost alone with him. We savored the moment before I urged him to abandon his reckless climbing, fearing scandal.His pallor revealed fear; I reminded him that if he had been caught, suspicion would fall on my family. He apologized, and I reassured him my actions were not as mad as they seemed.After a few moments of silence he expressed joy and reverence for me, acknowledging that my actions were sacred. I told him I was on the path to loving the man who understands me, but that my heart would belong only to someone who truly knows it.He expressed gratitude for my acceptance; I noted he should prove himself worthy of my affection. We walked and talked; his tenderness reminded me of divine love. His low, melodic voice conveyed his feelings beautifully.I dismissed him, aware of our meeting’s implications. Griffith remarked on my commitment, but I assured her that in France my intentions were merely exploratory.Once again I met Felipe; he appeared timid and reverent, which I found appealing. He seemed to sense my power, yet remained unaware of the depth of my feelings. I am still fond of him, but I keep my composure.Love appears in two forms: one that commands and one that obeys. Can both coexist? Will Felipe ever become my master? These questions unsettle me.He remains oblivious to my true nature. If I were in his position, I would see Mlle. de Chaulieu as a calculating coquette. I still care for Felipe, but I feel calm and in control now. The thrill of uncertainty has faded, and I fear I may have lost the excitement of our initial encounters.Is suspense always more pleasurable than fulfillment? Do we exaggerate our feelings by letting imagination run wild? These thoughts haunt me as I consider another secret meeting in the garden—this time without Griffith. How far can I go? Imagination knows no bounds, but pleasure has its limits. How can a woman reconcile these two aspects of her existence?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I am displeased with you. If you did not cry over Racine's Berenice and find it a profound tragedy, we have no connection and should part. Forget me; unless you answer satisfactorily I will forget you. You will be M. le Baron de Macumer to me, or cease to exist.At Mme. d'Espard's yesterday your self‑satisfied air disgusted me; your confidence of success alarms me. You have lost the humility of your first letter and, instead of the absent‑mindedness of a lover, you polished epigrams. That is not the behavior of a true believer, who should always stand in awe of his divinity.If you do not see me as the very breath of your life, a being above others, then I must be less than a woman in your eyes. You have bred a spirit of mistrust that drowns my tenderness. Reflecting on our past, I feel justified in my suspicions. Understand, Prime Minister of all the Spains, that I have contemplated the vulnerable state of our sex. My innocence has lit my path, and I have emerged unscathed.Let me reiterate what my youthful experience taught me: in most matters deceit and broken promises are punished, but love follows its own code—the victim is also accuser and judge. The most heartless betrayals remain secret, known only to the two hearts involved. How can the victim reveal them without harming herself? Love administers its own justice, which the world ignores.I have resolved never to forgive a serious offense; in love, what is not serious? Yesterday you looked like a man who had already succeeded. If that certainty has stripped you of the charming uncertainty that once characterized you, I would blame you. I want you neither timid nor presumptuous; I do not want you to fear losing my affection—that would be an insult—yet I also do not want you to treat your love lightly. Your heart should never be freer than mine. If you cannot grasp the torment a single stab of doubt can inflict, beware the lesson I might teach you.In a single glance I entrusted my heart to you, and you understood it. The purest feelings of a young girl are yours. My reflections have enriched my mind; yet if a wounded heart seeks counsel from reason, it may ally itself with the darker aspects of knowledge and daring.I swear to you, Felipe, if you love me as I believe you do, and if I sense even the least decline in the fear, obedience, and respect you have shown, or if the pure flame that ignited my heart grows dim, expect no reproaches. I would not burden you with letters of weakness, pride, or rage. But if I became silent, my face would announce that death was near. I would not perish, however, until I had branded you with infamy and sown eternal sorrow in your heart; you would behold the girl you loved dishonored and lost, condemned to suffering.Therefore, I implore you, do not make me envy the old, happy Louise, the object of your pure worship, whose heart thrived in happiness, as Dante wrote: Senza brama, sicura ricchezza! I have searched the Inferno for the most terrible punishment, a mental torture to link with divine vengeance.Yesterday, as I observed you, doubt pierced me like a cold dagger. I mistrusted you, and the pain was unbearable. If my service is too exacting, leave it; I would not retain you. I do not need proof of your cleverness. Keep your wit for me. Do not present a polished façade to others for flattery or praise. Come to me burdened with scorn or hatred, the target of calumny, told that no woman loves you; then you will discover the treasures of Louise's heart.We are truly rich only when our wealth lies so deep the world could trample it without knowing. If you were merely handsome, I doubt I would have noticed you or found the myriad reasons for my love. We understand little of those reasons, as little as we know why the sun makes flowers bloom and ripens fruit. Yet I can reveal one cause dear to me.The character and individuality that give meaning to your face are a mystery to all but me. My love transforms you into the most lovable of men, and that is why I wish to keep your mental gifts for myself. To others they should be as meaningless as your eyes and features. Let it be mine alone to ignite your intelligence, as I bring light to your eyes. I envision you as a proud Spanish grandee, cold and haughty, a monument to be admired from a distance—ruins of barbaric power—rather than a figure of Parisian affability.Have you forgotten my ideal portrait? Your excessive cheerfulness betrayed your love. Had I not restrained you, you would have revealed to the sharpest salons of Paris that your inspiration came from Armande‑Louise‑Marie de Chaulieu.I believe too much in your greatness to think your love is merely strategic; yet if you did not show childlike simplicity with me, I could only pity you. Despite this initial fault, you are still deeply admired by LOUISE DE CHAULIEU.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When God sees our faults, He also sees our repentance. My beloved mistress, you are right; I thought I had displeased you but did not know how. Now that you have explained, I find new reasons to adore you. Like the God of Israel, your jealousy is a holy and precious feeling. Be jealous of your servant, Louise; the more you strike, the more contrite I will be, and the more I will prove my devotion to you.If the effort to overcome my timidity was invisible to you, will Heaven reward it? It cost me much to show you my true self, as I was before love. In Madrid I wished you to see my few gifts; if that was vanity, I have been punished.Your last glance unnerved me. I quailed even more than when I faced peril at Cadiz. I sought the cause of your displeasure; the lack of sympathy between us was terrible. I wish only to act by your will, think your thoughts, and share your joys and sorrows. The anguish lay in the breach of our shared feelings."I have vexed her!" I repeated, distraught. My noble, beautiful Louise, your words have illuminated my feelings. If this is chastisement, what sweetness lies in your rewards?To be your servant is happiness enough. You have given me a life I thought lost. I no longer draw a useless breath; I have something to devote myself to, even if it means suffering for you. I will always remain as I was when I first offered myself as your bondman. If you were dishonored, my heart would cling to you even more, to heal your wounds and pray for your innocence.Did I not tell you my feelings are not just those of a lover? I will be father, mother, sister, brother—anything or nothing, as you wish. If the father or brother sometimes fade behind the lover, they remain, though hidden. If you could read my heart when you appear before me, radiant in beauty, you would know how free from selfishness my pride is when I hear praise of you. When you greet me, I feel as if God's blessing rests upon me, and the joy lingers long after.Are you unaware how you are loved? After seeing you, I return to my study and the glitter of its ornaments pales before your portrait. I lose myself in thoughts of you, linking my happiness to verse. Have you never heard the whispers of my prayers reaching you?Through silent contemplation of your face, I have deciphered every feature and its connection to your spirit's grace, penning sonnets in Spanish about your dual beauty. You will never see them, for my poetry is unworthy of you. Not a moment passes without thoughts of you; my whole being is bound to you, and without you I would ache.Now, Louise, can you realize the torture of knowing I displeased you without knowing why? My ideal life was cut short, and I feared you had ceased to love me. Your letter turned my despair into joy. Oh, might I listen forever to such chiding!Once, a child, after falling, turned to his mother and said, "Forgive me." Hiding his hurt, he sought pardon for the pain he caused her. Louise, I am that child, and I remain so. Here is the key to my character, which I humbly place in your hands.Fear not, there will be no more stumbling. Keep tight the chain that binds me to you, so that a touch may communicate your lightest wish to your devoted slave, FELIPE.---My dear friend, how can you, who married a broken-down invalid to mother him, understand the shifting drama of love, where death lies in a glance or a light reply?I reserved one last test for Felipe to see if his love was like a Royalist's for his King, who can do no wrong. He walked with me all night, and not a shadow of suspicion crossed his mind. The next day, he loved me more, but with reverence and humility. He is a true Spaniard, scaling my wall to kiss my hand from my balcony, risking his life.But this is nothing; Christians suffer for heaven. I spoke to my father, the royal ambassador-to-be, with a smile, saying some friends claim he is marrying Armande to an ambassador's nephew for wealth and title. Your daughter weeps but bows to her father's authority. Some say she hides a worldly soul behind her tears.We are going to the gentleman's box at the Opera tonight, and M. le Baron de Macumer will visit us there."Macumer needs a spur," my father said, smiling at me."You mistake Clarissa Harlowe for Figaro!" I replied, scornfully. "When you see me with my right hand ungloved, you will mark your displeasure at this gossip.""I can rest easy about your future. You have no more a girl's head than Jeanne d'Arc had a woman's heart. You will be happy, love no one, and allow yourself to be loved."This was too much; I laughed."What is it, little flirt?" he asked."I tremble for my country's interests..."Seeing him blank, I added, "At Madrid!""You have no idea how this little nun has learned to make fun of her father," he told the Duchess."Armande makes light of everything," my mother replied."What do you mean?" I asked."You are not even afraid of rheumatism on these damp nights," she said, glancing at me meaningfully."Oh!" I answered, "the mornings are so hot!""It's high time she were married," my father said. "It had better be before I go.""If you wish it," I replied demurely.Two hours later, my mother, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and Mme. d'Espard were blooming in the box. I sat sideways, unseen, to observe everything.Macumer arrived, putting his opera-glasses to look at me comfortably.In the first interval, Comte Henri de Marsay entered, all epigrams and smiles. He greeted my mother and others, then turned to me, saying, "I do not know whether I shall be the first to congratulate you on an event that will make you the envy of many.""Ah! a marriage!" I cried. "Is it left for me, a girl fresh from the convent, to tell you that predicted marriages never come off?"M. de Marsay whispered to Macumer; I sensed he was warning him about me.Macumer answered with a glance of noble scorn, claiming not to be "in love with any little coquette." His demeanor delighted me, and I caught sight of my father, ready to act.Felipe showed no fear or doubt. His faith is in me; society's lies cannot hurt him. Not a muscle of his face stirred, nor did his olive cheek flush.The two young counts left, and I laughed at Macumer, saying, "M. de Marsay has been treating you to an epigram on me.""He did more," he replied. "It was an epithalamium.""You speak Greek to me," I said, smiling at him.My father was talking to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, exclaiming about the scandalous gossip surrounding me. He intended to take a turn in the promenade to avoid suggesting the marriage.The Duchess and Mme. d'Espard exchanged glances, sensing something. Love is the least mysterious of mysteries; it exudes from women like perfume.Having enjoyed Felipe's response, I signaled him to come to my window by the dangerous route. Later, I found him, upright as a statue, studying the light in my room."My dear Felipe," I said, "You acquitted yourself well tonight; you behaved as I would have if told you were marrying.""I thought you would hardly tell others before me.""And what right have you to this privilege?""The right of one who is your devoted slave.""In truth?""I am, and shall ever remain so.""But suppose this marriage was inevitable; suppose I had agreed..."His eyes flashed, calculating the danger of a fall. A power stronger than passion held him back."An Arab cannot take back his word," he said. "I am your slave; my life is not mine to destroy."I placed my hand on his as I said, "Felipe, my beloved, from this moment I am your wife in thought and will. Go in the morning to ask my father for my hand. He wishes to retain my fortune, but if you acknowledge receipt of it in the contract, his consent will be given. I am no longer Armande de Chaulieu. Leave me; no breath of scandal must touch Louise de Macumer."He listened, pale and trembling, then cleared the ten feet to the ground in safety. It was agonizing, but he waved goodbye and disappeared."I am loved then," I thought, "as never woman was before." I fell asleep, content, my destiny fixed.The next day, my father summoned me to his private room, where I found the Duchess and Macumer. After civilities, I said that if my father and M. Henarez agreed, I had no reason to oppose their wishes. My mother invited the Baron to dinner, and we drove in the Bois de Boulogne, where I smiled ironically at M. de Marsay as he passed.Felipe has reprinted his cards as follows:HENAREZ(Baron de Macumer, formerly Duc de Soria.)Every morning he brings me a splendid bouquet, hidden within which is a letter containing a Spanish sonnet in my honor.To keep this letter concise, I send you only the first and last of these sonnets, translated for your benefit:FIRST SONNET (condensed)Many a time I've stood clothed in thin silken vest,  Drew sword in hand, waiting the raging bull;  I've scaled redoubts under fire, staked life on chance—  Yet now, when your sweet eye rests on me, I tremble, shrink, my courage gone.SECOND SONNET (condensed)Last night I sought sleep to dream of thee,  Jealous sleep fled my eyelids; I looked toward heaven—  The sky had lost its blue, the stars their warmth;  The moon hung mourning, for thou hast robbed the heavens of light.  Thy brow wears the moon's sparkle; heaven's blue centers in thine eyes.What a gracious way to tell a young girl that she fills your life! Tell me what you think of such love, which lavishes earth and soul. Only in the last ten days have I grasped that famed Spanish gallantry.Ah, dear, what is happening at La Crampade? How often I stroll there, inspecting our crops! Do you have news of our mulberry trees and last winter's plantations? Does everything prosper as you wish? While buds open on our shrubs, have they also blossomed in the bosom of the wife? Does Louis continue his policy of madrigals? Do you enter each other's thoughts? I wonder if your wedding peace is better than the raging torrent of my love! Has my sweet lady professor taken offense? If so, I would send Felipe post-haste to plead for my pardon or her head. Sweet love, my life here is a splendid success, and I want to know how it fares with life in Provence. We have just added a Spaniard with the complexion of a Havana cigar to our family, and your congratulations still await.Seriously, my sweet Renee, I am anxious. I fear you may be eating your heart out in silence, afraid to cast a gloom over my sunshine. Write to me at once, naughty child! Tell me your life in every detail; tell me whether you still hold back, whether your "independence" stands erect, or has fallen on its knees, or is sitting comfortably, which would indeed be serious. Can you suppose the incidents of your married life are without interest for me? I muse over all you have said. Often, at the Opera, while absorbed in watching the dancers, I think, "It is half-past nine; perhaps she is in bed. What is she doing? Is she happy? Is she alone with her independence, or has it gone the way of other dead and cast-off independences?"A thousand loves.---Saucy girl! Why should I write? What could I say? While your life is varied by social festivities and the passions of love, mine is as monotonous as a convent.We go to bed at nine and rise with daybreak. Meals are served with maddening punctuality. Nothing ever happens. I have adjusted to this routine, which may be the nature of things. Where would the universe be without the fixed laws that govern it?I have imposed certain rules of dress, which occupy my mornings. I feel it is my duty as a wife to look charming, and it pleases both Louis and the good old man. After lunch, we walk. When newspapers arrive, I disappear to manage household affairs or read—often writing to you. I return an hour before dinner, and after, we play cards or receive visits. Thus my days pass between a contented old man, who has done with passions, and the man who owes his happiness to me. Louis' happiness is so radiant that it has warmed my heart.For women, happiness cannot consist solely in satisfying desire. In the evenings, when I can relax, I imagine your life, its riches and passions, and wonder what such a stormy preface can lead to. May I not swallow the book itself? For you, my darling, love's illusions are possible; for me, only the facts of homely life remain. Your love seems a dream!I find it hard to understand why you insist on romanticizing it. Your ideal man must have more soul than fire, more nobility and self-command than passion. You demand sacrifices to reward them, testing Felipe to see if desire, hope, and curiosity endure. But behind your stage scenery lies the altar where everlasting bonds are forged. The day after your marriage, the graceful structure raised by your strategy may fall before the reality that transforms a girl into a woman, a gallant into a husband. Remember, there is no exemption for lovers. For them, as for ordinary folk like Louis and me, the great "Perhaps" of Rabelais lurks beneath wedding rejoicings.I do not blame you for your rashness in speaking with Felipe in the garden or spending a night with him; but you play with life, and I fear it may turn the tables. I dare not give you the counsel my experience suggests, but let me repeat from my valley that the viaticum of married life lies in resignation and self-sacrifice. Despite your tests, I see that marriage will mean to you what it has been to me. The greater the passion, the steeper the precipice we have hewn for our fall—that is the only difference.Oh, how I wish to see the Baron de Macumer and talk with him! Your happiness lies so near my heart.---As Felipe has acknowledged my fortune, my mother has become even friendlier, calling me sly-boots and a woman of the world."But, dear mama," I said before the contract was signed, "you attribute cunning to what is truly the outcome of the most unselfish love! I assure you I am not the 'woman of the world' you think.""Come, Armande," she said, drawing me close, "you did not want to return to the convent or die an old maid. Like a noble Chaulieu, you recognized the need to build up your father's family." I glanced at my father, who was listening, and said, "I love Felipe de Soria with all my soul. Though this love sprang up without my knowledge, I swear I never yielded until I recognized in him a character worthy of mine.""But, my dear," she interrupted, "he is as ugly as—""As anything you like," I retorted, "but I love his ugliness.""If you love him, Armande," my father said, "and can master your love, do not risk your happiness. Happiness in marriage depends largely on the first days—""Days only?" my mother interrupted. "You had better leave us to talk.""You are to be married in three days," the Duchess began, "so I must give you the serious advice every mother owes her daughter. You are marrying a man you love, and there is no reason for pity. I have only known you for a year, but it is long enough to love you. You have gratified maternal pride and shown yourself a sweet daughter. I promise you will always find a staunch friend in me.""You smile? Alas! it often happens that a mother who has lived well with her daughter as a girl comes to cross purposes with her as women.""I want your happiness, so listen. The love you feel is that of a young girl, natural to us all, for it is a woman's destiny to cling to a man. Unfortunately, there is but one man for a woman! Sometimes, the man fate has marked for us is not the one we choose as husband. If we cannot love the man we have chosen, the fault lies with both or circumstances beyond our control. Yet, there is no reason why the man chosen by our family should not be the one we can love. The barriers that arise between husband and wife often stem from a lack of perseverance on both sides. Transforming a husband into a lover is as delicate as making a lover into a husband, which you have just proved marvelously successful."Your happiness is my object. Never forget that the first three months of marriage may bring misery if you do not submit to the yoke with the same forbearance, tenderness, and intelligence you showed during courtship. You have indulged in the innocent pleasures of clandestine love. If the culmination of your love begins with disappointment, come and tell me. Do not hope for too much from marriage; it may bring more discomfort than joy. The happiness of your life requires as much patient cherishing as the early shoots of love."If you lose the lover, you will find in his place the father of your children. This is the secret of social life. Sacrifice everything to the man whose name you bear, whose honor cannot suffer without involving you in frightful consequences. Such sacrifice is not only a duty for women of our rank; it is also wise policy. This is the mark of great moral principles—they hold good from every aspect. But I need say no more."I fancy you are jealous, and if you knew how jealous I am! But do not be foolish about it. To publish your jealousy is like playing politics with your cards on the table; those who let their game be seen learn nothing of their opponents. Whatever happens, we must know how to suffer in silence."She added that she intended to have a serious talk with Macumer the evening before the wedding.I kissed her hand, dropping a tear, moved by her words. In her advice, I saw high principles worthy of us both, true wisdom, and a tenderness unspoiled by society's narrow code. Above all, I saw she understood my character. Her words summed up the lessons life had taught her, perhaps at a heavy price."Dear little girl, you've got a nasty crossing before you. Most women, in their ignorance or disenchantment, are as wise as the Earl of Westmoreland!"We laughed, but I must explain the joke. A Russian princess had told us of this gentleman, who suffered from sea-sickness and turned back from Italy upon hearing of "crossing" the Alps. "Thank you; I've had quite enough crossings already," he said.You will understand, Renee, that your gloomy philosophy and my mother's lecture revived fears that had disturbed us at Blois. As marriage approached, I needed all my strength, resolution, and affection to face this passage from maidenhood to womanhood. All our conversations returned to my mind, and I re-read your letters, sensing a vague sadness.This anxiety had one advantage; it helped me adopt the regulation expression for a bride. Consequently, on the day of signing the contract, everyone said I looked charming and quite right. This morning, at the Mairie, it was informal, with only witnesses present.I write this tail to my letter while they prepare my dress for dinner. We shall be married at midnight at the Church of Sainte-Valere, after a very gay evening. I confess my fears give me a martyr-like air to which I have no right, but which will be admired—why, I cannot conceive. I am delighted to see that poor Felipe is as timid as I; society grates on him, and he feels like a bat in a glass shop."Thank Heaven, the day won't last forever!" he whispered innocently.In his bashfulness, he wished for no one there.The Sardinian ambassador presented me with a pearl necklace linked with six splendid diamonds—a gift from my sister-in-law, the Duchess de Soria. Along with it was a sapphire bracelet engraved with the words, "Though unknown, beloved." I could not accept these without consulting Felipe."For," I said, "I would not want you to wear ornaments from anyone but me."He kissed my hand, moved, and replied, "Wear them for the inscription and the sincere feeling."Here, then, my poor Renee, are the last words of your girl friend. After the midnight Mass, we set off for an estate Felipe bought in Nivernais, on the way to Provence. My name is now Louise de Macumer, but I leave Paris in a few hours as Louise de Chaulieu. However I am called, there will always be but one Louise for you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I have not written to you, dear, since our marriage nearly eight months ago, and not a line from you! You are inexcusable.We set off in a post-chaise for the Castle of Chantepleurs, which Macumer has bought in Nivernais, on the Loire, sixty leagues from Paris. Our servants, except my maid, were there before us, and we arrived the next evening after a rapid journey. I slept most of the way from Paris to beyond Montargis, resting my head on Felipe's shoulder. His tender affection touched me, and I awoke to find his passionate gaze unchanged; he kissed my forehead twice.At Briare we breakfasted in the carriage and talked as we used to at Blois, admiring the Loire. By half-past seven we entered the beautiful avenue to Chantepleurs. We dined at eight and retired to our charming Gothic bedroom, comfortable with modern luxuries. Felipe, often thought ugly, appeared beautiful to me in his kindness. Throughout the journey he felt like an old friend, though he later told me of the storms of passion he concealed beneath a calm exterior."So far, I have found nothing terrible in marriage," I said, gazing at the moonlit park."Why fear it?" he replied. "Have I ever proved false to my promise? Why should I in the future?"His words and looks were full of mastery and thrilled my heart. I reflected on duty and the constraints placed on a woman's delicate feelings. What force has sought to crush a woman's sensitivity under obligation? Love should guard its rights, blooming freely under its warmth.Oh, my noble Renee! I understand you now. A woman who has not used marriage merely to legalize her heart's choice is left with motherhood. When earthly ties fail, the soul seeks the divine.A hard truth: only truly great men know how to love. They balance sensual and spiritual forces; weaker men confuse them. Great souls express natural instincts with grace, tempering passion with reverence for women. The depth of feeling corresponds to the caliber of intellect; thus only men of genius possess a woman's delicacy. When mind, heart, and senses unite in rapture, we soar to heaven, if briefly.This is the philosophy of my first three months of marriage. Felipe is angelic, another self; I can think aloud with him. His greatness surpasses me, and our bond deepens with possession. I foresee that years of marriage will only strengthen his affection and enhance our connection. What joy!Pleasure invigorates me; its sunshine becomes part of me. The intervals between ecstasies are like the brief nights between long summer days. What happy chance has given me such a destiny? My mother's fears proved unfounded; your fears and mine have vanished.We spent seven and a half months at Chantepleurs, like runaway lovers in blissful solitude. One morning, in a particularly happy moment, I reflected on my life and thought of your prosaic marriage. I grasped your life’s meaning. Oh, my sweet, why do we speak different languages? Your marriage of convenience and my marriage of love are worlds apart. You walk the earth, while I soar in the heavens. Love crowned me queen; you reign by duty. My heights are so lofty that a fall would shatter me.But I won't dwell on this. I hesitate to describe the vibrant joy of love's springtime as we know it.For ten days we have been in Paris, staying in a lovely house in the Rue du Bac, prepared by the architect who decorated Chantepleurs. I have been listening to Rossini's divine music, which once soothed my restless heart. People say I am more beautiful now, and I take childish pleasure in being called "Madame."Friday morning.Renee, my dear, my happiness draws me back to you. I feel I can be more to you than ever. I have studied your married life alongside my own, and you emerge as great, noble, and splendid in your goodness. I declare myself your humble admirer and friend. My marriage has been a joy; I would have perished had it been otherwise. And you live! What feeds your heart? I will never mock you again. Mockery stems from ignorance; we jest at what we do not understand. "Recruits laugh where veterans are grave," said the Comte de Chaulieu, whose experience has been limited to marches from Paris to Fontainebleau.I suspect you have not shared everything. You hide wounds, and I believe you suffer. From afar I have woven romantic theories about you. I thought, "She has merely experimented with marriage, and what is happiness for me has brought her only pain." Your sacrifice seems without reward; you would not make it greater than necessary. The axioms of social morality cloak your disappointment. If self-denial is noble, what of my joy, sheltered by the church's canopy and witnessed by the mayor's seal? Is it unnatural?For the honor of the law and to complete my happiness, I long to see you content. Oh, tell me you feel a dawn of love for Louis, who adores you! I return to this midday blaze, fearing it may consume me.Dear Renee, do you remember how you once said, "I love you so, Louise, that if God appeared to me, I would pray for all your sorrows to be mine"? Now I take up your prayer, imploring Heaven to grant you a share in my happiness.I have an idea. I suspect you are nurturing ambitious plans for Louis de l'Estorade. Very well; help him get elected deputy in the upcoming election. He will be nearly forty then, and the Chamber won't meet for six months, so he will qualify for a seat. You will come to Paris—wouldn't that be enough? My father and my future friends will come to know and admire you. If your father-in-law agrees to start a family, we could secure the title of Comte for Louis. That is something! And we shall be together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, your letter left me dizzy with joy and a touch of envy. I held it at sunset, tears glistening, beneath a barren rock overlooking the Mediterranean where I’ve planted climbers to one day cover it in greenery. This spot, named Louise's seat, symbolizes my solitude while reminding me I am not truly alone.As I read, the child within me stirred—a mix of warning, delight, pain, promise, and fulfillment that convinced me I will one day have a life full of laughter and love. I now understand my purpose: to bring forth life. The certainty of motherhood has turned my sacrifices into profound joy, surpassing any personal passion.For you, love reflects Felipe’s passion; my love will return in my children. Your golden harvest may fade, but my motherhood, though delayed, will be enduring and renewing. Love may be a beautiful gem taken from Nature, but motherhood is Nature’s purest joy. A smile has dried my tears; while love makes Louis happy, it is marriage that has made me a mother, and I find happiness in that.I returned to my white grange to share these thoughts. For five months the miracle of nature has unfolded within me, yet I remain emotionally detached. My family is excited, but I feel cold and hesitant to reveal my true feelings; motherhood is still abstract to me.Louis was equally surprised by my condition, which shows how little the father’s role seems to matter in this process. My doctor believes chance governs childbirth, though children born of passionate love are thought to be more fortunate. Perhaps motherhood holds joys for you I may never feel, since your love for Felipe differs from my sense of duty.These thoughts weigh on me as I anticipate family life. I live in a state of expectation, accompanied by physical discomfort: nausea and strange cravings, including an inexplicable desire for rotten fruit. While Louis brings home the finest oranges, I seek the half-rotten ones, finding them delicious despite their smell. This craving has eased my sickness and made me feel stronger, suggesting a natural process common among women.As my condition progresses, I plan to stay within the grounds to avoid being seen. I am curious when the sense of motherhood truly begins and hope it won’t coincide with suffering.Farewell, my fortunate friend! Your love story inspires me, and I long to hear every detail. Please remain faithful to our bond, and I promise to share everything with you.Yet after rereading your letter I felt dread. Your happiness seems to challenge Providence, and I worry calamity may take offense. Remember to pray and do good; religion has become more significant to me since my marriage. Your letter lacks mention of this, and I hope you still invoke God in your happiness.This concern comes from deep affection. I trust you and Felipe attend church and do good together. My fears may seem primitive, but they arise from a friendship that cares. You deserve happiness, and I cherish the bond we share, even in my uneventful life. God bless you!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame,—My wife wished you to hear from us first about an event that has brought us immense joy. Renée has given birth to a healthy boy, and we are postponing his baptism until your return to Chantepleurs. We sincerely hope you will come to La Crampade and consent to be the godmother of our firstborn, whom we have named Armand‑Louis de l’Estorade.Renée endured considerable pain but showed remarkable patience. You, who know her well, can understand that the thought of bringing happiness to us all helped her through this trying experience of motherhood.Without new‑father exaggeration, I can say that little Armand is a beautiful infant, resembling Renée in features and eyes, which suggests intelligence.The physician assures us that Renée is now out of danger, and as she proves to be an excellent nurse—thanks to Nature—we are free to express our joy. I hope this intense happiness, which has revitalized our home and transformed my wife’s life, will soon be yours as well.Renée has prepared a suite of rooms for you; while I wish they were more splendid, I hope the warmth of our welcome will compensate for any lack of luxury.I have heard from Renée about your kind thoughts regarding us, and I thank you for them, especially as they are so timely. The birth of a grandson has reconciled my father to sacrifices that had weighed heavily on him. He has recently purchased two estates, and La Crampade now yields an annual rental of thirty thousand francs. He plans to seek the King’s permission to establish it as an entailed estate, and if you could assist him in obtaining the title you mentioned in your last letter, it would greatly benefit your godson.As for me, I will follow your suggestions to facilitate your reunion with Renée during the Chamber sessions. I am working diligently to become a specialist, and nothing would encourage me more than knowing you will take an interest in little Armand. We earnestly invite you to come and, with your beauty, grace, playful spirit, and noble heart, be a good fairy to my son and heir. In doing so you will earn our everlasting gratitude.Your very humble, obedient servant,LOUIS DE L’ESTORADE.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXX. LOUISE DE MACUMER TO RENEE DE L'ESTORADEJanuary 1826.Macumer just woke me with your husband's letter. Yes, we will go to Chantepleurs at the end of April. I am delighted to travel, to see you, and to be godmother to your first child. I must have Macumer as godfather; sharing this role would be unbearable. If you could see the look he gave me, you would know how dearly he cherishes me!I look forward to visiting La Crampade; I might have a child there too. Can you imagine me torn between you and an infant? If I ever saw anything take my place in your heart, I can't promise how I'd react. Medea might have had a point!So, my dear, you have the fruit without the flowers; I the flowers without the fruit. Our lives contrast, but we have enough philosophy to find meaning yet. Ten months married is too soon to lose hope.We live happily, as happy people do. Society now finds the Baronne de Macumer prettier than Louise de Chaulieu; love is a wonderful cosmetic. Driving along the Champs-Élysées on a crisp January morning, I reflect on how far we've come since last year.If I lack a mother's joys, you can share them with me; I will learn by sympathy. Still, I cannot imagine anything surpassing the rapture of love. You may laugh, but I often wish to die at thirty, leaving life untainted by disappointment, having lived fully in love. To carry a young heart in an aged body, surrounded by cold faces, would be torment.Felipe and I quarrelled about this. I insisted he should have the strength to end my life in my sleep at thirty, so I might pass from one dream to another. He refused, turning pale. This distinguished statesman is surprisingly childlike. Now that I share such thoughts with him, we continually surprise each other.Felipe and I want to send you a gift, dear mother. We wish to choose something that will bring you joy and remind you of us. Tell me what you would like. I thought of a luncheon service with baby figures, since lunch is our happiest meal. Let me know soon; we must order it.Farewell, dear nursing mother. May you enjoy all the delights of motherhood! I eagerly await your first letter describing your experiences. Some parts of your husband's letter touched me; I will tell my godson how dearly he must love you. Much love, my sweet one.XXXI. RENEE DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE MACUMERIt has been nearly five months since the baby was born, and I have found no moment to write. When you become a mother, you will understand. Please write more often! Share your joys; I am happier than you can imagine.I went to the parish church for the Mass of recovery from childbirth, supported by both grandfathers. I knelt before God, overwhelmed with gratitude. I have so much to tell you, but one memory stands out: my prayer in the church.Transformed into a joyful mother, I felt the Virgin on the altar bow her head, pointing to the infant Christ, who smiled at me. With a heart full of love, I held little Armand for the priest to bless, anticipating his baptism later.My child—how sweet that word is! In the last two months I dragged myself through the gardens, scarcely conscious of the precious burden I carried. Gloomy forebodings haunted me, overshadowing curiosity about motherhood. My heart did not respond to the thought of the little one, though he made himself known with lively kicks.When the moment came, I summoned all my strength and braced for suffering. I bore the agony marvelously, sinking into a stupor. I felt like two beings—my body in pain and my soul at peace. The pain formed a halo around me, and at the climax I screamed, finding strength against the torture. Suddenly the newborn's cries silenced my pain.They laid me back in bed, and it felt like paradise despite my exhaustion. Happy faces pointed to the child, and I exclaimed in terror, "It looks like a little monkey! Are you sure it's a child?"My mother, now my nurse, reassured me, "You've got the finest baby in the world. Don't worry; focus on being a mother." I fell asleep, resolved to let nature take its course.Waking from the first days' pain was heavenly. A ray of light pierced the darkness, and my heart and soul emerged from suffering as a flower opens to the sun. The moment the little one latched onto my breast was pure joy—this is the dawn of motherhood!Here is happiness, though it comes with pangs. Oh, my sweet jealous soul, you will relish this delight that exists only for us, the child, and God. For this tiny creature, all knowledge is in its mother's breast. Its wants and thoughts revolve around this source of life, and its lips bring a sweetness beyond words.Nursing is a miracle of transformation. The cries go to your heart; the child's eyes and lips speak volumes. What else matters? The father? You could kill him if he disturbed the baby! The bond between mother and child is profound; the joy of nurturing is unparalleled.My little one has become the prettiest darling in five months. He cries little now, content in the love that surrounds him. I strive to envelope him in my affection.Dear, my feelings for Louis have evolved into a tender fondness, perhaps surpassing love. This unselfish gratitude reflects the joy of motherhood, lighting the future and recalling fond memories of the past.The old l'Estorade and his son have redoubled their devotion to me; I feel like a new person. Each time they see me it brings them joy, which touches me deeply. Louis is ecstatic about having a son and spends hours watching him sleep. He feared childlessness, but now he works harder, driven by fatherhood.I grow happier every moment. Each hour strengthens the bond between mother and child. My feelings are natural and enduring, unlike romantic love, which can wane. A mother's love grows with the child's needs, embracing passion, duty, and joy. Here lies a woman's true sphere, where self-sacrifice flourishes without jealousy.Society aligns with nature in this regard, reinforcing the maternal instinct. A mother cherishes the child who opens up these joys and teaches her the art of motherhood. The right of the eldest child, once part of the natural order, should never have been questioned. A mother learns so much from her child, and her character flourishes in fulfilling life's duties and pleasures. A woman without children is incomplete.Hasten, my sweetest, to fulfill your mission. Your happiness will multiply with the wealth of my delights.23rd.I had to leave you because my godson was crying; I can hear him from the garden. I wanted to say farewell. I reread my letter and am surprised at how ordinary my feelings seem; every mother must have felt this way. But I am as happy now as I once was miserable. This place is my land of promise. The desert is past. A thousand loves, darling. Write to me, for I can now read about your happy love without tears.
