Farewell.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Do you know, dear, that it has been over three months since I last wrote to you? I am to blame for not answering your last letter, but surely you won’t mind?Macumer and I took your silence as consent about the baby-wreathed luncheon service, which is now bound for Marseilles. It took six months to complete. When Felipe asked me to see it before packing, I realized we hadn’t exchanged words since your letter that revealed a mother’s heart.My sweet, it’s this dreadful Paris—my excuse. What is yours? Society is a whirlpool! Didn’t I tell you that in Paris one must conform? It drives out sentiment, monopolizes time, and can consume your heart. Moliere’s Celimene is the masterpiece of the society woman—timeless and apt.Without my breastplate—the love I have for Felipe—I would be lost. This morning, I told him he is my salvation. My evenings are filled with parties, but at night my heart heals in the embrace of passionate love.I only dine at home with friends and spend afternoons there one day a week—Wednesday. I’ve entered the social fray, competing with Mmes. d'Espard and de Maufrigneuse, and my house is lively. I allowed myself to become fashionable for Felipe’s pleasure. My mornings are his; from four in the afternoon until two in the morning, I belong to Paris. Macumer is an admirable host—witty, dignified, courteous. No woman could help but love such a husband.My parents have gone to Madrid. With Louis XVIII gone, the Duchess secured my father’s appointment as attaché. My brother, the Duc de Rhetore, now acknowledges me. My younger brother, the Comte de Chaulieu, owes me gratitude for spending my fortune to secure him an estate and a marriage with Mlle. de Mortsauf, an heiress. The King will confer the de Lenoncourt and de Givry titles on him to preserve their legacy. Mlle. de Mortsauf, sole heiress of the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, is expected to inherit over one hundred thousand livres a year. My father insisted the de Chaulieu arms appear on the de Lenoncourt escutcheon, making my brother Duc de Lenoncourt. The young de Mortsauf is gravely ill, so the marriage will wait until next winter after mourning.My father’s plan has justified itself, earning me compliments and smoothing over the explanation of my marriage to society. The Prince de Talleyrand, out of affection for my grandmother, has become warm to Macumer. Society that once criticized me now admires me.I now reign where I was once insignificant. Macumer is envied as the husband of "the most charming woman in Paris." Many women vie for that title. Men murmur sweet nothings or gaze longingly. This chorus flatters vanity, and I see why women pay dearly for such transient privileges. This triumph is intoxicating; I understand how women become selfish and frivolous under adoration. Society’s demands consume your spirit; you give your all for smiles and jealousy and receive only flattery. You know the cost, yet cannot escape the vortex.Ah, how I crave a true friend! Felipe’s love and devotion are precious to me, and my heart longs for you! We are joyfully preparing to move to Chantepleurs, where we can escape the farce of Parisian society. After rereading your letter, I realize no fashionable woman in Paris can be a good mother.Goodbye for now, dear. We will be at Chantepleurs for only a week and will see you around May 10th. We will finally meet again after more than two years! How we have changed! We are both matrons now—one in love, the other in motherhood.If I haven’t written, it isn’t because I’ve forgotten you. How is my little godson? Is he still lovely? He must be over nine months now. I long to witness his first steps, though Macumer says even precocious infants don’t walk at ten months.We will have delightful gossip and see if a child truly spoils a pattern.P.S.—If you reply, address it to Chantepleurs. I’m just leaving.---My child, if you become a mother, you’ll find it impossible to write during the first two months of nursing. Mary, my English nurse, and I are exhausted. I had resolved to do everything myself—sewing baby clothes, embroidering caps. I am a slave, day and night.Master Armand-Louis eats whenever he pleases, and I must constantly change, wash, and dress him. I love watching him sleep, singing to him, and carrying him about, leaving little time for myself. Society has been to you what motherhood is to me!I cannot express how rich my life has become, and I long for your visit to see for yourself. I fear he will soon be teething, and you may find a fussy baby. So far he hasn’t cried much because I’m always attentive. Babies cry when their needs aren’t met, and I’m vigilant. My heart has expanded while yours shrinks under society’s demands! I eagerly await your arrival, wanting your thoughts on l’Estorade, just as you’re curious about Macumer.Write to me from your last stop. The gentlemen want to meet our distinguished guests. Come, Queen of Paris, to our humble home, where love will greet you!---The name on this letter shows my petition has been granted. Your father-in-law is now Comte de l'Estorade. I wouldn’t leave Paris until I secured this for you, and I’m writing in the presence of the Keeper of the Seals, who has just informed me the patent is signed.Goodbye for now!---I am ashamed to think how my sudden departure will surprise you. But I must be honest: I am horribly jealous!Felipe’s eyes rested too often on you. Your little talks at the foot of your rock tortured me, and I became irritable. Your beauty reminded him of his homeland and Marie Heredia, and I can be jealous of the past too. Your magnificent features stabbed me in the heart. I tried to enhance my appearance, but everything paled beside you.Felipe doted on the child, whom I began to resent. Yes, I confess, I wanted that exuberance of life for myself. I saw regret in Macumer’s eyes, and I wept for two nights. I was miserable in your home. You are too beautiful and triumphant for me to endure.You complained of your lot, but L’Estorade is presentable, charming, and likely to become a deputy. His exile has given him a calm, dignified air, essential for a politician. Macumer, too, should achieve a high position.Having satisfied myself of your happiness, I am off to Chantepleurs, where Felipe must fulfill his aspirations. I won’t receive you there without a fine baby to match yours.I know I deserve your scorn. I am a fool, a wretch, an idiot. Alas, that is jealousy. I am not angry with you, but I was miserable; you will forgive my escape. Two more days and I would have made a scene.Despite my jealousy, I am glad to have seen you in the pride of motherhood. Even here in Marseilles, I feel proud of you as a splendid mother.You are born for motherhood, while I am not. A good mother and a passionate wife both require intelligence and grace. I watched you closely and admired you! Your children will be happy, not spoiled, with your tenderness and reason.Tell Louis the truth about my departure, but find a decent excuse for your father-in-law and family. Felipe doesn’t know why I left, and he never will. If he asks, I’ll fabricate a pretext—perhaps that you were jealous of me! Forgive my little fib.Goodbye. I write in haste, wanting you to receive this at lunch. The postilion is here, waiting.Kisses to my dear godson. Be sure to come to Chantepleurs in October. I’ll be alone there while Macumer is in Sardinia, planning improvements for his estate. That’s his current plan; it gives him a sense of control, which unsettles him when he shares it with me. Goodbye!---Dear sister, we were all astonished at lunch when we learned you had left, especially after receiving your letter. You were happy, and there was no justification for your objections. My sentence is that you return at my first summons. Your letter didn’t mention your next stop, so I’m addressing this to Chantepleurs.Listen, dear sister of my heart. My focus is your happiness. Your husband commands respect, not only for his gravity but for the power revealed in his plainness and the fire in his eyes. It took me time to converse easily with him. He has been Prime Minister and adores you, which makes him a profound dissembler. Extracting secrets from this diplomatic soul requires skill.You know my role is reason, while yours is imagination. I am a simple country vicountess, ambitious for my family’s prosperity. Macumer, the Duc de Soria, has a name, and you, a duchess by right, reign in Paris, where it’s hard to maintain power. You have a considerable fortune, which may double if Macumer develops his Sardinian estates. If anyone has the right to be jealous, it’s not you. Thankfully, we both have generous hearts that place our friendship above such pettiness.I know you must feel ashamed of fleeing. But your flight won’t spare you from my words today. Read carefully, as this concerns you more than Macumer.Firstly, you do not love him. In two years you will tire of adoration. You will never see Felipe as a husband; he will always be a lover to you. You do not revere him as a woman should her god. I have studied love, and this is not love.You cannot escape the fate of all queens. One day you will long to be treated as a light-o’-love, to be swept off your feet by a strong man who won’t worship you. Macumer loves you too much to resist or criticize. Sooner or later you will scorn this excessive devotion. He spoils you, as I did at the convent; you are enchanting, and there’s no escaping your charms.Worse, you are candid, and our happiness often relies on social hypocrisies you won’t embrace. Society won’t tolerate a wife’s power over her husband. The convention is that a man must not show himself as a lover to his wife, nor may a married woman act as a mistress. You both disregard this.The unpardonable sin in society is to be happy. If happiness exists, it must remain hidden. More importantly, equality between lovers should never appear in marriage, or it undermines society. It’s painful to see a man made a nonentity, and worse to see a capable man reduced to that. You will reduce Macumer to a shadow, and your household will consist of one imperfect person. You will regret it, but by then it will be too late. Women lack the qualities essential for family life. Macumer senses this; his visit to Sardinia suggests he hopes to regain his old self.You use the power his love gives you. Your position shows in your gestures and tone. You are the wanton your mother called you! Have you ever heard me contradict Louis? I respect his authority in public. If I want to advise him, I wait for private moments, but I never assume the role of mentor. If I didn’t remain a submissive wife publicly, he would lose confidence. The good we do for others is spoiled unless we erase ourselves so they feel no inferiority. There’s sweetness in these hidden sacrifices, and it’s a triumph when you praise Louis.This is the sum of my observations. Right now, you love Felipe, not as a husband, but as a reflection of yourself. Your father’s words hold truth; beneath your passion lies a selfishness typical of a great lady. How I must love you to speak such bitter truths!If you promise not to tell the Baron, I’ll share the end of our talk. We praised you, and he soon realized I love you like a sister. I ventured, “Louise has never struggled with life. She has been fortune’s spoiled child, and she might pay for this if you weren’t there to act as father and lover.”“Ah! but is it possible?” he broke off, on the verge of a precipice. That exclamation sufficed for me. If you had stayed, he would have spoken more freely.Think of the day when your husband’s strength wanes, when pleasure turns to satiety, and he feels diminished before you. The stings of conscience will awaken remorse, painful for you, as you will feel responsible and end up despising the man you failed to respect. Remember, scorn can lead to hatred. You are too noble to forget Felipe’s sacrifices, but what more can he give when he has already served himself at the banquet? Woe to those who leave no desire unsatisfied! Women are insatiable in love.Oh, Louise, change while there’s still time. If you treated Macumer as I treat l’Estorade, you could awaken the hero within him. Wouldn’t you feel pride in using your power to elevate a gifted man, as I do with one of lesser stature?Had you remained, I would have written this anyway, for in conversation you might have interrupted me. But I know you will read this thoughtfully and weigh my warnings. You have everything to be happy; don’t spoil your chances. Return to Paris as soon as Macumer does. The demands of society, which I complain about, are necessary; otherwise you’ll be lost in self-absorption. A married woman shouldn't be too generous with herself. A mother should reserve sacred hours for herself; even children shouldn’t take her presence for granted.Farewell, my dear jealous soul! Many women would be flattered by your pangs, but I can only mourn, for what isn’t mother in me is your dear friend. A thousand loves. Make whatever excuse you must for leaving; if you’re unsure of Macumer, I am sure of Louis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My beloved beauty,—I was eager to explore Italy and am thrilled to have brought Macumer—his Sardinia plans are postponed.This country is enchanting; its churches, especially chapels, could make any Protestant yearn for Catholicism. Macumer has been warmly welcomed; all are pleased to have such a distinguished Italian. Felipe could secure the Sardinian embassy in Paris if I wished—I am well regarded at court.Write to Florence. I have no time for details now; I’ll tell our travel stories when you visit Paris. We’ll stay here a week before heading to Florence, stopping at Leghorn; a month in Tuscany, another in Naples, and we aim for Rome in November. From there we’ll return via Venice—two weeks there in early December—and reach Paris through Milan and Turin in January.The journey feels like a perfect honeymoon; discovering new places revives our passion. Macumer had never seen Italy, and we began with the stunning Cornice road, as if made by fairies.Goodbye, darling. Don’t be upset if I don’t write—there’s no time while traveling; I’m consumed with admiring and absorbing my experiences. I’ll share more once I can reflect.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear,—a full reply to your letter from Marseilles awaits you at Chantepleurs. This honeymoon has heightened my concerns; please send my letter from Nivernais.Rumor says Government will dissolve Parliament—unfortunate for the Crown, since final session would have focused on laws to strengthen it. This worries us, as Louis will not be forty until the end of 1827. Fortunately my father has agreed to stand and will resign his seat at the time.Your godson has learned to walk without his godmother's help; he is delightful and shows signs of thought, not infancy; his smiles are meaningful. I have succeeded in nursing and will wean Armand in December; a year at the breast seems enough—longer nursing reportedly leads to dullness, and I trust popular wisdom.You must be making an impression in Italy, my fair one with golden locks. Thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXXIX. THE BARONNE DE MACUMER TO THE VICOMTESSE DE L'ESTORADEYour dreadful letter has reached me, forwarded by my steward as I asked. Oh, Renée—I'll spare you my turmoil and tell you its effect.We had just returned from an ambassadorial reception where I shone and Macumer was utterly captivated. Then I read your harsh reply and sobbed. My dear Arab threw himself at my feet, calling you mad. He led me to our balcony; speaking Italian, his declarations were like poetry. He swore that even if your predictions proved true, he would not trade one moment of our love for a lifetime of convention—he would choose our love and death over a dull existence with children.While he whispered, a bat’s cry startled us and I fainted. I am better this morning, though that sound haunts me. Upon waking I knelt before Felipe, looked into his eyes, and confessed: "My love, I may be naive, and Renée might be right. Perhaps I love only your love, but this is the only feeling in my heart. If anything about me disturbs you, tell me. I want to follow your wishes." Macumer was too choked with emotion to answer.I thank you, Renée; your letter revealed the depths of Macumer’s love. Rome is the city of love, where passion should flourish alongside art and faith.In Venice we shall meet the Duc and Duchesse de Soria. If you write, address Paris, as we leave Rome in three days after the ambassador’s farewell.P.S. Dear, naive child, your letter shows you know love only in theory. Love is a force that yields diverse fruits beyond any theory’s grasp.XL. THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE TO THE BARONNE DE MACUMER January 1827.My father has been elected to the Chamber, my father-in-law has died, and I am near my second confinement; these mark the year’s end. I mention them first to spare you the fright of the black seal.Your letter from Rome chilled me. You and Felipe behave like children. Either he is a deceitful diplomat, or his love resembles a man’s for a courtesan—lavishing affection while knowing she is unfaithful. Enough. You say I rave, so I will hold my tongue: from our differing fates I draw a harsh lesson—do not love if you wish to be loved.When Louis joined the provincial Council he received the Legion of Honor cross. Nearly three years on, my father asks the rank of Officer for his son-in-law. I would value your influence with the right person, but do not entangle yourself in my father’s affairs. The Comte de Maucombe seeks a marquisate for himself; reserve efforts for me. When Louis becomes a deputy next winter, we will be in Paris to secure him a government post so we may live on his salary. My father sits between center and right; a title will satisfy him. Our family was noble even under King René, and Charles X will likely grant a Maucombe title. My fear is my father using this to favor my younger brother—if a marquisate is dangled, he will focus on himself.January 15th.Ah, Louise, I have been through hell. I tell you because you are like a sister; still, I doubt I shall ever relive those five ghastly days. The word "convulsions" sickens me. Five days felt like five centuries. A mother who has not suffered this cannot comprehend it.The night before, the weather was close; I thought Armand affected by teething. Usually sweet, he grew peevish, crying and breaking his toys. I put him to bed beside me. Toward morning he cried "Mamma!" in a strange voice and twitched. I rose for a drink; as I handed him the cup he was motionless, repeating "Mamma!" in a voice hardly a voice. His hand was stiff. He gulped water convulsively, clung to me, eyes rolling back, limbs rigid. I screamed for Louis."A doctor! Quick!... he is dying," I cried.Louis vanished. Armand gasped "Mamma!" then lost consciousness. Veins on his forehead swelled, and convulsions began. For an hour before doctors arrived I held my lifeless baby—my pride and joy. His eyes! I cannot think of them without horror. My pretty Armand was a mere shell—black, shriveled.Two doctors from Marseilles hovered like birds of ill omen. One blamed brain fever, the other called them ordinary convulsions. Our country doctor offered no opinion. "Teething," said one; "fever," said the other. They agreed on leeches to the neck and ice to the head. To see a child like that—purple, silent—was horrible. They even cut his curls for the ice. The convulsions returned every ten minutes; the child writhed, white then violet, limbs clattering like wood. This unconscious flesh had smiled and said "Mamma!"—the thought pierced my soul.After four days and nights, the doctors recommended a dreadful ointment to create open sores. Sores on my Armand! Five days earlier he had been playing and laughing. I refused, trusting nature. Louis, who believes doctors, scolded me. But in those moments when the disease mimicked death, I felt that remedy might save him. His skin was dry and rough; the ointment would not work. I wept; the doctors were at dinner!Alone with him, I stripped off the medical appliances, pressed him to my bosom, and laid my forehead against his, begging God to spare him. For minutes I held him, longing to die with him. Then his limbs relaxed; the writhings ceased; the ghastly colors faded. I screamed; the doctors rushed in."He is saved!" cried the oldest doctor.What music those words were! Two hours later Armand returned to life. I was crushed, saved only by the joy of his recovery. What tortures bind a mother to her child! Must the nails be driven to fasten him to my heart? Was I not mother enough? I who wept at his first words and steps—why these horrors?Now Armand plays and laughs. What causes this dreadful disease in children? I ponder it, especially as I am again with child. Is it teething? A brain disorder? A nervous susceptibility? Our country doctor blames nervous trouble from teething. I would give every tooth in my head to see Armand’s through. One white pearl peeking from swollen gums brings a cold sweat. The heroism with which my angel bore suffering proves he is his mother’s son. A look from him pierces me.Medical science offers no clear explanation for this tetanus that appears and vanishes swiftly, unpreventable and uncured. One thing is certain: it is hell for a mother to see her child in convulsions. How passionately I hold him now! I could walk forever with him in my arms!To have endured this six weeks before my confinement made it worse; I feared for the coming child. Farewell, my beloved. Do not wish for a child—this is the essence of my letter!XLI. THE BARONNE DE MACUMER TO THE VICOMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE Paris.Poor dear, Macumer and I forgave you upon hearing of your terrible trouble. I felt pain reading of your double agony; there seem compensations in being childless.I write to tell you Louis has been promoted; he may now wear the ribbon of an Officer of the Legion. You are fortunate, Renée—likely to have a little girl, as you wished!My brother’s marriage to Mlle. de Mortsauf was celebrated on our return. Our gracious King, who is extraordinarily kind, has granted my brother the reversion of the post of first gentleman of the chamber, presently held by his father-in-law, on condition the Mortsauf scutcheon be placed alongside the Lenoncourt’s."The office ought to go with the title," he told the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry.My father is justified a hundredfold. Without my fortune none of this would have happened. My parents came from Madrid for the wedding and will return after the reception I am hosting for the newlyweds.The carnival will be lively. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria are in Paris, which unnerves me. Marie Heredia is one of Europe’s most beautiful women, and I dislike the way Felipe looks at her. Therefore I am extra sweet and affectionate. Every look and gesture says what my lips do not: "She could not love like this!" Heaven knows how lovely I am! Yesterday Mme. de Maufrigneuse said, "Dear child, who can compete with you?"I keep Felipe entertained, making his sister-in-law seem dull by comparison. I am less sorry that a little Abencerrage is not on the way, since the Duchess will likely remain in Paris for her confinement and lose her beauty. If the baby is a boy, he will be named Felipe in honor of the exile. Fate decrees I shall be godmother again.Goodbye, dear. I plan to go to Chantepleurs early this year: our Italian tour was shockingly expensive. I will leave around the end of March to economize in Nivenais. Besides, I am tired of Paris. Felipe and I long for the tranquility of the park, cool meadows, and the sparkling Loire. Chantepleurs will be delightful after Italy; splendor becomes tiresome, and a lover’s gaze is more beautiful than any opera or painting!We expect you there. Do not fear my jealousy. You are free to test Macumer’s heart and expose his doubts. I am utterly indifferent. Since that day in Rome Felipe’s love for me has deepened. He just told me (looking over my shoulder) that his sister-in-law, Princess Heredia, his childhood dream, has no brains. Oh, my dear, I am worse than a ballet dancer! If you knew the joy that remark brought me! I pointed out her faulty French—she says "esemple" for "exemple," "sain" for "cinq," "cheu" for "je." She is beautiful but lacks charm and wit. When praised she seems puzzled. Felipe could not have lived two months with Marie after marriage. Don Fernand, the Duc de Soria, suits her perfectly: generous but a spoiled child. I am tempted to be mischievous and make you laugh, but I will refrain. Much love, darling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My little girl is two months old, Jeanne-Athenais; my mother and Louis' grand-uncle are godparents. I plan to visit Chantepleurs soon, since you aren't worried about a nursing mother. Your godson can now say your name—calling Matoumer because he can't pronounce c. You'll be delighted: he has all his teeth, eats meat like a big boy, and scurries about like a little mouse. I watch him anxiously, miserable I can't keep him close while I recover. Doctors have advised precautions, making recovery feel long. Childbearing discomforts remain. But don't tell Felipe—this little girl resembles me and may outshine your Armand.My father noticed Felipe looks thin, and my pet isn't blooming. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria have left, so no reason for jealousy. Is something troubling you that you're not sharing? Your letter isn't as long or affectionate as usual—another whim of my whimsical friend?I'm writing too much; my nurse is upset, and Mlle. Athenais de l'Estorade is hungry. Farewell; please write me long letters.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For the first time, my dear Renée, I find myself alone and crying. I sat under a willow by the Chantepleurs marsh, a charming view that feels empty without children. Nearly three years into my marriage, I have no child. Your quiver full of little ones makes me search my heart.I would endure any suffering, even what you felt when my godson was born, to have a cherub of my own, like your Athenais. I can picture her beauty, but you tell me nothing—just like you, Renée! I believe you feel my trouble.Each disappointment plunges me into melancholy, and I write sad elegies. When will I embroider little caps and sew lace for a tiny head? Will I never hear "Mamma" or have my dress tugged by a beloved little despot? Will there be no carriage wheelmarks on the gravel, no broken toys in the courtyard? I long to witness the unfolding of a life—another Felipe, but dearer. I would love a son to understand how a lover can feel like a second self.My park and castle feel cold and desolate. A childless woman seems a monster of nature; we exist to be mothers. Oh, my wise friend, how well you understand life! Barrenness is dismal. My life resembles a Gessner or Florian sheepfold, which Rivarol wished to see invaded by a wolf. I have sacrifices to make; if I cannot be a mother, I must indulge in romantic sorrow.I told my Moor; it brought tears to his eyes. He cannot bear jokes about his love, so I let him off easily, calling him a paladin of folly.Sometimes I feel a desire to go on pilgrimage, to take my longings to a shrine or a watering place. Next winter I will seek medical advice. I am too angry with myself to write more. Goodbye.---A whole year has passed without a letter! What does that mean? I am a little hurt. Does your Louis, who visits me almost every other day, make up for your absence? It is not enough to know you are well; I want to know your feelings and thoughts, as I share everything with you, risking scolding or misunderstanding. Your silence and seclusion in the country, while your husband gains Parliamentary honors, worry me. His eloquence has earned him a fine position, and he will likely receive a good post when the session ends. Are you spending your time writing him advice?Why did you not come to Paris? I could have enjoyed your company for four months. Louis told me you would fetch him and have your third confinement in Paris—what a terrible mother Gigogne! After questioning Louis, I learned his grand-uncle, Athenais' godfather, is very ill. I suspect you, as a careful mother, would angle for a legacy from your husband’s last remaining relative. Rest easy; we are all working for Louis. Martignac will likely place him in the audit department. But if you won’t tell me why you hide in the country, I shall be cross.Are you afraid the political wisdom of the house of l’Estorade should seem to rest in you? Or is it the uncle's legacy? Perhaps you fear being less to your children in Paris? I would love to know if you are simply too vain to show yourself in the capital in your present condition! Vain thing! Farewell.---You complain of my silence; have you forgotten my two little brown heads, my subjects and tyrants? I didn’t want to drag a four-year-old and a soon-to-be three-year-old to Paris while expecting again. I didn’t want to trouble you and upset your husband with such a party. I also detest life in hotels.When I come to Paris for the session, it will be to my own house. Louis' uncle, hearing of his grand-nephew's rank, gifted me two hundred thousand francs to buy a place near you, and I have tasked Louis with finding one. My mother gave me thirty thousand francs for furnishing, and I will do my best not to disgrace you.I appreciate what you have done for Louis at Court. However, I don’t want him in an exposed position; it would commit him too much. I prefer the Audit Office for its permanence. Our affairs are in good hands; once the steward masters the details, I will support Louis.As for long letters, how can I write them? Describing a day would take a week, since they are all alike—either the children are well or not. In this solitary grange, hours shrink to minutes or drag, depending on their health.My delightful hours come when they sleep, and I am no longer needed to rock one or soothe the other. When they lie beside me, I think, “Nothing can go wrong now.” Every mother imagines dangers: razors, coats catching fire, ponds. A mother's life is a series of tender and terrible dramas. Each hour has its joys and fears.At night I dream of their future, bright under a guardian angel. Sometimes Armand calls me in his sleep; I kiss his forehead and his sister's feet, watching them in their beauty. These are my joys. Just last night I was roused to Athenais' cradle; her head was too low, and Armand was uncovered, his feet purple with cold.“Darling mother!” he cried, flinging his arms around me.How important it is for a mother to have her children by her side at night! A nurse, however careful, cannot comfort them after a nightmare. They are scared and half-awake; explaining away visions is arduous. I have learned to sleep lightly; a sigh or rustle wakes me. The demon of convulsions lurks by their beds.At first light, my babies stir. Their chatter blends with the morning air; Nais struggles to reach me, and Armand climbs up with monkey-like agility. They turn my bed into a playground. The baby pulls my hair, and Armand stands guard over my breast, defending his property. Their antics and laughter banish sleep.Then comes dressing. I slip on my gown, turn up my sleeves, don an apron. With Mary’s help, I wash and scrub my little blossoms. I control the bath temperature; so many cries come from mistakes here. The moment arrives for paper fleets and glass ducks; amusement is key to getting them cleaned. If you knew the diversions I invent to get them to allow a soft sponge, you would marvel at the ingenuity required in motherhood. Prayers, scoldings, and promises are all in play; Providence matches a child's cunning with a mother's. It takes little to make them laugh: a falling brush or slipping soap brings merry shouts. Our triumphs, though hard-won and unseen by most, are triumphs. Even their father knows nothing of it. Only God, His angels, and perhaps you can fathom the satisfaction Mary and I share when the children are finally clean amid nursery chaos.English women excel in organization; their arrangements are admirable. My children will always be bare-legged with woolen socks. No swaddling or bandages; they will never be left alone. The helplessness of many French infants gives nurses freedom—that explains it all. A true mother is never free.That is why I do not write. Besides managing the estate, I am raising two children.Motherhood involves silent self-denial and devotion to small details. I must watch the soup warming before the fire. Is a woman like me to shirk such cares? A child's laugh at a well-enjoyed meal repays a lifetime. Armand nods when pleased, and how could I leave the privilege of blowing on a spoonful too hot for my little Nais, who still remembers my breast? To mince a chop for Nais, who has just cut her last teeth, requires patience; only a mother can make an impatient child finish a meal.No number of servants or English nurses can replace a mother in that daily contest, where gentleness must address a child's little griefs. Caring for these innocent darlings engages the whole soul. A crying child condemns mother or nurse, except when the cry is from natural pain. Now that I have two to care for (and a third on the way), they occupy all my thoughts. Even you, whom I love dearly, have become a memory.My own dressing is often incomplete by two o’clock. I have no faith in mothers whose rooms are immaculate and who look perfect. Yesterday was a lovely April day, and I wanted to take my children for a walk while I still could—time is pressing. Such an expedition is an epic! I dreamed of it the night before: Armand in a little black velvet jacket, a new collar I made, and a Scotch cap; Nais in white and pink with a delightful baby cap. All was laid out. The little brown heads had to be brushed, chirping like birds. Armand's hair is in curls, while Nais' frames her cap. When they trotted off to the nursery, my heart raced. To see the children I dressed, their pure skin veined with blue, is a joy unmatched. I long for one more kiss on their white necks.Once outside, triumphant, I admired Armand's princely air as he helped Nais along. A carriage approached; I tried to move them. They tumbled into a puddle, ruining my work! We had to return and change. I took the little one in my arms, forgetting my ruined dress, while Mary seized Armand, and we re-entered. With a crying baby and a soaked child, what mind has a mother left for herself?Dinner comes, and I have done nothing else. Then the problem of feeding two children: bibs, sleeves, coaxing them to eat. Amid these recurring cares and small catastrophes, I neglect myself. If the children are naughty, I often don’t get rid of my curl papers all day. To write you these pages, I have let them cut pictures from my novels, build castles with books, and give Nais my silks to arrange, which has absorbed her.Yet I have nothing to complain of. My children are strong and independent; they amuse themselves more easily than you might think. They find joy in everything; a little freedom is worth many toys. A few pebbles, small shells, and the mysteries of sand bring them pleasure. I watch Armand talking to flowers, flies, and chickens, imitating them. He is friendly with insects and never tires of admiring them. Everything small interests them. Armand is beginning to ask “why” about everything he sees. He even asks what I am saying to his godmother, whom he sees as a fairy. Strange how children hit the mark!Alas, my sweet, I do not wish to sadden you with my joys. Let me tell you about your godson. The other day a poor man followed us begging—beggars know a mother with a child cannot resist. Armand, unaware of hunger and money, held out a trumpet I had just bought him, saying, “Here, take this!”What joy could compare with that moment?“May I keep it?” the poor man asked. “I too, madame, have had children,” he added, hardly noticing the money I gave him.I shudder at the thought of Armand going to school, knowing I have only three and a half years left to keep him with me. The flowers of his sunny childhood will fall before the scythe of a public school system; his gracious ways and candor will lose spontaneity. They will cut the curls I have brushed and kissed so often! What will they do with the thinking being that is Armand?And you—are you still in love with Felipe? I have no worries about the Saracen. Goodbye; Nais has just tumbled, and if I continue, my letter will become a volume.---My sweet, tender Renée, you will have learned from the papers of the terrible calamity that has overwhelmed me. I could not write a word. For twenty days I never left his bedside; I received his last breath and closed his eyes, keeping vigil with priests and repeating prayers for the dead. The cruel pangs I suffered felt like rightful punishment; yet when I saw the calm smile on his lips, his last farewell to me, how could I believe I caused his death?Be it so or not, he is gone, and I am left. To you, who have known us both, what more need I say? These words contain all. I would give my share of Heaven to hear that my prayers could bring him back! To see him again, even for a second, would let me breathe without agony. Will you not come soon and soothe me with such promises? Is your love strong enough to deceive me?You warned me beforehand that he would suffer through me. Was it so? Yes; I had no right to his love. Like a thief, I took what was not mine, and my frenzied grasp crushed the life out of my bliss. The madness is over, but I feel alone. Merciful God! what torture can exceed the misery in that word?When they took him away, I lay on the same bed, hoping to die. There was but a door between us, and I thought I could force it! I was too young for death; after forty days of cruel care and medical science they slowly nursed me back. Now I find myself in the country, seated by my window, surrounded by the flowers he made bloom for me, gazing at the view he loved. Ah, Renée, no words can express how new surroundings hurt when the heart is dead. I shiver at the sight of the moist earth in my garden, as if I walk on him! When I first went out, I trembled and could not move. It was so sad to see his flowers, and he not there!My parents are in Spain. You know my brothers, and you are detained in the country. But do not worry; two angels of mercy have come to my side. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria rushed to their brother in his illness and have been everything I could wish. The last nights before the end found the three of us in calm, wordless grief around the bed of this great man, rare in any age, head and shoulders above the rest of us. Felipe’s resignation was angelic. His last words to me were simple: “I had almost gone from life without leaving Fernand the Barony of Macumer; I must make a new will. My brother will forgive me; he knows what it is to love!”I owe my life to my brother-in-law and his wife; they want to take me to Spain!Ah, Renée, to no one but you can I speak freely of my grief. A sense of my own faults weighs on me; there is solace in pouring them out to you, poor, unheeded Cassandra. My exactions, jealousy, and nagging unrest wore him to death. My love was dangerous because we shared the same sensitive nature; nothing was lost on him, and my careless words struck straight to his heart. You cannot imagine how submissive he was. I needed only tell him to leave me alone, and he obeyed without a murmur. His last breath blessed me, saying a single morning with me was more precious than a lifetime with another woman, even the Marie of his youth. My tears fall as I write.This is my life now. I rise at midday and go to bed at seven; I linger over meals; I stand motionless for an hour before a plant; I gaze into the trees; I take an interest in mere nothings; I long for shade, silence, and night. I fight through each hour, taking a gloomy pleasure in adding it to the heap of the vanquished. My park gives me all the company I care for; everything there is full of glorious images of vanished joy, invisible to others but eloquent to me.“I cannot away with you Spaniards!” I exclaimed one morning as my sister-in-law embraced me. “You have some nobility that we lack.”Ah, Renée, if I still live, it is because Heaven tempers affliction to the strength of those who bear it. Only a woman can know what it is to lose a love that was genuine, a passion that satisfied spirit and flesh. How rare to find a man so gifted that to worship him brings no sense of degradation! If such fortune befalls us once, we cannot hope for it again. Men of true greatness, veiled by poetic grace, charm by high spiritual power—beware of love! Love will ruin you and the woman of your heart. This is my cry as I pace my woodland walks.And he has left me no child! That love, rich in smiles and joy, has left no fruit. I am accursed. Can it be that the purity and vehemence of a single-hearted passion render it barren? Is it only a marriage of reason, like yours, that is blessed with a family? Can Heaven be jealous of our passions? Such wild thoughts.You are the one person whose company I could endure. Come to me; none but Renée should be with Louise in her sorrow. What a day when I first donned my widow's bonnet! When I saw myself in black, I fell back and wept until night. I weep again recalling that moment.Goodbye. Writing tires me; thoughts crowd fast, but I have no heart to express them. Bring your children; you can nurse the baby here without making me jealous; all that is gone, he is not here, and I shall be glad to see my godson. Felipe wished for a child like little Armand. Come, then, come and help me bear my woe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My darling,—By the time you read this I will be near; I leave shortly after sending it this morning. We will be alone, since Louis must stay in Provence for the upcoming elections, seeking re-election against Liberal opposition.I do not come merely to comfort you; I bring my heart to resonate with yours and help you endure. Weep; only through tears will you find the joy of reuniting with him. Remember he is journeying to Heaven, and each duty you fulfill weakens the chain that separates you.In my arms you'll lift your head and go toward him—pure and noble, freed from defining errors, carrying the good you'll accomplish in his name.I write these hurried lines amid preparations for departure, interrupted by the children and Armand, who keeps calling, "Godmother! I want to see her," and makes me jealous. He could be your child!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yes, Renée, it is true: I have sold Chantepleurs and the farms in Seine-et-Marne, but I am neither mad nor ruined.After settling everything, I have about twelve hundred thousand francs from my late husband’s fortune. I invested a million in the Three per Cents at fifty, which yields sixty thousand francs instead of the thirty thousand the property brought. My former life was tedious—six months in the country managing leases and farmers who paid when they pleased, and the Paris house, though bringing ten thousand francs, came with delays and legal troubles that wore on me as a twenty-seven-year-old widow.Now my fortune is secured with the state. Instead of paying taxes, I receive thirty thousand francs every six months from the Treasury, handed by a pleasant clerk. If the nation failed, I would still be as well off as before, and I have time to save a little capital. The Comte de l’Estorade is a peer and friend at Court, so I have no cause for anxiety.What drives a woman to such financial calculations? Love, my dear.I must explain my actions. I am to be married in a village near Paris. I love and am loved. Forgive me for keeping this secret; I wished to spare you and de l’Estorade the inevitable worries. You know how jealous I can be; I wanted to avoid anxiety.The man I love had thirty thousand francs of debts, which I have paid. You might call him an adventurer, but I investigated him. He has courted me for nearly two years. I am twenty-seven; he is twenty-three. He is a poet who has lived hardly at all, more in dreams than in work. Practical people often see artists as unreliable; I understand that skepticism.Despite his debts and our age gap, I am ready to end a long period of restraint. This is not a naive surrender; my love is profound, and he has waited patiently. He is noble and tender, and his love is genuine. My beloved is Gaston, the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, who died tragically. He was raised by his brother Louis, a sailor who has not been heard from in years; his absence has deepened Gaston’s despair.Gaston believed genius would bring fortune, but his life has been hardship. He has struggled to make a name in literature, and his debts grew. I met him at Mme. d’Espard’s and felt an immediate connection. He has talent and pride, which can intimidate women.Gaston has no idea of the depth of my love. I am confident in our happiness, and I feel a passion I never knew. I adore him as Felipe once adored me, and I fear losing him or becoming less attractive as he remains youthful. Yet I believe I can nurture our love and keep it alive. If his love should wane, I would blame myself, not him. I know my nature leans toward being a lover rather than a mother. Should disaster come, I beg you, Renée, to care for my children as my legacy; your qualities would make my death less bitter.I wish to keep our marriage private, performed in secret, to avoid distressing you. I will retain control of my property; Gaston will not know its full extent. I plan to assign him stock yielding twelve thousand francs a year, which he will find on the eve of our wedding. If he refuses it, I would end our engagement.This long confession has exhausted me. I will continue later; I must spend tomorrow in the country.October 20th.I have taken steps to ensure secrecy. I purchased a small property near Ville-d’Avray: twenty acres, a lakelet, and a charming garden. The area is secluded, surrounded by woods, and beautifully laid out. My chalet, inspired by Swiss designs, is well equipped and hidden from view.The property includes a separate house for staff and a concealed entrance, ensuring privacy. I have carefully chosen servants who will be loyal and discreet. All my cherished belongings from Paris have been moved to the chalet, creating a simple but elegant home.The tranquility of this valley is unparalleled, filled with nature’s sounds. I worry such perfect happiness may attract misfortune, as you have often warned. When I visited recently I was moved to tears and paid my architect without hesitation, insisting on confidentiality about my ownership.Now you understand my mysterious actions and the purpose behind my investments. Love is serious, and I have removed financial burdens to focus solely on him.Gaston remains unaware of my plans. We will begin our life at the chalet soon; while our living will be modest, I intend to invest in my appearance for him. I want to maintain my beauty throughout our marriage so that I am loved as much at the end as at the beginning.In two days I will be Mme. Gaston. Is it fitting for a Christian to love so deeply?My man of business remarked that he was losing a client. And you, my dear, may feel you are losing a sister.Please address future letters to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We will keep our lives secret from the locals and procure provisions from Paris. Our retreat has been prepared in silence; only the architect knows of our existence.Farewell. As I write I feel a mix of grief and joy, proving my love for Gaston is as deep as my sorrow in leaving you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My Dear Daniel,I need two witnesses for my marriage and ask you to come tomorrow evening with our friend Joseph Bridau. My future wife wishes to live in complete retirement, which explains our rare meetings this past year. After the wedding we will be apart for a long time, but I know you will understand. Friendship can endure absence; while I will miss you, I shall not see you in my own home. She has sacrificed the closeness of a childhood friend for me, and I must do the same.This proves our love is deep and genuine, founded on mutual understanding. It gives me immense joy, yet I carry a painful secret. You know how desperate my finances have been, and only through your support have I managed. My wife is wealthy; I am not. I once joked about wanting a rich heiress, but now that it is real I feel a bitter humiliation I cannot shake. This shame confirms my love for her, for I do not recoil from it, even if it overwhelms me.Otherwise, my dreams have come true. She is the most beautiful and noble woman, with a charming personality and a sharp mind. At twenty-seven she is a widow without children, full of life yet occasionally melancholic. Despite her aristocratic background, she loves me despite my humble origins. Our love has grown over time; we share a deep emotional connection and avoid the physical aspect of love out of mutual respect.One day she confessed that her marriage was her regret, revealing the depth of her feelings. You, Daniel, are a giant among men, and you love a remarkable woman, just as I love my Armande.---Louise, can it be that you intend a life of wedded solitude after sacrificing your first husband? You seem to court future misery. I see you are resolved, and the man who won your heart must possess a rare charm. But have you forgotten your earlier criticisms of young men, when you claimed they had tarnished purity?You are lucky to believe in happiness, and while I cannot blame you, I must warn you. Nature and society conspire against absolute happiness, and I foresee some disaster arising from your boundless joy. I have no objections to your fiancé, but I hope you will describe him to me one day.I have not told Louis about your marriage; it would only invite his ridicule. I will simply say you wish to keep it secret. You are brave to face winter first, and I will be your discreet friend. Your journey into this emotional wilderness is like an explorer’s perilous expedition, though fortunately only a short distance from Paris. I wish you a safe journey and a swift return.---What has happened to you, my dear? After two years of silence, Reneé has a right to worry about Louise. Is this what love does, scattering friendships like ours? You must admit that, while I am devoted to my children, a mother’s love is expansive and does not diminish other affections. I miss your letters and long to see your face.Regarding our situation, I will be brief. Your last letter contained sharp comments about our political position. You mocked us for keeping our posts in the Audit Department, which Louis owes to Charles X’s favor. But how could we provide for Athenais and Rene on an income of forty thousand livres, of which thirty thousand is entailed? We must live on our salary and save the estate’s income for their future. In twenty years we will have saved enough to provide for both children.Louis has been raised to the Peerage and is now a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. He has made himself useful in the Chamber and was offered an embassy, which I declined for the sake of our children’s education. I will not leave Paris until Rene has finished his schooling.We could not remain loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without jeopardizing our children’s prospects. A mother is not called to be a Decius, especially now. In fifteen years, Louis will retire on a good pension, and I hope to secure a post for Armand in the Audit Department.I am a happy mother; my children give me endless joy. Armand is now a day scholar at Henry IV’s school. I wanted him in public school but could not bear to part with him, so I arranged for him to attend by day and return home each evening. He is the same charming boy you loved, and his tutor praises his progress.Home education has drawbacks: children raised at home may be exposed too early to the world’s passions and may lack the social skills gained by peers. A mother must keep her children from society’s distractions, as I have done.Now that I am thirty, I feel a duty fulfilled. My children seem to sense my thoughts and respond. Armand, once dull, has lately excelled, winning prizes for translation and essays. I felt immense pride when his name was called and longed to shout, “I am his mother!”Rene, inspired by Armand, wants to attend school too. The three children can be noisy, but I cherish every moment. I am the only one allowed to care for them; the joys of motherhood outweigh the challenges.I must study their characters closely, for a mother alone discerns her children’s natural aptitudes. Home-bred children often develop ease and tact, compensating for lack of innate ability. I believe Armand will become an admirable public servant, while Rene will be a bold sailor.Nais is so dear to me I can hardly see her as separate from myself. I want her to be happy and will only give her to a man who truly loves her. Yet I worry about her future and the risk of a poor marriage. Your silence has reminded me of the anguish marriage can bring a mother. Farewell for now, dear Louise, and please write back.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################So, after two years of silence, you wonder about my lack of letters. My dear Renée, words fail to express my happiness. We bear everything with ease; we are in perfect harmony. There has been no discord in our feelings, and each day brings its own joy. We do not fear monotony; we regret that our time together may not suffice for all the poetic expressions of our love. We take more pleasure in each other's company than on the first day, and every hour reveals new reasons to love one another.Every evening we speak of visiting Paris, of the new boulevards and the finished Madeleine, but in the morning we linger over breakfast and take a siesta. Gaston gazes at me as if I were a painting, and we both shed tears of joy reflecting on our love. I still play the mistress, pretending to give less than I receive, and I delight in this little play. There is something sweet about passion tempered by tenderness; Gaston, my master, remains a timid suitor at my command.You asked me to describe him, yet it is impossible to portray the man we love without emotion. Society alters men so that the man in public can seem quite different from the man of strong feeling. The charming suitor may lack deeper qualities a woman values, while an unrefined man may possess a profound capacity for love. Our ideal is a man whose outward appearance aligns with his true nature, embodying a grace that cannot be acquired. This ideal haunts every woman's imagination, and in Gaston I have found it.Ah, dear, I never knew love could be so intertwined with youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston is unpretentious, moving with effortless grace. When we walk together, our bodies fit perfectly, seeming a single figure gliding through the woods. Our thoughts, desires, and words harmonize. Sometimes we walk in silence, listening to the rain and admiring the sunset, feeling as if in silent prayer for our happiness. A timid kiss exchanged in nature feels like a divine act of worship.We return home each time more in love than before. Such passionate love between older married people would be scandalous in Paris; only in the woods can we express it freely.Gaston is of average height, well-proportioned, and agile. He has a fair complexion, dark hair, and a striking resemblance to Louis XIII. His honorable poverty has spared him the vices that ruin many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he possesses a robust constitution. His blue eyes, filled with tenderness for me, can flash with intensity at inspiring thoughts.He has a remarkable temperament, surprising in its evenness. I have heard many tales of women’s troubles with men who are dissatisfied with themselves, but Gaston is free from such burdens. I have learned that marrying an older man can lead to unequal unions, and I urge you not to let Athénaïs marry one.His smile is a treasure—always fresh and full of unspoken love and gratitude. Our shared experiences have become part of our joy, and even the smallest details of nature remind us of our time together. We share a deep connection, as if we are two editions of the same book. Our ability to view subjects broadly brings constant pleasure, and this unity of thought feels like a pledge of love.Despite these pleasures, life here is laborious. I do my own room, refusing to let a hired hand intrude on my personal space. My room is meticulously arranged so that Gaston finds it pleasing. I rise early, bathe in cold water to refresh myself before he wakes, emerging like a newly opened flower. He appreciates this freshness without knowing the effort behind it.My daily grooming is handled by my maid, and I dress several times a day for Gaston’s delight. We take great interest in our flowers and horticulture, surrounding our chalet with beauty. Our garden is a source of pride, and we eagerly tend our fruits and vegetables.To keep Gaston's mind engaged, I encourage him to finish plays he began during earlier struggles. This literary work requires reflection and inspiration, and I assist him, ensuring our winter evenings are never dull.Our servants have an easy time; they need no reprimand. They have cleverly presented us as a traveling couple, content with their roles. The gardeners sell surplus produce, and we enjoy an abundance hard to find in Paris.I strive to fill the role of society for Gaston, ensuring his solitude does not become tedious. I have learned true jealousy—the kind born of love. The thought of him ceasing to love me sends shivers through me, and I tremble before him.Alas, I am still without a child. I long for the day when our home will be filled with the laughter of children running through our garden. It pains me to see your lovely children, for my life feels narrower without them. Motherhood expands a woman's heart, while love can be selfish. I envy your happiness and the joy you find in your children.As I approach thirty, I feel the limits of my beauty closing in. Though still attractive, I worry about aging while Gaston remains young. I often ask him to promise he will tell me if his love ever fades, and he swears it will not, finding the idea absurd.Goodbye, dear friend. Shall we let years pass without writing again? Happiness can be monotonous, and perhaps that is why Dante seems greater in the Paradiso than in the Inferno. I am not Dante, only your friend, and I do not wish to bore you. You can write, as your children provide endless joy, while I... No more of this. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, I have read your letter repeatedly; it sounds more like a child's than a woman's. You forget that pure love belongs to nature and is distorted by civilization. Society created the family as the foundation of organized life at the cost of individual desires. A married man dedicates himself to his wife, who benefits from his efforts.You have taken a misguided path: the dream of being adored suits the young, not a mature woman. Vanity may enjoy inspiring adoration, but true fulfillment lies in the life of wife and mother. Return to Paris; fear not misfortune but happiness.Life's simple pleasures—bread, air, silence—sustain us. Passionate love exhausts; motherhood’s affections endure and reward. I chose self-sacrifice and would choose it again. Your life, despite its romantic veneer, is rooted in selfishness; I plead with you as I watch your happiness tested.Life is balance; excess brings pain or only fleeting pleasure. You are unwell because you cling to a passion a marriage should steady. The glory of home is calmness and a shared life, which you overlook. You, more passionate than tender, would be both wife and mistress, undermining marriage's sanctity.If you could understand a mother's sweet self-discipline. My proud nature softened into gentle melancholy, rewarded by motherhood's joys. I fear your life may darken. I wish you could witness family delights, but my arguments seem futile against your illusions. I had hoped time would restore reason, but your appetite for love seems insatiable. Farewell; I despair my letter, meant to reconcile you with society, only revealed your selfishness.---Renee, calamity has struck—no, a thunderbolt for poor Louise. Doubt is my calamity; certainty would be death. The day before yesterday, after my first toilette, I looked for Gaston to walk but found him gone. In the stable his mare Fedelta was in a lather, implying he'd been to Paris without telling me. Suspicion seized me; I struggled to compose myself.When Gaston found me I was pale and shaken. He carried me to the parlor; I would not speak and fled to my room to weep. He waited; I said I'd see him when ready. After composing myself he returned, and I asked about mud on Fedelta's legs. He playfully threatened to withhold explanations; yet I saw no disloyalty in his eyes, though I remained unconvinced.We spent the day together, and my heart clung to him. I hated that mare for my distress. I asked if I bored him; he reassured me. I suggested selling Fedelta; his response crushed me, making me feel inadequate. Alone, I was consumed by despair, surrounded by darkness.Next morning I told him to take Fedelta to Paris; my tone betrayed turmoil. He urged trust; I tried to suppress suspicion. He left, and I grappled with fear. I felt a strange solemnity in my love and watched him go with a sense of doom.When he returned late with a whip as a gift I felt it only distracted from something deeper. I later learned he bought it from Verdier, confirming my suspicions. I resolved to keep watch while staying silent.Days passed; Gaston changed, working harder—seeming to need money. His drawer was empty; he received letters he quickly tore up. Jealousy consumed me; I followed him to the post office and confronted him about a letter from Nathan announcing a play's acceptance.Despite his success, I couldn't shake that he hid something. I kept suspicions to myself until confirming fears. A growing certainty that he had a rival drove me to discover the truth.After investigation I learned Gaston visited a woman in Paris; I followed and found she was his brother's widow, legally married. The revelation shattered me: he had been providing for her and her children. I returned tormented by thoughts of betrayal and jealousy.Despite the pain, my love for Gaston remained. I struggled with his past and wanted to confront him about his feelings for the widow, but feared the truth would destroy me. I even planned my own end, unable to bear the suffering.---My dear Louise, I write urgently; your last letter alarmed me. I confided in Louis to save you. Though our methods may seem extreme, the outcome is satisfactory. I even involved the police, kept secret among us.Gaston is not as you feared. His brother Louis died in Calcutta, leaving a widow and two children. Gaston has secretly supported them, using his earnings for his sister-in-law and her children. The woman you mistook for a rival is not a threat but a mother in need; Gaston acted from kindness and duty.You should love him more; he has shown sensitivity and care. Welcome the widow and her children into your life—perhaps adopt the boys. This revelation should give you peace, not despair.Ah, my dear friend, what can I say? It is too late for me. My actions have sealed my fate. Tormented by love for Gaston, I cannot bear losing him. I am filled with despair and long for relief. Come, dear friend, and witness my decline.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My love,—Take the children to Provence without me; I must stay with Louise, who has only a few days left. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whom I worry.You know the letter that sent me rushing to Ville d'Avray, gathering doctors along the way. Since then I have not left my dear friend, sitting up every night for two weeks.When I arrived, Louise was with Gaston, looking beautiful and happy, but it was a façade. She whispered, "He must not know; I am dying." A chill ran through me as I felt her burning hand and saw the red spots on her cheeks. I was glad I had left the doctors in the woods until needed."Leave us for a little," she told Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have secrets to share."Once alone she collapsed into my arms, unable to hold back her tears. "Tell me about it," I urged. "I have brought the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, along with Bianchon.""Ah! Have them in at once if they can save me! The passion that drove me to death now cries for life!""But what have you done to yourself?""I have brought myself to the last stage of consumption in just a few days.""But how?""I got myself into a profuse perspiration at night, then lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, but I am dying!""Send him to Paris; I will fetch the doctors myself," I said, rushing out to find them.Alas! After the consultation, none of the doctors gave me hope; they all believe Louise will die with the fall of the leaves. Her constitution had aided her plan, but a few days of folly made her case desperate.I cannot express my feelings upon hearing this. I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. I was crushed and could not escort the doctors out. I was lost in bitter thoughts when I heard Louise's clear voice: "So there is no hope for me!"She made me get up and took me to her drawing-room. "Stay with me to the end; I won't have sad faces around me. I must keep the truth from him. I know I have the strength to do it. I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood.""For him, I can see I would have made his life miserable. My jealousy has already wounded him. When my suspicions met indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. There are people who have lived sixty years without two years of real life, while my thirty years are doubled by the intensity of my love.""For him and me, the end is happy. But for you, dear Renée, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss that cannot be repaired. You alone have the right to mourn my death."After a long pause she continued: "The moral of my death is cruel. My dear doctor was right; marriage cannot rest on passion or even love. Your life is noble, giving your husband growing affection, while my passionate eagerness diminished. Twice I have gone astray, and twice Death has reached for my happiness. The first time, he took the noblest of men; now he takes me from my poet husband, with all his beauty and grace.""I would not complain. I have known two men of nobility—one in mind, the other in form. I die adored—what more could I wish? Perhaps time to draw near to God, whom I may have thought too little of. My spirit will fly towards Him, full of love, praying to be reunited with the two who made my life a heaven. Without them, paradise would be a desert.""My example would be fatal to others; to meet a Felipe or a Gaston is more than mortals can expect. The doctrine of society regarding marriage aligns with natural law. Women are weak and should sacrifice their will to their husbands, who should lay their selfishness at their feet. The stir women have created by their whining is ridiculous, showing how well we deserve the epithet of children."She spoke in her gentle voice, expressing grave truths prettily, until Gaston entered with his sister-in-law, the children, and the English nurse he had fetched from Paris."Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!"She welcomed Mme. Gaston and insisted she consider the chalet her home, playing the hostess in her best manner.I wrote to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, as well as to Madeleine. It was time. The next day Louise, exhausted from the exertion, could not go out; she only got up for dinner. That evening Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her brothers, and her mother arrived. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage faded. Every day since, her father and brothers have visited, and the duchesses spend their evenings at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences petty feelings.Louise remains charming, graceful, sensible, witty, and tender. She has retained her perfect tact and lavishes her brilliant mind on us, which made her one of Paris's queens."I want to look well even in my coffin," she said with her matchless smile as she lay down on the bed where she would linger for a fortnight.Her room lacks the sick-chamber atmosphere; medicines and nursing supplies are concealed."Is not my deathbed pretty?" she asked the priest who came to confess her.We watched her like misers. The anxiety and harsh truths have prepared Gaston for the worst. He is brave, but the blow has struck deep. I would not be surprised if he followed her soon. Yesterday, while walking by the lake, he said, "I must be a father to those two children," pointing to his sister-in-law with the boys. "But I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them and persuade your husband to accept the role of guardian."He spoke simply, like a man who knows his time is short. He wears smiles for Louise, but I see through his façade. He matches her courage.Louise wishes to see her godson, but I prefer he remains in Provence; she might want to remember him generously, putting me in a difficult position.Goodbye, my love.August 25th (her birthday).Yesterday evening Louise was delirious for a short time, but her babbling was beautiful, showing that even the madness of gifted people is not that of fools. In a faint voice she sang Italian airs from I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moïse, while we stood around her bed in silence. Not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes, realizing her soul was departing. She could no longer see us, yet her charm lingered in the melody, sweet and otherworldly.During the night the death agony began. It is now seven in the morning, and I have just raised her from bed. A flicker of strength returned; she wished to sit by the window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving us with an empty shell.The last sacrament was administered the evening before, unbeknownst to Gaston, who was sleeping during the ceremony. After she was moved to the window, she asked me to read the De Profundis in French, facing the lovely scene she created. She repeated the words after me and pressed her husband's hands.August 26th.My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. Oh! I want my children! Bring me my children!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Comtesse Bolognini, nee Vimercati.Madame, recalling the pleasure your conversation gave a traveler in Milan, I lay this work at your feet, hoping you will protect it with your name as you once shielded the tales of a beloved writer. You have a daughter, Eugenie, whose beauty and intelligence promise a joyous childhood, unlike the rigid upbringing of the Eugenie in these pages. While Frenchmen are often thought fickle, I remain Italian in constancy and memory. Writing “Eugenie” recalls the laughter and warmth of our time in Milan; I regard you now as a distant, treasured masterpiece.Your devoted servant,De Balzac.A DAUGHTER OF EVECHAPTER I. THE TWO MARIESIn an elegant blue-velvet boudoir on rue Neuve-des-Mathurins, two young women wept before a fireplace. The room belonged to Ferdinand du Tillet, a wealthy banker who had married the daughter of a prominent magistrate. Despite his fortune, du Tillet lacked genuine social standing, having married into the aristocracy for a dowry.Madame du Tillet held her sister Madame Félix de Vandenesse’s hand and listened to intimate confessions. The sisters, raised by a strict, pious mother, had entered marriage without ever enjoying youthful freedoms. Their upbringing was so severe they married naive about life and love, their spirits stifled by maternal control that forbade the wider world and taught only religion and needlework. Marriage had seemed an escape, though they were unprepared for what awaited them.CHAPTER II. A CONFIDENCE BETWEEN SISTERSMadame de Vandenesse sought solace in her sister’s company, overwhelmed by marital troubles and despair over a husband seemingly indifferent to her happiness. Madame du Tillet, feeling helpless, listened as her sister lamented; their sisterly love transcended their husbands’ indifference.Madame du Tillet revealed her own marriage’s emptiness, describing her husband’s coldness and life’s flatness, contrasted with their shared warmth. Both voiced desires for passion and excitement, recognizing societal and marital limits. Each encouraged the other to find happiness within those constraints.CHAPTER III. THE HISTORY OF A FORTUNATE WOMANFélix de Vandenesse, of noble lineage, had married hoping to recreate an idealized love he once knew. Initially content, Madame de Vandenesse gradually felt monotony and lack of passion. She longed for excitement and adventure, restricted by a predictable life. Her husband, kind and attentive, could not satisfy her craving for deeper emotional connection, and she grew alert to the allure of romance beyond marriage.CHAPTER IV. A CELEBRATED MANRaoul Nathan, an emerging literary figure, entered society with talent and audacity. Known for sharp wit and caustic humor, he both charmed and alienated. His unconventional appearance and manner set him apart, making him captivating to women.At a gathering hosted by Madame de Montcornet, Nathan caught Madame de Vandenesse’s attention. Their initial interactions carried unspoken attraction. Managing a complex career and private life, he became drawn to her, seeing her as a muse. Yet society’s norms and their circumstances threatened their budding connection.CHAPTER V. FLORINEFlorine, a talented actress and Nathan’s long-time companion, embodied another side of his life. Their relationship mixed affection and practicality: she buoyed his spirits and career while relying on him for her own success. Their bond was complicated by public demands and private needs.As Nathan’s ambitions grew, he found himself torn between loyalty to Florine and growing feelings for Madame de Vandenesse. The actress, aware of his infatuation, felt threatened by the countess and the possible shift in Nathan’s affections.CHAPTER VI. ROMANTIC LOVEMadame de Vandenesse and Nathan’s relationship deepened, fueled by shared passion for art and literature. Their encounters grew more intimate, and both felt exhilarated by the connection. But societal expectations and their roles cast long shadows, creating tension.Madame de Vandenesse wrestled with guilt and desire, torn between loyalty to her husband and attraction to Nathan. The emotional turmoil intensified her longing for a life of passion, even as she feared the consequences.CHAPTER VII. SUICIDENathan, under mounting pressure professionally and personally, sank into despair. The weight of ambition and fear of failure drove him to contemplate suicide. A chance encounter with Madame de Vandenesse, however, rekindled his will to live.Seeing his desperation, the countess resolved to help. She recognized his struggle’s depth and sought to encourage him, offering protection from his darkest impulses and from the destructive side of his ambitions.CHAPTER VIII. A LOVER SAVED AND LOSTAfter Nathan’s near-suicide, Madame de Vandenesse became more involved in his life, balancing protection and her own feelings. Their relationship became a source of strength and turmoil as they faced the moral and practical realities of love amid social scrutiny.As Nathan’s work began to flourish, he remained torn between Florine’s practical attachment and the countess’s inspiring influence. Florine, feeling endangered, grew increasingly possessive, heightening tensions among the three.CHAPTER IX. THE HUSBAND’S TRIUMPHFélix de Vandenesse, aware of his wife’s attachment to Nathan, acted to preserve his marriage. Determined to uncover the truth and shield her from scandal, he sought to protect her honor and well-being. His resolve to defend their union showed his love and commitment.The story follows characters entangled by love, ambition, and societal expectation, leading to a dramatic conclusion that highlights conflicts of desire and duty.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Louis Boulanger, Painter.I. EARLY MISTAKESOn a Sunday morning in April 1813 a luxurious cabriolet drew up before the Tuileries, carrying an anxious, aged father and his beautiful daughter, Julie. He took pride in the attention she attracted as they walked through the gardens; she was eager to see the military review commanded by Napoleon, the last before a disastrous campaign. The father noted her impatience and felt both joy and sorrow for her fleeting happiness.They reached the gates to disappointment; the review had not begun, and he suggested leaving. Julie insisted on staying, afraid of missing the Emperor. An officer recognized her and admitted them to a prime vantage point. The spectacle was grand: the Old Guard prepared, the crowd buzzed, and anticipation mounted for Napoleon's arrival.When Napoleon appeared, cheers arose. Julie was captivated, but her attention soon fixed on a young officer, Colonel Victor d’Aiglemont, on the Emperor's staff.
