Calyste, guilty and easily moved, shed tears that deceived Sabine. The physician’s invented story—that Calyste had lost thirty thousand francs gambling—was accepted; the duchess had repaired the blunder, at least temporarily.Two days later, Sabine seemed out of danger. Calyste, foolhardy and in love, went to Madame de Rochefide to boast:“Beatrix,” he said, “you owe me happiness. I have sacrificed my poor little wife to you; she nearly died. That fatal paper bore your name and coronet—though the ‘B’ was accidentally effaced. Your perfume and my lies betrayed my happiness. Sabine’s milk turned; erysipelas set in; she may bear the marks for life.”Beatrix listened with a hard, icy face.“So much the better,” she said. “Perhaps it will whiten her for you.”She replied with bitter sarcasm. There is no greater mistake than for a man to speak of his virtuous wife to his mistress; Calyste lacked the Parisian education of deceit. He could neither lie to his wife properly nor be candid with his mistress. He begged Beatrix’s pardon for hours; she refused, maintaining the dignity of her rank and dignity. She declared she loved his soul and had higher standards, attacking his appearance and virtues with bitter flattery disguised as contempt. Her method was deliberate: to make him feel repulsive, to provoke him to prove his power by conquering her disgust. For conquering temperaments, being resisted renews the triumph of conquest. Beatrix combined this contempt with constant comparisons between her poetic, comfortable home and the hotel du Guenic, belittling bourgeois Faubourg Saint-Germain luxury.She had deliberately transformed herself into a new incarnation at each passion, refining the arts of a courtesan. Fallen from Conti’s favor and proud of her name, she sought at least the fame that unprincipled conduct affords. The misfortune of the rich, beautiful, virtuous Grandlieu would be her pedestal. Thus she demanded that Calyste swear hatred for his wife and take up the role she deemed fitting for him, while she deployed every artifice—poetry, disdain, calculated emotion—to bind him to her and humiliate the rival who threatened her claims.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When a woman returns to ordinary life after nursing her first child, she often seems rejuvenated and charming, bringing freshness to young mothers. But when Sabine comes back to her husband Calyste, she finds he is not the man she married. Instead of security she feels anxiety and looks for certainty about their relationship. That certainty arrives in a letter from her mother hinting at Calyste's discontent and the presence of another woman, Beatrix.Sabine's heart sinks as she reads the vague note, which reveals nothing explicit about Calyste's feelings. In despair she scrawls a comment on the letter to signal her awareness and leaves it where Calyste will find it. He discovers the note but ignores it, throwing the paper into the fire. Sabine is left in agony, convinced she has displeased him and humiliated her idol.As days pass, her torment deepens. Beatrix, emboldened, writes to Calyste; Sabine, recognizing the perfume, realizes the extent of her rival's influence. In a moment of madness she dances with her child, seeking solace in motherhood's innocence, yet her heart remains heavy and she weeps alone when Calyste leaves to read Beatrix's message.Her suffering grows with each attempt to regain Calyste's affection. She fills their home with flowers to please him, but his indifference only deepens her despair. Obsessed with understanding his feelings and comparing herself to Beatrix, she becomes trapped in jealousy and self-doubt. Efforts to charm him by cooking and tending her appearance prove futile as Calyste stays unresponsive.Despite her struggles, Sabine's love persists. She strives to embody the devoted wife's virtues, but these efforts bring disappointment. One evening, after failing to move him, she succumbs to despair and tears off her beautiful dress in frustration.Over time Sabine's health declines under the weight of grief. The duchess, her mother, notices and tries to draw her away from the source of suffering. Sabine stays silent, fearing interference in her marriage. Eventually she confides in her sister, telling of her anguish and asking that her child be cared for if she should die.Vulnerable, Sabine warns her sister Athenais about love's dangers, advising caution in marriage. She reflects that her virtues have become obstacles to happiness, lamenting that straightforwardness and passion have failed to win Calyste's heart. Instead she feels pushed toward the duplicity she believes necessary to retain his affection.Concerned, the duchess consults Abbe Brossette, a respected priest, revealing a plan to intervene in Calyste's attachment to Beatrix and restore her daughter's happiness. The abbe cautions against mixing spiritual authority with worldly intrigue but recognizes the seriousness of Sabine's plight.The duchess, weighing his advice, becomes determined to act for her daughter's sake, even if that means resorting to morally questionable measures. She resolves to seek a solution that will restore peace to the family and spare Sabine further suffering.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A storm was brewing for Monsieur de Rochefide, who was enjoying Parisian life as fully as he had with Beatrix and thought such a satisfactory existence nearly impossible to disrupt. To see how society treats men and women differently in similar situations, we follow Rochefide's life after Beatrix left—a tale that might encourage women to stay home and defend their dignity, as Sabine du Guenic did.After Beatrix's departure, Arthur de Rochefide, now an only child after his sister’s death, became sole owner of the hôtel de Rochefide and inherited a large fortune. His income reached a thousand francs a day, ample for his happiness. While Beatrix fulfilled maternal duties, Rochefide enjoyed his wealth without extravagance. His vanity, aided by good looks, let him dismiss women and appropriate others' wit. His military background lent a jaunty tone to his conversation, making him seem witty to acquaintances.As a husband he was pitied; society blamed Beatrix for leaving such a “good” man. He joined clubs and championed fashionable causes to distinguish himself. His main indulgence was racehorses, run by an English groom, costing him a sizable monthly sum. Despite wealth, he lacked taste and intellect, relying largely on inherited fortune. After the downsides of marriage he welcomed bachelorhood, claiming he was "born with a caul," destined for luck.Rochefide's indifference to domestic life was clear: he rarely dined or slept at home. After several affairs he married Madame Schontz, a woman of dubious reputation known in circles around Fanny Beaupré and others. That world—where many women ended in poverty or early death—required a nuanced grasp of social dynamics. Madame Schontz, born La Petite-Aurélie, emerged from a difficult upbringing: orphaned and educated at Saint-Denis, a school that schooled girls but offered few prospects.Aurélie, daughter of Colonel Schiltz, led a tumultuous life of various relationships, which brought her by chance to Rochefide at Valentino’s. Captivated by her charm, Rochefide married her two years after Beatrix left, and society did not condemn him for the choice.Rochefide's relationship with Madame Schontz unfolded in four phases. The first was courtship: he provided a modest allowance and a furnished apartment. As he learned her character he found her noble and charming; she revealed talents he admired, though ambition soon surfaced and she sought a more comfortable life.In the second phase Madame Schontz moved to a better apartment; Rochefide lavished gifts and support. She ran their household efficiently, impressing him with budgeting and superior dinners. This domestic competence convinced Rochefide she loved him for himself, since she rebuffed wealthier suitors.The third phase cast Madame Schontz as mother figure to Rochefide's son, overseeing his education and finances. She advised Rochefide on investments, producing significant gains she did not claim. Taking on more responsibilities, she became integral—managing affairs and ensuring his comfort.In the fourth phase she established herself socially, curating her guest list and preserving reputation. She amassed a small fortune through savings and investments, supplementing it by wearing and selling jewelry. Despite financial success she downplayed her wealth, framing expenses as necessary to maintain her lifestyle.Through these phases Madame Schontz demonstrated tactical superiority, transforming the relationship into mutual benefit while skillfully navigating their social world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous of Madame du Bruel for two years, she was determined to marry in both church and civil ceremonies. This required a second Arthur, but Rochefide’s secret rival remained unknown. Bixiou suspected Leon de Lora; the painter thought Bixiou himself was favored. Victor de Vernisset, a poet, and Stidmann, a young sculptor, were also considered. None truly understood Madame Schontz, whose focus was solely on securing a good marriage.The only man of questionable reputation in her salon was Couture, a stock-gambler who had fallen from grace. Despite his failures he remained loyal, and when he introduced her to a provincial, Fabien du Ronceret, Madame Schontz began to consider him. Fabien, son of a chief justice, had left a judgeship in Alencon for Paris with a modest income and a desire to exploit changing social mores.The modern era encouraged pride, vanity, and ambition; people sought recognition at any cost. Fabien, not particularly clever, grasped this shift and aimed to make a name for himself by being talked about. He pursued attention through extravagant living and connections.His Paris debut was marked by ostentation: a lavish apartment and a greenhouse intended as a spectacle. He sought association with influential figures and entry into Madame Schontz’s circle. After failed attempts through established acquaintances, he turned to Couture, who recognized Fabien’s potential and recommended him to Madame Schontz.Madame Schontz quickly assessed Fabien and saw a suitable candidate for her ambitions. She flattered him, leading him to suppose he was the object of her affection. Fabien, eager to impress, presented himself in his finest attire, captivated by her charm. A month before a significant meeting, Madame Schontz revealed her true identity and aims to Fabien, who failed to grasp her intentions.Realizing his misunderstanding, she took Fabien for a carriage ride, expressing her desire to marry a man of ambition who could appreciate her character. Despite her wealth and encouragement for him to rise, Fabien hesitated at the prospect of marriage, and she then turned her attention to Couture, who quickly proposed upon learning of her fortune.Meanwhile the Duchesse de Grandlieu sought the Comte Maxime de Trailles’s help to manage a delicate family situation. Maxime, a seasoned political operator, understood the motivations behind the duchess’s invitation and agreed to assist, but insisted on conditions, including the duchess’s acceptance of his future wife into her social circle.Maxime’s plan included removing a rival from the picture, requiring the duchess to follow his instructions closely. He acknowledged the emotional entanglements—especially Calyste’s deep infatuation with Béatrix—and recognized the difficulty of manipulating such feelings. His grasp of love and manipulation revealed the complexities of the relationships as he prepared to execute his scheme.As the duchess agreed to support Maxime’s wife, he assured her he would handle the intricacies, albeit with a hint of moral ambiguity. Their conversation underscored the lengths people would go to secure desires, often at the expense of others’ feelings and reputations.The next day Rochefide informed Madame Schontz of Maxime’s impending visit, prompting her to ready her house and appearance to impress him. Maxime’s reputation as a powerful figure in Parisian society preceded him, and his presence was both a privilege and a challenge for Madame Schontz as she navigated the complexities of her ambitions and relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot to arrange a breakfast at the Café Anglais with Couture and Lousteau, where they discussed Madame Schontz. Maxime loitered on Tortoni’s portico under the pretense of transacting with du Tillet while waiting for the Comte de la Palferine. When La Palferine arrived, Maxime questioned him about his debts; the Comte wryly acknowledged his straits, and Maxime, admiring his spirit, offered to help him woo a woman, promising a loan of twenty thousand francs for the campaign.Maxime’s scheme targeted a well-bred marquise; he urged La Palferine to court her sincerely and they agreed to meet later at Madame Schontz’s. Meanwhile Maxime visited the duchess, who fretted over her daughter Sabine’s distress at Calyste’s absence. He asked her to keep his plans secret and requested twenty thousand francs for his maneuver, assuring her it would not demand undue expense.Madame Schontz prepared a dinner, inviting prominent guests including Claude Vignon and La Palferine; she dressed carefully to impress. Maxime praised her success with Rochefide, noting how she had cost him less than other conquests. Madame Schontz insisted she was an artist rather than a courtesan and expressed a desire to make a good marriage.At dinner Maxime conferred with La Palferine, giving him the promised funds and detailed instructions for making a striking impression on the marquise. He emphasized the necessity of an unforgettable display. As the evening continued, Maxime and Madame Schontz plotted to manipulate Rochefide’s affections; Maxime vowed to secure a position for Couture in return for his cooperation.The next day Calyste, feeling neglected, confronted Beatrix, who was torn between genuine feeling for him and a growing attraction to La Palferine. Their relationship tightened into strain as Beatrix wrestled with guilt and desire, fearful of consequences. La Palferine’s charm and wit increasingly drew her, making her question her loyalty to Calyste.Maxime intensified his scheming, arranging encounters between Beatrix and La Palferine that further complicated the dynamics. Calyste, increasingly desperate, tried to reclaim Beatrix’s affection, but she remained entangled by La Palferine’s allure. The tension peaked with a public humiliation for Beatrix at the opera, where she was left exposed and alone.Afterward Beatrix’s attachment to Calyste began to wane as she sank deeper into La Palferine’s orbit. Maxime, watching the drama unfold, perceived shifting loyalties and the emotional turmoil of all involved. He counseled Calyste to reconsider his tactics, suggesting the pain he endured might not be worth persisting.As events progressed, characters navigated conflicting desires and ambitions, prompting confrontations and revelations. Beatrix’s internal conflict deepened and her relationship with La Palferine grew more complicated as she struggled with identity and social expectations. Ultimately the narrative examines love, betrayal, and the complexity of human relationships, where ambition and affection produce disillusionment and force characters to question past choices and future paths.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ######################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################If the French have a strong aversion to traveling, the English have a propensity for it; both nations have reasons. Other countries may offer admirable scenery and comfort, but the unique charms of France—its wit, conversation, and unspoken understanding—are hard to replicate. Many Frenchmen therefore take pleasure in encountering their homeland’s ways even abroad, finding comforts reminiscent of Paris.In 1836, during the Sardinian Court's stay in Genoa, two notable Parisians found themselves in a palazzo, a remnant of Genoese nobility. Genoa’s evenings, especially after rain, create a serene atmosphere suited to friends and Italian cuisine. At a dinner hosted by the French Consul-General were the celebrated Mademoiselle des Touches, known as Camille Maupin, and her companions: the painter Léon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon.Camille had gone to Florence on business and brought Léon to experience Italy. Their stop in Genoa was a courtesy to the Consul, who wished to honor her presence. Despite her fame, Camille was cautious about engaging with the ambassador and preferred to enjoy the evening without social pressure. The dinner was lively, conversation flowed freely, though literature was scarcely mentioned until the Consul decided to share a personal story.The Consul, a man of thirty-four who resembled Lord Byron with a dreamy expression, had married Onorina Pedrotti, the only child of a wealthy banker, after a period of reluctance. Their union had been influenced by various factors, including the ambassador's intervention and the Consul's hidden struggles. Onorina, a beautiful Genoese woman, embodied the elegance of her heritage, and their family life appeared harmonious though Camille perceived an underlying melancholy in him.As the evening progressed, debate turned to women’s morality and accountability in cases of infidelity. The three women judged the woman harshly, while the men argued for a more nuanced view. Seeking to shift the mood, the Consul began recounting a pivotal moment from his youth.At twenty-two, his uncle, the Abbe Loraux, arranged for him to become secretary to Comte Octave, a prominent statesman. The uncle’s influence secured a position promising a bright future, despite the young man’s challenges. The Consul’s narrative revealed his initial innocence and naivety, having been sheltered from society’s complexities.Settling into his role, he observed the Count’s character and burdens. The Count, outwardly composed and pious, harbored a profound sadness the Consul gradually understood. Their relationship deepened: the Count treated him with kindness and respect, often blurring lines between master and mentor. The Consul was struck by the Count’s intellect, devotion to duty, and the contrast between his public success and private sorrow.The Consul reflected on the Count’s meticulous nature and his love for flowers, a tenderness that mirrored his own faded beauty. The Count’s life was marked by relentless duty and a search for a happiness that eluded him. Though outwardly devoted to work and religion, he seemed to be seeking something more—an inner peace he could not attain. This tension between public expectations and private despair shaped their relationship and the Consul’s understanding of human complexity.The Consul’s story, rich in detail and feeling, captivated his audience, revealing intricate dynamics of love, duty, and the human condition. When he concluded, the guests were left pondering the depths of the Count’s character and the struggles beneath the surface of seemingly perfect lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le Président de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice‑President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three with me as secretary. The Count had secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All the necessary documents for their inquiry lay in the library; MM. de Grandville and de Serizy relied on the Count for the preliminary examination. To avoid carrying all the papers to M. de Serizy, they agreed to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Tuileries valued this work, and it led to my appointment as Master of Appeals that year.“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, who rarely dined out, were still discussing late into the evening, a servant interrupted me: ‘MM. the Cures of Saint‑Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing‑room for two hours.’“It was nine o’clock.“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown—’“‘It depends on the priest.’“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbé Gaudron,’ I said. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbé Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint‑Paul.’“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the Président de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’“We entered the drawing‑room. The dinner was delightful. Politicians with real knowledge make excellent storytellers—either heavy or sublime; Prince Metternich is as good as Charles Nodier. My uncle’s wit was refined and elegant, typical of those who conceal thoughts under a black robe. There was nothing vulgar in this light talk; I compared it to Rossini’s music.“The Abbé Gaudron, as M. de Grandville observed, was more like a Saint Peter than a Saint Paul: a peasant full of faith, whose ignorance of worldly matters enlivened the talk with guileless amazement. They began discussing a social issue—adultery. My uncle pointed out the contradiction between civil and religious law that legislators had left after the Revolution.“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ he said, ‘adultery is a crime; in your tribunals, it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar. Napoleon’s Council of State, touched with tenderness towards erring women, was quite inefficient. Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents; monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbé—give to God what society would have none of?’“‘Oh!’ said Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They had to leave the husband free to take proceedings; well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’“‘M. l’Abbé preaches for his own saint,’ said Comte Octave; ‘in the East, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, modern Europe—daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage and made it a sacrament.’“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count continued with a smile. ‘But its practices will never match a climate where women marry at seven and are old at twenty‑five. The Catholic Church overlooked half the globe. Let us discuss Europe only.“‘Is woman our superior or inferior? That is the real question. If woman is inferior, placing her on such a high level as the Church does required severe punishments for adultery. Formerly, cloister or death summed up legislation. But practice has since modified the law. The throne served as a hotbed for adultery, and the growth of this inviting crime marks the decline of Catholic dogmas. Nowadays, where the Church demands only sincere repentance from the erring wife, society is satisfied with a brand instead of execution. The law still condemns the guilty, but it no longer terrifies. In short, there are two moral standards: that of the world and that of the Code. Where the Code is weak, the world is audacious and satirical. Few judges would not gladly have committed the faults they condemn. The world punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The entire marriage law seems to require reconstruction. The French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’“‘We three know the question thoroughly,’ said Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and if divorce is ever brought in again, we shall form the committee.’“Octave’s fork dropped, breaking his glass and plate. He turned pale and shot a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence.“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the President continued. ‘Serizy and I, after witnessing your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in front of these venerable priests.’“M. de Serizy changed the subject, recounting his efforts to please his wife without success. The old man concluded that human sympathies and antipathies cannot be regulated; social law is most perfect when closest to natural law. Nature does not consider the affinities of souls; her aim is the propagation of the species. Thus, the Code, in its current form, wisely leaves latitude to chance. The incapacity of daughters to inherit while male heirs exist is an excellent provision, whether to prevent degeneration or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions.“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand in disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country that insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators! After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’“‘Setting aside the religious question,’ my uncle remarked, ‘Nature only owes us life; society owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ he asked.“‘And I—do I have any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, making such an impression that the topic of wives and marriage was dropped.“When coffee was served, the two Counts and the two priests quietly left, noticing that poor Octave had fallen into a melancholy fit, oblivious to their departure. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, crushed.“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said to me when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, I came home one evening to find a letter from the Countess announcing her flight. The letter was dignified; women often retain some virtues even when committing such a sin. The story now is that my wife went abroad on a ship that was wrecked; she is presumed dead. I have lived alone for seven years! Enough for tonight, Maurice. We will discuss my situation when I am ready to speak of it. When suffering from a chronic disease, it takes time to adjust to the idea of improvement. That improvement often seems merely another aspect of the complaint.’“I went to bed greatly agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure than ever. I anticipated a strange drama, realizing that the events leading the Countess to leave such a noble and loving man must have been extraordinary. M. de Grandville’s remark had illuminated the caverns I had been navigating, and though the light was dim, I could perceive their vastness. I could imagine the Count’s suffering without grasping its depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, and moments of absentmindedness stood out vividly during the twilight of mental questioning before sleep.“Oh! how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a poem of melancholy, seeing perpetual activity in the heart I had accused of being torpid. Must not supreme grief eventually lead to stagnation? Had this judge, with so much power, ever sought revenge? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable to harbor anger for ten years in Paris? What had Octave done since this great misfortune—when the separation of husband and wife has become a social question?“We allowed a few days to pass, for great sorrows have their own diffidence; but eventually, one evening, the Count said gravely:“‘Stay.’############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This, as nearly as may be, is his story.“My father had a ward, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I returned from college to live in this old house. Honorine, raised by my mother, was just awakening to life. Full of grace and childish ways, she dreamed of happiness as one dreams of jewels; even religion was poetry to her innocent heart. She viewed the future as a perpetual fête, awaiting marriage without desiring it, her imagination untainted by corruption. We lived together like siblings.“After a year, I asked her one day in the garden, by the basin where we fed the fish, if she would like to marry me. She told my mother, who insisted we wait eighteen months to see if our feelings remained. When I was twenty-six and Honorine nineteen, we married. Out of respect for my parents we did not modernize the house; I introduced my wife to society as a duty to guide her.“I later realized marriages like ours often harbor hidden dangers. The husband becomes a teacher, and love can wither under such authority. I trusted her innocence too much. Who can say if empires and happiness are destroyed by too much confidence or too much severity? I recall the self-reproaches I made, like an anatomist seeking a disease’s cause. His merciful indulgence reminded me of Christ’s compassion for the woman taken in adultery.“It was eighteen months after my father’s death, shortly after my mother passed, that I received Honorine’s farewell letter. What delusion had led her to leave? I was stunned for a month, then chose ignorance, learning too much about such matters through her misfortunes. So far, Maurice, the story is commonplace, but one word changes everything: I love Honorine, and I have never ceased to worship her. Since her departure, I have lived on memories, recalling pleasures she may not have appreciated.“Do not make a hero of me; I sought no diversion. I was either too young or too in love; I have not met another woman like her. After struggling to forget, I stood on the brink of infidelity, but Honorine’s memory stopped me. I fled temptation, haunted by thoughts of her. My life is a constant paroxysm of fears, joy, and despair.“I felt no anger towards her, like a father seeing a beloved child in danger. I understood I had idealized my wife, believing she shared my intoxication. An uncritical passion in a husband can lead to a wife’s infidelity. I may have exhausted her with my love before she was ready. Too young to understand that a wife’s constancy is the seed of maternal devotion, she mistook our early marriage for life itself. In her cruel position, she was defenseless against any man who stirred her deeply. I, who should have been wise, failed to grasp the unwritten laws of a woman’s heart until it was too late. I absolved her and condemned myself. Yet love held me like a desperate passion.“Society laughs at such a predicament. Where it pities a lover, it mocks a husband who cannot keep his wife. I had to remain silent. Serizy is happy; he can see and protect his wife, enjoying the joys of a benefactor. But I have nothing; I live solely on a love that starves. I loathe prostitution and remain faithful under a spell. Without religious faith, I might have killed myself. I threw myself into work, fevered and sleepless.“You can imagine my feelings when he concluded with this revelation: the interior drama interests nobody; one can only know another’s sorrow vaguely. I calm despair by contemplating a miniature of her, kissing her brow, breathing her fragrance. I have fits of collapse, wild hilarity, and terrors like a murderer meeting a sergeant of police.“The real drama is this: you think I am occupied with the Council of State, the Chamber, the Courts, Politics. Seven hours at night suffice for that. Honorine is my true concern. To recover my wife is my only study; to guard her without her suspecting my presence, to satisfy the little pleasures she allows herself, to be always near her like a sylph without her knowing it—that is my life. For seven years, I have never gone to bed without checking on her night-lamp or her shadow on the curtains.“She left my house, taking only the dress she wore that day. Eighteen months later she was deserted by her lover, who was appalled by poverty. Honorine has sixty thousand francs a year of her own. In November 1820, I persuaded the best accoucheur to act as a humble suburban apothecary and induced the parish priest to assist her as an act of charity. Then, to hide my wife, secure her against discovery, and find a devoted housekeeper who would be my confidante—it was a task worthy of Figaro! Finding where my wife had taken refuge was not difficult for me.“After three months of desperation, I devoted myself to Honorine, with God as my only witness. Love must have its daily food. Should I not protect this child, whose guilt sprang from my imprudence, against further disaster? At seven months, her infant died, happily for her and for me. For nine months she lay between life and death, deserted when she needed a man’s support. But this arm was extended over her head. Honorine was cared for as she would have been in her home. When she recovered and asked who had assisted her, she was told it was the Sisters of Charity, the Maternity Society, and the parish priest.“This woman, whose pride is almost a vice, has shown resistance in misfortune that I sometimes call obstinacy. Honorine was determined to earn her living. For five years, I have lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur, in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers. She believes she sells her elegant creations to a shop, earning twenty francs a day, living as if she had fifteen thousand. She is devoted to flowers, paying a gardener a hundred crowns, which costs me twelve hundred in wages. I promised the gardener a market-garden with a house close to the porter’s lodge in the Rue Saint-Maur, held in the name of a clerk of the law courts. The smallest indiscretion would ruin the gardener’s prospects. Honorine has her little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for a rent of five hundred francs a year. She lives under the name of her housekeeper, Madame Gobain, an old woman of impeccable discretion whose affection Honorine has won. For three years, Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort to her own toil.“Oh! I know what you are about to say,” cried the Count, seeing a question in my eyes. “Yes, I have made the attempt. My wife was living in the Faubourg Saint-Antoine. One day, believing in a chance for reconciliation, I wrote her a letter, which I rewrote twenty times. I will not describe my agonies. I went from the Rue Payenne to the Rue de Reuilly like a condemned wretch going to execution. It was dark and foggy; I went to meet Madame Gobain. Honorine, recognizing my handwriting, threw the letter into the fire without reading it, exclaiming, ‘I leave this tomorrow.’“What a dagger-stroke to a man who takes pleasure in trickery! I had just succeeded in deceiving my wife—sending her an Indian shawl, presented as the property of an actress, which I had wrapped myself in for a night! My life may be summed up in two words: I love, and I wait! I have in Madame Gobain a faithful spy on the heart I worship. I visit the old woman every evening to hear about Honorine’s day; for a single exclamation might reveal the secrets of that soul which is willfully deaf and dumb. Honorine is pious; she attends church and prays, but she has never confessed or taken Communion, foreseeing what a priest would say. She will not heed advice to return to me. This horror of me overwhelms me, for I have never harmed her. I have always been kind. Even if I was hasty in teaching her, is that a reason for her determination, which only the most implacable hatred could inspire? Honorine has never told Madame Gobain who she is; she keeps absolute silence about her marriage, so the housekeeper cannot speak in my favor. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and respect the power of a Minister. Hence, it is impossible for me to penetrate her heart; the citadel is mine, but I cannot enter it. I have no means of action. An act of violence would ruin me forever.“How can I argue against reasons of which I know nothing? Should I write a letter, have it copied by a public writer, and laid before Honorine? But that risks a third removal, which cost me fifty thousand francs. The last purchase was made in the name of the secretary who succeeded you. The unfortunate man, unaware of my vigilance, was caught opening a box containing a private agreement. I coughed, and he panicked; the next day, I forced him to sell the house to the man in whose name it now stands, and I turned him out.“If I did not feel my noblest faculties satisfied, happy, and expansive; if I were not playing the role of divine fatherhood; if I did not drink in delight by every pore, I might believe I was a monomaniac. Sometimes at night, I hear the jingling bells of madness. I dread the violent transitions from feeble hope to complete despair. Recently, I considered the tragic end of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe, thinking that if Honorine were the mother of my child, she must return under her husband’s roof.“I have such complete faith in a happy future that ten months ago, I bought one of the finest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win back Honorine, I will not allow her to see this house again, nor the room from which she fled. I intend to place my idol in a new temple, where she may feel that life is altogether new. That house is being transformed into a marvel of elegance and taste. I was inspired by a poet who, mad with love for an actress, bought the finest bed in Paris without knowing how she would reward his passion. Well, one of the coldest lawyers, thought to be the gravest adviser of the Crown, was stirred to the depths of his heart by that anecdote. The orator of the Legislative Chamber can understand the poet who fed his ideal on material possibilities. Three days before Maria Louisa’s arrival, Napoleon threw himself on his wedding bed at Compiègne. All great passions share the same impulses. I love as a poet—like an emperor!”As I heard the last words, I believed Count Octave’s fears were realized; he rose, walking and gesticulating, but stopped, shocked by the vehemence of his own words.“I am very ridiculous,” he added after a long pause, looking at me, as if craving pity.“No, monsieur, you are very unhappy.”“Ah yes!” he said, resuming his confidences. “From the violence of my speech, you must believe in the intensity of a physical passion that has absorbed me for nine years; but that is nothing compared to the worship I feel for her soul, mind, and heart—all the divinities of Love. By retrospection, I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart I overlooked in happiness—like all who are happy. Day by day, I appreciate my loss, discovering the exquisite gifts of that capricious and proud young creature who has grown strong under poverty and the cowardly shock of desertion. And that heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding! The law we discussed is a squad of gendarmes—my wife seized and dragged away by force! Would that not be to triumph over a corpse? Religion has no hold on her; she craves its poetry, prays, but does not heed the Church’s commandments. I have exhausted all mercy, kindness, and love; I am at my wits’ end. Only one chance of victory remains: the cunning and patience of bird-catchers who finally trap the wariest birds. Hence, Maurice, when M. de Grandville’s indiscretion revealed my life’s secret to you, I regarded it as fate’s decree, one of the utterances gamblers listen for in their most impassioned play. Have you enough affection for me to show me romantic devotion?”“I see what you are coming to, Monsieur le Comte,” I interrupted. “Your first secretary tried to open your deed box. I know your second’s heart—he might fall in love with your wife. Can you send him into the fire? Can anyone put their hand into a brazier without burning it?”“You are a foolish boy,” replied the Count. “I will send you well-gloved. It is not my secretary who will be lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur in the little garden-house I have at his disposal. It is my distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office…”After a moment of silent surprise, the gate bell rang and a carriage entered. The footman announced Madame de Courteville and her daughter. The Count had family connections on his mother’s side. Madame de Courteville, a widow, had a daughter and no fortune. What could a woman of nine-and-twenty be compared to a lovely young girl of twenty, ideal for a mistress?“Baron, and Master of Appeals, until you get something better, and this old house settled on her—would you not have enough reasons not to fall in love with the Countess?” he whispered as he introduced us.I was dazzled, not so much by advantages I had never dreamed of, but by Amelie de Courteville, whose beauty was adorned by a mother’s artful clothing.“But I will not talk of myself,” said the Consul after a pause.Three weeks later, I moved into the gardener’s cottage, cleaned, repaired, and furnished with the celerity explained by three words: Paris; French workmen; money! I was as much in love as the Count could desire as security. Would the prudence of a young man of five-and-twenty be equal to the part I was undertaking, involving a friend’s happiness? I confess I counted on my uncle’s advice; for I had been authorized by the Count to confide in him if necessary. I engaged a garden, devoted myself to horticulture, and worked frantically, turning up the soil and appropriating it to flower cultivation. Like the maniacs of England or Holland, I claimed devotion to one kind of flower, especially dahlias. My conduct, even in small details, was dictated by the Count, whose intellect focused on the tragi-comedy enacted in the Rue Saint-Maur. As soon as the Countess went to bed, around eleven, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I held council. I heard the old woman’s report on Honorine’s daily activities, meals, frame of mind, and plans. Octave lived only for that hour.For two months, while I worked in the garden, I never saw the little house where my fair neighbor lived. I had not even inquired whether I had a neighbor, though the Countess’ garden was divided from mine by a paling, along which she had planted cypress trees already four feet high. One morning, Madame Gobain announced that an eccentric neighbor intended to build a wall between the two gardens at the year’s end. I will say nothing of the curiosity that consumed me to see the Countess! My wish almost extinguished my budding love for Amelie de Courteville. My scheme for building a wall was indeed a dangerous threat: no fresh air for Honorine, whose garden would then be a narrow alley. This dwelling, once a summer villa, was like a house of cards; the garden front imitated a trellis with flowers up to the second floor, a charming Pompadour style. My wall would cut away three-quarters of the plot.“The Countess was in despair.“‘My good Gobain,’ she said, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’“‘He seems to dislike women,’ said the housekeeper. ‘He is the nephew of a Paris cure. It is quite possible that this priest encourages his nephew in his love of flowers, to prevent anything worse…’“‘What do you mean?’“‘Well,’ said Gobain, ‘he is a little cracked!’“Now a harmless lunatic is the only man no woman ever distrusts in matters of sentiment. You will see how wise the Count was in choosing this disguise for me.“‘What ails him then?’ asked the Countess.“‘He has studied too hard,’ replied Gobain; ‘he is misanthropic and has his reasons for disliking women…’“‘Madmen frighten me less than sane folks; I will speak to him myself! Tell him that I beg him to come here. If I do not succeed, I will send for the cure.’“The day after this conversation, as I walked along my graveled path, I caught sight of the half-opened curtains and a woman’s face peering out. Madame Gobain called me. I hastily glanced at the Countess’ house, shrugging rudely to express indifference.“‘Madame,’ said Gobain, ‘the madman bid me leave him in peace, saying that even a charcoal seller is master in his own premises, especially when he has no wife.’“‘He is perfectly right,’ said the Countess.“‘Yes, but he ended by saying, “I will go,” when I told him that he would greatly distress a lady living in retirement, who found her greatest solace in growing flowers.’“Next day, a signal from Gobain informed me that I was expected. After the Countess’ breakfast, when she was walking in front of her house, I broke some palings and approached her. I had dressed like a countryman, in old gray flannel trousers, heavy wooden shoes, a shabby shooting coat, a peaked cap, a ragged bandana, and hands soiled with mold.“‘Madame,’ said the housekeeper, ‘this good man is your neighbor.’“The Countess was not alarmed. At last, I saw the woman whose conduct and my husband’s confidences had made me so curious to meet. It was early May. The air was pure, the weather serene; the fragrance of spring formed a setting for this creature of sorrow. As I saw Honorine, I understood Octave’s passion: ‘A heavenly flower!’“Her pallor struck me with its unique tone of white—there are as many tones of white as of red or blue. At the slightest emotion, blood mounted under the surface in rosy flushes. The sunshine through the acacia cast a pale gold glow around Honorine, like the ambient glory Raphael and Titian gave the Virgin. Her brown eyes expressed tenderness and vivacity, their brightness reflected through long downcast lashes. Merely lifting her eyelids, Honorine could cast a spell; she could freeze or give life with a look. Her light-brown hair framed a high, dreamy brow. The mouth was voluptuous. Her head bore a stamp of nobility that would defy the outrages of time.“Though slight, Honorine was not thin; her figure could revive love when it believed itself exhausted. She embodied the idea of mignonne, one of those pliant women who allow themselves to be petted. Her small feet made a light sound on the gravel, harmonizing with the rustle of her dress, producing a feminine music distinct from others. Her gait bore the pride of her race; even a working man would make way. Gay and tender, haughty and imposing, she combined incompatible qualities yet remained a child. But this child could be as strong as an angel; once hurt, she would be implacable.“Coldness on that face must be death to those on whom her eyes had smiled, for whom her lips had parted, whose souls had drunk the melody of her voice. Inhaling the perfume of violets that accompanied her, I understood how the memory of this wife had arrested the Count on the threshold of debauchery. Honorine inspired devotion, chivalrous devotion, regardless of reward. A man seeing her must think of offering his life to procure one day’s happiness for her.“Many women study their expressions; in her it was the outcome of a delightful nature that went straight to the heart. If I tell you all this, it is because her soul, thoughts, and exquisite heart are what we are concerned with, and you would have blamed me had I not sketched them for you.“I was near forgetting my part as a half-crazy lout, clumsy and by no means chivalrous.“‘I am told, madame, that you are fond of flowers?’“‘I am an artificial flower-maker,’ she said. ‘After growing flowers, I imitate them, like a mother painting her children… That shows I am poor and unable to pay for the concession I long to obtain from you.’“‘But how,’ said I, grave as a judge, ‘can a lady of such rank ply so humble a calling? Have you, like me, good reasons for employing your fingers to keep your brains from working?’“‘Let us stick to the question of the wall,’ she said with a smile.“‘Why, we have begun at the foundations,’ said I. ‘Must I not know which of us ought to yield to the other in behalf of our suffering, or, if you choose, our mania?—Oh! what a charming clump of narcissus!’“She had created a perfect museum of flowers and shrubs, arranged as only the sun might see, prompted by artistic genius; even the most heartless landlord must have treated it with respect. The garden breathed comforting scents, suggesting only sweet thoughts. I looked alternately at the mass of narcissus and at the Countess, pretending to be far more in love with the flowers than with her, to maintain my part.“‘So you are very fond of flowers?’ she said.“‘They are,’ I replied, ‘the only beings that never disappoint our care and affection.’ I spoke at length comparing botany and the world, and we ended miles away from the dividing wall, which the Countess naturally brought us back to; for women, when not in love, have all the cold blood of an experienced attorney.“‘If you insist on my leaving the paling,’ said I, ‘you will learn all the secrets of gardening I want to hide; I am seeking a blue dahlia, a blue rose; I am crazy for blue flowers. Is not blue the favorite color of superior souls? We are neither of us really at home; we might as well make a little door of open railings to unite our gardens… You, too, are fond of flowers; you will see mine, I shall see yours. If you receive no visitors at all, I have none but my uncle, the Cure of the White Friars.’“‘No,’ said she, ‘I will give you the right to come into my garden at any hour. Come and welcome; you will be admitted as a neighbor with whom I hope to keep good terms. But I like my solitude too well to burden it with any loss of independence.’“‘As you please,’ said I, and with one leap I was over the paling.“For a fortnight I seemed to take no heed of my neighbor. Towards the end of May, one lovely evening, we were out on opposite sides of the paling, both walking slowly. Having reached the end, we exchanged a few civil words; she found me in deep dejection and spoke of hopefulness in brief sentences like lullabies. I then leaped the fence and found myself at her side again. The Countess led me into the house, wishing to soothe my sadness. At last, I had penetrated the sanctuary where everything harmonized with the woman I have tried to describe.“Exquisite simplicity reigned there. The little house was a dainty box of eighteenth-century art. The dining-room was painted in fresco with flower garlands; the staircase was charmingly decorated in monochrome. The little drawing-room was faded but hung with panels of tapestry; upstairs was one bedroom, a dressing-room, and a library used as her workroom. The kitchen was in the basement, raised by several steps outside. Only the lead cornices were visible. In this retreat, one was a hundred leagues from Paris.“But for the bitter smile that occasionally played on her lips, one might have believed the violet buried in her thicket of flowers was happy. In a few days we reached a degree of intimacy born of proximity and the Countess’ conviction that I was indifferent to women. A look would have spoiled everything, and I never allowed a thought of her to be seen in my eyes. Honorine regarded me as an old friend. Her manner stemmed from a kind of pity. Her looks, voice, and words showed she was a hundred miles away from coquetry. She soon granted me the right to enter her pretty workshop, full of books and curiosities, as smart as a boudoir where elegance emphasized the vulgarity of her tools. The Countess had poetized a thing far from poetry—a manufacture.“Perhaps of all the work a woman can do, making artificial flowers allows her the most grace. For coloring prints one must bend over a table; embroidery entails consumption; engraving requires minute care. But making flowers and light articles permits a pretty woman to remain herself—chatting, laughing, singing, thinking.“There was certainly an artistic feeling in how the Countess arranged petals on a long table. The saucers of color were white china, arranged so the eye could see each shade. A little cabinet with a hundred drawers held the steel molds; a Japanese bowl held the paste. Under her eyes, in a Venetian glass shaped like a flower-cup, was the living model she strove to imitate. She had a passion for achievement; she attempted the most difficult things—tiny corollas, wildflowers—in movements like an accomplished pianist. Her fingers seemed fairies, infinite in the actions of twisting, fitting, and pressure.“I could not tire of admiring her shape a flower. She displayed the genius of a painter in bold attempts; she copied faded flowers and yellowing leaves. ‘This art,’ she said, ‘is in its infancy. If the women of Paris had a little of the genius of the harem, they would lend a complete language of flowers to the wreaths they wear. To please my taste as an artist, I have made drooping flowers with leaves the hue of Florentine bronze, such as are found before or after winter. Would not such a crown on the head of a young woman whose life is a failure have a certain poetical fitness? How many things a woman might express by her head-dress! Are there not flowers for drunken Bacchantes, flowers for gloomy and stern bigots, pensive flowers for bored women? Botany can express every sensation and thought of the soul.’“She employed me to stamp out leaves, cut material, and prepare wires. My affected desire for occupation made me skilled. We talked as we worked. When idle I read new books to her, to keep up my part as a man weary of life, gloomy and soured. My person led to adorable banter about my resemblance—except for his club foot—to Lord Byron. It was tacitly acknowledged that her troubles far outweighed mine, though the causes I assigned for my misanthropy might have satisfied Young or Job.“I will say nothing of the shame that tormented me as I inflicted false wounds on my heart to excite this enchanting woman’s compassion. Her renunciation of love, her dread of what is called happiness, she proclaimed with vehemence and candor. These happy days proved to me that a woman’s friendship is far superior to her love.“I suffered the revelations of my sorrows to be dragged from me with as many grimaces as a young lady allows before the piano, so conscious of the annoyance to follow. The Countess found in me such an exact counterpart of her antipathy to love that I fancied she was content with the chance that had brought a sort of Man Friday to her desert island. Solitude weighed on her. At the same time there was nothing of the coquette in her; nothing remained of a woman; she did not feel she had a heart, except in the ideal world where she found refuge. I compared the two lives—his, all activity and emotion; hers, all inaction and quiescence. My misanthropy allowed cynical sallies against both sexes, hoping to bring Honorine to confidence; but she was not to be caught, and I began to understand that mulish obstinacy is common among women.“‘The Orientals are right,’ I said one evening, ‘when they shut you up and regard you merely as playthings. Europe has been punished for admitting you to society on an equal footing. Woman is the most dishonorable and cowardly being to be found. Nay, and that is where her charm lies. Once a woman has inspired a man’s passion, she is sacred to him forever; but to you women, the man you loved is as nothing; more, you feel the popular calumny that, when you have had your fill, nothing should survive but the remembrance of pleasure.’“‘God has, no doubt, reserved such perfect bliss for Paradise,’ she said. ‘But if your argument seems witty to you, it has the disadvantage of being false. What can those women be who give themselves up to a succession of loves?’ she asked, looking at me as the Virgin in Ingres’ picture looks at Louis XIII offering his kingdom.“‘You are an actress in good faith,’ said I. ‘Still, lovely as you are, you have loved; ergo, you forget.’“‘I!’ she exclaimed, evading my question, ‘I am not a woman. I am a nun, and seventy-two years old!’“‘Then, how can you so positively assert that you feel more keenly than I? Sorrow has but one form for women. The only misfortunes they regard are disappointments of the heart.’“She looked at me sweetly and persisted in her willfulness.“‘I am a nun,’ she said, ‘and you talk to me of the world where I shall never again set foot.’“‘Not even in thought?’ I said.“‘Is the world so much to be desired?’ she replied. ‘Oh! when my mind wanders, it goes higher. The angel of perfection, the beautiful angel Gabriel, often sings in my heart. If I were rich, I should work all the same, to keep me from soaring on the many-tinted wings of the angel and wandering in fancy. There are meditations that ruin us women! I owe much peace to my flowers, though sometimes they fail to occupy me. I feel some great event is impending; my life is about to change; I listen vaguely; I have no liking for my work, and after fatigues I find everyday life once more. Is this a warning from heaven? I ask myself…’“After three months of this struggle between two diplomats—costumed melancholy and a woman whose disgust for life made her invulnerable—I told the Count it was impossible to draw this tortoise from her shell; it must be broken. The evening before, Honorine had exclaimed: ‘Lucretia’s dagger wrote in letters of blood the watchword of woman’s charter: Liberty!’ From that moment, the Count left me free to act.“‘I have been paid a hundred francs for the flowers and caps I made this week!’ Honorine exclaimed gleefully one Saturday evening when I visited her in the ground-floor sitting-room, which the unavowed proprietor had had regilt.“It was ten o’clock. The twilight of July and a glorious moon lent us misty light. Gusts of mingled perfumes soothed the soul; the Countess jingled the five gold pieces given to her by a supposititious dealer in fashionable frippery, another of Octave’s accomplices.“‘I earn my living by amusing myself,’ she said; ‘I am free, when men, armed with their laws, have tried to make us slaves. I have transports of pride every Saturday! I like M. Gaudissart’s gold pieces as much as your double liked Mr. Murray’s.’“‘This is not becoming in a woman,’ said I.“‘Pooh! Am I a woman? I am a boy with a soft soul, that is all; a boy whom no woman can torture…’“‘Your life is the negation of your whole being,’ I replied. ‘What? You, on whom God lavished His choicest treasures of love and beauty, do you never wish—’“‘For what?’ said she, disturbed by a speech that contradicted my assumed role.“‘For a pretty little child, with curling hair, running among the flowers, calling you mother!’“A prolonged silence followed. Leaning on her sofa, the Countess did not faint, but froze under a nervous attack, feeling like the influence of an insidious poison. I called Madame Gobain, who led her mistress away, laid her on her bed, unlaced and undressed her, restoring not life, but consciousness of dreadful suffering. I walked the path behind the house, weeping and doubting my success. I wished to abandon the part of the bird-catcher I had assumed. Madame Gobain found me with my face wet with tears and hastily went up to say to the Countess: ‘What has happened, madame? Monsieur Maurice is crying like a child.’“Roused to action by the evil interpretation that might be put on our behavior, she summoned strength to put on a wrapper and come down to me.“‘You are not the cause of this attack,’ she said. ‘I am subject to these spasms, a sort of cramp of the heart…’“‘And will you not tell me of your troubles?’ I asked, wiping my tears. ‘Have you not just now told me that you have been a mother and so unhappy as to lose your child?’“‘Marie!’ she called as she rang the bell. Gobain came in.“‘Bring lights and some tea,’ said she, with calm decision.“When the housekeeper had lit the tapers and closed the shutters, the Countess showed a mute countenance; her indomitable pride and gravity had reasserted mastery. She said:“‘Do you know why I like Lord Byron so much? It is because he suffered as animals do. Of what use are complaints when they are not an elegy like Manfred’s, nor bitter mockery like Don Juan’s, nor a reverie like Childe Harold’s? Nothing shall be known of me. My heart is a poem that I lay before God.’“‘If I chose—’ said I.“‘If?’ she repeated.“‘I have no interest in anything,’ I replied, ‘so I cannot be inquisitive; but if I chose, I could know all your secrets by tomorrow.’“‘I defy you!’ she exclaimed, uneasy.“‘Seriously?’“‘Certainly,’ she said, tossing her head. ‘If such a crime is possible, I ought to know it.’“‘In the first place, madame,’ I went on, pointing to her hands, ‘were those pretty fingers made for toil? Then you call yourself Madame Gobain, you who, on receiving a letter, said to Marie: “Here, this is for you?” Marie is the real Madame Gobain; so you conceal your name behind that of your housekeeper. Fear nothing, madame, from me. You have in me the most devoted friend you will ever have: friend, do you understand me? I give this word its sacred and pathetic meaning, so profaned in France. Your friend, who will defend you against everything, only wishes your happiness. Who can tell whether the pain I have involuntarily caused you was not a voluntary act?’“‘Yes,’ she replied with threatening audacity, ‘I insist on it. Be curious, and tell me all that you can find out about me; but,’ and she held up her finger, ‘you must also tell me by what means you obtain your information. The preservation of the small happiness I enjoy here depends on the steps you take.’“‘That means you will fly—’“‘On wings!’ she cried, ‘to the New World—’“‘Where you will be at the mercy of the brutal passions you inspire,’ I interrupted. ‘Is it not the essence of genius and beauty to shine and attract men’s gaze? Paris is a desert with Bedouins; it is the only place where those who must work can hide. What have you to complain of? Who am I? An additional servant—M. Gobain, that is all. If you have to fight a duel, you may need a second.’“‘Never mind; find out who I am. I have already said that I insist. Now, I beg that you will,’ she said with the grace you ladies command.“‘Well, then, tomorrow, at the same hour, I will tell you what I may have discovered,’ I replied. ‘But do not therefore hate me! Will you behave like other women?’“‘What do other women do?’“‘They lay upon us immense sacrifices, and when we have made them, they reproach us for it later as if it were an injury.’“‘They are right if the thing required appears to be a sacrifice!’ she replied pointedly.“‘Instead of sacrifices, say efforts and—’“‘It would be an impertinence,’ said she.“‘Forgive me,’ said I. ‘I forget that woman and the Pope are infallible.’“‘Good heavens!’ she said after a pause, ‘only two words would be enough to destroy the peace so dearly bought, and which I enjoy like a fraud—’“She rose and paid no further heed to me.“‘Where can I go?’ she said. ‘What is to become of me? Must I leave this quiet retreat, which I arranged with such care to end my days in?’“‘To end your days!’ I exclaimed with alarm. ‘Has it never struck you that a time would come when you could no longer work, when competition will lower the price of flowers and articles of fashion—?’“‘I have already saved a thousand crowns,’ she said.“‘Heavens! what privations such a sum must represent!’ I exclaimed.“‘Leave me till tomorrow. This evening I am not myself; I must be alone. Must I not save my strength in case of disaster? If you should learn anything, others besides you would be informed, and then—Good-night,’ she added shortly, dismissing me with an imperious gesture.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“The battle is to-morrow, then,” I said with a smile, trying to seem indifferent. As I walked down the avenue the words rang in my ears: “The battle is to-morrow.”Octave shared Honorine’s agitation. The Count and I walked together until two in the morning, pacing by the trenches of the Bastille like generals preparing for a decisive engagement, each aware that victory depended on seizing a single moment. We lay awake in different moods—one hopeful, the other dreading reunion. Real dramas are not in events but in feeling, in that Spiritual World where Octave and Honorine existed as lofty beings.The next evening I arrived punctually at ten and entered a charming bedroom in white and blue—the sanctuary of this wounded dove. The Countess looked as if to speak but fell silent at my respectful manner.“Madame la Comtesse,” I said with a grave smile.She sank back, wrapped in grief—an attitude I thought a great painter might wish to render.“You are,” I continued, “the wife of the noblest and most respected of men, a man honored by the world yet nobler in his conduct toward you than public opinion knows. You and he are two lofty natures. Where do you suppose you are living?”“In my own house,” she answered, astonished.“In Count Octave’s,” I corrected. “You have been deceived. M. Lenormand, the usher, is only a front for your husband. The seclusion you enjoy, the money you earn, the protection around every detail of your life—all come from him. Your disappearance has been plausibly explained; he even hopes you escaped the wreck of the Cécile. He takes as many pains to hide you as you take to hide yourself. In short, he obeys you...”“That is enough,” she interrupted. “I want to know one thing more. From whom have you obtained these details?”“My uncle procured a place for a young man as secretary to the local police commissary. That youth told me everything,” I answered. “If you leave this house tonight your husband will know it; his care will follow you. How could a clever woman like you suppose shopkeepers really lose when they sell at such prices? Your shawl cost six thousand francs; old-clothes dealers supply your second-hand wares fresh from the best makers. The Count comes at night behind the fence to see the glimmer of your nightlight. You live here like Venus in Vulcan’s forge but you are alone by a sublime magnanimity that has endured seven years.”The Countess trembled, defiant. Her eyes, dry and fierce, eventually yielded to tears—tears of helplessness rather than tenderness. She had believed herself free; marriage weighed upon her like a prison.“I will go!” she cried through her tears. “He forces me to it; I will go where no one will come after me.”“What—kill yourself?” I asked. “You must have powerful reasons to refuse going back to Comte Octave.”“Certainly I have!” she said.“Then tell me; tell my uncle. You will find two devoted advisers. Though my uncle is a priest in the confessional, he is no pedant in a drawing-room. We will listen and try to find a solution. If you are the dupe of some misunderstanding we may clear it. Your soul is pure, and if you have done wrong, the fault is expiated. In me you have a sincere friend. If you wish to evade the Count’s tyranny I will find you the means; he shall never find you.”“Oh! there is always a convent!” she said.“Yes, but the Count, as a Minister of State, could secure your rejection from many convents. Yet I will save you from him—if you prove you cannot and ought not to return to him. Do not fear to fall from his power into mine,” I added, noting her suspicion. “You shall have peace, solitude, and independence. I would never see you without your consent.”“And how? By what means?” she asked.“That is my secret. Prove to me this is the only life you can lead, preferable to that of Comtesse Octave—rich, admired, in one of the finest houses in Paris, beloved by her husband, a happy mother—and I will decide in your favor.”“But will there never be a man who understands me?”“No. That is why I appeal to religion to decide between us. The Cure of the White Friars is a saint of seventy-five. My uncle is no Grand Inquisitor but Saint John, gentle as Fenelon.”“Nay, monsieur, the convent is my last hope. There is none but God who can understand me. No man, not even Saint Augustine, could enter the scruples of my conscience, which are to me like circles of Dante’s hell. Another man, however unworthy, has had all my love. For me there can never be two loves. It is complete when it comes; eighteen months were to me like eighteen years; I poured out my being. The cup of happiness is broken; nothing can refill it. I have no weapons left. I had but one heart. My husband had the girl; a worthless lover had the woman—there is nothing left! I will not be a prostitute. I could imagine surrendering to another man’s love, but to Octave’s?—No, never.”“Ah! you love him,” I said.“I esteem, respect, venerate him; he has never hurt me; he is kind, tender; but I can never love him. Let us speak no more. I will write my notions to you. I am suffocated. All I see now, the things I believed I earned by labor, remind me of what I wish to forget. I must flee as I fled from my home.”“Where will you go?” I asked. “Can a woman exist unprotected? At thirty, in all your beauty, rich in powers you don’t suspect, are you ready to live in the wilderness where I could hide you?—Be at ease. The Count, who for nine years has not allowed himself to be seen here, will never go there without your permission. You have his devotion of nine years as a guarantee for your tranquillity. Discuss the future in confidence with my uncle and me. He wields moral weight equal to a minister. A priest with white hair, who has borne the hearts of kings in his hands, will understand you; in presence of your flowers he will be as tender as they are.”I left the Countess at midnight; she seemed calm but depressed, harboring a secret purpose I could not divine. I found the Count a few paces away in the Rue Saint-Maur; drawn by irresistible attraction, he had left our agreed spot.“What a night my poor child will go through!” he exclaimed after I told him. “Suppose I were to go to her! What if she saw me suddenly?”“At this moment she might throw herself out of the window,” I answered. “She is one of those Lucretias who could not survive the violence, even if into Octave’s arms.”“You are young,” he said; “you do not know that in a soul tossed by such choices the will is like a lake lashed by a tempest; the wind changes and the waves drive to one shore then the other. Tonight, the chances are as great she might rush into my arms as that she would throw herself out the window.”“And you would accept the equal chances?” I asked.“Well, come,” he said, “I have a dose of opium at home Desplein prepared to send me to sleep without risk!”Next day at noon Gobain brought me a letter informing me the Countess had gone to bed at six, exhausted, and after taking a chemist’s draught had fallen asleep.“This is her letter, of which I kept a copy—for you, mademoiselle,” said the Consul, addressing Camille. “Literature cannot equal such language in real pathos; truth is its own terror. Here is the letter written by her anguish:“‘MONSIEUR MAURICE,—“I know all your uncle would say; he is as uninformed as my own conscience. Conscience is God’s interpreter to man. I know that if I do not reconcile with Octave I shall be damned; that is the sentence of religious law. Civil law condemns me to obey. If my husband does not reject me, the world will deem me pure whatever I have done. That is the sublime thing about marriage; society ratifies the husband’s forgiveness; but it forgets that forgiveness must be accepted. Legally, religiously, and socially I ought to go back. It is cruel to refuse him happiness, to deprive him of children, to blot his name from the Golden Book and the peers’ list. My sufferings, repugnance, feelings, all my egoism—I know I am selfish—ought to be sacrificed to family. I should be a mother; a child’s caresses would wipe many tears! I should be happy; I should be admired, wealthy, riding in a handsome carriage, the queen of innumerable parties. The world will receive me handsomely. God, the law, society are all in accord.“‘“What are you rebelling against?” they ask—from heaven, the pulpit, the judge, and even the throne. Your uncle would speak of celestial grace which will flood my heart when I do my duty.“‘Well, if there were no other difficulty, my reply is this: I will not live. I will become white and innocent again; I will lie in my shroud. This is not obstinacy; that obstinacy in women is the result of confidence, a vision of the future. Though my husband may forget, I shall not. Does forgetfulness depend on will? A widow remarries and loves again; but I cannot love Octave. Every time my eyes met his I should see my sin in them, even when they are full of love. His generosity would measure my crime. My eyes would read an invisible condemnation. Marriage could never give me the rapture of passion. I should kill my husband by coldness and unconscious comparisons which he would guess. I might die the victim of his impatience or some unjust suspicion. Oh, what torture on both sides! Octave would doubt me and I him. I should by not loving give him a rival unworthy of him, a man I despise but with whom I had raptures branded on me by fire—my shame, but I cannot forget.“‘Have I shown you enough of my heart? No one can convince me love can be renewed; I neither can nor will accept it from anyone. A young bride is like a plucked flower; a guilty wife is a flower trodden underfoot. You, who are a florist, know whether a broken stem can be set right. God alone could remake me! I am drinking the bitter cup of expiation; but expiation is not annihilation.“‘In my little house I eat my bread soaked in tears; no one sees me weep. If I go back to Octave I must give up my tears—they would offend him. Oh monsieur, how many virtues must a woman tread underfoot to restore herself to a betrayed husband? God alone knows the horrible refinements such hypocrisy demands. A woman can be brave in the presence of an ignorant husband, but common knowledge is degrading. Suppose I could exchange humiliation for ecstasy—would not Octave feel my consent as depravity? Marriage is built on esteem and mutual sacrifice; neither Octave nor I could esteem each other after reunion. He would have disgraced me by a love like an old man’s for a courtesan, and I should feel I were a chattel not a lady. These are the bitter fruits of such a sin. I have made a bed of burning coals.“‘Here, in suffering, I bless my sufferings. In my husband’s house, I should be full of terror, tasting joys I have no right to. Most of all, I must confess I shall always carry the memory of the child conceived in rapture and nursed for seven months; if other children draw from me, they would drink tears in the milk. I have a child’s memory that returns at death. So you see, not one situation in that beautiful life to which the world and my husband wish to recall me but hides a snare or a precipice. For five years I have wandered in a sandy desert of the future without finding a place to repent, because my soul is possessed by true repentance.“‘Religion answers: this suffering is your punishment and God will give you strength. That may comfort some pious and energetic souls; I lack that energy. I have chosen between this hell, where God does not forbid my blessing Him, and the hell awaiting me under Count Octave’s roof.“‘One word more. If I were a girl now with my experience, he would be the man I should choose; that is the reason I refuse. I could not bear to blush before him. What! I should kneel while he stands; if positions reversed I should scorn him. I will not be better treated because of my sin. The angel who might venture liberties permissible when both are blameless is not on earth; he dwells in heaven. Octave is full of delicacy, but even in him there is no guarantee for the new life I should lead with him.“‘Come then, and tell me where I may find the solitude, peace, and silence you promised me.’”After copying the letter I went to the Rue Payenne. Anxiety had conquered the opium’s power. Octave paced his garden like a madman.“Answer that!” I said, giving him the Countess’s letter. “Try to reassure modesty with experience. It is harder than seducing ignorance.”“She is mine!” cried the Count, joy lighting his face as he read.He waved me away; I understood that extreme happiness and extreme pain obey the same laws. I went on to receive Madame de Courteville and Amelie, who were dining with the Count. Seeing Mademoiselle de Courteville beside the Count, I felt that love has three aspects and women who inspire perfect love are rare. Involuntarily comparing Amelie with Honorine, the erring wife seemed more attractive: fidelity had not been a duty to her but inevitable, while Amelie would promise solemnly without grasping their meaning. The crushed, the dead woman, the sinner to be reinstated, appeared sublime; she demanded a man’s special generosity, made life rich with conflict; whereas Amelie, chaste and confiding, would settle into peaceful motherhood where the commonplace is its poetry.Of the plains of Champagne and the storm-beaten Alps, what young man would choose the monotonous level? Such comparisons are fatal and wrong at the threshold of the mairie. Life teaches that marriage excludes passion, that family cannot be founded on tempests of love. Having dreamed of impossible love and tasted its torment, I saw modest reality. Pity me; at twenty-five I did not trust myself; yet I took a manful resolution.I returned to the Count to announce his relations’ arrival and saw him grow young in the reflected light of hope.“What ails you, Maurice?” he asked, struck by my changed look.“Monsieur le Comte—”“No longer Octave? You, to whom I shall owe my life, my happiness—”“My dear Octave, if you succeed in bringing the Countess back, I have studied her well,” I said—(he looked at me as Othello might have at Iago)—“she must never see me again; she must never know that Maurice was your secretary. Never mention my name to her, or all will be undone... You got me an appointment as Maître des Requêtes—well, get me instead some diplomatic post abroad, a consulship, and do not think of my marrying Amelie—Oh! do not be uneasy,” I added as he straightened, “I will play my part to the end.”“Poor boy!” he said, taking my hand and pressing it while holding back tears.“You gave me the gloves,” I laughed, “but I have not put them on; that is all.”We agreed on that evening’s part at Honorine’s, and I returned to her house. It was August; a hot, stormy day had left the air heavy with scent. The Countess sat on a wooden bench beneath an arbor in a loose white muslin dress with blue bows, hair in loose waves, feet on a small stool. She did not rise but gestured me to sit.“Now, is not life deadlocked for me?” she asked.“Life as you have made it,” I said. “But not the life I propose; if you choose you may be very happy...”“How?” she asked, her whole person a question.“Your letter is in the Count’s hands.”She started like a frightened deer, paced away, then came in to the drawing-room where I joined her after letting the shock settle.“You—a friend?
