Say rather a traitor! A spy sent by my husband.”Women’s instinct is as sharp as men’s perspicacity.“You wanted an answer to your letter, did you not? There was but one man in the world who could write it. You must read his reply; if after that you still find life a deadlock, the spy will prove a friend; I will place you in a convent impervious to the Count’s power. But first consider the other side. There is a law, divine and human, which even hatred obeys: do not condemn without hearing the defense. Till now you have judged like a child with ears stopped. The devotion of seven years has claims. You must read the answer your husband will send. I forwarded your letter through my uncle and asked him what he would reply if his wife wrote thus. You are not compromised. He will bring the Count’s answer. In the presence of that saint and me, out of respect for your dignity, you must read it, or you will be no better than a wilful child. Make this sacrifice to the world, to the law, and to God.”Seeing no attack on her resolve, she consented. Months of effort had led to this point. The Count had pinned all hopes on this instant, and he had reached it.In all my life I remember nothing more formidable than my uncle’s entrance into that little Pompadour drawing-room at ten. His fine head, silver hair set against black, and divinely calm face had a magical effect on the Countess; she seemed soothed by that virtue.“Monsieur the Cure of the White Friars,” said old Gobain.“Are you come, uncle, with a message of happiness and peace?” I asked.“Happiness and peace are always found in obedience to the Church,” replied my uncle, handing the Countess the following letter:“‘MY DEAR HONORINE,—“If you had but trusted me and read the letter I wrote five years ago, you would have spared five years of useless privations. In it I proposed an arrangement that would have relieved your fears and made domestic life possible. I have much to reproach myself with; in seven years of sorrow I have discovered my errors. I misunderstood marriage. I failed to scent the danger; an angel was in the house and the Lord punished me for my audacity.“You cannot lash yourself without striking me. Have mercy on me, my dear Honorine. I have so appreciated your susceptibilities that I would not bring you back to the Rue Payenne, where I could not bear to see you. I am decorating another house in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré, to which I would, in hope, conduct not a wife secured by ignorance and law, but a sister who will allow me to press on her brow such a kiss as a father. Will you bereave me of the right I have won from your despair—that of watching over you? Women have a mother’s heart; you never knew any mother but mine, who would have brought you back. But have you never guessed I had for you a mother’s heart, both of my mother and of your own? My affection is neither mean nor grasping; it will not let annoyances wrinkle your brow. What can you think of the companion of your childhood if you believe him capable of accepting trembling kisses and living between delight and anxiety? Do not fear passion; I would not want you back until sure of my strength to leave you free.“Your solitary pride exaggerates difficulties. You may, if you will, live under the life of a brother or father without suffering or joy; you will find neither mockery nor indifference. The warmth of your life will be equable and genial, without tempests. If later you desire pleasures or amusements, expand their circle at will. The tenderness of a mother knows neither contempt nor pity. In me, admiration shall hide every sentiment that might offend you.“Thus, living side by side, we may be magnanimous. In you, the kindness of a sister and the thoughtfulness of a friend will satisfy him who wishes to be your companion; and you may measure his tenderness by the care with which he will conceal it. Neither of us will be jealous of the past; we will look only forward.“You will be at home in the new house as in the Rue Saint-Maur: unapproachable, alone, occupied as you please, living by your own law; but with legitimate protection and the fortune for good works. When you long for absolution ask for it; it will not be forced by the Church or Law; it will wait on your pride. My wife might fear what you do; but not my friend and sister. To see you happy is enough for me; I have proved this for seven years. The guarantee is in the flowers you made, carefully preserved, watered by my tears. If this compact does not suit you, I begged the saintly man who takes charge of this letter not to speak in my behalf. I will not owe your return to ecclesiastical terrors or legal bidding. I will not take the quiet happiness I ask from anyone but you. If you persist in condemning me to lonely life, my will yields to yours. You shall be troubled no more than you have been. I will rid you of the meddling fellow who has annoyed you...’”“‘Monsieur,’ said Honorine, folding the letter and placing it at her bosom, looking at my uncle, ‘thank you very much. I will avail myself of Monsieur le Comte’s permission to remain here----’“‘Ah!’ I exclaimed.My exclamation made my uncle look at me uneasily and drew from the Countess a mischievous glance revealing her motive.Honorine had wanted to test whether I was an actor or a spy; I had the melancholy satisfaction of deceiving her—the cry I could not control came from the heart, which women read so well.“‘Ah, Maurice,’ she said, ‘you know how to love.’”The light in my eyes replied. The Count found me useful to the last.Honorine took out the letter again. My uncle signed to me, and I rose.“Let us leave the Countess,” he said.“You are going already, Maurice?” she asked without looking at me.She rose, still reading, followed us to the door. On the threshold she took my hand, pressed it affectionately, and said, “We shall meet again...”“No,” I replied, wringing her hand so that she cried out. “You love your husband. I leave tomorrow.”I rushed away, leaving my uncle, who explained my agitation to her as a touch of madness, a theory he really believed.Six days later I set out with an appointment as vice-consul in Spain, in a large commercial town where I could rise. After I had established myself there I received this letter from the Count:“‘MY DEAR MAURICE,—“If I were happy I should not write, but I have entered a new life of suffering. I have grown young again in desire, with a diplomatist’s prudence, moderating passion. When you left, I had not yet been admitted to the pavilion in the Rue Saint-Maur, though a letter promised permission—the mild melancholy of a woman who feared agitation. After waiting more than a month, I called and asked Gobain if I could be received. I sat near the lodge, head buried in my hands, for almost an hour.“‘“Madame had to dress,” said Gobain to hide her hesitation, flattering my hopes.“‘At last we were overcome by a trembling like a speaker’s nerves, speaking sacred phrases. “You see, Honorine,” I said, “the ice is broken; I am so tremulous with happiness that you must forgive incoherency.” “There is no crime in being in love with your wife,” she said with a forced smile.“‘“Do me the favor,” I said, “no longer work as you do. I have heard you have been living on savings; you have sixty thousand francs a year of your own; if you cannot give back your heart, at least do not abandon your fortune to me.”“‘“I have long known your kindness,” she answered.“‘“Though you prefer to remain here,” I said, “still do not toil.”“‘I gave her three certificates worth twelve thousand francs each; she took them, opened them languidly, and giving me only a look as reward understood I offered not money but freedom.“‘“I am conquered,” she said, holding out her hand, which I kissed. “Come and see me as often as you like.”’“He had made himself do violence in receiving me. Next day she put on affected spirits; two months of habit brought out her true character. It was a delicious springtime of love that gave me ineffable bliss; she was no longer afraid; she studied me. When I proposed she go to England to resume her rank and life in our new residence she was seized with alarm.“‘“Why not live always as we are?” she said.“‘I submitted. I asked myself if she were making an experiment. On my way from my house to the Rue Payenne I said, as a young man, “This evening she will yield.” All my force was blown away by a smile and a command from those proud calm eyes. I felt how necessary her consent was and how impossible to wring it out. Could she guess the storms that distracted me?“‘At last I painted my case in a letter and begged her answer. She made none and seemed sad; I pretended not to have written. She read my heart and forgave me. Three days ago she received me in her blue-and-white room, bright with flowers. She was in a dress that made her bewitching; her hair framed her face. She was like a bride of long past days. My joy chilled; her face was terribly grave, fires beneath ice.“‘“Octave,” she said, “I will return as your wife when you wish. But this submission has dangers. I can be resigned—”“‘I made a movement.“‘“Yes,” she went on, “I understand: resignation offends you; you want what I cannot give—Love. Religion and pity led me to renounce solitude; you are here!” There was a pause.“‘“At first you asked no more. Now you demand your wife. Well, here I give you Honorine as she is without deceiving you as to what she will be. What shall I be? A mother? I hope it eagerly. Try to change me; you have my consent; but if I should die do not curse my memory or call obstinacy what I should call the worship of the Ideal.”“‘She sat calm as you used to admire and watched me turn pale. Her words took effect; she took my hands and said:“‘“Octave, I do love you, but not as you wish. I love your soul... Yet I love you enough to die in your service without regret. It will be my expiation.”“‘She knelt and added sadly: “And perhaps I shall not die!”“‘For two months I have struggled with myself. What shall I do? My heart is too full; I seek a friend and cry, “What shall I do?”’”I did not answer this letter. Two months later the newspapers announced the Comtesse Octave returned on an English vessel, restored to her family after adventures contrived with plausible invention.When I moved to Genoa I received formal notice of the birth of a son to the Count and Countess. I held that letter two hours on the terrace bench. Two months later, urged by Octave, by M. de Grandville and M. de Serizy, and broken by my uncle’s death, I agreed to marry.Six months after the Revolution of July I received a letter concluding the story:“‘MONSIEUR MAURICE,—I am dying though I am a mother—perhaps because I am a mother. I have played my part as a wife well; I have deceived my husband. I have had happiness as genuine as the tears of actresses on stage. I am dying for society, for family, for marriage, as early Christians died for God! I do not know of what I am dying; I try to find out; I am not perverse; I was bent on explaining my malady to you—who brought the heavenly physician, your uncle, at whose word I surrendered. He was my director; I nursed him in his last illness; he showed me the way to heaven, bidding me persevere in duty.“‘I have done my duty. I do not blame those who forget; I admire them as strong natures; but I have the malady of memory! I have not been able twice to feel that love which identifies a woman with a man. To the last moment, as you know, I cried in the confessional and to my husband, “Have mercy!” But there was no mercy. I am dying with stupendous courage. No courtesan was ever gayer than I. My poor Octave is happy; I let his love feed on illusions. I throw all my powers into this masquerade; the actress is applauded and smothered in flowers; but the invisible rival—my dead child—comes every day to seek its prey. I am rent and I smile. I smile on two children, but the elder, the dead one, will triumph. I told you so before. The dead child calls me.“‘The intimacy of marriage without love degrades my soul every hour. I can never weep or dream but when alone. Social exigencies and care of my child leave me no moment to renew myself as I could in solitude. The constant need for watchfulness startles my heart. I have not the sharp vigilance of others; it is not the lip of one I love that drinks my tears; my burning eyes are cooled with water and not tender lips. My soul acts a part and perhaps that is why I am dying. The doctors, who discovered my secret, allow me to die of some plausible complaint. Octave believes I adore him, so I fear lest he should follow me. I now beg you to be my little Count’s guardian. You will find a codicil with my wish; do not produce it except if needed. Do not mourn for me; I have been dead this many a day if Saint Bernard was right that where there is no love there is no life.’”“And the Countess died,” said the Consul, putting away the letters and locking the pocket-book.“Is the Count still living?” asked the Ambassador; since July he had vanished from politics.“Do you remember, Monsieur de Lora,” asked the Consul-General, “seeing me go to the steamboat with—”“A white-haired man? an old man?” said the painter.“An old man of forty-five, going in search of health and amusement in Southern Italy. That man was my poor friend and patron, passing through Genoa to take leave of me and place his will in my hands. He appointed me his son’s guardian. I had no need to tell him of Honorine’s wishes.”“Does he suspect himself of murder?” asked Mademoiselle des Touches to the Baron de l’Hostal.“He suspects the truth,” replied the Consul, “and that is what is killing him. I stayed on board the steam packet till it departed; a small boat brought me back. We sat for some time taking leave—for ever, I fear. God only knows how much we love the confidant of our love when she who inspired it is no more.“‘That man,’ said Octave, ‘holds a charm and wears an aureole.’ The Count went to the prow and looked down at the Mediterranean. It was fine; moved, he spoke his last words: ‘Ought we not, in the interests of human nature, to inquire what irresistible power leads us to sacrifice an exquisite creature to the most fugitive of pleasures, and in spite of reason? In my conscience I heard cries. Honorine was not alone in her anguish. And yet I would have it!... I am consumed by remorse. In the Rue Payenne I was dying of joys not had; now I shall die in Italy of joys I have had... Wherein lay the discord between two equally noble natures, I dare assert?’”A profound silence reigned on the terrace.Then the Consul, turning to the two women, asked, “Was she virtuous?”Mademoiselle des Touches rose, took his arm, and said, “Are not men wrong too when they take a young girl to wife while cherishing at heart some angelic image, comparing us to unknown rivals and finding us wanting?”“Mademoiselle, you would be right if marriage were based on passion; and that was the mistake of those two, who will soon be no more. Marriage with mutual heart-deep love would be Paradise.”Mademoiselle des Touches turned and Claude Vignon whispered: “A bit of a coxcomb is M. de l’Hostal.”“No,” she replied; “he has not yet guessed that Honorine would have loved him.—Oh!” she cried, seeing the Consul’s wife approaching, “his wife was listening! Unhappy man!”At eleven by all clocks the guests went home along the seashore.“Still, that is not life,” said Mademoiselle des Touches. “That woman was rare, perhaps the most extraordinary exception in intellect—a pearl! Life is various, pain and pleasure alternating. Dante’s Paradise is found only in the soul; to ask it of life is a luxury against which nature protests. For such souls the six feet of a cell and the kneeling chair suffice.”“You are right,” said Leon de Lora; “but good-for-nothing as I am, I cannot help admiring a woman who could live by a studio, under a painter’s roof, never coming down, never seeing the world.”“Such a thing has been known—for a few months,” said Claude Vignon with irony.“Comtesse Honorine is not unique,” replied the Ambassador. “A man, a politician, a bitter writer, was the object of such a passion; the pistol that killed him hit him and made the woman live like a nun.”“Then there are yet some great souls in this age!” said Camille Maupin, and she stood for some minutes pensively leaning on the quay’s balustrade.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!” cried the lawyer’s clerk Simonnin, leaning from a window and munching dry bread as he playfully shot a crumb at a stranger crossing the courtyard of Maître Derville’s office in the Rue Vivienne. “Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks on people, or I will turn you out of doors. However poor a client may be, he is still a man!” admonished the head clerk, who was busy with a bill of costs and tried to check the boy’s mischief. Simonnin, a typical messenger of thirteen or fourteen, was known for impudence and mischief; he and the other clerks, including the mischievous Godeschal, gossiped and joked about the shabby figure they called “old Box-coat,” even inventing tricks and false rules — Godeschal claiming M. Derville received clients only at odd hours — to make sport of him. The office buzzed with laughter and camaraderie: cluttered, dimly lit, the smell of food mingling with the musty odor of old papers, a disorder the clerks accepted as part of their chaotic, monotonous work. When the old man entered, looking weary, out of place and civil, he politely asked if M. Derville was at home; preoccupied, the clerks dismissed him. Boucard, the head clerk, brusquely told him to return at one in the morning, leaving the visitor bewildered. The clerks resumed their mockery, trading extravagant guesses about whether the man had once been a nobleman or a porter to explain his shabby coat; when the old man insisted he was Colonel Chabert, supposed to have died at Eylau, they erupted in laughter and treated the claim as a joke. Amid the jeering they argued over which theatre to attend that evening, their playful disputes underscoring a lively, youthful spirit. Finally Boucard quelled the chatter and refocused them on work, stressing efficiency; the scene captured their exuberant, irreverent, self-contained camaraderie in stark contrast to the old man’s somber, unsettling presence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At about one in the morning Colonel Chabert knocked at the door of Maître Derville, a prominent Paris attorney. The porter said Derville had not returned, but Chabert insisted and was shown upstairs, where the head clerk, arranging papers, explained that Derville preferred to work at night and that the Colonel was only the third person to seek him so late.When Derville arrived he was struck by the Colonel's strange appearance: thin, pale, his head scarred as if by a traumatic past. Chabert introduced himself as the Colonel presumed killed at Eylau, prompting Derville and the clerk to suspect madness, but Chabert was insistent and began to relate his story.Accustomed to varied clients, Derville urged brevity. The Colonel recounted his service at Eylau, where he was gravely injured and buried among the dead. Miraculously he survived because his horse's body had shielded him; he described awakening in a grave, struggling to escape, and finally being rescued by a woman who took him to a hospital. After months of recovery, Chabert discovered he had lost everything, including proof of his identity. With no documents, his attempts to reclaim his life met disbelief and ridicule. He wandered as a beggar, was often treated as a madman, and was confined in a mental institution for two years. Released but haunted by the past, he had lost his wife, the Countess Ferraud, who had remarried and borne children.Derville, sensing the gravity of the claims, expressed skepticism but also a willingness to help. Chabert said he sought to reclaim his rights and fortune, which he believed had been unjustly taken, lamenting that no lawyer had taken him seriously enough to help prove his identity and recover his estate. Moved by Chabert's plight, Derville offered to obtain necessary documents from Germany and gave him a small sum to subsist on. Overwhelmed with gratitude, Chabert felt hope for the first time in years. He voiced a longing for vengeance against his wife, revealing his deep emotional turmoil.Derville acknowledged the case's complexity and the likely legal obstacles. Chabert, resolute, declared that if he failed he would rather die than continue living in obscurity. Derville promised to treat the matter seriously and provided a letter to a notary for further assistance.As Chabert left, he experienced a renewed sense of purpose and determination. He had received immediate aid and the promise of legal support in his quest to reclaim identity and rights. The encounter marked a turning point: after years of despair, the Colonel departed with a glimmer of hope for the future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About three months after the interview, in Derville’s room, the notary Crottat, charged with advancing half-pay to Derville’s eccentric client, raised a serious matter and began by asking him to refund the six hundred francs the old soldier had received.“Are you amusing yourself with pensioning the old army?” Crottat laughed; he was a young man who had recently taken over the office after his chief fled in bankruptcy.“Thank you for reminding me of that affair,” Derville replied. “My philanthropy will not extend beyond twenty-five louis; I fear I have already been duped by my patriotism.”As he spoke, Derville noticed papers on his desk, including a letter with stamps from various post offices.“Ah ha! Here is the last act of the comedy; now we shall see if I have been taken in!” he exclaimed, opening the letter, but it was written in German.“Boucard, have this translated immediately,” Derville ordered his head clerk.The notary in Berlin informed Derville that the requested documents would arrive shortly and were regular and legally stamped. Most witnesses to the recorded facts were still in Eylau, Prussia, and the woman who saved Comte Chabert was alive in Heilsberg.“This looks like business,” Derville said after Boucard translated the letter. He then asked Crottat for information from his office about Chabert’s estate, recalling the rogue Roguin.“Let’s call him the unfortunate Roguin,” Crottat interjected with a laugh.“Wasn’t it that ill-used man who absconded with eight hundred thousand francs of his clients’ money?” Derville continued. “I believe he handled Chabert’s estate.”“Yes,” Crottat confirmed. “I copied the papers when I was third clerk. Rose Chapotel, wife of Hyacinthe, called Chabert, Count of the Empire, married without settlement, so they held all property in common. The personalty was about six hundred thousand francs. Before his marriage, Colonel Chabert made a will leaving one-quarter to the hospitals and the State to take the other quarter. The will was contested, leading to a forced sale, and the widow received the portion bequeathed to the treasury.”“So Comte Chabert’s personal fortune was no more than three hundred thousand francs?” Derville asked.“Exactly!” Crottat replied. “You lawyers can be quite clear-headed despite accusations of false practices.”Colonel Chabert was lodging in the Faubourg Saint-Marceau with an old quartermaster named Vergniaud. Derville had to walk because his coachman refused to navigate the unpaved street. He found shabby stone gate-posts with a sign, “Vergniaud, dairyman.” The yard was dismal; a dilapidated house seemed on the point of collapse.The house was ill-built, with mismatched windows and a trodden-earth floor. Derville noted the squalor but also the Colonel’s calm demeanor when he emerged smoking a pipe, still displaying military coolness.“Why did you not write to me?” Chabert asked, directing Derville to a better path to avoid the manure.Derville entered the Colonel’s room, which contained only a chair and a bed of straw. The walls were damp; the atmosphere was grim, yet Chabert seemed content. Derville expressed concern over his living conditions.“Here, we are not distinguished for luxury,” Chabert replied. “But I have done no one wrong, and I sleep in peace.”Derville hesitated about the money he had advanced, instead asking why Chabert had not moved to Paris, where he could live better.“The good folks here have taken me in and fed me for a year,” Chabert explained. “I cannot leave them now that I have a little money.”Derville told him the papers from Heilsberg were expected and that the woman who saved him was alive.“Curse the money! To think I haven’t got any!” Chabert exclaimed, throwing his pipe down in frustration.Derville explained the legal complexities: proving Chabert’s identity would require lengthy, costly proceedings. He warned that the case could be prolonged by the bigamy issue with Comtesse Ferraud, who had remarried.“Things are not done so in the legal world,” Derville cautioned. “You will face many challenges, and your opponents will demand inquiries that could take years.”Chabert, overwhelmed by the prospect of a drawn-out lawsuit, expressed despair. Derville reassured him that the outcome could be favorable, suggesting a friendly suit to annul both the death certificate and the subsequent marriage.“I will send you a power of attorney to sign,” Derville concluded, offering financial help.Chabert, feeling hopeless, agreed to trust Derville completely. As Derville left, he encountered Louis Vergniaud, who expressed concern for Chabert’s well-being and requested a loan to improve the Colonel’s living conditions.Derville agreed to provide the funds but insisted that Chabert should be the one to help his friends. He then planned to visit Comtesse Ferraud, considering her position and the potential leverage he could gain.Monsieur le Comte Ferraud, a former Councillor’s son, had returned to France after the Revolution and married Madame Chabert, who had turned her share of her husband’s fortune into a substantial income. Their marriage was socially accepted; Ferraud was politically ambitious but had begun to regret his union with Madame Ferraud.Derville, reflecting on the situation, recognized the Countess’s precarious position. He arrived at the Hotel Ferraud and found her in luxurious surroundings, a stark contrast to Chabert’s squalor.“Good-morning, Monsieur Derville,” she greeted him, but he quickly shifted to serious matters.“I have come to speak with you on a very serious matter,” he stated, revealing that Colonel Chabert was alive.The Countess laughed dismissively, but Derville’s serious demeanor silenced her. He warned her of the danger she faced if Chabert’s identity were proven, hinting at the potential legal ramifications.She insisted her second marriage was secure because of her children, but Derville countered that the law might not favor her if Chabert proved his identity. He suggested Ferraud might not support her if he learned of the risk to his name and fortune.Derville’s words struck a nerve; the Countess’s manner shifted as she considered the implications. He proposed a compromise, sensing her vulnerability.“Does he still love me?” she asked, contemplating how to use Chabert’s affection to her advantage.Derville left her with the suggestion to consider a compromise, confident he had gained the upper hand in the situation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A week after Derville's visits, Colonel Chabert and his wife, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet at their lawyer's office. Thanks to Derville's support, Chabert arrived in a dignified blue suit with the Legion of Honor ribbon, regaining a soldierly bearing and transforming from a shabby figure into one reflecting his past nobility.When Chabert entered Derville's office, Madame Ferraud arrived shortly after, elegantly dressed to appear youthful. The office was unchanged; clerks made jesting remarks about his transformation. Aware of the couple's tension, Derville separated them to discuss terms and presented a memorandum: Chabert would formally acknowledge his identity in exchange for the financial support his wife would provide.The Countess was taken aback by the terms, especially the financial obligations, and resisted letting the matter go to court. When Chabert returned he confronted her about their past, exposing the pain of their separation. She feigned surprise and attempted to leave, but Derville urged her to stay. Chabert, bitter and wounded, pressed for justice while Derville warned against rash action.After a heated exchange, the Countess led Chabert to her carriage and they went to her country house in Groslay. During the ride her tone shifted, revealing turmoil: she confessed love for another man, Comte Ferraud, and wanted to preserve her dignity without public scandal. Chabert, though hurt, offered a measure of understanding, recognizing the complexities and social consequences she feared.At the country house she treated him kindly, trying to efface past pain. They reminisced, and Chabert found temporary solace in her presence even as resentment and a sense of betrayal lingered. The Countess, meanwhile, worked discreetly to secure her position and extinguish Chabert's claim.As days passed her unease grew; she wrote to Delbecq to manipulate the situation further. When Chabert discovered this, he felt deeply betrayed and resolved to confront her. Their relationship became a battleground of emotion, with Chabert torn between lingering affection and mounting resentment.Ultimately he decided to sacrifice his identity for the sake of the Countess and their children, accepting anonymity to avoid scandal, but her continued manipulations revealed the depth of her betrayal. He confronted her, voiced his disdain, and chose to live without reclaiming his former name. The Countess, recognizing the consequences, understood she could live peacefully on his silence.Chabert then vanished from society. Months later Derville learned of his fate when he encountered him in a courtroom, now reduced to a vagabond. Despite his circumstances, Chabert retained a measure of dignity and reflected on the indifference of the world. Derville, moved by his plight, sought to help, but Chabert's contempt for societal norms led him to reject material concerns.In the end Chabert's story is one of loss and resilience, illustrating the complexities of love, betrayal, and the struggle for identity in a world that often overlooks the individual.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In June 1840, the attorney Godeschal accompanied Derville to Ris. Near Bicêtre they noticed an old pauper under an elm drying his handkerchief in the sun. In a shabby wrapper-coat with a striking look, the man prompted Godeschal’s comparison to grotesque German carvings; Derville recognized him as Comte Chabert, husband of the pious Comtesse Ferraud, who had likely had him sent to the workhouse because he reminded her of their past.Intrigued, Godeschal urged Derville to tell Chabert’s story. Two days later they returned and found him sitting on a tree trunk, drawing in the sand. When addressed he insisted he was no longer Chabert but “Hyacinthe,” merely a number in the institution, and envied a condemned man for the freedom of not being married.Godeschal offered him money for snuff; Chabert accepted eagerly, thanking them as “brave troopers,” playfully brandishing his stick and crying “Vive Napoleon!” Another pauper observed that Chabert, though sometimes childish, could be philosophical and imaginative, recalling how he had once impressed a Prussian officer with his military history.Derville reflected on Chabert’s tragic trajectory: from foundling to Napoleonic soldier to the workhouse. He lamented the disillusionment suffered by the priest, the doctor, and especially the lawyer, who sees human nature at its worst—betrayal, greed, and moral decay. Derville cited cases of familial ruin: fathers abandoned by daughters and mothers who exploit their children for gain.Godeschal confessed he had witnessed similar horrors in practice and voiced a wish to escape Paris’s corruption for a quieter rural life with his wife.The narrative closes with an addendum listing characters from Balzac’s “Human Comedy,” noting their appearances in other stories and referencing figures such as Napoleon Bonaparte and Comtesse Ferraud.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Bianchon, a physician known for his theoretical physiology, trained in surgery under the celebrated French surgeon Desplein. Desplein’s genius lay in diagnostic and operative skill, but it remained personal rather than a broader legacy shaping future generations. His atheism—reinforced by dissecting bodies without perceiving any soul—formed a core of his outlook and informed his skepticism.Despite his brilliance, Desplein's life was full of contradictions critics used to diminish him. His eccentricities—alternating lavish and indifferent dress—and a temperament swinging from kindness to harshness produced mixed judgments. He understood human nature but often displayed contempt after observing its follies.Among his students, Horace Bianchon endured poverty while studying medicine. Noted for integrity, loyalty, and cheerfulness despite financial hardship, Bianchon was mentored by Desplein, who introduced him to medical practice and Parisian social dynamics. His compassion, often private, contrasted with public harshness, surprising those who knew him superficially.Once Bianchon told him of a destitute water-carrier with a debilitating illness. Moved, Desplein rushed to help, revealing compassion beneath his stern exterior. This sympathy for the less fortunate, especially fellow Auvergnats, recurred.Bianchon later saw Desplein enter a church—odd for an outspoken atheist. Curious, he followed and observed him at mass—at odds with his declared disbelief. At a later dinner Desplein dismissed the mass as a farce, but Bianchon was puzzled.Months later another physician questioned Desplein; he gave a matter-of-fact explanation, but Bianchon resolved to investigate. He returned a year later and again found Desplein at mass, confirming a pattern. The sacristan disclosed Desplein had attended that mass for twenty years and had founded it.This deepened the mystery, revealing a complexity that resisted labels. Bianchon confronted a man who professed atheism yet engaged in ritual—an apparent contradiction highlighting the complex relation between belief, practice, and behavior. The episodes suggested Desplein's public declarations and private practices sprang from a multifaceted character shaped by skill, skepticism, compassion, and paradox.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Some time passed before Doctor Bianchon, despite their friendship, found an opportunity to discuss a significant incident in Desplein's life. Their meetings, professional or social, rarely allowed private conversation. Finally, seven years later, after the Revolution of 1830, Bianchon saw Desplein entering Saint-Sulpice and knelt beside him without surprise. They attended mass."Can you explain your sudden piety?" Bianchon asked as they left. "You, who do not believe in God, attending mass? You owe me an explanation.""I am like many devout people—outwardly religious but inwardly skeptical," Desplein replied, then launched into a critique of certain political figures."That doesn't answer my question," Bianchon insisted. "Why did you found this mass?""Well, my dear boy," Desplein said, "I am nearing the end of my life; I can tell you its beginnings."They stood in the Rue des Quatre-Vents, a grim Paris street. Desplein pointed to a building where he had lived for two years. "I lived up there," he said, recalling the hardships of his early life. He had known hunger, poverty, and isolation—no money for books or medical training, an irritable temperament that kept him from friends, and a constant struggle to rise from the depths of destitution. He described meager meals and tireless work without sympathy or support.He expressed frustration at those born into wealth who could not grasp his struggles. "They ask why I got into debt, as if it were a choice. They have no idea what it means to be truly destitute."Early experiences shaped his view of humanity. "If I seem hard or bitter, it's because I've witnessed the selfishness and envy around me. In Paris, mediocrity often seeks to undermine those who strive for excellence."He recalled a pivotal moment when, facing utter poverty and contemplating enlisting, he had hoped for a box of linen from a relative. When it arrived, the porter demanded too much to release it. Despairing, he met his neighbor, Bourgeat, a water-carrier who offered help.Bourgeat, despite his own hardships, paid Desplein's debts and helped him find new lodgings. "He devoted his savings to my future," Desplein said, visibly moved. "He understood my mission and supported me selflessly."He described Bourgeat as a nurturing figure who cared for him like a mother. "He took pride in my success and never sought recognition for his sacrifices. When I became a house surgeon, I suffered leaving him, but he was proud of me."Desplein later repaid Bourgeat by buying him a horse and barrel, fulfilling a long-held dream. "He was furious I had spent my money on him, yet he was delighted. He never claimed credit for my success, though I owed him everything."When Bourgeat fell ill, Desplein devoted himself to his care, but Bourgeat died in his arms. "He left me everything he had. He had a strong faith, and I honored his wishes by having masses said for him after his death."Desplein concluded, "I can only offer this as a tribute to Bourgeat's faith. I hope that if there is a paradise, he finds peace there. I would give anything for the faith he had."Bianchon, who had witnessed Desplein's last days, could not definitively say whether the great surgeon died an atheist. He pondered whether Bourgeat might have opened the gates of Heaven for Desplein, just as he had opened the doors to the earthly temple they had shared.PARIS, January 1836.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:Bianchon, HoraceFather GoriotCesar BirotteauThe Commission in LunacyLost IllusionsA Distinguished Provincial at ParisA Bachelor's EstablishmentThe Secrets of a PrincessThe Government ClerksPierretteA Study of WomanScenes from a Courtesan's LifeHonorineThe Seamy Side of HistoryThe Magic SkinA Second HomeA Prince of BohemiaLetters of Two BridesThe Muse of the DepartmentThe Imaginary MistressThe Middle ClassesCousin BettyThe Country ParsonIn addition, M. Their talk turned to the Marquise d’Espard. Bianchon distrusted her character; Rastignac, captivated by her rank and beauty, believed she could raise his social standing and help pay his debts.Bianchon warned Rastignac that pursuing the Marquise was a mistake, likening her to a “one-eyed horse” and suggesting she was older than she claimed. As a physician, he insisted physical signs reveal truth despite cosmetics and expressed disdain for fashionable women, whom he saw as lacking genuine feeling and motivated by selfishness. He described the Marquise as a polished figure hiding her true nature. Rastignac dismissed these moral reservations: for him a woman of fashion could be a valuable ally, offering connections, influence, and opportunities.Despite misgivings, Bianchon agreed to introduce Rastignac to his uncle Jean-Jules Popinot, a judge who might intervene in the Marquise’s affairs, though he doubted Popinot’s willingness given the judge’s reputation for inflexibility.The next morning Bianchon went to Popinot’s residence in a poor, dilapidated quarter of Paris, where the building’s aged decay contrasted with former grandeur. Popinot himself was unkempt and unambitious; his career had stalled despite talent—he had been passed over for promotion and remained a supernumerary judge.Popinot’s life was defined by a strict commitment to justice, often to his own detriment. He was known for kindness and a rare ability to win confessions without coercion, blending compassion with firmness. His dedication coexisted with frustration at legal injustices.As Bianchon approached Popinot’s door, he reflected on how difficult it would be to persuade his uncle to help Rastignac, aware of the legal complexities and obstacles ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Twelve years before this narrative, in 1816, during a severe food scarcity in France, Popinot was appointed President of the Commission Extraordinary to distribute food to the impoverished in his neighborhood. He had intended to leave the Rue du Fouarre. As a prominent lawyer and criminal judge, Popinot had spent five years observing legal outcomes without understanding their causes. His work among the destitute fostered deep compassion, transforming him into a protector of the poor, akin to a modern Saint Vincent de Paul.The change was gradual; charity, like vice, has its temptations. Popinot moved from one act of benevolence to another, becoming central in his community and involved in various charitable organizations. He worked tirelessly to prevent crime, provide employment, and assist the vulnerable, while keeping a private life few in Paris recognized. The gratitude of those he helped was often unspoken, their burdens leaving them little energy to acknowledge him.By the end of his second year of charitable work, Popinot had converted a storeroom in his home into a parlor for the needy. Furnished simply with wooden benches and basic chairs, the room became a hub where the poor could seek assistance. Popinot meticulously recorded donations and requests to manage resources effectively. His aide, Lavienne, handled pawn tickets and visited the areas most affected by famine.From early morning until late, the parlor was crowded with women, children, and the destitute seeking help. The atmosphere was frequently stifling, but the crowd's warmth made heating unnecessary. The poor revered Popinot, often waiting outside before dawn; even criminals respected his house. He dedicated mornings to the needy, afternoons to legal work, and evenings to his judicial duties.Popinot’s keen observation let him discern hidden virtues in the impoverished and detect faint signs of wrongdoing in others. His modest inherited fortune was supplemented by his late wife's wealth, but his judge’s salary was limited, requiring frugality that his charity further strained. Despite significant contributions, he remained one of the few judges in the Court of the Seine without the Legion of Honor.The President of the Second Chamber of the Court tasked Popinot with examining the Marquis d’Espard at the Marquise’s request for a Commission in Lunacy. The Rue du Fouarre, bustling with the needy in the early hours, would be desolate by nine. Bianchon, Popinot’s nephew, rode to find his uncle amid his charitable duties, expecting a contrast between Popinot’s appearance and the Marquise d’Espard’s expectations.On arrival, Bianchon found the parlor full—a tableau of hardship, faces showing despair and resilience. Popinot, in a worn nightcap and dressing gown, was engrossed in his work, embodying a man committed to alleviating suffering. Lavienne facilitated the flow of requests, keeping the process efficient.Bianchon interrupted with an urgent matter while Popinot handled consultations. After a brief exchange, Popinot resumed attending to a woman who needed funds to sustain her grocery business while her husband was imprisoned for debt; he provided assistance and documented the transaction in his ledger.As the morning progressed, Bianchon learned Popinot had seen over two hundred individuals that day, with more waiting. He was sent to attend urgent medical matters; on his return he found the parlor empty, the last of the needy having been helped.Popinot’s home reflected his chaotic life: dust, disarray, papers cluttering every surface, and the lingering remnants of his late wife’s possessions. Despite the disorder, tokens of gratitude from those he aided adorned the room, poignant reminders of his impact.Preparing for breakfast, Bianchon urged Popinot to dress suitably for the upcoming meeting with the Marquise d’Espard. Popinot resisted, citing legal restrictions against magistrates accepting hospitality from parties involved in cases they oversee. Bianchon argued it would be beneficial for Popinot to meet her in her own environment, where she could express concerns more comfortably.After some persuasion, Popinot agreed to visit the Marquise, recognizing the importance of understanding her perspective in the case. He made a note of the appointment and prepared to delve into the complexities surrounding the Marquis d’Espard’s mental state and the implications for his family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next evening at nine, Bianchon climbed his uncle’s dusty staircase to find him bent over a complicated judgment. Since the tailor had not sent the coat Lavienne ordered, Popinot wore his old stained coat, amusing those unaware of his private life. Bianchon straightened his uncle's cravat and buttoned his coat to hide the stains, but the judge's habitual movements soon rucked it up, revealing a gap between his waistcoat and trousers. Bianchon realized the absurdity just as his uncle entered the Marquise’s room.To follow the ensuing interview, a brief account of Madame d’Espard is necessary. For seven years she had been a fashionable figure in Paris, where trends can elevate or diminish people. After separating from her husband in 1815, the Marquise, about thirty-three, maintained her vogue despite being the mother of two boys, fifteen and thirteen. Her ability to remain in fashion—usually favoring youth and beauty—was remarkable.Appearing much younger than her years, she used elaborate hairstyles and a regimen of cold baths and minimal food to preserve her looks, an effort likened to a famous Polish lady who retained youthful vitality. Her complexion was clear and her figure retained allure; emotionally she remained detached, knowing neither love nor hatred. She parted from M. d’Espard with satisfaction, for he took their children, whom she regarded as obstacles to her ambitions.Living on an income of twenty-six thousand francs a year, the Marquise hesitated before deciding how to manage her life after the separation. Though able to live lavishly, she chose a more subdued existence for some years. By 1820 she re-emerged into society, hosting gatherings and establishing herself among the elite, eventually taking the place of other prominent women in Paris.Madame d’Espard cultivated ties with influential figures—a duchess, a countess—which amplified her political clout. Her drawing-room became a hub for political discussion; she was known for discretion and an ability to navigate Parisian society’s complexities. Ambitious young men admired her, and she skillfully maintained independence while wielding influence.Despite her charm, she was also indiscreet and vain, treating relationships as means to extend her power rather than for genuine connection. Her drawing-room drew notable figures, and she managed interactions to avoid personal compromise.Rastignac, a young man seeking alignment with her, recognized her as a potential ally but found her cunning a challenge. The Marquise’s household reflected wealth and status, and when Popinot arrived he noted the opulence. The Marquise welcomed him with feigned weakness; a third man, the Chevalier d’Espard, stood silently observing.Popinot, though outwardly simple, was perceptive and quickly assessed the situation. He engaged Madame d’Espard about her husband’s mental state and their separation. She offered a crafted narrative: a change in her husband’s temperament led to estrangement. Popinot remained skeptical, probing circumstances and the children’s welfare.As he questioned her, inconsistencies emerged. Popinot noted the lavishness of her life against claims of financial hardship, suggesting her motives for gaining control over her husband’s affairs might be self-serving. The Marquise, feeling cornered, tried to deflect, but Popinot’s persistence forced her to face the implications.Tension rose as Popinot outlined potential consequences. He emphasized transparency and warned that the court would scrutinize her motives. Despite efforts to remain composed, the Marquise realized her carefully constructed facade risked collapse.Afterward Bianchon and Rastignac discussed Popinot’s character and the case’s implications. They recognized its complexity and the potential for scandal; Bianchon worried about the Marquise’s influence. Popinot resolved to investigate further, planning to summon Madame Jeanrenaud, the woman linked to M. d’Espard, to clarify matters.The following day Madame Jeanrenaud arrived at Popinot’s office, presenting a stark contrast to the Marquise. Stout and unrefined, she denied wrongdoing and expressed concern for M. d’Espard’s well-being. Her honest, straightforward manner contrasted sharply with the Marquise’s calculated charm.Intrigued by Jeanrenaud’s sincerity, Popinot began to piece together M. d’Espard’s financial dealings. He recognized the case was more complicated than it had first appeared, with potential implications for both husband and wife. Preparing to unravel these entanglements, he understood the truth would require careful examination of all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################People who have matured know how trivial incidents can shape major events: Popinot's minor illness delayed a crucial meeting. The judge, feeling unwell, stayed home instead of visiting the Marquis d’Espard, losing a day whose absence echoed other historic delays.Before following the lawyer and his clerk to the Marquis’s residence, consider this father's home life, which his wife’s petition branded as mad. In old Paris, some buildings still show a taste for decoration and permanence. The house where M. d’Espard lived on Rue de la Montagne-Sainte-Genevieve was such a stone structure: once grand, now weathered and scarred by time and revolutions. Formerly home to dignitaries, it had become a multi-tenant dwelling, its interior degraded by a printing establishment.M. d’Espard took the ground floor, likely for his children’s sake, since the garden was unusually large for the area. He secured the rooms at a reasonable rent given the building’s disrepair and the depressed market. The house, dating from the reigns of Henry III–Louis XIII, still bore traces of former glory, including a perron and the garden.The Marquis furnished his rooms in rich browns, creating a peaceful atmosphere with carefully chosen pieces and a harmonious scheme. Few visitors were admitted to the family’s private life, which remained largely secluded. The household consisted of the Marquis, his two sons, and a small staff — a long-serving cook and a man-servant — both contributing to the house’s orderly quiet.M. d’Espard avoided socializing with other tenants to shield his children from outside influences. That choice, however, bred gossip and suspicion: neighbors labeled him mad and his servants untrustworthy. Tenants believed the Marquis was financially ruined, a view reinforced by his extreme frugality and habit of paying cash, avoiding credit.The Marquis and his family were noble by birth, embodying ideals becoming rare in France. They retained a deep sense of dignity and pride in their lineage, which clashed with egalitarian sentiments among their neighbors. This disconnect fostered animosity, as tenants resented the d’Espards for perceived superiority despite apparent financial strain.M. d’Espard, about fifty, exemplified the aristocracy of his time. He had a distinguished, somewhat cold appearance that commanded respect. Physical traits — a slightly crooked nose and a prominent forehead — lent an air of eccentricity that fueled rumors of madness. Despite elegant manners, he was frugal, wearing the same coat for years.His sons, Comte Clément de Négrepelisse and Vicomte Camille d’Espard, were handsome and displayed aristocratic grace. They inherited their father’s features and their mother’s dignified manner. The elder, nearly sixteen, had moved to more fashionable attire, while the younger still wore a round jacket. The Marquis concentrated on giving them a rounded education, emphasizing mathematics, Eastern languages, and history, while avoiding what he considered unproductive studies.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Popinot planned to question M. d’Espard fell on a Thursday holiday. About nine, before their father rose, the two brothers were in the garden. The younger begged to visit the shooting-gallery for the first time and pressed Clément to support the request to the Marquis. The Viscount often took advantage of Clément’s weakness and delighted in wrestling with him. Their noisy quarrel roused their father, who watched amused from the window. Two neighbors commented that the Marquis seemed mad to make his children fight. Noticing them, the Marquis called the boys; they leapt into his room and Clément, after a moment’s hesitation, obtained permission from Camille.All through the house people were talking about the Marquis’ supposed new madness. When Popinot arrived near noon with his clerk, the portress conducted him up to the third floor, remarking that M. d’Espard had made his children fight that morning and had laughed when the younger bit the elder until he bled; she added that the Marquis rarely showed himself. The landing’s muddy floor and grimy banisters lent some credibility to her gossip and made Popinot, though impartial, receptive to the tale.“This is the man who swallows enough to feed the whole neighborhood,” the porter’s wife declared. The clerk smiled; Popinot fought to remain composed. They entered an outer office where an old man, probably the office clerk, sat amid shelves of published numbers; a grating separated a private room, and a cash-desk hinted at the enterprise’s commercial aspect.“M. d’Espard?” Popinot asked the man in a gray blouse. The shopman opened the next room to reveal the Marquis, a venerable, simply dressed old man wearing the Cross of Saint-Louis, seated at a desk. Popinot explained they wished to speak on private business. The old man announced their intent to the Marquis, who rose and listened.After formalities the Marquis asked their purpose. Popinot measured him and said, “Monsieur le Marquis, my duties and the inquiry that brings me here require us to be alone, although such inquiries are usually public. I am a judge on the Inferior Court of Appeal for the Department of the Seine, charged with examining you regarding a petition for a Commission in Lunacy filed by the Marquise d’Espard.” The old man withdrew; the clerk sat to take notes. The Marquis reddened and trembled, then steadied himself.“How is it,” he asked, “that I have had no notice of such a petition?” Popinot explained that notice is unnecessary because those deemed subject to such a commission are presumed to lack reason; the Court must verify the petitioner’s claims. The Marquis accepted this and asked what he must do. Popinot urged him to answer fully and confidentially, especially concerning delicate accusations Madame d’Espard might make. The Marquis feared that his explanations could bring blame on his wife. Popinot promised discretion and the customary courtesy should the inquiry reveal a misunderstanding.Then Popinot confronted the major allegation: that the Marquis had dissipated his fortune for the benefit of Madame Jeanrenaud, widow of a bargemaster, or her son Colonel Jeanrenaud, for whom he allegedly procured an appointment and exhausted influence with the King. The Marquis flushed; tears came, but pride returned quickly.“You place me in a strange dilemma,” he said. Revealing his motives would mean disclosing secret wounds and trusting the family’s honor to strangers. Popinot assured him of confidentiality. Freed from his usual reserve, the Marquis then spoke with clarity and passion.After his marriage his wife had incurred heavy expenses that forced him to borrow. He could not afford a steward; most of his title-deeds had been taken to Paris by his father, who feared scrutiny. Their family name was Negrepelisse; the title d’Espard had been acquired through marriage in the time of Henri IV, when they lost the town of Negrepelisse during religious wars and an ancestor was ruined by the destruction of his property. Though a later ancestor secured favor with Louis XIV and fortune, a stain of blood and disgrace remained, and the Marquis was determined to erase it.He discovered that his grandfather had obtained the lands of Negrepelisse through means tied to the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes: a Protestant merchant was arrested and hanged, and the land passed to his ancestor by iniquitous means. The merchant’s family had the rightful claim; the victim’s name was Jeanrenaud. The Marquis could not bear the disgrace and resolved to find the heirs. Eventually he located M. Jeanrenaud, reduced to poverty and serving as a lieutenant under Napoleon. Believing the heir’s rights incontestable and their lack of means to sue, he offered to pay them the lands’ value at the time they were taken—eleven hundred thousand francs—payable at his convenience and without interest.To effect restitution he lived on little for a long time. When he proposed to Madame d’Espard that they move to the country to live on half her income, she treated him as mad; her coldness convinced him she would have condoned his ancestors’ crimes. Terrified by her attitude, he resolved to leave her in control of her fortune after settling their debts, and to take charge of educating their children himself, determined to raise them as gentlemen. He invested prudently, repaid the debt sooner than expected, and had recently completed the payment of the eleven hundred thousand francs, making restitution without harming his children's prospects. These were the reasons for payments to Madame Jeanrenaud and her son.Popinot asked whether Madame d’Espard knew his motives for retiring from public life. “Yes, monsieur,” the Marquis replied. Popinot, visibly moved, excused his clerk. He then inquired about other allegations, such as that the Marquis was engaged in a business unbecoming his rank. The Marquis declined to discuss it there and summoned Comte de Nouvion, an old family friend, explaining that he used the rooms as offices for the publication of the Picturesque History of China by a reputable firm, not as his private residence.He showed Popinot his ground-floor apartment—a place of simple, touching harmony that struck the judge. The pleasant weather and garden convinced Popinot that a madman would hardly produce such beauty. The Marquis said he intended to remain until his younger son finished his studies, then travel with the children to European capitals to further their education and languages. He explained his China project: Comte de Nouvion, returned to France without fortune, was his partner; the Abbé Grozier, Keeper of the Books at the Arsenal, had been his tutor and taught him about China. Though enamored of China’s history, he remained a French gentleman. Financially, they had two thousand five hundred subscribers—twelve hundred in France—the book would cost about three hundred francs, and the Comte would earn six to seven thousand francs a year from it; the hundred thousand francs he hoped to make would cover his children’s education and pleasures. Twelve years of retirement to educate his children had cost him his memory at Court and political prospects, but he believed he had purified his family’s past and secured a glorious future for his children—a noble, if secret, task.At that moment horses clattered in the courtyard. The two boys entered, robust and sunburnt, dressed for riding. They greeted their father and the lawyer. Popinot needed no further evidence of the Marquis’ bond with his sons. The boys reported shooting success and that they had ridden in the Bois, where they had seen their mother. “Why didn’t you speak to her?” the Marquis asked. “I think I noticed, father, that she doesn’t want us to speak to her in public,” Clément whispered. A cloud passed over the Marquis’ brow; Popinot felt deep sympathy at the scene of fatherly devotion. “Justice may look in here at any time,” the Marquis said. “If anyone is mad, it can only be the children, who are a little crazy about their father, and the father who is very crazy about his children—but that kind of madness rings true.”Madame Jeanrenaud then entered, disregarding protests from the man-servant. “I take no roundabout ways!” she exclaimed. Bowing to those present, she announced she had come too late because the criminal judge was there. She declared that she and her son were of one mind to return everything, for their honor was at stake; they preferred to give back everything rather than cause trouble. “They must be as stupid as pans without handles to call you a lunatic,” she added. The boys clung to their father, alarmed.“Silence, madame,” Popinot interposed. The Marquis sent his children to the garden. Popinot addressed Madame Jeanrenaud: the payments made by the Marquis were legally due, though prompted by an expansive sense of honesty. If every possessor of confiscated goods were compelled to make restitution every century and a half, few legitimate owners would remain in France. The law allowed M. d’Espard to dispose of his income without accounting for it. A Commission in Lunacy is appropriate only when actions lack reason; here, the remittances were driven by the highest motives. She could keep the money without remorse and let the world misinterpret a noble action. Popinot declared he would rather see the Marquis crowned than subjected to a Commission. Madame Jeanrenaud applauded his defense, calling it judge-like and warmly praising his words.The Marquis extended his hand to Popinot, who pressed it gently; a private understanding passed between the humane judge and the noble, sublime man. “Monsieur le Marquis,” Popinot added, “from the first words of this inquiry, I regarded my clerk as unnecessary.” Leading M. d’Espard to the window, he said it was time to return home and that Madame la Marquise had likely acted under an influence the Marquis should counteract. Popinot left, the scene lingering with him. “Those rooms would suit me perfectly,” he thought. “If M. d’Espard leaves them, I will take up his lease.”The next morning, having written his report the night before, Popinot went to the Palais de Justice to pursue prompt justice. As he prepared to put on his gown, the usher said the President wished to see him privately. “Good morning, my dear Popinot,” the President greeted. “I’ve been waiting.” Popinot asked if something was wrong. The President called it a silly trifle: the Keeper of the Seals, with whom he had dined, had suggested it would be best if Popinot did not sit on the case. Despite Popinot’s protest that he had left Madame d’Espard’s house before tea, the President cited propriety—“Caesar’s wife must not be suspected”—and the need to avoid any appearance of conflict that might expose the Bench to journalistic malice. It was to be a matter of propriety, not discipline.Popinot was to signal his retirement from the case as a personal favor; in recompense, the President would secure the Cross of the Legion of Honor for him, long deserved. M. Camusot, a newly appointed judge from a provincial court, had been named in Popinot’s place. Popinot noted with ironic amusement Camusot’s pale ambition. He bowed and withdrew, choosing not to contest the implication.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyPIERRE GRASSOUBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDedicationTo The Lieutenant-Colonel of Artillery, Periollas, As a Testimony of the Affectionate Esteem of the Author,De BalzacPIERRE GRASSOUSince the revolution of 1830 the Salon has lost its former force. Once a selective exhibition that honored a small number of works and provoked lively debate—helping to create names like Delacroix and Ingres—it has become overwhelmed by sheer numbers. The public is numbed; exhibitions close quickly, individual pieces lose impact, and the excitement around art fades. Before 1817 only a limited number of paintings were shown; now galleries overflow, diluting quality and attention.The Salon’s essence is dissipated in this chaos. What once was refined selection is now a tumultuous bazaar where great artists are obscured. Decamps’ notable works, for example, would have stood out more amid fewer high-quality pieces than they do amid mediocrity. Ironically, as the number of artists grows, talk of unrecognized genius increases. When the Academy judged strictly, true talent was acknowledged; without that judgment, art suffers and the Salon risks perishing.Amid the swelling catalogue stands Pierre Grassou, nicknamed “Fougeres.” In 1832 he lived in a cramped rue de Navarin apartment with a studio overlooking Montmartre. Modest and tidy, his studio reflected his careful nature and limited means. Avoiding the burdens of history painting, he focused on easel and genre work.One December morning Grassou, preparing to work, was visited by Elie Magus, a picture dealer. Magus habitually bought Grassou’s paintings cheaply and proposed a business arrangement: Grassou would paint portraits of the bourgeois Vervelle family, who wished to enter the art world. Their daughter had a substantial dowry, and Magus hinted that marriage could benefit Grassou financially.Grassou, living simply and solitary, was startled by talk of marriage. The idea of painting three family portraits, however, appealed as a chance to establish himself as a portraitist. Magus left to find the Vervelles, leaving Grassou to weigh the opportunity’s potential.To understand Grassou’s situation, we look back. He studied under various masters—Servin and Schinner among them—but never fully grasped their techniques. His education was marked by a lack of recognition; he was often mocked for mediocrity. Gentle and diligent, he nevertheless failed to stand out among flashier or more eccentric peers.His first Salon attempt, a village-wedding scene in 1819, was rejected. Rather than despair, he sought Schinner’s critique, who bluntly advised him to give up painting. Still determined, Grassou labored to refine the rejected piece, only to sell it to Magus for a meager sum. That sale began a long, difficult career of perseverance against discouragement.As Grassou navigated his art life he remained committed to his craft, embodying the struggles and hopes of many contemporaries. His story reflects broader themes of ambition, recognition, and the harsh realities of an art world where circumstance often overshadows talent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For several days Pierre Fougeres lingered near Elie's shop hoping his painting would attract notice, but it went unnoticed. After a week it vanished; when he casually asked, Elie Magus said he had sold it and would buy more if Fougeres produced Flemish interiors, anatomy lessons, and landscapes. Overjoyed, Fougeres felt validated.He missed the irony in Elie's manner that a more cynical artist might have seen. He met his friend Joseph Bridau but, consumed with painting, could not enjoy the evening. Back home he sketched furiously, inspired by newfound confidence, bought supplies, hired models, and devoted himself to his craft.After two months he produced four paintings. Schinner and Bridau dismissed them as derivative and suggested he try literature; undeterred, Fougeres retouched the canvases and sold them to Elie for twenty-five francs apiece, barely breaking even. He noticed a strange mist over his paintings in the shop but dismissed it as imagination.Over seven years he honed his skills, producing competent but not exceptional work. Elie sold the paintings, letting Fougeres earn a modest living. At the 1829 Exhibition, with friends' help, he entered a powerful piece—a young man in prison inspired by Gerard Douw—which drew significant attention, even from Charles X. Madame purchased the painting and commissioned more.Fougeres' success came from his ability to imitate rather than innovate, a lesson he learned well. Despite criticism, his modesty and perseverance won him supporters, and he gained a reputation for dedication. With his earnings he furnished a studio and completed commissions punctually, a rarity among artists.By thirty-seven he had produced around two hundred paintings, achieving a style that appealed to the bourgeoisie. His friends often preferred his character to his art and regretted his career choice, yet he still gave sound advice and maintained a keen appreciation for beauty.After the July Revolution he continued submitting work to the Salon, living frugally and managing finances with his notary Cardot. He dreamed of a comfortable life as an artist, aspiring to join the Institute and gain recognition.One day the wealthy Vervelle family visited his studio for portraits. The father, once a bottle-dealer, was impressed by Fougeres' Legion of Honor cross. The family admired his work, and Pierre was charmed by their daughter Virginie. On subsequent sittings they grew familiar, discussing their art collection and financial stability.During sessions Pierre flattered the family and endeared himself to them. The father boasted of their valuable collection, while the notary praised Fougeres' character and financial prudence, stressing his ability to provide for a family. This reputation strengthened the Vervelles' interest, and they began to see him as a suitable match for Virginie.As sittings progressed Pierre's feelings for Virginie deepened and he began to envision a future with her, despite initially focusing on his art. The Vervelles' admiration and talk of his success further fueled his aspirations, intertwining personal hopes with professional ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Fougeres began painting Mademoiselle Virginie, he was already practically part of the Vervelle family. They liked the tidy studio, which felt like a second home. Near the end of the sitting, Joseph Bridau burst in, disheveled and startling them; he scanned the room, approached Grassou, and struggled with his coat. "Wood is dear," he remarked, and asked for five hundred francs to fend off creditors. Grassou agreed to write a note. Bridau then criticized his painting style and urged him to accept nature's imperfections.As Bridau left, Madame Vervelle worried about his influence on Virginie's portrait, but Grassou reassured her he had only improved it. Intrigued by the artist’s supposed genius, the Vervelles invited Fougeres to their country house the following Sunday, hoping to flatter him with hospitality and their paintings.Flattered, Grassou went to Ville d'Avray and was warmly received. They showed their well-kept grounds, gave a lavish dinner, and treated him like a celebrated artist. After the meal they led him to their picture gallery, eager for his opinion on a collection they believed rivaled King Louis-Philippe's.As Grassou examined the canvases, he was shocked to recognize many as his own works long ago sold to the dealer Elie Magus. Vervelle boasted the collection was worth three hundred thousand francs; Grassou, aware of the irony, whispered that he had sold those pictures for far less. Impressed, Vervelle promised to double his daughter's dowry if Grassou could substantiate his claim. The revelation raised Grassou’s standing with the family, who continued to regard him as a great artist. When he completed their portraits he presented them as gifts, further cementing their bond.Today Pierre Grassou is known as a competent portrait painter, earning about twenty thousand francs a year. He lives comfortably with his wife, whose dowry was six thousand francs, and paints family portraits for five hundred francs each. His financial success has enhanced his reputation, and he even received the Legion of Honor for his conduct during civil unrest.Despite these achievements, Grassou is insecure, convinced other artists mock him and critics overlook him. He strives for recognition—hoping for the Academy—while secretly replacing his poorer works with those of more celebrated artists in the Ville d'Avray collection. His mediocrity, tempered by kindness and generosity, makes him a complex and sympathetic figure in the artistic community.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyEUGENIE GRANDETBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Maria.May your name, that of one whose portrait is the noblest ornament of this work, lie on its opening pages like a branch of sacred box, taken from an unknown tree, but sanctified by religion, and kept ever fresh and green by pious hands to bless the house.De Balzac.EUGENIE GRANDETIIn some provincial towns certain houses give off a melancholy like that of cloisters, moorlands, or ruins. Within them silence reigns, life seems frozen, and the routine is punctuated only by the cold, solitary figure at a window.Such a somber air hung over a house in Saumur, at the end of a steep street leading to the château. The street, rarely used, had a pebble pavement and the stillness of three-century-old houses. Built of wood, these buildings show features prized by artists and antiquarians.Passing them, one notices great oaken beams, elaborately carved; some façades are covered with slate, others have worn window-sills barely holding flower-pots. Doors studded with large nails bear traces of history—Protestant marks or the insignia of forgotten bourgeois families. In these structures the history of France seems recorded.Beside a dilapidated house stands a country gentleman's mansion, its stone arch still faintly showing armorial bearings—worn by revolutions since 1789. The merchants’ ground floors are not modern shops but simple dark rooms without glass fronts, where goods are exposed plainly. A young girl knitting calls to her parents to attend customers, served with varying courtesy.In this region the weather shapes commerce. Merchants, wine-growers, and coopers watch the sky anxiously, fearing frost or rain that could ruin them. On Saturdays vineyards call their owners away and the streets fall silent, where life unfolds plainly and neighbors see one another’s doings.The old town of Saumur, scene of this tale, contains a particularly melancholy mansion owned by Monsieur Grandet. To grasp the house’s meaning one must know the man.Monsieur Grandet began as a master cooper in 1789 and married the daughter of a rich wood merchant. Using his savings and his wife’s dowry he bought prime vineyard land during the sale of church property. He rose in reputation as protector of local nobility and supplier to Republican armies, accumulating wealth and influence.By 1806 Grandet was mayor and continued to swell his fortune through shrewd investments in vineyards and estates. That year he inherited three fortunes, cementing his standing in Saumur. His estate included vast vineyards, farms, and an abbey, making him among the region’s wealthiest men.The townspeople speculated about the extent of his wealth. He had a knack for timing sales to secure maximum profit, and his financial shrewdness made him both admired and feared. By 1816 estimates put his fortune at nearly four million francs, and many believed he kept an equal sum in cash. His reputation had replaced earlier mockery; his acts came to be regarded as community law. Frugality governed his life; he rarely spent beyond necessities.Grandet’s manner was plain and unpretentious. He spoke little and stuttered if forced into long conversation—a seeming lack of eloquence that concealed calculation in business. He would never commit to anything without consulting his wife, whom he kept in a subservient state.Physically he was stout, with a round face and a crafty look. His dress was simple and practical, signaling thrift. Only a few had access to his house, among them a young magistrate, C. de Bonfons, who hoped to marry Grandet’s daughter, Eugénie.Social life in Saumur centred on competition for Eugénie’s hand, chiefly between the Cruchots and the des Grassins. The Cruchots, a powerful family, wanted Eugénie for their nephew; the des Grassins sought a match for their son Adolphe. This rivalry buzzed through the town with much speculation about Grandet’s intentions for his daughter.In early 1811 the Cruchots gained advantage when the estate of Froidfond came up for sale.
