The old maid’s suspicion escalated into open hostility and confrontation, further undermining Pierrette’s well‑being. As tensions peaked, Pierrette’s condition worsened and she longed for escape from her torment.Amid this turmoil, love, jealousy, and ambition became entangled, setting the stage for a tragic sequence that would affect everyone involved. The struggle for power, affection, and security within the Rogron household threatened devastating consequences for Pierrette, whose innocence and vulnerability made her an easy target in a world rife with envy and desire.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For a month Rogron had stopped delivering the Constitutionnel to Gouraud, who then came for it himself, often lingering to gossip and walk with Sylvie. Eager to see the colonel, Sylvie dressed in her finest. When Gouraud arrived, Rogron excused himself, leaving Sylvie alone with the colonel.Noting her solemnity, Gouraud asked about her cousin's health; she said the cousin was unwell. The colonel, uneasy about Vinet's influence over the Rogrons, felt marginalized. Vinet seemed to dominate their political plans, while Gouraud, a half-pay officer, feared being sidelined. He was also aware of Rogron's growing infatuation with Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, which threatened his prospects.Gouraud mentioned an interest in Bathilde, making Rogron pale at the idea of competition. The colonel suspected Vinet might be manoeuvring him into abandoning Sylvie for Pierrette as a ploy to benefit Vinet and Bathilde.Left alone with Sylvie, Gouraud observed her unease and suspected Vinet's meddling. Sylvie, testing his feelings, raised Pierrette; the colonel dismissed any affection for her, saying he preferred a more mature partner. Relieved, Sylvie concealed her intentions and suggested her brother had considered Pierrette as a match.Sensing Sylvie's interest, Gouraud declared his intention to marry her, delighting Rogron. Sylvie began planning their future home but remained wary of Pierrette's interactions with the colonel and of how easily Vinet could alter alliances.As dinner neared, Sylvie scolded Pierrette over a stain on her dress, blaming her for the mishap. Despite her harshness she ordered Pierrette to dress nicely for the evening, suspecting collusion with Gouraud.The Chargeboeuf ladies arrived; Bathilde was spectacularly dressed. Under Vinet's guidance, Bathilde planned to use a marriage to Rogron as a stepping stone to power, treating him as a means rather than a partner. Vinet had instilled in her ambition for independence and influence, urging her to exploit beauty and connections.The evening grew tense. Bathilde's condescension toward Pierrette was obvious, and Vinet's remarks heightened the rivalry. They played cards; Sylvie tried to manipulate the game while Pierrette's innocent comments provoked Sylvie's ire.When Pierrette left the room in distress, Sylvie's anger flared and she resolved to make her pay for the perceived slight. The evening ended with Sylvie frustrated and feeling betrayed, reflecting on men's duplicity and her precarious position. The next day Pierrette suffered a painful fall, which Sylvie blamed on disobedience, showing no sympathy. Rogron suggested caring for Pierrette, but Sylvie dismissed the idea, revealing her coldness and the widening rift between the cousins.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The week ended in relentless torment for Pierrette under Sylvie's increasing cruelty. She kept silent about Brigaut’s arrival to avoid more anger, but his memory sustained her; she longed for him and feared for his safety. Their connection felt vital, and even one glimpse was a lifeline.Determined to communicate, Brigaut arranged to exchange letters. One morning he slipped a note to her while pretending to buy fruit. The exchange unleashed deep feelings in both, exceeding ordinary affection. Sylvie remained unaware, and Pierrette returned home with the note.That night Pierrette read Brigaut’s heartfelt letter: he expressed longing, a wish to escape their oppressive circumstances, and asked her to lower a string from her window so they could pass notes nightly. Moved, Pierrette resolved to reply despite the difficulty of obtaining paper and ink without waking Sylvie. She poured out her heart, confessing her love and describing the suffering inflicted by her relatives.At midnight she sent her letter down to Brigaut, who awaited it eagerly. Their correspondence continued—full of hope and plans—despite Sylvie’s simmering jealousy. Observing Pierrette’s newfound joy, Sylvie grew watchful, suspecting she had seen someone.Under Sylvie’s scrutiny Pierrette’s health declined and one evening she fainted, drawing concern from the colonel and others. Sylvie dismissed the illness as a ruse, but Pierrette’s condition worsened. In desperation Pierrette cried for help, triggering a violent confrontation as Sylvie tried to seize her letters. At the height of Sylvie’s fury, Pierrette’s grandmother arrived, witnessed the struggle, and rushed to her granddaughter’s aid.Her arrival brought hope after days on the brink of despair. Sylvie, confronted, seemed confused and frightened. The grandmother, determined to protect Pierrette, sought medical help, fearing for her life.Meanwhile Brigaut wrote urgently to Madame Lorrain urging her to come and rescue Pierrette. He stressed the danger—he feared for her health and safety. Upon receiving Brigaut’s appeals, the grandmother felt both joy and dread and immediately traveled to Provins.She found Pierrette distressed and traumatized, bearing physical and emotional wounds; the sight of her injured hand—wounded during Sylvie’s attempts to control her—filled the grandmother with anguish. She vowed to secure justice and ensure Pierrette’s safety.As Pierrette recovered, her bond with her grandmother deepened, giving her strength to face what lay ahead. The arrival of medical attention and the promise of a better life with her grandmother and Brigaut offered a glimmer of hope amid recent darkness. The struggle for freedom and love continued, with Pierrette determined to reclaim her life from oppressive relatives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At nine o’clock that morning Monsieur Martener visited Monsieur Tiphaine to report the mistreatment Pierrette had suffered at the Rogrons’ hands. Tiphaine summoned Auffray, a notary and Pierrette’s relative. Tensions between the Vinet and Tiphaine factions were rising, fueled by damaging truths about Madame Tiphaine’s family, which intensified animosity and tied personal grievances to local political strife.Tiphaine saw an opportunity to weaken the Rogrons, influential opponents of the monarchy. After consulting the public prosecutor, he, Auffray, and Martener arranged to petition the courts for Auffray to become Pierrette’s guardian, enabling a Family Council—backed by medical testimony—to remove her from the Rogrons’ control.By midday the town buzzed: neighbors had heard Pierrette’s cries the night before, and speculation spread. Doctor Bianchon and Brigaut examined her injuries and confirmed the Rogrons’ abuse. Vinet, the Rogrons’ lawyer, rushed to warn of potential criminal charges. Sylvie, initially evasive, eventually confessed to her jealousy and the truth of the incident, grasping the severity of their situation.Exploiting their fear, Vinet suggested a marriage announcement might quiet the scandal. Pressured and manipulated, the Rogrons agreed to a marriage contract that served their political interests. Vinet then filed a petition alleging Brigaut and the widow Lorrain had abducted Pierrette, attempting to cast the Rogrons as victims.The court hearing was postponed, fueling public excitement. The Tiphaines rallied support for Pierrette, who remained unaware of the turmoil and was cared for by her grandmother and Brigaut, devoted to her recovery. The doctors, appalled by her condition, unanimously condemned the Rogrons’ treatment.When the court reconvened Auffray presented the physicians’ report arguing for Pierrette’s protection. Vinet countered, but the judge ordered that Pierrette be kept away from the Rogrons until a Family Council could convene—a significant public rebuke.The Family Council, composed of Pierrette’s relatives and allies, voted to remove Rogron as guardian and appoint Auffray in his place. Despite Vinet’s lengthy defense, the evidence against the Rogrons was overwhelming, prompting a civil suit for neglect and abuse.As the legal battle continued Pierrette’s health worsened. Despite medical efforts, she faced a grim prognosis. Martener and the Auffray family grew fond of her; her grandmother’s steadfast care and Brigaut’s visits provided comfort.In March 1828 a critical operation was performed, but recovery was unlikely. As her condition declined Pierrette expressed a desire to forgive her cousins, showing grace amid suffering. She died soon after. The funeral drew the town, while the Rogrons, fearing public outrage, stayed away.Grief-stricken, Brigaut made her coffin, pouring his sorrow into the task. The Rogrons tried to exploit the situation legally, but community support for Pierrette blocked their efforts. Vinet, attempting to defend his clients, faced backlash as the truth of the Rogrons’ actions became more widely known.Afterward Brigaut left Provins to seek a new life in the military. Vinet and the Rogrons, however, continued to prosper politically; despite the scandal their reputations remained largely intact. The story of Pierrette’s fate became distorted, with many in town believing the Rogrons had acted nobly. Only a few—Martener, Brigaut, and kindred spirits—remembered the reality of her suffering and the injustices she endured.The account closes with a reflection on justice and the social forces that allowed such tragedies: the need for accountability and the lasting impact of Pierrette’s life and death on those who truly understood her plight.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo David, Sculptor:The permanence of the work on which I inscribe your name—twice made illustrious in this century—is very problematical; whereas you have graven mine in bronze which survives nations—if only in their coins. The day may come when numismatists, discovering amid the ashes of Paris existences perpetuated by you, will wonder at the number of heads crowned in your atelier and endeavor to find in them new dynasties.To you, this divine privilege; to me, gratitude.De Balzac.---THE VICAR OF TOURSIIn the autumn of 1826 Abbé Birotteau, the story’s protagonist, was caught in a rainstorm returning from a friend’s house. He hurried across the deserted square called “The Cloister,” behind the cathedral of Saint-Gatien in Tours.Short, corpulent, about sixty and subject to gout, Birotteau hated wet shoes. Yet he was in good spirits—he had won at cards with Madame de Listomère and was buoyed by hope of a long-desired canonry at the cathedral. This ambition consumed him, making him almost oblivious to rain and discomfort.The Cloister had once housed Chapter dignitaries but, after ecclesiastical confiscations, became the narrow Rue de la Psalette. On its left stood a single house, shadowed by the cathedral and long occupied by priests; its owner, the old maid Mademoiselle Gamard, was devout and discreet and had kept the property through the Restoration by taking in priests as boarders.Birotteau had lived there two years. To be Mademoiselle Gamard’s boarder and to secure a canonry were his two chief ambitions, representing the comforts and status he craved. His desire for the apartment had become an obsession, pursued with a mix of hope and impatience.Gamard could take only two lodgers. For twelve years she had housed the Abbé Troubert and the late Abbé Chapeloud. When Chapeloud died, Birotteau took his place; he had long admired the apartment’s furnishings during his visits. Chapeloud had been a close friend; his death left Birotteau bereaved but also relieved to inherit the library and furniture he had coveted.Chapeloud had gradually enriched the rooms with books and tasteful furniture, inciting Birotteau’s envy and longing. The vicar’s wish for that comfort grew until it bordered on obsession; he sometimes found himself wishing for Chapeloud’s passing to obtain the apartment, though he never schemed about his friend’s will. Chapeloud, aware of these feelings, forgave him and, just before dying, told him, “This time you will certainly get the apartment. I feel it is all over with me now.” After the funeral, Birotteau set to arranging his inherited possessions and felt contented despite being passed over for the canonry.Mademoiselle Gamard welcomed him as boarder; he enjoyed the attentions and comforts he had long imagined. Yet his gratitude soon shifted back to the canonry, which again obsessed him. Preoccupied, he left his umbrella in the rain. Arriving home, he found the door locked and had to ring several times. His servant Marianne said Gamard assumed he was already in. He noticed the absence of usual attentions—no slippers, no fire—and felt slighted.These small neglects pricked his vanity and made him suspect intentional disrespect. He reflected on possible causes and convinced himself he must have displeased her somehow. His innocence and lack of worldly experience left him vulnerable; he failed to perceive that Gamard’s coolness was growing into subtle persecution. Where Chapeloud had managed the relationship with shrewd tact, Birotteau’s simplicity left him blind to mounting resentment.Mademoiselle Gamard’s discontent had been building over the eighteen months of his tenancy. She prized social recognition and had hoped to create a salon, ambitions frustrated by Birotteau’s indifference. As he resumed former social habits and spent evenings with friends who provided the engagement she desired, he further distanced himself from her. She felt her plans slipping away and began to nurse a quiet vengeance.The neglect he experienced—the locked door, the omission of small domestic attentions—manifested her growing animosity. Birotteau, naive and self-absorbed in his desire for preferment, remained oblivious to the calculations behind these omissions. He confronted only the symptoms, not their cause, and hoped for clarity in the morning, unaware the reasons for Gamard’s hostility would remain hidden to him.The contrast between the two priests was clear: Chapeloud had navigated Gamard’s vanity with worldly tact; Birotteau’s simplicity made him an easy target for her petty reprisals. As the vicar sank into discontent, he sought solace in outside company, further alienating Gamard who, thwarted in her ambitions, began to plot revenge. Thus small domestic grievances escalated into a deeper conflict, revealing how unnoticed slights and unacknowledged ambitions can shape human relationships in a provincial setting. The events of these nights are only a prologue to a fuller exploration of the intricacies of pride, naiveté, and social maneuvering that will follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Birotteau awoke preoccupied with thoughts of his potential canonry, momentarily forgetting the ominous signs from the night before. He rang for Marianne to light a fire; when she did not appear he was about to ring again when Abbé Troubert entered after knocking. This monthly visit was expected; Troubert noticed the absence of a fire, called for Marianne, then inquired about Birotteau’s health and any news of the canonry. Birotteau, unaware that Troubert resented Madame de Listomère for not inviting him to her home, shared details of his efforts to secure the position.The two abbés presented a sharp contrast. Troubert was tall, thin, serious, his wrinkled face hinting at sarcasm and contempt; Birotteau was plump, with a round, cheerful face. Troubert’s calm exterior masked a dullness some attributed to his subservience to Mademoiselle Gamard, while Birotteau’s jovial nature made him well liked. Neither seemed likely to rise above his present station—Troubert because of restrained ambition and Birotteau because of amiability.At fifty, Troubert had smoothed his ambitions by adopting a saintly life, and his declining health suggested he might soon be appointed vicar-general. Birotteau’s robust health made him seem likely to endure. Abbé Chapeloud, a respected diocesan figure, had quietly opposed Troubert’s elevation, yet Troubert treated him with respect despite Chapeloud’s skepticism.During the visit Troubert asked to borrow the “Register of Bishops,” which Birotteau gladly supplied. They went downstairs together, each carrying a large book, and were met with Mademoiselle Gamard’s sharp disapproval. She scolded Birotteau for cluttering her dining room; he felt the sting of her ill temper, a jarring contrast to his usual cheerfulness.Breakfast was tense. Mademoiselle Gamard complained about being disturbed the previous night, and Birotteau, weighed by her displeasure, struggled to engage. He tried to break the silence with remarks about the coffee and the weather, but met disdain. Her demeanor reflected the bitterness of an unmarried woman who, feeling unfulfilled and rejected, harbored resentment toward others; lives without romantic fulfillment bred in her both self-loathing and an ungracious manner. Her sharp features and abrupt movements mirrored that inner turmoil, and having never married she bore the social stigma of perceived failure.Feeling the oppressive atmosphere, Birotteau left the dining room with a heavy heart and wandered the garden, only to find Mademoiselle Gamard and Troubert watching him, which intensified his discomfort. Distracted by their scrutiny, he sought refuge in church activities and momentarily forgot his troubles. When he returned home he discovered he had missed dinner: Mademoiselle Gamard had set the clock ahead to avoid waiting for him. This manipulation exemplified her maliciousness—she took pleasure in undermining Birotteau’s comfort while maintaining an appearance of propriety.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eight days after the affair began, the relationship between Abbé Birotteau and Mademoiselle Gamard revealed a six-month plot. Birotteau had been blind to Gamard’s malice, but after the candlestick-and-clock incident he finally recognized her hostility. The discovery devastated him; her presence became suffocating. Gamard, savoring vengeance, had meticulously plotted against the unsuspecting priest, who was too absorbed in petty concerns to confide in friends.Birotteau’s once-mundane life turned to torment. Anxiety about confronting Gamard worsened his health, and he sought help from Abbé Troubert, though he felt embarrassed to discuss Gamard’s provocations with someone so grave. Troubert listened coolly, tamping down a smile, and suggested understanding Gamard as a health-related problem.Desperate and bewildered, Birotteau thought a temporary departure from Gamard’s house might calm things. He planned to stay at Madame de Listomère’s country house, not seeing how this would play into Gamard’s hands. After ten days at the Alouette, Monsieur Caron, Gamard’s lawyer, informed him she was anxious about his prolonged absence. Startled, Birotteau insisted he intended to return, but Caron said Gamard wanted clarification to avoid disputes.Back in the dining room, Birotteau recounted his troubles to friends intrigued by the drama. Madame de Listomère suggested Troubert coveted Birotteau’s apartment. The group parsed Gamard’s motives; an old landowner warned this was only the start of long troubles. Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix arrived with news that the vicar-general was ill and Troubert might be appointed in his place, a development that would give Troubert appointment power.Madame de Listomère urged Birotteau to consider the canonry rather than endure Gamard. The group rallied, proposing he let Gamard and Troubert have their way if it might advance him. But tensions rose, and friends summoned Caron again to clarify matters. Caron returned with a document asking Birotteau to relinquish his residence; pressed by friends, he signed.Birotteau’s despair deepened when he realized the document also cost him his belongings, which Gamard claimed as compensation for board price differences. Confronting her, he found she coldly cited the agreement. Devastated, he fled to Madame de Listomère, where Mademoiselle Salomon, fearing for his sanity, took him back to the Alouette to confide in friends.Monsieur de Bourbonne examined the deed and deemed it exploitative. Outraged, he urged legal action. The group debated suing while others favored compromise to avoid scandal. They resolved to support Birotteau against Gamard and Troubert, though Bourbonne warned many would abandon him once the struggle intensified.Madame de Listomère’s invitation for Birotteau to stay at her house provoked gossip and divided the town; Gamard, feeling slighted, sought to undermine his reputation. Friends began gathering evidence for a lawsuit, complicated by political divisions and anti-clerical sentiment. An expert appraisal of Birotteau’s belongings revealed their true value, suggesting Gamard’s claims were fraudulent.As the legal battle opened, Birotteau’s position grew precarious, town gossip fueling the conflict. Stakes were high and the outcome uncertain as he faced the formidable alliance of Gamard and Troubert determined to maintain their power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few days after hostilities began between Birotteau and the old maid, Baron de Listomere, who expected promotion to captain of a corvette, received a letter warning of possible retirement. Alarmed, he rushed to Paris to see the minister of the Navy, who laughed off his concerns. Further inquiries, however, produced a document confirming his impending retirement, pending the director's signature.Desperate, the baron sought help from his uncle, a deputy, who learned the minister believed the baron had sided with the Radicals in Tours and had offended the influential Abbe Troubert, the vicar-general. His uncle advised reconciling with Troubert, stressing the importance of good relations with the clergy for the baron’s career. Realizing the gravity, the baron resolved to avoid further conflict.Three days later he told his aunt their family was precarious because of their support of Birotteau. They consulted Monsieur de Bourbonne, who advised retreating gracefully from the dispute with Troubert: appease the vicar while distancing themselves from Birotteau, now a liability.The baroness approached Birotteau, asking him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and leave her house. Distressed, Birotteau agreed, believing himself a burden. He requested the return of a portrait of Chapeloud that belonged to him; the baroness promised to negotiate with Gamard.Soon Mademoiselle Gamard fell ill, and the town buzzed that Birotteau’s lawsuit had caused her distress. The baroness visited but could not see her, then sought an audience with Troubert, where they engaged in a duel of insinuations and civility, each trying to outmaneuver the other.The baroness presented Birotteau’s relinquishment document; Troubert affected indifference but was secretly pleased. He later returned with Gamard’s refusal to part with Chapeloud’s portrait, which the baroness accepted to preserve peace. Gamard died shortly after, leaving her fortune to Troubert, further complicating matters for the Listomeres.At Gamard’s funeral, Troubert delivered a grand eulogy that masked his true feelings. Birotteau, guilt-ridden over her death, mourned alone. The Listomeres struggled with the fallout and Troubert’s growing power.Five months later Troubert was appointed Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere died, leaving an annuity to Birotteau. Troubert continued to manipulate events to his advantage, ensuring Birotteau’s legacy would be contested.In the end Birotteau was exiled to a distant parish, ill and grieving. Troubert’s ascent, marked by cunning and manipulation, reflected broader struggles within society and the Church. The narrative closes on a meditation about power, individualism, and the moral complexities of human relationships, suggesting the pursuit of personal ambition often exacts a heavy cost from others.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy, etc.Dear Nodier, this book reveals actions concealed behind domestic doors, where supposed divine intervention often replaces human justice. Its moral concerns are family and motherhood. We may yet see the consequences of weakening paternal authority, historically the sole tribunal for domestic wrongs; a mother, however loving, cannot replace that patriarchal function. This story emphasizes the indissoluble marriage bond and the perils of unchecked selfishness. May a society built on wealth recognize the law's impotence in a system that exalts success at any cost, and may it return to Catholicism for moral uplift through a different education.In “Scenes from Military Life,” I will show noble self-sacrifice while exposing war’s corrupting influence on those who live domestically as if on a battlefield.You have observed our times keenly, and your reflections on political chaos have been insightful. I entrust this story to your esteemed name, which may shelter it from inevitable criticism.Your sincere admirer,De Balzac  Paris, November 1842.THE TWO BROTHERSCHAPTER IIn 1792 the townspeople of Issoudun disliked Dr. Rouget. Rumor held he made his beautiful wife unhappy, though she may have been somewhat foolish. Despite gossip, the household’s real life remained hidden. Rouget was ill-liked but treated civilly. His wife, a demoiselle Descoings, had been frail in youth; some said that explained Rouget’s choice. They had a son, Jean-Jacques, and, unexpectedly ten years later, a daughter, Agathe.These facts illuminate Rouget’s character: he believed men must show strength, a notion that caused much female suffering. The Descoings, his in-laws, were wealthy wool merchants who grew rich and miserly. Descoings, Rouget’s brother-in-law, left Issoudun for Paris, failed as a grocer, and was driven by love for a beautiful widow.By 1792 Descoings was thought prosperous; his parents had moved from wool into investments in forfeited estates. Expecting his wife’s death, Dr. Rouget sent Agathe to Paris to stay with Descoings, hoping she would win their favor. Agathe, the most beautiful girl in Issoudun, resembled her mother, which caused a rift between Rouget and his friend Monsieur Lousteau, who left town after their quarrel.Madame Rouget, learning of her daughter’s departure, predicted she would never see Agathe again—a sentiment that proved true. Her health then declined, worsened by her son’s neglect. Jean-Jacques, like his father, offered little comfort.Agathe’s arrival in Paris coincided with Descoings’ imprisonment after imprudent political remarks about the famine that angered powerful men. His wife’s attempts to free him failed; he was executed alongside the poet André Chénier. The episode made local grocers wary of politics for years and affected the community more than Chénier’s death at the time, though history later judged otherwise.After this, Agathe married Bridau, a ministry secretary, with Dr. Rouget’s approval; Rouget sought to profit from the Descoings inheritance. Bridau, captivated by Agathe’s beauty, married unaware of Rouget’s manipulations.Once the Descoings’ wealth passed on, Dr. Rouget descended into debauchery until his death in 1805, leaving Jean-Jacques, who remained a bachelor for reasons tied to his father’s influence. Agathe, resembling her mother, became a devoted wife and mother, yet her ignorance of the world made her vulnerable.Bridau, an ardent supporter of Napoleon, was promoted in 1804. He worked relentlessly, neglected his health, and in 1808 died from overwork just as a further promotion loomed—the Emperor had intended to reward him but was too late.After Bridau’s death no one in Issoudun wrote to Agathe. She lost her mother shortly before giving birth to her second son; her father’s death coincided with the Emperor’s coronation, which kept Bridau occupied. Her brother had not contacted her since she left home, and she gradually accepted the family’s indifference.Madame Hochon, Agathe’s godmother, warned that Agathe would inherit nothing unless she granted power of attorney to her brother. Agathe hesitated to burden him; Bridau, either unaware or indifferent, did not contest the arrangement. Their tacit acceptance left them financially exposed.Concerned for Agathe, Bridau had invested their remaining funds prudently to secure a modest income. After his death, Agathe and Madame Descoings pooled resources and lived on a combined income of twelve thousand francs a year. This practical arrangement concealed underlying strains.By 1809 Madame Descoings, now sixty-five, retained youthful looks and charm, enjoyed good food and the theater, and secretly gambled on lotteries. She also harbored a significant secret about her son, Bixiou, a major in the army, who had a son being educated alongside Agathe’s children.Agathe, devoted to her children, chose not to remarry, believing it her duty to be both mother and authority. Her lack of worldly understanding and blind trust in Madame Descoings, however, led to ruin. Madame Descoings, hoping to enrich the family through lotteries, racked up heavy debts and finally confessed.Agathe, without reproach, took charge: she sold possessions and liquidated investments to settle the debts, a decisive turning point in their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the bleakest spots in Paris was the stretch of rue Mazarin between rue Guenegard and rue de Seine, hemmed in by the high gray walls of the college and library given by Cardinal Mazarin. There, on the third floor of a damp, cold building, lived a poor widow, Madame Bridau. Opposite were the Institute buildings, where aspiring artists — the rapins — strove amid the tumult of exams to win prizes; that artistic center has since moved to the Palais des Beaux-Arts.From her windows Madame Bridau watched the students’ cages below. To brighten her rooms she risked police fines by setting boxes of earth outside the windows to grow little gardens. The house was shallow, reached by a winding staircase, and contained three rooms — a dining room, a small salon, and a bedroom — plus a kitchen and two single rooms. She had chosen it because it was affordable (four hundred francs a year), near her sons’ school, and in a neighborhood she knew.The interior matched the house’s general dreariness. The dining room, with yellow wallpaper and unglazed tile, held only essentials. The salon bore an Aubusson carpet given to her and a mahogany sofa that supported a pastel portrait of her late husband, Bridau. The portrait captured his nobility; above a card table hung a vivid painting of Napoleon.After her husband’s death Madame Bridau had taken to keeping birds: two large cages, one with canaries, the other with Java sparrows. Her room grew cluttered with cat hair, millet, and bits of chickweed — signs of grief and neglected self-care. She preserved everything belonging to Bridau, even wrapping his last pen with a note. Having lost him, she seemed to lose a sense of herself as a woman, concentrating entirely on her children.Agathe now lived for her sons and fretted over their prospects. Since moving to rue Mazarin a melancholy had settled on her face. She looked to the Emperor for support; he granted three hundred francs a year for each child, along with scholarships. Her aunt, Madame Descoings, on the second floor, had assigned three thousand francs from her annuity to help Agathe. The Descoings themselves lived modestly on twelve hundred francs a year, employed a woman for morning chores, and shared dinners with the two widows. Madame Descoings doted on Philippe and Joseph, indulging them with treats and small sums.Driven by maternal love and distrustful of the future, Madame Bridau kept expenses low, cutting small pleasures and saving three thousand francs from her pension to secure a modest inheritance for her children. Thus both widows moved from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty, each for different reasons.The view from her windows, the students’ tumult, and her husband’s portrait would shape her younger son, Joseph Bridau, into a notable painter of the modern French school.Philippe, the elder, resembled his mother in looks and carried a bold demeanour often mistaken for courage. Agathe believed physical likeness signaled moral likeness and hoped he would inherit her sensitivity. At fifteen Philippe’s charm reinforced those hopes. Joseph, three years younger, took after his father in less praise-worthy ways: untidy, frequently scolded, and plainly less favored by his mother.Joseph’s artistic bent began during a visit to the Tuileries, where a caricature drawn on an Institute wall enthralled him. This sparked his desire to draw and led him to sneak into the Institute courtyard, where older students at first teased him. His persistence won their respect, and they encouraged him toward art.He began visiting the atelier of the sculptor Chaudet, who recognized and fostered his talent. Agathe feared for Joseph’s future and tried to dissuade him, convinced an artistic life meant hardship. Chaudet defended the vocation passionately, insisting true talent is a calling.As Joseph pursued art, Agathe’s anxiety increased when Philippe expressed a wish to join the military. Enchanted by soldiering, Philippe secretly petitioned the Emperor to enlist. He was soon sent to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr and rose rapidly, becoming a captain by nineteen.Meanwhile Joseph’s scholarship was withdrawn and he sought to earn a living through art, encouraged by Madame Descoings. Despite Agathe’s concerns about his health and security, Joseph thrived in the artistic milieu, forming close bonds with fellow students and mentors.As politics shifted, Philippe returned home decorated while Joseph faced uncertainty. Agathe’s preference for Philippe became more evident as she admired his military achievements and sacrifices for the Emperor. Joseph, less celebrated, remained devoted to his mother, who struggled to grasp his artistic aims.Despite their divergent paths, both brothers navigated the challenges of their careers, with Agathe’s love and persistent concern for their futures shaping their choices and fortunes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1816 Joseph won his mother’s consent to convert the adjoining garret into an atelier, partly funded by Madame Descoings. The two widows treated painting as a trade, but Joseph embraced it passionately, installing a skylight and chocolate-colored walls while continuing to study under Gros and Schinner.Political unrest simmered in France: conspiracies and riots surrounded the Chamber of Deputies. Philippe, implicated in plots, was arrested and released for lack of evidence, but his military pay was cut and Agathe feared for him. The widows’ friends urged Agathe to send Philippe abroad for safety; she resisted the idea of him serving a foreign power.Ultimately Philippe joined General Lallemand in the United States to help found the Champ d’Asile, an enterprise that failed. Agathe equipped him with ten thousand francs, and by late 1817 she had adjusted to reduced means. Joseph lived frugally to support the family while his artistic talent developed.In 1818 Roguin, the notary, absconded with funds meant for Madame Descoings. Soon after, Philippe sent a bill for a thousand francs, having lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. Agathe, distraught, blamed herself for urging his departure. Despite their own difficulties, Madame Descoings continued to support her.Desperate for funds, Joseph turned to Gerard, who arranged for him to copy a portrait of Louis XVIII. Joseph worked tirelessly, selling panels to cover Philippe’s debt. Philippe then returned from America; Agathe welcomed him with open arms, not perceiving how much he had changed.Time abroad had hardened him. He became self-centered, indulged in drinking and gambling, and adopted a swaggering arrogance. Agathe, blinded by maternal love, excused his faults, viewing him as a victim of circumstance. The family’s finances deteriorated, and friends discussed selling possessions to help Philippe.Philippe’s conduct alarmed acquaintances: he rejected serving the Bourbons, which hampered employment prospects, yet he lived as if supported by others. He frequently disappeared for days, sustained a lavish lifestyle, and grew distant from family responsibilities. He favored cafes and gambling halls, deepened friendships with former comrades, and showed increasing disregard for others.Still, Philippe sought to impress Mariette, a young dancer. Giroudeau encouraged the pursuit and promised to help Philippe enter journalism, advising him to exploit his experiences. Philippe’s ambitions clashed with reality; he struggled to adapt to civilian life and grew more hostile to the Liberal party, blaming it for his misfortunes and nursing a desire for revenge against those he felt had wronged him.When a potential job materialized, Agathe and Madame Descoings breathed easier and agreed to secure Philippe’s position, overlooking the sacrifices already made. The role—as cashier at a newspaper—brought temporary relief but did little to reform him. His arrogance continued to grow, and Joseph felt increasingly overshadowed and unappreciated despite his sacrifices and rising talent.By the end, Philippe’s return and changed behavior had strained relationships and exposed fault lines in loyalty, ambition, and judgment. The once close-knit household faced tests that revealed the consequences of choices in pursuit of dreams, the limits of maternal devotion, and the fragile balance between personal aspiration and familial duty.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Three months later the colonel, who ate enough for four, still criticized the food while the widows, reluctant to press him for payment, paid lavishly for meals he did not help fund. His mother and Madame Descoings hesitated to remind him of his promise. A year passed without any of the “tigers with five claws” Leon Gozlan mentioned moving from Philippe’s pocket into the household purse. The colonel rarely dined at home. “Well, he is happy,” his mother remarked, “he has a place.”Mariette, influenced by a feuilleton edited by Vernou, made her debut at the Porte-Saint-Martin, triumphing with the famous Begrand. A wealthy general, enamored with an actress and acquainted with Philippe and Giroudeau, became her impresario and arranged her first engagement—an example of the solidarity among those captivated by folly.Bixiou soon revealed to his grandmother and Agathe that Philippe, the cashier, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. This shocked the widows; Agathe’s piety made her view all stage women as morally corrupt, and both believed such women lived extravagantly.“Do you think my brother is foolish enough to spend money on Mariette? Such women ruin rich men,” Joseph objected. Bixiou reassured them that Mariette might be engaged by the Opera and that diplomatic visitors to the Porte-Saint-Martin could soon take her from Philippe; he even heard an ambassador was madly in love with her.Philippe, partly motivated by securing her financially, proposed marriage, but Mariette—anticipating an engagement at the Grand Opera—declined, perhaps sensing his motives or valuing independence. For the rest of the year Philippe visited his mother only twice a month, spending his time at the office, the theatre, or with Mariette. He enjoyed himself with entertainers like Tullia and others.This carnival lasted eighteen months but brought trouble. After her Opera debut in January 1821 Mariette quickly attracted a distinguished duke. Philippe, driven by passion, began dipping into the funds he managed, taking eleven hundred francs by May. Mariette then left for London to seek lords who might support her while the temporary opera house was prepared. Despite her infidelities, Philippe remained infatuated; she saw him as a stepping stone.Forced to let Mariette go, Philippe returned to his mother’s apartment, unable to live without the pleasures of the past year. Suicide crossed his mind—not because of the deficit alone but because he could not endure life without Mariette. He entered Joseph’s studio, where Joseph was painting, and began a fragile conversation. When Joseph explained his work and earnings, Philippe, pale and subdued, asked how long it would take to paint his portrait—revealing he wished to leave his mother a likeness. When Joseph asked if he meant to go away again, Philippe replied, “I am going never to return.”Pressed by Joseph, Philippe confessed he had taken money from the funds and could not live with the dishonor. Joseph reassured him he would not be disgraced if he repaid the sum, but Philippe, hopeless, rushed downstairs, leaving Joseph anxious.Joseph informed Madame Descoings, who was horrified. Agathe, learning the truth, was devastated, fearing for Philippe’s life and the family’s honor. “He! to fail in honor! the son of Bridau to take the money that was trusted to him!” she cried. Madame Descoings urged hope, attributing Philippe’s ruin to the influence of a bad woman; Agathe, however, lived in terror for her son.That day Philippe calmly submitted his accounts, inventing that he had merely retained the money for safekeeping. He then went to a gambling house, where he first won but ultimately lost everything. Returning home he found concern and affection from his family, which comforted him.“Promise not to kill yourself, and all shall be forgotten,” his mother pleaded. Philippe, realizing the appeal had worked, feigned gratitude and affection, embracing her. They shared dinner, and Philippe, despite earlier despair, felt relieved.The next day Agathe appeared older and more anxious, aware their financial situation was dire. Madame Descoings, mindful of the family’s reputation, suggested consulting Desroches, who could manage the affair. Agathe agreed, hoping to protect Philippe’s name.Desroches, acting in his diplomatic capacity, advised Agathe to sign the transfer of funds and petition for Philippe’s reinstatement in the army. He assured them Philippe would soon regain his rank, relieving some worry. Still, Agathe’s faith was shaken and she lived in constant fear of his potential downfall.Philippe resumed a life of leisure—gambling, drinking, and appearing disheveled—indifferent to his family’s plight. His passion for Mariette had hardened him; he treated women with contempt. Meanwhile Joseph devoted himself to painting, gaining recognition and support from Madame Descoings, who believed in his future success.As Madame Descoings pursued her lottery dreams she remained oblivious to growing tensions. Joseph, noticing discrepancies in his finances, suspected Philippe and confided in Madame Descoings, who shared his concerns. They planned to catch Philippe in the act, hoping to confront him.When Joseph discovered Philippe had indeed taken money from him, he felt deeply betrayed. Despite anger, he still recognized Philippe as his brother and struggled with confronting him. Madame Descoings urged sympathy for Philippe’s struggles, but Joseph remained wary of his brother’s influence on the family’s future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite Philippe's careful approach to his nightly gambling, he sometimes found himself "cleaned out." Needing his evening stake of ten francs, he began stealing from his family—taking from his brother and whatever Madame Descoings or Agathe left about. After a distressing dream in which Philippe robbed her, Agathe realized the depth of his decline. She begged him to ask for money instead of stealing, but within two weeks her savings were gone and she considered work. She took worsted work for a meager twenty sous a day. The strain of her hidden anguish showed in her appearance; she spent sleepless nights waiting for Philippe, noting his drunkenness and mood swings tied to gambling.Madame Descoings suggested Philippe get new clothes; he retorted that his mother had no money and that he had mortgaged his pension. He had borrowed from Giroudeau, calling it a "debt of honor." Agathe defended him, believing he was out of his element, while Joseph criticized his neglect of appearance and duty. Philippe's gambling worsened and he became more debauched, losing his former good looks.One day Philippe asked Joseph for money to pay a debt to the tobacconist; Joseph refused. Philippe then schemed to find where Madame Descoings kept her savings, guessing she would be out buying Christmas treats. He broke into her room and discovered twenty napoleons hidden in the mattress. He took the money, intending to gamble it.At the gambling house Philippe won substantial sums over several sessions. By the end of the night he had amassed over one hundred fifty thousand francs. Intoxicated by success, he pressed on and lost everything. The next morning he woke in despair, having ruined his family.Agathe and Madame Descoings waited for Philippe to return for Christmas dinner. When he failed to show, they worried. Madame Descoings had pinned hopes on her lottery stake, but when she checked her savings she found the money gone and fainted. Agathe, horrified, sought confirmation from the concierge, who revealed Philippe had been in the apartment that day.Agathe returned pale and shaken; the family confronted Philippe. He dismissed their accusations, claiming he had taken what was owed him. The confrontation escalated into a physical altercation between Joseph and Philippe. As tensions peaked, Madame Descoings suffered a stroke and the family rushed to her side.Afterward Joseph tried to help his mother with money, but she refused, clinging to the lottery hope. When the winning numbers were announced it became clear she had missed her chance, and she declined further. She died shortly after, leaving Agathe and Joseph to deal with the aftermath of Philippe's betrayal.As Philippe recovered from his own illness, Agathe told him their financial situation had drastically changed and urged him to rejoin the army and support himself. He responded with indifference, feeling abandoned. Despite her tears she offered him money; he took it and left, seeking the company of those he called true friends. Agathe, heartbroken, prayed for her son and resigned herself to the loss of the child she once loved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By February 1822, Madame Bridau had moved into the attic above the kitchen of her former apartment, recently occupied by her son Philippe. Seeing his mother’s reduced circumstances, Joseph resolved to make her comfortable: he rearranged the garret, added tasteful decor and a double door, and took Agathe to dine daily at a table-d’hôte that cost him nearly a hundred francs a month. Alarmed by the expense, Agathe, with her confessor’s help, accepted a seven-hundred-franc-a-year post at a lottery office that demanded long hours.Agathe adapted to the routine, preparing Joseph’s breakfast and managing his household despite her lack of art knowledge. She mended his clothes, kept the studio tidy, and aided his work; their bond deepened through small acts of tenderness. They discussed Philippe, whose reckless life worried Agathe, but Joseph dismissed her fears, convinced Philippe was prospering in New York.One afternoon Joseph visited Philippe, who worked at a newspaper. Philippe invited Joseph to dine, but Joseph declined to prioritize his mother. When he later told Agathe about Philippe, she felt renewed hope. She decorated Joseph’s studio and prepared a special breakfast for Philippe’s portrait sitting. During the session she hid, overwhelmed as she watched the two sons together.After posing, Philippe invited them to dinner, which made Joseph uneasy because of Philippe’s apparent wealth; Agathe eagerly accepted. The lavish meal heightened Joseph’s suspicions about Philippe’s finances. At the next sitting, Agathe learned more about Philippe’s social circle—journalists and actresses—and heard his boastful ambitions, though he remained evasive about money.Joseph had to postpone another sitting because of a pressing commission. When Philippe visited the studio he mistakenly took a valuable copy of a Rubens, believing it the original, and sold it for a fraction of its worth. Discovering the theft devastated Joseph; Agathe was heartbroken, feeling she had lost her son to vice.Two months later an old officer told Agathe of Philippe’s dire condition: ill and needing money for care. Working at the lottery office, she had little to spare and refused to help the son she had banished from her heart. Joseph agreed, insisting Philippe should go to the hospital.Some time after, Agathe encountered Philippe on the street—destitute and recently discharged from the hospital. Maternal instinct overcame her; she gave him money despite knowing it would likely be squandered, then fell into despair over his future.Agathe later learned Philippe had been involved in a conspiracy that led to his arrest; she fainted when she heard his name. Joseph consulted Desroches, who advised them to secure the family inheritance. Desperate, Agathe wrote to her godmother for help but received a discouraging reply about her brother’s unwillingness to assist.Desroches urged quick action to protect the children’s rights, suggesting they gather evidence of undue influence over Philippe and announcing plans to visit Issoudun during the holidays to help. Agathe and Joseph set off for Issoudun, leaving Philippe to face the consequences. As they traveled, Agathe reflected on the changes in Issoudun and the long estrangement from her brother, determined to confront the situation on her return.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Issoudun, one of France's oldest cities, is noted by Caesar and Rigord for its population and commerce. Recent excavations by Monsieur Armand Peremet uncovered a fifth-century basilica, likely unique in France, built from stones of a Roman temple to Isis. The town’s name—derived from "Isis" with "dun" indicating a religious and military settlement—points to ancient Celtic origins.In the Middle Ages Issoudun was a center of power: Richard Coeur-de-lion built a tower above the basilica as part of fortifications, and the town later served as a stronghold for Routiers and Cottereaux mercenaries sent by Henry II against his son Richard. Yet its prominence has faded. After the 1830 insurrection vine-growers resisted government intervention, burned tax offices, and asserted autonomy, showing the force of a community of about eight thousand vine-growers.In the fourteenth century Issoudun housed sixteen to seventeen thousand people, prospering on the wool trade and producing blankets and gloves. By Louis XIV’s time it enjoyed some elegance and social standing. Today the population has shrunk to about twelve thousand, including vine-growers from nearby suburbs. The local economy, especially wine, suffers from outdated practices; vine-growers oppose modernization, producing poor-quality wine that undermines potential prosperity.This decline stems from a pervasive spirit of stagnation. When a proposal arose for a new road linking Paris and Toulouse through Issoudun, local leaders opposed it, fearing higher food prices. Their resistance exemplifies a broader provincial tendency to favor tradition over progress.Issoudun’s social fabric has also deteriorated. The disappearance of the nobility after historical upheavals removed a source of social competition and dynamism. The bourgeoisie, preserving routine social forms, have grown complacent, contributing to stagnation in governance and community life. Administration is dominated by intertwined local figures whose familial ties produce ineffectual leadership and weak accountability. The clergy exert little influence, further diminishing civic vitality.Against this background the town’s youth, feeling trapped, formed the "Knights of Idleness," led by Maxence Gilet. Max, rumored to be the illegitimate son of a local official, was charismatic and adventurous. Raised in poverty and directionless, he enlisted in the military and distinguished himself in campaigns. Returning to Issoudun after the war, he clashed with the bourgeoisie, whose disapproval of his lifestyle and pranks only boosted his notoriety.Max became a local hero after a violent confrontation with royalist officers, and his leadership of the Knights invigorated the town with mischief. The group staged pranks that both entertained and alarmed townspeople, executing elaborate hoaxes and frightful stunts that revealed their creativity and camaraderie. While disruptive, these antics brought excitement to an otherwise stagnant community.The local tavern, run by Père and La Cognette, served as the Knights' secret meeting place where they planned escapades and shared meals. The couple, unaware of the full extent of the Knights’ activities, supported them out of self-interest, ensuring their silence and cooperation.Issoudun’s ancient grandeur and archaeological significance contrast sharply with its modern decline—marked by economic backwardness, social complacency, and weak institutions. The Knights of Idleness, under the magnetic Maxence Gilet, embody the youth’s frustration and thirst for excitement in a town that has lost much of its former vibrancy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Joseph and Madame Bridau traveled from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness played a clever prank. An old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war, left his empty cart at the foot of the Issoudun tower one morning. Maxence, arriving at a Knights' rendezvous, was asked, “What are we to do tonight?”“Here’s Père Fario’s cart,” he replied. “Let’s get it up on the embankment first, and then decide.”The tower of Issoudun, built by Richard Coeur-de-Lion, stands on the ruins of a basilica, which itself rests above a Roman temple and a Celtic dun — a mound rich with layers of history. Its pedestal towers eighty feet above the town. In an hour the cart was disassembled and hoisted piece by piece to the top of the embankment, like soldiers moving artillery over the Grand Saint-Bernard. Once reassembled, the Knights, now hungry and thirsty, returned to Mère Cognette’s to await the old Spaniard’s astonishment when he came for his cart the next morning.They did not prank people nightly; sometimes members refused for personal reasons. But they gathered regularly for daytime hunts, autumn vintages, and winter skating. Among the twenty youths some were especially close to Max, who had become their idol. The grandsons of Madame Hochon, François Hochon and Baruch Borniche, regarded Max as a cousin and relied on him for money and forbidden adventures.That night François and Baruch sat near Max at a dimly lit table after twelve to fifteen bottles of wine. Emboldened, Baruch warned Max, “You are threatened in your stronghold.”“What do you mean?” Max asked.“My grandmother received a letter from Madame Bridau, her goddaughter, saying she and her son are coming here. She’s preparing two rooms for them.”“What’s that to me?” Max replied, drinking.Max, thirty-four, had a striking, soldierly appearance: a sharply cut face, clear skin, expressive eyes, slightly curly black hair, and a winning smile. He was well proportioned and might have been a general.“Don’t you know, Max,” called a young man from the other end of the table, “that Madame Hochon’s goddaughter is Rouget’s sister? If she’s coming with her son, she likely wants to secure the property when he dies, and then—goodbye to your harvest!”Max frowned, assessing the group. “What’s that to me?” he repeated.“But,” François urged, “if old Rouget revokes his will—”Max cut him off. “I’ve silenced those who meddled with you, François. Is that how you repay me? By calling a woman I care about a contemptuous name?”Max had never before spoken so openly about Flore Brazier, Rouget’s servant-mistress, whom society disparaged. He had always avoided discussing her, knowing it was sensitive. His friends had long learned to tread carefully.François hesitated; Max encouraged him. “Go on.”“Faith, no!” François replied.“Don’t be angry, Max,” said Goddet. “Didn’t we agree to speak freely here? If François slipped up, is that a crime against the Order of Idleness?”“No,” Max conceded, “but it is against our friendship. I remembered we were in session, which is why I said, ‘Go on.’”An awkward silence followed. Max then declared, “I’ll continue for him—and for all of you—and tell you what you’re thinking. You believe Flore, the Rabouilleuse, has been providing for me since I returned to Issoudun. If I can throw away three hundred francs a month and treat you to dinners, you think it comes from her purse? Yes, ten thousand times yes! Flore aims for Rouget’s property.”“She gets it from father to son,” Goddet remarked.“You think I plan to marry Flore when Rouget dies, and that the arrival of these Parisians threatens my future?” Max continued.“That’s it,” François affirmed.“Don’t worry, friends,” Max reassured them. “If I need help to deal with these Parisians, will you assist me? Within the limits of our fooleries, of course. I’m not asking you to kill or poison them. If the Bridaus succeed and Flore has nothing but what she stands in, I’d be satisfied. I love her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet, if she would have me.”Mademoiselle Fichet was the wealthiest heiress in Issoudun, courted by Goddet. The Knights, moved by Max’s frankness, rose in unison.“You’re a fine fellow, Max!”“We’ll stand by you!”“A fig for the Bridaus!”“After all, it’s only three swains to a shepherdess.”“Père Lousteau loved Madame Rouget; isn’t it better to love a housekeeper who isn’t tied down?”“If Rouget is Max’s father, the affair is in the family.”“Liberty of opinion nowadays!”“Hurrah for Max!”“Down with all hypocrites!”“Here’s to the beautiful Flore!”Their cheers reflected the Knights’ easy morality. Max’s leadership furthered his interests, creating a support network to secure his position. He raised his glass of claret.“As a mark of the ill-will I bear you, I wish you all a mistress as fine as Flore! As for this influx of relatives, I feel no immediate unease; we’ll see what the future holds—”“Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”“That’s safe enough!” Goddet replied.“I’ll handle that,” Max assured them. “Be in the market early, and let me know when the old fellow comes for his cart.”At half-past three the Knights slipped away quietly to their homes, moving along the walls in soft shoes. Max returned to the place Saint-Jean, concealing his unease. His time in Cabrera had taught him deep dissimulation. The forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s property underpinned his affection for Flore. Despite outward confidence, the news of legitimate heirs unsettled him. Rouget’s accumulated savings remained in his name, and even if Flore claimed a will in her favor, those funds could be secured under her name.“That fool of a girl never mentioned her sister and nephews in all these seven years!” Max exclaimed as he turned from the rue de la Marmouse into the rue l’Avenier. “Seven hundred and fifty thousand francs with various notaries can’t be liquidated quickly without everyone knowing in this gossiping town! The priority is to get rid of these relatives; once they’re gone, we can secure the property. I need to think this through.”Exhausted, Max entered Père Rouget’s house with a pass-key and went to bed, telling himself, “Tomorrow, my thoughts will be clear.”Now it’s essential to recount how Flore Brazier acquired the nickname “Rabouilleuse” and became the quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s household.As Doctor Rouget aged, he recognized his son’s ineptitude and treated him harshly, trying to impose a routine. This inadvertently prepared Jean-Jacques to submit easily to the first authority he found.One day in September 1799, returning from his rounds, the doctor encountered a captivating little girl by a brook. She emerged from the water, a vision of rustic beauty, with a tattered petticoat and a makeshift hat. The child, Flore, was catching crabs for her uncle Brazier.“Where do you come from?” the doctor asked.“I belong in Vatan,” she replied.“Why are you here?” he continued.“I’m catching crabs for my uncle.”“Has your uncle permission to fish here?” the doctor inquired.“Hey! Aren’t we all under a Republic that is one and indivisible?” the uncle called from the brook.Recognizing the child’s striking looks, the doctor offered to take her in, promising a better life. Despite the uncle’s reluctance, he agreed to leave Flore with the doctor, who would pay her wages.Flore’s life changed as she moved into the doctor’s home, where she was well-fed and educated. She wore fine clothes and received gifts to encourage her studies. Yet her peasant upbringing bred a deep aversion to books; the doctor eventually halted her schooling. The townspeople speculated about the nature of the doctor’s intentions toward Flore, interpreting his care in various ways. Gossip swirled about the propriety of his attention given his age.For five years the doctor enjoyed the pleasures of educating Flore without complexities. Flore, born in 1787, grew up amid the late 18th-century upheavals that had loosened traditional morals. By 1802 the Catholic Church was only beginning to reestablish itself, and social norms had shifted. Flore was envied by local girls for her privileged life despite the moral implications. Raised during upheaval, she viewed her circumstances through self-interest rather than virtue. The doctor’s affection for her became fodder for gossip that often obscured truth.As Rouget neared death, he was urged by his notary to make provisions for Flore. His cynical response reflected a man who harbored resentment and sought to take revenge on the innocent object of his unfulfilled desires. His refusal to leave anything to Flore underscored his bitterness, suggesting he believed her beauty would suffice for her future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son unhappy, especially after Jean-Jacques came of age in 1791, but he provided Flore with the material comforts that made country life bearable. After the funeral, when Fanchette asked Flore what would become of her now that Monsieur was dead, Jean-Jacques’s demeanor changed; for the first time his dull face showed animation.“Leave the room,” he instructed Fanchette, who was clearing the table.At seventeen, Flore still had the beauty that had attracted the doctor, though she was beginning to show the plumpness common among countrywomen. Jean-Jacques, feeling an urge to speak, hesitated, recalling his father’s recent death and his benefactions. Flore, unable to understand his silence, left him without knowing what to think.At his father’s death Jean-Jacques was thirty-seven, timid and submissive, resembling a child. His timidity sprang from a torpor of the faculties that a strong influence might have roused. He had a passionate nature, but lack of confidence kept him from courting any woman in Issoudun. His awkwardness and unattractive features made him feel isolated, and he sought solitude where he felt at ease.The doctor recognized too late the damage his son’s temperament had caused. Wishing to marry Jean-Jacques but fearing loss of control over his property, he searched for a suitable daughter-in-law and tried to instill avarice in his son to give him some financial instinct.Despite his father’s efforts, Jean-Jacques was secretly in love with Flore, the only woman he could comfortably observe. He delighted in the education his father provided her, believing it would make her easier to win. Flore, unaware of his feelings, waited for him to express himself, but he remained silent.Three weeks passed in this uncertain situation. One night Flore awoke to find Jean-Jacques crouched outside her door, which led her to believe he loved her. The next day she noticed a change in her feelings toward him, finding him less unattractive.Their conversations continued, with Jean-Jacques asking if she wanted to return to the fields. Flore, curious about his intentions, assured him of her honesty. These exchanges revealed Jean-Jacques’s growing affection, though he struggled to articulate his feelings.After ten months Jean-Jacques transformed; his demeanor brightened and he became more attentive to his appearance, much to Flore’s satisfaction. The townspeople noticed and speculated about their relationship and Flore’s intentions.Fanchette, the cook, was the only one who disapproved of Flore’s position in the household. She eventually left, and Flore, anticipating this, had learned to cook and manage the house. Flore’s culinary skills surpassed those of the former cook, and she took pride in keeping a clean, orderly home.During the nine years that followed, Flore gained complete control over Jean-Jacques. She treated him with an easy familiarity and a calculated respect, gradually becoming the dominant figure in their relationship. Jean-Jacques, childlike in his dependence, relied on her for everything, and she indulged his whims while shaping their life.By 1815 Flore had reached the peak of her beauty, resembling a buxom housewife. In 1816 she fell in love with Maxence Gilet, a penniless officer. Her newfound passion altered her relationship with Jean-Jacques, who noticed her changing demeanor.Flore’s anger and frustration with Jean-Jacques grew as she felt he neglected her needs. She confronted him about his wealth and the empty rooms in their home, suggesting he invite Max to live with them. Jean-Jacques, fearing the loss of Flore, reluctantly agreed.After Max moved in, Jean-Jacques found comfort in his company; Max provided companionship and the conversation Jean-Jacques had lacked. Flore, however, began to distance herself from household duties, claiming they spoiled her hands. A new cook was employed, and Flore adopted a more elegant appearance to impress Max.As their household expanded, Flore’s ambitions grew. She bought a horse for Max and welcomed a servant to assist him. The household dynamics shifted: Flore and Max became the central figures while Jean-Jacques receded into the background, increasingly dependent on them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim her threatened inheritance, Jean-Jacques Rouget had become almost semi-vegetative. After Max’s financial aid, Flore improved Rouget’s living conditions and fed him from Vedie’s kitchen. Despite better food, he weakened physically; utterly submissive, his devotion to Flore was his sole motive, making him seem older than his fifty-seven years.Max’s apartment showed his devotion: provincial comforts, colored tiles, elegant wallpapers and mahogany furniture. A rush matting on the stairs muffled footsteps so Max could come home late without waking anyone — Rouget included, who remained ignorant of Max’s nocturnal activities.One morning Flore, in a cotton gown, found Max asleep and noticed his late return. He told her a serious matter: Rouget’s family was coming to Issoudun, likely to evict them. Flore vowed confrontation; Max urged caution, asking for coffee and suggesting they plan later. As Flore began making coffee, Baruch announced that Fario was searching for his cart.Max joined the crowd. Fario, a small peculiar man, claimed his cart had been stolen. Max led him to the tower embankment where the cart was inexplicably found at the top. Amid the crowd’s laughter, Max pretended to assist, theatrically tossing the cart down to Fario’s dismay.The incident became Issoudun gossip: Fario’s anger and Max’s antics drew attention. Max grasped the implications and felt the need to manage the fallout, especially with the Bridaus’ arrival.François informed Max that the Bridaus would arrive that night. Max decided they should not appear hostile; he proposed letting the Hochons drive them out instead. Meanwhile Flore, unable to contain her anger, punished Rouget by withdrawing her affection, leaving him confused and distressed.Flore’s harshness struck Rouget hard; he depended on her emotionally. He asked what he had done, while Vedie urged him to placate Flore. Flore’s fury sprang from learning Rouget’s sister and nephew were coming, whom she saw as threats.Rouget, bewildered, denied inviting them and blamed Madame Hochon for the visit. Max arrived and argued Rouget should welcome his kin, since public perception mattered. Flore, mollified, prepared breakfast. Rouget thanked Max, who counseled him to keep his will while being polite to his sister.After breakfast Max and Flore discussed finances and plans to secure Rouget’s wealth while handling the incoming family. Flore admired Max’s cleverness; they plotted so the Hochons would help deal with the Bridaus.The Hochon household, opposite Rouget’s, was similarly modest. Monsieur Hochon, a retired tax collector, was famed for extreme frugality. His wife, Madame Hochon, resolved to properly receive her goddaughter Agathe and prepared the house despite her husband’s reluctance.Madame Hochon cleaned and borrowed furniture, hoping to shield Agathe from Max and Flore’s influence. She prayed for Rouget’s awareness and Agathe’s success. Her granddaughter Adolphine secretly hoped to win Joseph Bridau’s affection.News of the Bridaus’ arrival split Issoudun into factions: the older bourgeoisie, who offered measured support from a distance, and the Knights of Idleness, more vehemently loyal to Max. The tension around the Bridaus foreshadowed a clash of interests in the small town.Throughout, Max maneuvered socially and politically to protect Flore and Rouget’s position, exploiting public spectacle and allies. Flore used affection and withdrawal to control Rouget; the Hochons prepared to receive the intruding relatives as a counterweight. The Fario incident and the town’s divided sentiment underscored how fragile reputations and power were in Issoudun, and how a few calculated actions could shape the looming confrontation over inheritance and influence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales in Issoudun at three o’clock. Though tired, Madame Bridau felt revived by familiar sights and memories of youth. News of their arrival spread quickly; Madame Hochon greeted Agathe warmly, treating her like a daughter after years of loss and monotony had made her cling to that affection.Agathe met Monsieur Hochon, as uninviting as ever, and the rest of the family: the young men Baruch and François, and shy little Adolphine, who noticed Joseph’s presence. The miserly Hochon observed Joseph’s recent hospital stay and apparent hunger; the youths were intrigued by his looks. “This is my son, the painter; my good Joseph,” Agathe said with maternal pride. Joseph, blunt as usual, remarked on his likeness to his father, which Madame Hochon took as a sign of his affection for Agathe. Baruch hurried off to spread the news, concentrating on Joseph’s striking appearance.Townspeople soon gossiped, likening Joseph to a brigand and airing their disdain for artists. Their curiosity about the Parisians grew; many planned to visit the Hochons that evening to see them for themselves.Settled into modest attic rooms, Joseph contrasted his artistic life in Paris with the drab provincial existence. Dinner revealed the Hochons’ frugality: plain bouilli, hard-boiled eggs, and meager sides. Accustomed to better fare, Joseph found the meal disappointing but tried to adapt.Agathe learned her childhood home had been sold to the Borniches, and Madame Hochon braced for many visitors that night. Joseph observed the family’s strict ways and the limits of their hospitality.After dinner the women gathered in the salon. Agathe and Madame Hochon exchanged life stories: Agathe spoke of her struggles and Madame Hochon offered hard-won wisdom about enduring trials. Their conversation was interrupted by a stream of callers eager to meet the Parisians. Joseph remained detached, studying the visitors with a critical eye that made them uneasy. Later, as the house quieted, Agathe and Madame Hochon resumed their heartfelt exchange, reflecting on the burdens they carried.Elsewhere in town, the Knights of Idleness convened to plot mischief against the Parisians. Max, their leader, proposed a prank involving rats to sabotage the grain supply of a disliked merchant, Fario. The gang welcomed the scheme and sought a way to drive the Bridaus away without harming the Hochons.The next morning, at breakfast, Joseph glimpsed his brother Jean-Jacques Rouget, unrecognizable and disheveled, accompanied by Flore Brazier, a striking and conspicuous woman. Joseph’s artistic eye admired Flore’s beauty, but Agathe feared her influence over Jean-Jacques. The family debated how to approach him; Joseph suggested he visit first to assess matters.During breakfast a letter arrived from Jean-Jacques inviting Joseph to dine with him. The Hochons received the invitation with mixed feelings, suspecting Flore’s involvement and worrying over motives. They discussed strategies for handling the tangled family dynamics.Gritte, the servant, later reported the Rouget household’s ostentation and Flore’s extravagant display. The Hochons realized the need to dress suitably for the occasion, and Joseph prepared to meet his uncle, hoping to unearth the truth about the family’s affairs.Monsieur Hochon advised Joseph to be observant and to speak frankly with Jean-Jacques about his nephews, stressing the importance of understanding the situations with Max and Flore. With tensions simmering and rival schemes in motion, the stage was set for a complex family reunion that promised confrontation and revelations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At four o’clock Joseph crossed from the Rouget to the Hochon house, where Kouski announced him. The table was set in the large hall; Joseph greeted his uncle, Flore, and Max.“We haven’t seen each other since I was born, dear uncle,” Joseph said cheerfully.“You are very welcome,” Rouget replied, looking at him dully.“Madame,” Joseph told Flore, “I envied my uncle the pleasure of admiring you every day.”“Isn’t she beautiful?” Rouget remarked.“Beautiful enough to be a model for a great painter,” Joseph replied.Prompted by Flore, Rouget introduced Maxence Gilet, who had served in the Imperial Guard like Joseph’s brother. Joseph noted Max’s handsome features and thought the household formed a triangle: Rouget dull, Flore compensating, Max the attractive outsider.Baruch and Francois entered. Flore asked Joseph if he had seen the tower of Issoudun; when he had not she suggested a walk before dinner. While she prepared, Joseph eagerly examined the pictures in the house. Rouget said they came from the Descoings, who had bought them during the Revolution. Joseph admired them openly.“There are more upstairs,” Max said, and took Joseph to see them. Back downstairs ecstatic, Joseph heard Flore whisper to Rouget, urging him to give the paintings to Joseph since he “didn’t care for them.”“It seems you are a painter,” Rouget said.“Only a ‘rapin,’” Joseph replied.“If these pictures can help you, I give them to you, but without the frames,” Rouget said.“Uncle, I’ll make you copies!” Joseph exclaimed.Flore suggested paying Joseph four thousand francs for the copies; Rouget agreed. Overwhelmed, Joseph accepted.As they left, Joseph felt triumphant; the news spread through Issoudun, enhancing Flore’s status. Dinner that night was splendid, attended by local dignitaries. Joseph, somewhat tipsy, returned home at eleven, while Rouget was carried to bed, having indulged heavily.Max, alone with Flore, observed it was better to befriend the Bridaus than antagonize them. They planned to send the pictures to Joseph the next day.The following morning Joseph awoke late to find the pictures leaning against the wall. His mother, Agathe, visited Rouget to thank him, encouraged by old Hochon, who warned her, “Your adversaries are cunning. Joseph has been fooled.” Hochon advised Agathe to cultivate a relationship with Flore to gain access to Rouget. She was warmly received by her brother, who was ill from the previous night’s excesses. Flore feigned servitude; Agathe expressed gratitude. “Your brother should marry her,” Agathe suggested, but Flore insisted Rouget would remember her in his will.In the next days the Knights of Idleness captured rats and mice to release in Fario’s grain store, causing havoc. Agathe believed Rouget had not made a will and intended to ask him about his intentions regarding Flore. Meanwhile the Knights sought ways to drive the Parisians away.After a week the Bridaus had made little progress. Hochon urged Agathe to stay longer, insisting religious influence was needed to counter Max and Flore’s hold over Rouget.
