Dinah, maintaining composure, grappled with complexities, balancing roles as mother, Countess, and a woman shaped by her experiences.The narrative ends with unresolved tension: Dinah inhabits a dual existence, torn between love and ambition, public success and private longing, reflecting the intricate interplay of passion, social pressure, and personal sacrifice that defines her journey.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Eugene-Auguste-Georges-Louis Midy de la Greneraye Surville, Royal Engineer of the Ponts et Chausses. As a testimony to the affection of his brother-in-law, De BalzacCHAPTER I. ONE OF MANY CHEVALIERS DE VALOISAcross France there were many Chevaliers de Valois; one in Alençon is the subject here. Though sharing the name, these chevaliers mostly did not know one another; they accepted the Bourbon monarchy, acknowledging Henri IV’s succession after the Valois lacked a male heir. The Alençon chevalier, like the others, was an elderly gentleman, tall and thin, whose Parisian youth and romantic life had been interrupted by the Revolution.Well regarded among local aristocrats, he was famed for manners, wit, and stories of Louis XV’s court. A regular at social gatherings, his charm and discretion made him a favorite. At fifty-eight he insisted he was fifty, keeping a youthful appearance despite age. He had a large nose and a pale complexion reddened on one side from a self-explained “hot liver,” an excuse for his voracious appetite.Though seemingly frail, the chevalier was vigorous and passionate; his face showed traces of a full life. He groomed himself carefully, projecting an elegance that belied his years. His clothes, though worn, were always clean, reflecting his adherence to aristocratic standards. He lived modestly in a rented room above Madame Lardot’s laundry, dined out daily, and subsisted on a small annuity.Beloved in Alençon for card-playing and amiability, he mastered conversation and engaged with all ranks; his presence was sought at events. Despite his charm, curiosity surrounded his private life, including rumors of a secret marriage to a woman named Césarine. His finances were precarious, yet he maintained dignity and the reputation of a gentleman by navigating provincial life with grace.CHAPTER II. SUSANNAH AND THE ELDERSOne spring morning the chevalier was visited by Suzanne, a young girl from Madame Lardot’s laundry. She brought news that might bring pleasure and pain, though she feigned indifference to his rumored link with Césarine. Ambitious and clever, Suzanne wanted to leave Alençon for Paris, convinced she could better herself there.Struck by her charm and resolve, the chevalier encouraged her in a paternal tone and advised her to court Monsieur du Bousquier, a wealthy bachelor who had shown interest. He understood social dynamics and how Suzanne might use beauty and wit to secure a favorable match.The narrative shifts to du Bousquier, a man of dubious repute who had once enjoyed wealth and influence. Now back in Alençon, he hoped to marry Mademoiselle Cormon, a rich old maid, to restore his fortunes. His past as a purveyor during the Revolution had tarnished his standing, yet he remained determined to regain status.Du Bousquier contrasts with the chevalier: the latter embodies the elegance of the old regime, the former the brusque opportunism of the new order. Their rivalry over Mademoiselle Cormon is subtle but intense, each seeking her favor while navigating social position.As the story unfolds, Suzanne’s ambitions and the machinations of both men intertwine, revealing the intricate dance of desire, ambition, and societal expectation in provincial Alençon. Their interactions reflect broader themes of class, gender, and the pursuit of happiness in a changing world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Granson, a widow living on a modest pension and modest property, struggled to support her son Athanase, who contributed a small salary from the mayor’s office. Their provincial home was simple and marked by poverty, which shaped their lives and morals. Athanase, a pale, introspective twenty-three-year-old, harbored a deep passion for his cousin, Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he believed to be wealthy. His feelings were complicated by social expectations and the ridicule he might face for loving an older woman.What began as a practical consideration to secure a better life for himself and his mother grew into genuine affection. He admired Mademoiselle Cormon from afar, feeling both drawn to her and constrained by his circumstances; his talent and potential went unrecognized in a place where ambition often clashed with harsh realities.Madame Granson, aware of her son’s feelings, saw the potential benefits of a match despite Athanase’s lack of fortune and the cousin’s age. Hopeful and patient, she waited for the right moment to act. Meanwhile Athanase’s thoughts were consumed by Mademoiselle Cormon; he often fantasized about a future together despite barriers that seemed insurmountable.The arrival of Suzanne, a young woman from a less fortunate background, introduced a new dynamic. She felt an immediate connection to Athanase, sensing his struggles and aspirations, yet her own ambition pushed her toward deception as a means to a better future. After a brief encounter with Madame Granson, Suzanne left town with money and new clothes, while the community lamented her departure.As Athanase prepared for a dinner with Mademoiselle Cormon, his mother urged him to present himself well, hoping to improve his chances. Unaware of the full extent of her plans, Athanase navigated his feelings of love and ambition under the constraints of provincial life. The evening promised to be pivotal as the fates of Athanase, Mademoiselle Cormon, and Suzanne began to unfold, each driven by desire and societal pressure.Suzanne’s departure to Paris marked a turning point; she sought to redefine her life away from past constraints, reflecting the struggles of many women caught between social expectations and personal aspirations. Athanase remained in Alençon, grappling with unfulfilled dreams and the weight of his love, as the complexities of their lives continued to intertwine against the backdrop of provincial society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In many second-class prefectures of France there exists a salon that gathers influential—but not elite—members of the community. Its hosts are prominent locally and invited to events, yet they do not mix with the highest social echelons, who prefer distance and formal invitations.This salon, where lesser nobility, clergy, and magistrates convene, wields considerable local influence. Its members are indifferent to luxury and fashion, focused on practical matters like land acquisition. They resist outside influences, particularly from Paris, and their gossip often carries the force of judgment. Young women are seldom present; when they appear it is usually to seek validation.In Alençon, the upper classes gathered at the house of an old maid, Mademoiselle Rose-Marie-Victoire Cormon, whose fortune was coveted by relatives and two old bachelors. She lived with her uncle, a former grand-vicar, and her family had long served the nobility. Through marriages with noble families they acquired a semblance of aristocracy despite a fundamentally middle-class status.Mademoiselle Cormon's house, dating from the time of Henri IV, provoked envy by its prime location and careful upkeep. The mansion featured a courtyard with a flowerbed—a mark of her meticulous care and insistence on cleanliness. Its robust architecture and decorative elements recalled its historical significance.Inside, the house was arranged for reception: a salon hung with family portraits and a dining room exuding provincial charm. The salon was a hub for social life—card games and conversations about local affairs took place there. Mademoiselle Cormon's circle numbered about 150 people, mostly mature, who rarely traveled and had long been rooted in provincial life.Wednesdays were special: she hosted dinners that drew a larger assemblage. The women dressed modestly and the atmosphere was one of familiarity and routine. Conversations typically centered on local gossip, land disputes, and judgments about aristocratic behavior, reflecting the community's concerns and values.Despite her wealth and standing, Mademoiselle Cormon remained unmarried because she insisted on a noble match and the political upheaval from 1788 to 1815 made suitable partners scarce—many eligible men were too old or too young, or lacked the qualities she sought. Fear of being married for her fortune led her to set subtle tests for suitors to expose mercenary intentions without their knowledge.As she aged, her longing for love intensified, yet she remained oblivious to some genuine affections, including those of Athanase Granson, one of her suitors. Her innocence and naivety prevented her from recognizing sincere attachment, leading to missed opportunities. Over time her character grew more rigid and the weight of social expectations pressed on her.Although pious and virtuous, she became a figure of local ridicule; her unmarried status turned into a proverb. She lived modestly, shunning ostentation and keeping a lifestyle aligned with provincial values. Her considerable fortune did not exempt her from scrutiny, and marriage attempts were viewed skeptically.The return of disbanded soldiers and officers offered occasional glimmers of hope, but none met her standards. Her yearning for companionship and motherhood turned into a source of despair as she confronted the realities of her situation.Mademoiselle Cormon’s story illuminates the complexities of love, social expectation, and the particular challenges faced by women in her position. Her life—marked by a deep desire for connection and a struggle against restrictive social norms—serves as a poignant commentary on happiness and fulfillment in a world governed by rigid class conventions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################To complete the picture of Mademoiselle Cormon’s household, we must include her and the Abbé de Sponde alongside their loyal servants: Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who together ensured the comfort of the uncle and niece.Jacquelin, a short, stout man with a ruddy complexion, had served the family twenty-two years, doing everything from waiting at table to grooming the mare Pénélope. He was in love with Josette, a thirty-year-old maid whom Mademoiselle would dismiss if she married him. The couple quietly saved their wages, hoping Mademoiselle’s eventual marriage would free them to wed. Josette, plump and practical, was said to rule her mistress. Mariette, the cook, had been with the household fifteen years and excelled at preparing local delicacies.Pénélope, the old brown-bay mare, attracted unusual affection: without children, Mademoiselle Cormon directed maternal instincts toward the animal. The servants and mare maintained a steady routine shaped by long service and mutual dependence. Mademoiselle, however, grew increasingly hard to please; her idle mind fixed on trifles. She fretted over linens and the timing of Pénélope’s meals, turning small disputes into a sense of purpose.Her life centred on the household, her uncle, and religious duties, which magnified the minutiae of existence. Concerned about her health, she obsessed over minor ailments and diet. Each season she visited her estate at Prébaudet to inspect the apple trees and prepare for harvest, marking the occasion with a farewell dinner for friends, a notable event in Alençon.On the day of her departure the town buzzed with gossip about suitors, especially M. du Bousquier, whom she had rebuffed. The farewell dinner teemed with social maneuvering; Mademoiselle strove to engage guests but often missed the right words. Her attempts at amiability won mixed reactions—her lack of wit entertaining the sharper guests.As the evening progressed, talk turned to local politics and the controversial du Bousquier, rumoured to be involved with a young woman named Suzanne. In defending du Bousquier, Mademoiselle made a remark that unexpectedly drew laughter and admiration, showing she could contribute to the evening’s entertainment, though unintentionally.The dinner closed with light banter about du Bousquier’s character, revealing a town divided. Mademoiselle’s innocence and naivety were plain as she navigated social complexities and tensions around marriage and morality. When the guests departed, conversation lingered on her prospects and the implications of du Bousquier’s behaviour. The evening left her contemplating her role in the community and her own desires, setting the stage for future developments where love, ambition, and social expectation would continue to intertwine in Alençon.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The following day Mademoiselle Cormon, packed into her old carriole with Josette, set off for Prebaudet, unaware an unexpected event would hasten her marriage. The morning after her arrival, while having breakfast, Jacquelin brought a letter from her uncle, the Abbé de Sponde. He announced that Monsieur de Troisville, a grandson of one of the abbé’s friends, wished to stay in Alençon and needed hospitality. Alarmed by the urgency, she decided to return to town immediately to help her uncle prepare for the guest.Despite the rain she insisted on leaving at once, driven by excitement and the prospect of marriage. Their arrival in Alençon caused a stir, with townspeople speculating about the reason. Josette and Jacquelin were left to manage the household while she concentrated on preparing for Monsieur de Troisville’s visit.News of the viscount’s impending arrival spread quickly and the town buzzed with gossip. Hopeful, she fretted over her home and appearance, fearing her old-fashioned decor might not impress him; she even sacrificed some prized liqueurs to make a better impression.When Monsieur de Troisville arrived he was greeted by the abbé and Mademoiselle Cormon. He looked distinguished and she felt an immediate attraction. Their dinner conversation flowed: the viscount complimented her home and expressed admiration for the region, making her feel at last noticed and desirable.The evening shifted when the Chevalier de Valois and other guests, drawn by curiosity about the newcomer, arrived. During introductions the viscount casually mentioned he was married, which devastated her. She fainted; Du Bousquier, a local suitor, caught her and carried her to her room while the townspeople, watching eagerly, were left to speculate.As she recovered Mademoiselle Cormon realized her hopes for marriage had been dashed. The next day news of her fainting and the viscount’s marital status swept through Alençon, prompting a mixture of laughter and sympathy. Feeling the weight of her position she considered her options as both Du Bousquier and the Chevalier sought her attention, each hoping to win her favor.Ultimately she accepted Du Bousquier’s proposal as a way to regain standing in the community, requiring him to agree to a deceit about their engagement, which he readily accepted. As they prepared to announce it the Chevalier de Valois arrived, unaware of the developments. The evening ended with Mademoiselle Cormon feeling a blend of triumph and uncertainty as the town buzzed with news of her engagement, marking a new chapter in her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The news of Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage devastated Athanase Granson, though he concealed his turmoil. He first heard it at Chief Justice du Ronceret’s house, where his mother was playing cards. Athanase had long pinned his hopes on Mademoiselle Cormon; the announcement felt catastrophic. Shaped by an Imperial education that bred fatalism, he drifted into indifference and despair. His mother noticed his pallor but assumed he was merely preoccupied with work.After the marriage announcement, Athanase wandered the picturesque Sarthe region, finding bitter solace in its overlooked beauty. He withdrew from politics and society, becoming of little interest to townspeople who were unaware of the depth of his suffering. His mother worried and speculated he might be contemplating a major project, not grasping the real cause of his withdrawal.Suzanne, a romantic young woman inspired by tales of adventure, returned to Alençon intending to help Athanase financially, though their paths did not meet. The town buzzed with gossip about Mademoiselle Cormon’s match, with conflicting reports about its legitimacy. Eventually the marriage contract was signed and the wedding took place amid ominous signs—a priest accidentally read from the De Profundis—setting an uneasy tone.The marriage of Mademoiselle Cormon and du Bousquier lacked celebration; the couple soon retreated to Prébaudet. Du Bousquier, intent on modernizing the Cormon estate, began renovations that alarmed locals who feared losing their provincial charm. Consumed by grief, Athanase ultimately took his own life, leaving a letter his mother misread as evidence of newfound independence rather than the despair it expressed.Athanase’s suicide shocked Alençon. Madame Granson was left to mourn privately, grappling with the full weight of her loss that others barely comprehended. The Chevalier de Valois revealed Athanase’s unrequited love for Mademoiselle Cormon, deepening the town’s sense of tragedy. Madame Granson’s grief was profound; she alone understood the true nature of her son’s struggles and bore them in silence.Madame du Bousquier returned to Alençon and encountered Madame Granson in a meeting heavy with unspoken words. Madame Granson sought solace in the church and implored the rector for a proper burial for her son; the ceremony was conducted discreetly to avoid scandal, reflecting the community’s desire to contain the episode.As time passed, Madame du Bousquier’s marriage became both gossip and cautionary tale. At first she appeared content, but the reality was different. Du Bousquier, outwardly successful and politically ambitious, proved a tyrant at home. The marriage brought social advantage but left her feeling powerless and unfulfilled; they had no children, and her longing for motherhood deepened her pain.The Chevalier de Valois, embittered by the match, sought revenge against du Bousquier, whose conduct made him a figure of ridicule. Public mockery at Mademoiselle Cormon’s misfortune gave way to growing sympathy for Madame du Bousquier as the true hardships of her life became known. The chevalier’s schemes only widened the rift between du Bousquier and the local aristocracy, intensifying animosity rather than restoring honor.Madame du Bousquier lived amid contradictions: she performed the duties of a devoted wife while inwardly resenting her husband’s oppression. The marriage, beneficial in status, left her emotionally desolate; she clung to duty even as she mourned the life she might have had.The Chevalier de Valois’s death coincided with sweeping political changes in France, marking an end of an era and creating a void for those who had known him. Suzanne, seeking to honor the past, purchased a memento from the chevalier’s estate, a small symbol of lost connections and vanished possibilities.Ultimately, Madame du Bousquier’s life illustrates the complexities of love, duty, and social expectation. She remained both pitied and admired—enduring the consequences of her choices and reflecting on what might have been. The narrative offers a poignant exploration of personal desires constrained by society, showing how private suffering and public reputation intertwine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author of the History of the Ottoman Empire.Dear Baron, your encouragement of my “History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century” led me to associate your name with my work. As a representative of studious Germany, your approval lends credibility to my efforts. Your dedication to research has sustained me in my labor on modern civilization. I hope this token of respect finds you well in Dobling and reminds you of your sincere admirer and friend.DE BALZAC.THE COLLECTION OF ANTIQUITIESIn a small French town stands the house known as the Hotel d’Esgrignon, residence of the old Marquis Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol. What began as a jest in naming became earnest through time. The d’Esgrignon family, descended from powerful Northmen, kept a proud lineage, never bowing to kings or commoners. As provincial nobles, they were locally revered, preserving status despite neglect by the court. Their history featured marriages without dowries and a steadfast refusal of titles that would diminish their nobility.During the Revolution the d’Esgrignons were imperiled. The Marquis, respected in the countryside, avoided emigration but had to hide. Family lands were sold, yet Mlle. d’Esgrignon managed to save some property through the family steward, Chesnel. After the Terror the Marquis returned to a dilapidated castle, mourning lost heritage. The old Hotel d’Esgrignon had been demolished; Chesnel used the last of the Marquis’s money to purchase a new home for the family, wryly named the Hotel d’Esgrignon.Around 1800 émigrés began returning, including the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter, whom the Marquis married. She died in childbirth, leaving a son to carry the family name. Grief burdened the Marquis, now confronting diminished circumstances. A proposal from a local contractor to Mlle. d’Esgrignon — deemed insulting by her and her brother — drove a wedge between them and Chesnel, who had acted for the contractor.Chesnel, a respected notary, had always been more than a servant: confidant, manager, and protector of the family’s interests. The altered dynamics troubled him; the Marquis saw Chesnel as a loyal companion, while Chesnel felt the strain of fidelity to a declining house. Politically, the era shifted as some nobles aligned with Napoleon and others remained monarchist. The d’Esgrignons held fast to their principles.By 1804 many émigrés returned, yet the d’Esgrignons’ fortunes did not recover. The Marquis became a symbol of loyalty and old nobility, a leader among remaining royalists. His salon, nicknamed the “Collection of Antiquities,” gathered those clinging to the past. What began as a playful sobriquet assumed a more solemn tone as political tides moved.The family faced challenges from the rising bourgeoisie, epitomized by du Croisier, who sought to undermine their standing. Rivalry intensified as du Croisier’s faction gained strength while the d’Esgrignons remained a bulwark of old-world values. Political life in the town grew polarized; du Croisier adopted an increasingly aggressive stance against the nobility.By 1822 the d’Esgrignons’ situation remained precarious. The Marquis, now elderly, was uncertain of his role in a changing society. The Restoration had not delivered expected relief; financial struggles continued. The family lived modestly, their income barely meeting needs, while political tensions simmered.As society evolved, the d’Esgrignons clung to their ideals, navigating a world increasingly hostile to their way of life. The Collection of Antiquities stood as a testament to a bygone era and the enduring legacy of the d’Esgrignon family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Marquis was oblivious to managing his estate, and the idea that the d’Esgrignons could recover their former glory struck others as absurd. People pinned hopes on Victurnien, the handsome heir, marrying into nobility to restore the family's prestige. In truth the d’Esgrignons lacked the political connections and finances to reclaim status, and their continuity had been broken by events of the past forty years.Only Chesnel, the notary, perceived the futility of such hopes. He understood the social transformations wrought by the Revolution and the altered political landscape; despite his loyalty, he could not ignore their predicament. He tried gently to persuade the Marquis that adaptation was necessary, but the old noble remained convinced that fate or divine intervention would restore their fortunes.Victurnien, adored by his father and aunt, was a spoiled young man who had never faced real opposition. Indulgence left him selfish and reckless. The Chevalier, a relic of the old order, fed his extravagance with romantic tales of past grandeur, inflating the young man’s sense of superiority. Surrounded by flattery and exempt from ordinary constraints, Victurnien came to regard himself as above the common people, worsening his moral and practical flaws.When he entered society, the gap between his upbringing and modern realities became apparent. His behavior alienated townspeople, and escapades produced legal troubles Chesnel managed discreetly. Encouraged by companions who enjoyed his company, his arrogance hardened into a life of excess; he assumed lineage alone would shield him from consequences.Du Croisier, a rival, sought to exploit Victurnien’s weaknesses and hasten the d’Esgrignons’ ruin. Meanwhile Victurnien’s vanity and appetite for pleasure led him into gambling and frivolity, sustained by Chesnel’s financial sacrifices, which he accepted as his due. Sent to Paris with high expectations, he quickly found the court far less welcoming than imagined; the nobility he met were largely self-interested and indifferent to reviving the d’Esgrignon name.Undeterred, he tried to secure a place by adopting the extravagant lifestyle of Parisian elites. His charm and looks opened doors, and he became a fixture in high society, especially captivated by the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Enchanted by her beauty and the glitter of her world, he fell intensely in love, blind to her debts and convinced their passion was pure.As he indulged in Parisian pleasures his debts mounted beyond his means. Friends who enjoyed his company also recognized his precarious state and egged his spending, entertained by the prospect of his fall. Victurnien’s naivety and lack of foresight led him to believe he could sustain lavish living without consequence.Chesnel, ever loyal, continued to prop him up financially but grew increasingly alarmed. Despite warnings, Victurnien remained absorbed in fantasy, oblivious to reality. The tension between his noble pretensions and dire finances would precipitate his downfall.Victurnien’s passage through Parisian high society exposes the dangers of vanity, excess, and the illusion of nobility. His entanglements—most notably with the Duchesse—become enmeshed in deceit and financial ruin, underscoring the fragility of status and the consequences of living beyond one’s means. The narrative is a cautionary tale about pride’s perils and the need to recognize one’s limits in a rapidly changing world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1823 the Kellers had advanced Victurnien two hundred thousand francs, without Chesnel or Mlle. Armande knowing. He had also received two thousand crowns from Chesnel to conceal his sources. Victurnien wrote deceitful letters to his father and aunt, who remained unaware of the disaster looming over their house. Only du Croisier knew, and he delighted not merely in ruining the d’Esgrignons but in dishonoring them; he sensed his revenge as Victurnien’s debts mounted.Du Croisier’s first move targeted Chesnel, who lived modestly in the Rue du Bercail. One afternoon, after dinner, Chesnel’s housekeeper announced du Croisier. The old lawyer’s heart sank. Du Croisier presented a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs, demanding payment or threatening to expose Victurnien’s debts. Overwhelmed, Chesnel realized he could raise the money only by mortgaging his lands. Du Croisier seized the opportunity, proposed to buy Chesnel’s property—and did so within two days. Stripped of his lands, Chesnel was left with only his practice and his house, consumed by worry for Victurnien’s future.Chesnel dreaded telling Mlle. Armande about the family’s ruin. She had just received a charming, hypocritical letter from Victurnien describing his travels in Italy with the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse and remained enchanted and oblivious. Chesnel’s arrival shattered her reverie: all was lost, and Victurnien risked bringing dishonor on their house. Devastated, she realized they had given Victurnien too much freedom.The Marquis, having read Victurnien’s letter, remained unaware of the crisis. Mlle. Armande and Chesnel shared a silent understanding of their concern for the young Count. They discussed intervening to save him from his reckless lifestyle, fearing he would be left destitute. Mlle. Armande resolved to go to Paris to rescue Victurnien, masking the trip as a medical consultation to avoid gossip.Chesnel gave her his last bag of louis, and she set off, determined to confront the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Du Croisier, meanwhile, had planned his revenge to ensure Victurnien would be cut off from support. He wrote to Victurnien, informing him that the Kellers would advance no more money without security, timing the letter to coincide with Victurnien’s increasing desperation.Victurnien, until then living in luxury, suddenly faced the reality of his debts. Despite previous gambling wins, he could not procure funds and felt the weight of impending ruin. In despair he briefly considered suicide but rejected it. Instead, he thought of fleeing with the Duchess and forging a bill of exchange to finance their escape, though he hesitated, wanting the Duchess’s agreement first.When Victurnien confessed his financial troubles to the Duchess, she assured him money could be found. Encouraged, he proposed they flee together; she agreed, eager to break free from their current lives. Buoyed by her promise, Victurnien resolved to forge the bill.The next day he presented a bill for three hundred thousand francs to the Kellers, who advanced the money, unaware of the deceit. Du Croisier, learning of the transaction, realized his plan for revenge was bearing fruit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Victurnien received his money, he gave it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who locked it away and suggested one last visit to the Opera. He considered confessing to Chesnel but, despite doubts, lingered at the Duchess's behest; her enchanting glance kept him longer than he intended.The next day he returned to the Hotel de Maufrigneuse to plan an escape, only to find the Duchess resolved to stay. She thought it more remarkable to face the storm than to flee, and Victurnien, torn between admiration and despair, realized how deeply he loved her.After their argument Diane's coldness left him distraught. He begged forgiveness, but she remained unyielding—an angelic indifference that intensified his anguish. As time passed he felt increasingly lost and unable to think clearly.Meanwhile the Count took Diane's hand and found it lifeless. Victurnien’s emotional turmoil was compounded by the discovery that his financial misdeeds had placed him in serious trouble. When Josephin told him a warrant for his arrest had been issued, he panicked, convinced he was ruined.Josephin urged him to flee to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine, where Mlle. Armande waited. Clinging to his determined aunt, Victurnien discussed hiding and the possibility of slipping into Chesnel’s house unnoticed.Executing the plan, he unwittingly walked into a trap. Chesnel, meanwhile, was selling his practice to a rival notary, M. Lepressoir, who hoped to profit from Victurnien's troubles. Learning of Victurnien's plight devastated Chesnel; fearing for the young man’s future, he even contemplated taking the blame for the forgery himself.Chesnel resolved to confront du Croisier, Victurnien's creditor, and negotiate a settlement. Du Croisier, however, reveled in his power over the d’Esgrignon family, seeing the affair as an opportunity to assert dominance and exact revenge on the aristocracy. At a gathering at his house, news of Victurnien's arrest spread among guests who were shocked and intrigued; the atmosphere was tense as allies weighed the implications and a possible trial.Realizing the gravity of the situation, Chesnel tried to influence the examining magistrate, M. Camusot, by appealing to his ambitions and connections, hoping Camusot's support might mitigate damage to Victurnien's reputation. As legal proceedings unfolded, Chesnel intensified his efforts and sought Mme. de Maufrigneuse’s assistance. Initially unaware of the forgery's full extent, she felt compelled to help once she understood Victurnien's love and the consequences he faced.Negotiations with du Croisier proved difficult; he demanded not only money but political favors and social recognition—demands Chesnel could scarcely satisfy. In desperation Chesnel offered a substantial sum to have the charges withdrawn, but du Croisier’s pride and ambition made him resistant. Both sides were unwilling to yield, and the conflict escalated as the trial approached.Chesnel’s determination to save Victurnien pushed him toward morally questionable options, including lying under oath. He understood that the d’Esgrignon family’s honor hung in the balance and that dishonor could be devastating. The legal battle thus became emblematic of broader tensions between aristocracy and bourgeoisie: its outcome would decide Victurnien's fate and signal shifting power dynamics.Throughout, Chesnel’s unwavering loyalty to the d’Esgrignon family and his willingness to navigate provincial politics highlighted the complexities of love, honor, and ambition in a world where social status could dictate destiny.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Blondet, a knowledgeable judge, was overshadowed by his passion for gardening—particularly pelargoniums—which consumed his time and ambition. His marriage to a much younger woman, who had an affair with the local prefect, further stalled his career. Though he had a legitimate son, Joseph, Blondet favored his illegitimate son, Emile, sending Emile to Paris with little support after his wife’s death.Blondet’s modest brick house and well-tended garden reflected his horticultural devotion. He intended to leave his estate to Joseph, having disinherited Emile, whom he regarded with disdain. Emile, however, prospered in Paris thanks to his mother’s family connections—especially Mlle. de Troisville, who promised support and helped secure his literary recognition—achievements Blondet never learned of, absorbed as he was in legal work and gardening. The household ran on strict frugality, enforced by a maid who managed daily life.Joseph Blondet was being groomed for the bench and spent time with the Blandureau family. Michu, a young lawyer in those circles, often outshone the older judges. Meanwhile, a shrewd public prosecutor became involved in a plot against the d’Esgrignons, leading to a forgery charge against Victurnien d’Esgrignon, a young nobleman.The President of the Tribunal, pursuing personal advantage, manipulated events to benefit du Croisier, a rival of the d’Esgrignons. Influenced by the President’s wife, the prosecutor pursued the case against Victurnien despite likely political fallout.As the trial approached, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse intervened, bringing funds to secure Victurnien’s release. Her efforts, with Chesnel the family notary’s support, aimed to restore the d’Esgrignon name. The trial ultimately revealed the truth, exonerating Victurnien and exposing du Croisier’s machinations.Afterward the d’Esgrignons endured social scrutiny, but their honor was preserved. Victurnien’s future remained uncertain as he grappled with his past and his family’s expectations. The story reflects on the changing nature of nobility and the enduring bonds of loyalty and service—embodied by characters like Chesnel—examining ambition, familial loyalty, and the complexities of social standing in a transforming France.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyTHE LILY OF THE VALLEYBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Monsieur J. B. Nacquart, Member of the Royal Academy of Medicine.Dear Doctor--Here is one of the most carefully hewn stones in the second course of the foundation of a literary edifice which I have slowly and laboriously constructed. I wish to inscribe your name upon it, as much to thank the man whose science once saved me as to honor the friend of my daily life.De Balzac.THE LILY OF THE VALLEYENVOIFelix de Vandenesse to Madame la Comtesse Natalie de Manerville:I yield to your wishes. It is the privilege of the women whom we love more than they love us to make the men who love them ignore the ordinary rules of common-sense. To smooth the frown upon their brow, to soften the pout upon their lips, what obstacles we miraculously overcome! We shed our blood, we risk our future!You exact the history of my past life; here it is. But remember this, Natalie; in obeying you I crush under foot a reluctance hitherto unconquerable. Why are you jealous of the sudden reveries which overtake me in the midst of our happiness? Why show the pretty anger of a petted woman when silence grasps me? Could you not play upon the contradictions of my character without inquiring into the causes of them? Are there secrets in your heart which seek absolution through a knowledge of mine? Ah! Natalie, you have guessed mine; and it is better you should know the whole truth. Yes, my life is shadowed by a phantom; a word evokes it; it hovers vaguely above me and about me; within my soul are solemn memories, buried in its depths like those marine productions seen in calmest weather and which the storms of ocean cast in fragments on the shore.The mental labor which the expression of ideas necessitates has revived the old, old feelings which give me so much pain when they come suddenly; and if in this confession of my past they break forth in a way that wounds you, remember that you threatened to punish me if I did not obey your wishes, and do not, therefore, punish my obedience. I would that this, my confidence, might increase your love.Until we meet,Felix.CHAPTER I. TWO CHILDHOODSTo what genius fed on tears shall we some day owe that most touching of all elegies,--the tale of tortures borne silently by souls whose tender roots find stony ground in the domestic soil, whose earliest buds are torn apart by rancorous hands, whose flowers are touched by frost at the moment of their blossoming? What poet will sing the sorrows of the child whose lips must suck a bitter breast, whose smiles are checked by the cruel fire of a stern eye? The tale that tells of such poor hearts, oppressed by beings placed about them to promote the development of their natures, would contain the true history of my childhood.What vanity could I have wounded,--I a child new-born? What moral or physical infirmity caused my mother’s coldness? Put to nurse in the country and forgotten by my family for over three years, I returned to a home where even the servants pitied me. My brother and two sisters found amusement in making me suffer. The compact by which children hush each other’s faults and learn honor was null for me; worse, I was often punished for my brother’s misdeeds without being allowed to vindicate myself. My brother and sisters, taught by imitation and self-preservation, joined in the persecutions to keep in favor with our mother, whom they feared as much as I did.Disinherited of all affection, I could love nothing; yet nature had made me loving. In many such hearts the crushed feelings turn to hatred; in mine they condensed and hollowed out a depth from which, in after years, they gushed upon my life. While some become timid and slavish through repeated humiliation, my trials hardened me. Constant expectation of fresh grief bred a sullen resignation that smothered the gaiety of childhood and made me appear idiotic, seeming to justify my mother’s warnings. The certainty of injustice prematurely roused my pride and restrained the worst effects of my harsh upbringing.Though my mother neglected me she sometimes spoke of my education and seemed inclined to attend to it. That prospect chilled me; I blessed the neglect that let me stay alone in the garden, play with pebbles, watch insects, and gaze into the sky. My liking for solitude was first kindled by a single incident. Once the governess forgot to send me to bed; I crouched beneath a fig-tree watching a star. When darkness fell my absence was noticed. Mademoiselle Caroline, a severe woman, told my mother I had a horror of home and would long ago have run away if watched less; that I was sullen and of a bad disposition. Hearing this, my mother accused me of playing at the reservoir and flooding the garden--a prank my sisters had performed to frighten each other. I was punished, branded as a liar, and forbidden to stay in the garden after dark.Forbidden things obsess children more than adults; I was whipped and yet clung inwardly to my star, telling it my troubles in that secret prattle of early thought. At twelve, long after school began, I still watched that star with undimmed delight; first impressions at life’s dawn are enduring.Charles, my brother, five years my senior and handsome, was our parents’ favorite and the household sovereign. Robust and well-schooled, he had a tutor; I, puny and sickly, became a day-pupil in town taken by the valet and given a scanty lunch. Such small privations marked me to my fellows, who flaunted rillons and pastries, inspecting my meagre basket as proof of difference. That contrast nipped my budding youth. I was mocked by comrades who withheld treats they pretended to offer but then pulled away. I fought to escape persecution; desperation lent me courage, yet I remained hated and isolated. Once a boy struck me with stones; my mother, told of it, cried, “That dreadful child! he will always be a torment to us.”At school my distrust of myself grew. I received only three francs pocket-money monthly, barely enough for school supplies, and could not buy playthings that won favor. Shy of flattering the rich or cringing, I lived under a tree, dejected, reliant on library books. The first time I won prizes for essay and translation, neither parent was in the audience; I burst into tears on receiving my rewards and burned my crowns in the stove that night. My comrades went home with families; I remained with the “outremers,” neglect that marked me further.I wrote my parents pleadingly but their silence persisted; my letters, deemed too emotional, were mocked by my mother. I sought help from my sisters, wrote on their birthdays, but it availed nothing. I strove to bring them to the prize distribution; they did not come, though only a few miles away. My expectation turned to hurt when my name was not called with the others. I even thought of suicide at Blois, but the bridge parapet was too low.My confessor urged patience, pointing to heavenly rewards; from my first communion I took refuge in prayer, drawn to religious visions that fed my imagination and strengthened my mind. These visions, like a guardian angel, prepared my spirit to read inner truths and gave me the gift of expression.My father, uneasy about Oratorian teaching, moved me to Paris at fifteen to an institution in the Marais. Examined, I was placed in a lower class; at Lepitre Academy my family neglect continued. Lepitre, a royalist, gave me small aid but my allowance remained insufficient. In the Joyeuse mansion, pupils breakfasted with the porter Doisy, a smuggler who guarded their pranks. I resisted the place’s attractions for a time, but temptation proved strong, and pride and delicacy made resistance hard.When my family visited Paris, my brother, who lived there, told me of a planned dinner at the Palais-Royal. I owed Doisy a hundred francs. My brother interceded, but my mother, stern and denunciatory, rebuked me harshly, threatening that such folly at seventeen would ruin the family. As punishment I missed the dinner and the theatre and saw my mother’s first visit to me in twelve years turn into a torrent of insults.Kept then under Lepitre’s guardianship after school, I was to study mathematics, law, and philosophy but was granted little freedom. In Paris, danger to youth was feared; my mother arranged for me to dine with an aunt, the Marquise de Listomere, a ceremonious woman of the old stamp. I planned to slip away after dinner to the Palais-Royal--a place of indulgence and fascination--but fate intervened thrice. Each time I nearly escaped, Lepitre or my aunt delayed me, and finally my mother herself arrived unexpectedly and took me back to Tours to remove me from Parisian temptations. By such accidents my life was kept safe from early excesses.Study became my refuge; it was almost a passion that confined me in a period when life’s pleasures normally preoccupy young men. At twenty, overeager in spirit but small and pale in body, I had read and thought so much that I knew life philosophically as I was entering its tangled paths. This prolonged adolescence prepared me to feel and to love intensely. My mother’s coldness persisted; at times I tried to plead and cling, but she dismissed me as unnatural and playing a comedy. I fell into despair and at times would withdraw into my father’s library to study, which, though a saving, also aggravated my inward danger.Events then swelled in the country: the Duc d’Angouleme’s passage roused Touraine, and a ball was given. I begged my mother to let me attend; her demeanor at first froze me, yet she arranged garments for me and I found myself dressed in new clothes. At the ball, among military music and acclamations, I felt disgust at the partisan display; yet the sight of power stirred in me an ambition for glory that would later guide me.Among the crowd I sat sullen and awkward; a woman, mistaking me for a sleepy child, slipped to the seat beside me. The warmth and presence of a real woman struck me with such force that I laid my head upon her shoulder and kissed it. She cried out, then, seeing my tears and contrition, moved away with the dignity and forgiving gravity of a noble woman. I was ashamed but not repentant; that stolen contact changed me. A new soul had been stirred; my star had descended to earth as a woman. I loved without knowing what love was.The thought that she might live in Touraine made life itself sweeter. I longed to find her name and home. My mother sent me to Frapesle, a château between Montbazon and Azay-le-Rideau, where a friend would look after me. I set out to wander Touraine, hoping each château might shelter my unknown.Crossing the Charlemagne moors and approaching Sache and the Indre valley, I reached Pont-de-Ruan and then the park of Frapesle. The view—mills, river, poplars, the Indre winding like a ribbon—seemed made for love. From beneath a walnut-tree I first saw the white spot in a garden that marked the house I had admired. I told myself, “If that perfect woman is on earth, she is here.” My heart leapt; the chateau that had caught my eye belonged to Clochegourde—home of the Comte de Mortsauf and his wife, Madame de Mortsauf, whom my host praised as one fitted for the highest rank though she kept secluded.We crossed the river and called at Clochegourde. The house, modest yet elegantly sited, had terraces, vineyards, orchards, and a roof stained by lichens; a little tower held a bell and the family escutcheon. Seen from the valley it seemed dainty as a flower. Its arrangement and surroundings filled me with delight and premonition.Entering, we were announced. “Come in, gentlemen,” said a voice I had heard at the ball. Madame de Mortsauf appeared, and our eyes met. Though confused, she took her seat at her embroidery and spoke gently. My companion explained I had walked from Tours and was ill and that he had brought me to rest. The countess, at first distrustful, offered me refreshments and comfort. Her voice, which I now recall as entering my soul, restored me. I asked only to rest there; she consented and asked my companion to dine with them. He hesitated but accepted when she insisted, and by such small mercies I secured an evening under her roof that I treasured like an eternity.Madame de Mortsauf spoke of local matters and the harvest; her silence toward me at first seemed like reserve, yet it was full of meaning. Her voice was music—each syllable breathed like a flute; I listened to it as to a rare sound. I permitted myself to appear ignorant rather than interrupt those harmonies. Listening to her made me feel a closeness I could not have otherwise attained.I examined her with the reverent attention of a lover. She had hair shining like gold, a round brow full of reserved thought, eyes green flecked with brown that usually looked wan but flashed lighted with feeling, a Grecian nose, softly curved lips, and a complexion of camellia whiteness with soft rose. Her figure was plump but graceful, a girl and a mother at once; simple, bashful, fastidious—she charmed unconsciously. Her movements and rare glances made all she said solemn. That morning she wore a rose-striped gown and her hair simply twisted. Such outlines cannot fully capture her; her inward life, her daily emanations toward family and duty, were the true sources of her attractiveness.Clochegourde’s salon matched its mistress—clean, plain, and refined. Panelled and painted in two greys, with porcelain vases of Cape heather, a backgammon board, cambric curtains; its simplicity ascended to grandeur. From its windows the valley spread, with Pont-de-Ruan, Azay, Sache, and the meadows along the Indre. The room, calm and regular, suited her life. There, in that serene atmosphere, my most original ideas and passions began to form; the place was a cradle for the plant of influence that would fertilize my soul.The count entered while we talked. A little girl, Madeleine, who seemed nine though looking frail, bounded in exclaiming “Here’s papa!” Her pallid eyes and thin limbs revealed a delicate health sustained by country air and maternal care. She and her brother Jacques, both fragile, looked to their mother for life. The children’s silent affection and the count’s awkward unease when he greeted us suggested much sorrow veiled beneath his quiet manners.The count was perhaps forty-five but looked nearer sixty, aged by the reverses of the Revolution. His dominant trait was a candor mingled with bewildered distrust; he received us graciously. My father had once served the Bourbons and the honor of our family name made him warm toward me. After the children rejoined their mother, I watched the count, trying to discern his character, and found myself drawn into the household’s quiet but intense life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though ten years of emigration and farm life had altered his physique, the count still bore traces of nobility. Even the most ardent liberal would have acknowledged his chivalric loyalty and steadfast convictions: a man devoted to a cause, honest in his political views, yet ignorant of France’s real affairs. He was one of those upright souls who, while unable to serve his party, might inadvertently harm it—willing to sacrifice his life rather than his money.At dinner I noticed the count’s flaccid cheeks and the furtive glances he cast at his children, signs of a burdened mind and a frail constitution he had passed to them. Though he might blame himself, he refused to be judged by others. His harsh demeanor, born of self-awareness of his faults, lacked grace and increased the misery he had brought upon his family. When his wife returned with the children, an atmosphere of unhappiness settled over the house, as if walking over a vault and feeling cold depths below.After the conversation shifted, the count introduced me to his wife and revealed details about my family I had not known. He asked my age; when I answered the countess expressed surprise at her daughter’s age, perhaps thinking me younger. I sensed a connection forming: her maternal instincts sparked hope that her children might thrive. She scrutinized me, and in that moment some barriers between us dissolved. She asked many questions but spoke little.“If study has made you ill,” she said, “the air of our valley will soon restore you.”“Modern education harms children,” the count remarked. “We burden them with mathematics and sciences, aging them prematurely. You must stay and rest here,” he urged. “You are overwhelmed by the avalanche of ideas. What future will universal education have unless we return it to religious hands?”His words echoed his later refusal, during elections, to support a talented candidate: “I shall always distrust men of talent.”He then proposed a tour of the gardens.“Monsieur—” his wife interjected.“What is it, my dear?” he replied, revealing his desire to control his home.“Monsieur de Vandenesse walked from Tours this morning, and Monsieur de Chessel has already taken him on foot over Frapesle.”“Very imprudent of you,” the count said to me, shaking his head.I quickly realized the rigidity of his royalism required careful navigation. The manservant announced dinner. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to Madame de Mortsauf, while the count took mine, and we went to the dining room.The dining room, with its white tiled floor and varnished paper adorned with floral designs, was charming though unpretentious. Dinner was plentiful but not luxurious: family silver, mismatched china, flowers on the table. I found joy in these quaint details, feeling contentment that blinded me to the obstacles of country life. I sat beside her, serving her wine, and felt a deep connection. That kiss had bound us in a secret that filled me with both joy and shame. I endeavored to please the count, lavishing attention on the dogs and trying to engage the children, whom I hoped would accept me as one of their own. I sensed any sign of gloom would jeopardize my place in their lives.Dinner passed in a haze of happiness: I reveled in being under her roof, ignoring her apparent coldness and the count’s polite indifference. Love, like life, has its adolescence, and I was content in my feelings. I made several foolish remarks, driven by passion, but no one noticed their cause; even she was oblivious to love. The rest of my visit felt dreamlike as I returned across the Indre, enchanted by moonlight and nature’s sounds. That evening marked a turning point; I felt the weight of past struggles clash with the joy I had just experienced.Approaching Frapesle, I glanced at Clochegourde and saw a small boat tied to a tree—Monsieur de Mortsauf’s fishing boat.“Well,” said Monsieur de Chessel, once we were out of earshot, “I needn’t ask if you found those shoulders; I must congratulate you on the reception Monsieur de Mortsauf gave you. You stepped into his heart at once.”His words lifted my spirits, though I sensed underlying bitterness.“How do you mean?” I asked.“He has never received anyone so well,” he replied.I admitted my surprise.Monsieur de Chessel, whose real name was Durand, had abandoned his father’s name—a successful merchant during the Revolution—to adopt an old parliamentary name. His ambition led him to claim a count’s title under Louis XVIII, a title his children enjoyed without understanding the sacrifices it cost him. His character bore pettiness and envy; his ambition, recently rekindled by royal favor, made him ostentatious yet kindly toward me, providing a peace I had longed for. His interest in my affairs felt paternal, a contrast to the neglect I was used to; I was grateful for his hospitality.The owners of Frapesle were intertwined with the dawn of my happiness, and I cherished those memories. Monsieur de Chessel’s ostentation annoyed some neighbors—fine horses, lavish entertaining—while the Comte de Mortsauf, with limited means, struggled to maintain his estate. Their interactions were polite but lacked intimacy.Jealousy was not the sole cause of the Count de Mortsauf’s isolation. His education was superficial, lacking the depth suited to his nobility: he had emigrated just as his education should have deepened, and a long idleness followed. Hopes for a restored monarchy kept him from seeking work; the trials of life eroded his spirit.Reflecting on the count’s life, I felt no bitterness, even at his worst. His natural gaiety faded into moroseness; he fell ill with a condition prone to hypochondria. Hidden love affairs further deteriorated his health and prospects.After twelve years of hardship he returned to France under a decree allowing emigrants to come back. Crossing the Rhine, he cried “France! France!” like a child calling for its mother. Once wealthy, he returned impoverished, stripped of authority and influence, burdened by his name. His unyielding opinions and past left him vulnerable to ridicule in a society that often mocked weakness. He eventually settled in Maine on a forgotten farm that gave him some stability.The Lenoncourt family, having lost their fortune, welcomed him; he stayed with them while preparing a home. Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt, rather than rejecting him, accepted his proposal and lived with her aunt, the Duchesse de Verneuil, who guided her life.Madame de Verneuil, a member of a devout society, profoundly influenced the countess, who remained devout. After her aunt’s death, Madame de Mortsauf received visits from Saint-Martin, who corrected his last works at Clochegourde. The duchess had endured trials and left Clochegourde to her niece, but her sudden death cast a shadow over the early years of the marriage.Monsieur de Mortsauf found some solace in the valley, but the birth of his first child, Jacques, brought despair: the doctor pronounced the child unviable, a truth the count concealed from his wife. Subsequent births confirmed his fears, deepening his sense of failure.The countess, sensing her husband’s turmoil, devoted herself to his care, striving to reconcile him with himself. She accepted his miserliness and mistrust, guiding him toward what was right, often at the expense of her own happiness. She resolved to remain at Clochegourde, fearing that leaving would harm their children. Her efforts masked the count’s incapacity, allowing him to find some peace in her presence.This brief history also illuminated Monsieur de Chessel, who, despite worldly knowledge, could not fully grasp Clochegourde’s complexities. My own understanding deepened as I spent time there and felt the weight of unspoken truths. One evening I found Madame de Mortsauf watching a sunset, and I sensed a shared sorrow.“Some days are hard to bear,” I remarked.“You read my soul,” she replied, surprised.“We touch at many points,” I said, recognizing our shared experiences of joy and sorrow.She shuddered, and I felt compelled to express my understanding of our mutual suffering.“Ah! My childhood was like a long illness—”“I hear Madeleine coughing,” she interrupted, and left abruptly.The countess welcomed my visits, seeing in me an amusement for her husband. I often played backgammon with the count, enduring his harshness and criticism. Despite his tyranny, the countess’s presence softened my hardships.As I grew better at the game, the count’s temper flared when I began to win. I learned to balance our matches—letting him win at first, then evening the score. The countess understood my strategy and thanked me with a grateful gaze that filled me with joy.One evening, after an intense game, the count’s mood shifted dramatically into rage. I left the room and waited in the garden. When she joined me, she revealed her husband’s condition and asked me to forget the incident.“Promise me to bury the recollection of that scene,” she implored. “I don’t ask you to swear it; just give me your word of honor.”“I will do all you wish,” I replied, “but I cannot change my opinion of him.”She acknowledged my feelings and urged me to understand his struggles.“Perhaps for my sake, you will forget what he said to you today,” she suggested. “He will wish to undo the wrong he did.”“Five days without seeing you?” I lamented.“Do not put such warmth into your manner of speaking to me,” she cautioned.We walked in silence; she eventually said, “It is late; we will part.”I wished to kiss her hand; she hesitated before allowing it.“Never take it unless I give it to you,” she warned. “Leave me my freedom.”As I departed I felt a profound connection, pledging my love in silence.The next day I reflected on our bond, feeling purpose and commitment to her. I recognized her sacrifices and strength. Our shared sufferings had forged a connection beyond friendship, binding us in a deep understanding of each other’s pain and resilience.In the days that followed, small moments deepened my affection. I was determined to support her, to be a source of strength, and to honor the bond we had formed. Navigating my feelings, I understood our connection was rare and precious, something I would cherish and protect. The love I felt for Madame de Mortsauf was more than passion; it was a commitment to stand by her, to share her struggles, and to celebrate the beauty of our shared existence.Beneath the stars I vowed to honor her spirit and nurture the love that had blossomed between us—an affection meant to endure through life’s trials.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This scene took place on a Tuesday. I had waited until Sunday and not crossed the river. During those five days much had happened at Clochegourde. The count received his brevet as general of brigade, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a pension of four thousand francs. The Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, made peer of France, regained two forests, resumed his place at court, and his wife reclaimed unsold property that had once been imperial crown lands. Consequently the Comtesse de Mortsauf became an heiress. Her mother arrived at Clochegourde, bringing a hundred thousand francs saved at Givry—the amount of her dowry, which the count had never demanded despite his poverty. With his own savings the count bought two neighboring estates yielding about nine thousand francs a year. His son would inherit the grandfather’s peerage; the count now saw a way to entail the estate upon him without harming Madeleine, and the Duc de Lenoncourt would likely help secure a good marriage.These events soothed the count’s troubled mind. The presence of the Duchesse de Lenoncourt at Clochegourde was significant for the neighborhood. I reflected gloomily on her caste spirit, which my love for Henriette had previously hidden beneath noble sentiments. Who was I—poor, insignificant, with no future but my courage and faculties? I did not think of the Restoration’s wider implications. On Sunday morning, in the private chapel where I sat with Monsieur and Madame de Chessel and the Abbé de Quelus, I glanced eagerly at the duchess’s chapel, where the duchess and her daughter, the count and his children, were praying. Henriette’s large straw hat did not tremble; her unconsciousness of my presence bound me to her more than our past had. She prayed with such abandonment that she seemed a religious statue, which moved me deeply.By village custom, vespers followed mass. After church, Madame de Chessel suggested going to Frapesle rather than cross the Indre twice in the heat. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to the duchess, Madame de Chessel took the count’s, and I offered mine to the countess, feeling her arm against my side for the first time. As we walked through the woods of Sache, light filtering through foliage, sensations and proud thoughts overwhelmed me; my heart beat violently.“What is the matter?” she asked after a moment.“I have heard of your good fortune,” I said. “Will your new dignities change you and lessen your friendship?”“Change me!” she cried. “Oh, fie! Another such idea and I shall—not despise you, but forget you forever.”I looked at her with ecstasy.“We profit by new laws which we have neither made nor sought,” she said. “We are neither place-hunters nor beggars; neither Monsieur de Mortsauf nor I can leave Clochegourde. By my advice he has declined the command at the Maison Rouge. We are content my father should have it. This forced modesty has benefited our son. The king, whose household my father joins, will favor Jacques’ education. He must represent both Lenoncourt and Mortsauf. I can have no ambition except for him; he must live and be made worthy. Who will keep him safe in Paris? My friend,” she said, her voice breaking, “be some day his guardian and sponsor. Go to Paris; if your family will not support you, ours—especially my mother, who rules society—will help you. Put the strength of your desires into a noble ambition—”“I understand,” I interrupted. “Ambition will be my mistress. I do not need it to be wholly yours. No, I will not be rewarded for my obedience here by favors there. I will go and make my own way. From you, I would accept everything; from others, nothing.”“Child!” she murmured, pleased.“Besides, I have taken my vows,” I continued. “I am resolved to bind myself to you by ties that can never be broken.”She trembled and stopped.“What do you mean?” she asked.“First tell me how you wish me to love you.”“Love me as my aunt loved me,” she answered. “I gave you her rights when I permitted you to call me by her chosen name.”“Then I must love without hope and with absolute devotion. I will enter a seminary, become a priest, and educate your son. Jacques shall be myself in his own form; political conceptions, energy, patience—I will give him all. I will live near you; my love, enclosed in religion, can never be suspected of evil. I will consume myself in the flame and love you with a purified love.”She turned pale. “Félix, do not bind yourself in a way that might be an obstacle. I should die of grief. Do you think despairing love a life’s vocation? Wait for life’s trials. Marry neither the Church nor a woman—marry not at all—I forbid it. Remain free. You are twenty-one—My God! can I have mistaken him? I thought two months sufficed to know some souls.”“What hope have you?” I cried.“Accept our help, rise in life, make your way and your fortune, and you shall know my hope. And,” she added as if whispering, “never release the hand you are holding at this moment.”She bent to my ear as she said it.“Madeleine!” I exclaimed. “Never!”We neared a wooden gate into the park of Frapesle. Suddenly an idea—the count’s death—flashed through me, and I said, “I understand you.”“I am glad of it,” she answered, as though surprised at my thought.Her purity drew tears of admiration that mingled with the bitterness of passion. I thought she did not love me enough even to wish me liberty; love that recoils from crime seems limited. “She does not love me,” I thought.To hide my pain, I kissed Madeleine’s hair. “I am afraid of your mother,” I said to the countess.“So am I,” she answered, playful. “Don’t forget to call her Madame la duchesse and speak to her in the third person. Young people have lost polite manners; you must learn them for my sake. It is good taste to respect women, no matter their age, and to recognize social distinctions. The respect shown to established superiority guarantees what is due to you. You have drunk the milk of the Revolution in your academy; but as you advance, you will find crude principles of liberty powerless to create happiness. Before considering what an aristocracy ought to be, my common sense tells me societies can exist only through a hierarchy. Be on our side—especially now,” she added, laughing, “when it triumphs.”I was touched by her combination of political feeling and affection. In defending her husband she had foreseen criticisms I might make on seeing the servility he showed her mother. Monsieur de Mortsauf, king in his castle, had a certain dignity; yet I was astonished at the obsequious distance he placed between himself and the duchess. Humiliated by his degradation, I understood the martyrdom of generous women yoked to petty men. I was respectful to the duchess because of my youth; but where others saw only a duchess, I saw Henriette’s mother, and that gave sanctity to my homage.At Frapesle the count presented me to the duchess, who examined me coldly. Madame de Lenoncourt was fifty-six, wonderfully preserved, erect and imposing, with a hard face. Her courtly manner reminded me of my own mother. I behaved well; as I passed the countess she said softly, “You are perfect.”The count took my hand. “You are not angry with me, Félix, are you? If I was hasty, pardon an old soldier. We shall probably stay for dinner, and I invite you on Thursday, the evening before the duchess leaves. I must go to Tours tomorrow. Don’t neglect Clochegourde. My mother-in-law is an acquaintance I advise you to cultivate. Her salon will set the tone for the faubourg St. Germain. She has all the traditions of the great world and immense social knowledge.”The count’s changed circumstances showed in his softened manner. The duchess was not patronizing; Monsieur and Madame de Chessel accepted the invitation. The duchess questioned me about my family and, hearing that the Vandenesse in diplomacy was my brother, became almost affectionate. She told me my great-aunt, the old Marquise de Listomere, was a Grandlieu, and that by a provision of the Charter my father was once more Marquis de Vandenesse.“I am but one thing, the serf of Clochegourde,” I said to the countess.The Restoration’s transformation bewildered our generation. To me, the revolution meant nothing; the least word from Madame de Mortsauf was the sole event of importance. I was ignorant of privy councils and politics; my sole ambition was to love Henriette better than Petrarch loved Laura. This indifference made the duchess take me for a child. A large company assembled at Frapesle, and to a young man unused to the world, seeing the woman he loves admired by all is intoxicating. The count, delighted, seemed almost young; his wife hoped for change; I amused myself with Madeleine, whose clever observations made others laugh. It was a happy day. A word, a hope awakened in the morning and illuminated nature. Seeing me joyous, Henriette was joyful too.“That happiness smiling on my gray and cloudy life seems good,” she said to me the next day.I spent that day at Clochegourde. The count left early for Tours. A serious disagreement arose between mother and daughter. The duchess wanted the countess to move to Paris, where she promised a place at court and where the count might accept a position. Henriette, hiding her husband’s incapacity and her own sufferings, opposed leaving Clochegourde because its air and sky calmed his excitements and soothed her children’s health. She had persuaded him to go to Tours to transact business so as to hide his condition from the duchess. I alone knew Clochegourde’s dark secret.Henriette saw that the duchess cared little for Jacques and Madeleine—a painful discovery. The duchess brooked no opposition; she used sweetness, then cold severity, then sarcasm, to force compliance. In those ten days Henriette endured all the contentions a young woman must face to establish independence. To picture their struggle, imagine a lily bruised under a polished wheel. That mother had nothing in common with her daughter; she could not even imagine the difficulties that forced Henriette to remain at Clochegourde. Families hide internal dissensions; behind scenes almost all contain deep wounds that lessen natural affections. Tortured and wounded, Henriette loved the one who did not strike her, who would build a shield around her. I was happy when she rested upon my heart; she told me of her troubles. Day by day I learned more fully what she meant by “Love me as my aunt loved me.”“Have you no ambition?” the duchess asked me at dinner.“Madame,” I replied, “I have enough in me to conquer the world; but I am only twenty-one, and I am all alone.”She looked at her daughter in astonishment. The duchess’s visit meant constraint. The countess begged me to be cautious. The great Thursday was a day of wearisome ceremonial. But the duchess returned to court pomps and Clochegourde recovered its order.My quarrel with the count had made me more at home; I could go there at all times. I clung like a plant to the beautiful soul that had opened the world of shared emotions. Our fraternal marriage, founded on trust, became surer; Henriette enfolded me with maternal care, while my love for her burned like hot irons when away. I loved her with a double love—seraphic in her presence, searing when absent. At my age I was not yet man enough to torture her, who feared catastrophe to her children, who sat beside them when her husband allowed her a little rest. The mere sound of ardor shook her; she needed veiled love, support mingled with tenderness—that which she gave to others. This situation brought delightful languor and secret immolation. Her conscience and self-devotion awed me; the piety that was the bond of her virtues filled the air with spiritual incense. She gave me only the back of her hand to kiss, never the palm, as though drawing the line of sensual emotions. No two souls ever clasped each other with such ardor yet no earthly tie bound us. Later I understood why that sufficed: in youth we love the whole woman, her children and interests, as our own.I was now one of the household and knew infinite sweetness—the privilege of being the mistress’s friend in a strange family. Servants treated me well; the children divined my affection and were discreet. True passions bloom more charmingly in barren soil.But I also saw the full extent of Monsieur de Mortsauf’s failings. He scolded perpetually about nothing, complained of imaginary evils, and had an inward dissatisfaction that demanded victims. On walks he selected the way and was bored whichever way we went; at home he blamed others. He found domineering pleasure in touching his wife’s sensitive chords. Sometimes he fell into gloomy silence that alarmed her and brought tender attentions. Like petted children, he let her serve him as she did Jacques and Madeleine. I discovered his petty tyranny in the household details and realized how it stifled the family, hindered management, and retarded improvement of the estate. The countess had been noble in describing these daily struggles. My admiration for her rose higher than my love; I yielded to be a voluntary victim to come nearer her. She allowed me a place beside her and permission to share her sorrows; eager to die in her arena, I became a repentant apostate seeking heaven with my brethren.“Were it not for you, I must have succumbed under this life,” Henriette said when the count had been more venomous than usual.He had gone to bed. Henriette and I stayed under the acacias with the children playing in the setting sun. Our few words revealed mutual thoughts; when language failed our silence served, and our souls seemed to enter one another. We luxuriated in pensive languor and asked by a look, “Among all our days on earth, will there be one for us?”Despite the evening’s poetry, desire crept through me. After three months of repression I was unable to accept my assigned fate. I took Henriette’s hand and caressed it. She at once withdrew it; tears from my eyes fell and she offered her hand to my lips.“You must know,” she said, “that this will cause me grief. A friendship that asks such a favor is dangerous.”Then I lost control; I reproached her, spoke of my sufferings and of a slight alleviation I dared ask. I told her that at my age the soul had a sex and that I could meet death but not silence my lips. She forced me to silence with a proud glance. Ever since the gate of Frapesle, when I attributed to her the hope that our happiness might spring from a grave, I had shunned the thought of staining her soul. She said she could never be wholly mine; if I could not be to her somewhat as her old confessor—less than a lover yet more than a brother—she must never see me again. She could die and offer her sufferings to God.“I gave you,” she said, “more than I ought, so that nothing might be left to take, and I am punished.”I calmed her, promised never to cause pain, and to love her as an old man loves his youngest child.The next day I came early; there were no flowers in her gray salon. I rushed into the fields and vineyards to make two bouquets; as I gathered them it occurred to me that color and foliage have meanings, and like melody awaken memory. I enlisted Jacques and Madeleine and arranged two bouquets on the portico steps to convey a sentiment—white roses and lilies, with blue flowers intermingled, symbolizing two purities: the child and the martyr. The countess discerned it inwardly and gave me a poignant glance that was reward enough. I pressed the theories of Père Castel into service and recovered a lost science: the language of flowers. What charm in expressing our sensations through these daughters of the sun! I communed with the flora of the fields, seeking varieties that would complete my messages.Twice a week I pursued this poetic enterprise, walking long distances to find flowers, initiating myself into pleasures unknown to the man of science—a poet studying spirit rather than form. Nature offered scenes whose significance rose to the highest moral conceptions: heather with dew like diamonds, a mossy rock-clad corner like a savage cry, anemone pulsatilla like my pure idol alone in her valley, sheets of water with floating worlds of life, a cottage garden as the image of humble existence, or a forest path like a cathedral nave. I gathered such images and poured them into bouquets.In September and October no bouquet cost less than three hours’ search.
