These floral symphonies expressed what no declaration of love could; my baffled desires found expression as Beethoven found in notes. In their presence, Madame de Mortsauf was my Henriette. She looked at them constantly; they nourished her spirit. These neutral pleasures soothed me: abstinence brings deadly exhaustion, and a few crumbs of heavenly comfort keep one from disaster. Sometimes I found her standing before the bouquets lost in agitation; she would lift her head and murmur, “Ah, how beautiful!”Throughout the month I often saw her at the window at my arrival. If I was late, she wandered on the terrace and told me she had come to meet me and tend her youngest child.Backgammon had ended. The count’s purchases engaged him inspecting his new properties. He was neither arrogant nor offensive, and our walks together were mutual joys. On one occasion the count, wishing to know what we talked about, asked; Henriette answered in words with a double meaning that satisfied him. Madeleine laughed. Our spiritual union continued.The days passed with new joys. The grape harvest—the vintage—began in late September. It is easier to gather grapes than to mow wheat. The vintage brings abundance and hilarity: coopers and carts, girls laughing, men and panniers. Classes are equal for the day; everyone shares the harvest. Jacques and Madeleine, who had never seen a vintage, were full of delight. We went to Villaines for baskets; none of us were to eat too many grapes. Jacques made me swear to stay at Clochegourde. The day in the vineyard was glorious: the children’s chatter, their racing about, the mother laughing with the joy of youth; she passed a hand round my neck and tapped my cheek, “You are melting away”—the only caress she ever gave me. I filled my basket and emptied it into the vat, moving slowly in regulated motion that kept my spirit free. I found the pleasure of external labor that regulates passion; I learned monastic discipline.For the first time in long weeks the count was neither surly nor cruel. His son’s rosy face rejoiced him. He had promised a dance in front of Clochegourde to celebrate the Bourbons’ return. As we returned, the countess leaned on my arm and whispered, “You bring us happiness.”To me, burdened with knowledge of her sleepless nights and cares, those words were more than any other woman could give.“The terrible monotony of my life is broken; all things are radiant with hope,” she said. “Oh, never leave me! Be the elder son, the protector of the younger.”This is not romantic; in youth passion restrained by obstacles can fill the crater of a volcano with pure water.We had another fête. The countess, wishing to accustom her children to practical life, gave Jacques the produce of the walnut trees and Madeleine that of the chestnuts. Gathering chestnuts and walnuts, seeing the children take pride in their small granaries, the petty joys of harvest and calculation, made charming scenes of innocent happiness amid autumn’s fading colors. Jacques’ walnuts suffered from rain; I advised him to hold back and sell later. His father urged prudence and reserve funds; the mother rejoiced to see Jacques listen. These homely scenes attached me ever more closely to Henriette.I learned how the countess had, over ten years, changed cultivation methods—rotating crops and dividing the estate into four farms on share leases. She induced the count to cultivate a model farm and rebuild two farmhouses to relet for money rents afterward. Fearing to die before her husband, she sought to secure his income and her children’s property. Fruit trees planted in the last ten years were in full bearing; hedges and elms grew well. With the new purchases and farming system, Clochegourde—divided into four farms—could bring in forty thousand francs a year, not counting vineyards and woodlands. The new road to Chinon would make tenants plentiful. The countess wished to invest fifteen thousand francs in each new estate to improve houses so they would yield better rents; this required thirty thousand francs she and her husband argued about. The fear “If I die tomorrow, what will become of them?” drove her. The count objected, but common sense carried the day. That day we went to the Cassine and the Rhetorière to decide on buildings; she spoke gently, confident of success. A carrier wanted a large farm on the new route; another honest man was ready to take la Baude; the soil would attract tenants. In two years Clochegourde might yield twenty-four thousand francs. Gravelotte in Maine was rented for seven thousand a year; his pension was four thousand. Later additions might let her go to Paris and arrange Jacques’ education.With what feeling she uttered “Paris!” I knew she wished not to be separated from her friend. I told her I would finish my education and work to be Jacques’ tutor. She looked grave.“No, Félix,” she said. “That cannot be, any more than your priesthood. I thank you as a mother, but as a woman who loves you, I can never allow you to be the victim of your attachment. Such a position would be a social discredit to you. My father would never forgive your taking such a humble place; he would refuse you his protection. You must accept society as it is; never fly in its face.”“Love,” I whispered.“No, charity,” she said, controlling tears. “This wild idea enlightens me as to your character; your heart will be your bane. I shall claim the right to teach you sometimes. Let my woman’s eye see for you. From the solitudes of Clochegourde I will share in your successes. As to a tutor, do not fear; we shall find some good old abbé, my father will devote sums to the boy’s education. Jacques is my pride; he is eleven years old.”At the Cassine Jacques, Madeleine, and I followed her like children. I left to speak with Martineau and saw how beloved she was. A laborer told me she was good and knew our hardships and treated the people well. I gave that man all the money I had.A pony arrived for Jacques; the father, an excellent horseman, would accustom him gradually. The morning of the first lesson, with Madeleine shouting and all the household clustered round, was a maternal festival. Jacques wore a blue collar embroidered by his mother and rode fearlessly. The count smiled for the first time in months; the mother burst into tears and leaned on my arm, “I think I have never suffered. Do not leave us today.” The child jumped into her arms; I and Madeleine arranged bouquets for dinner. She said, “The fifteenth of October is certainly a great day for me. Jacques has taken his first riding lesson, and I have just set the last stitch in my furniture cover.”“Then, Blanche,” the count laughed, “I must pay you for it.”He offered her his arm; an open carriage her father had sent awaited with two English horses. As we returned, she whispered anxiously, “I am too happy; happiness is like illness—it overwhelms me; I fear it may vanish.”I loved her so passionately I was jealous. She asked me what my eyes said; I told her. She was more touched than by any gift, then poured comfort into my heart. “Love me as my aunt loved me,” she said. “If I take your life, must I not be grateful?”“It was time I finished my tapestry,” she added. “Men find occupations a resource against troubles; we women need physical exercise to control inward sufferings. Each stitch rocked my thoughts; to every stitch I confided my secrets. While doing my last chair I thought much of you. What you put into your bouquets I have said in my embroidery.”Dinner was lovely. Jacques offered his garland to his mother with charming grace. In the afternoon we played backgammon; the count was charming. They accompanied me to Frapesle in the tranquil twilight. It was a day of days for the poor woman.Soon the riding lessons became a source of contention. The countess feared the father's harsh reprimands; Jacques grew thin and wore dark circles. I advised him to tell his father when tired, but the expedient failed, and the old huntsman took over. Angry reproaches resumed. Then the cost of the Cassine and Rhetorière buildings exceeded estimates by half. The news, given first to Monsieur de Mortsauf instead of his wife, became the ground of a quarrel that grew bitter until the count’s malady demanded its arrearages.That day I had gone from Frapesle to search for flowers with Madeleine. Returning, I could not find her and heard cries in the house. Madeleine ran out: “The general,” she cried, “is scolding mamma; go and defend her.”I reached the salon without being seen. Monsieur de Mortsauf was in violent tirade.“Never marry, Félix,” he said. “A woman is led by the devil; the most virtuous would invent evil if it did not exist; they are all vile.”Arguments flew. He harked back to old troubles, cursed the new farming system, declared he could have been twice as rich if he had managed Clochegourde. He sprang around, displacing furniture; then stopped, moaned his marrow was on fire, that his wife had ruined him. The countess smiled and looked upward.“Yes, Blanche,” he cried, “you are my executioner; you are killing me; you are a monster of hypocrisy. She is smiling! Do you know why she smiles, Félix?”I kept silence.“That woman denies me all happiness; she is no more to me than to you, and yet she pretends to be my wife! She fulfills none of the duties of laws, human and divine; she lies to God and man. She puts all her art into keeping me away; she has made me mad through privations; she is killing me by degrees, and she thinks herself a saint and takes the sacrament every month!”Henriette wept bitterly, answering only, “Monsieur! monsieur! monsieur!”The count’s words made me burn with shame for him and stirred my heart because they struck at chastity and delicacy—the warp and woof of first love.“She is virgin at my expense,” cried the count.At this, the countess cried, “Monsieur!”“And what mean you by your imperious ‘Monsieur!’?” he shouted. “Am I not your master? Must I teach you that I am?”He came toward her, thrusting forward his white wolf’s head; his yellow eyes looked savage. Henriette slid from her chair to avoid a blow, lay at full length, and lost consciousness. The count stopped bewildered. I took her in my arms; the count opened the door of her bedroom. We laid her, still dressed, on the bed. When she revived she motioned to unfasten her belt; the count found scissors; I made her breathe salts and she opened her eyes. The count left the room, ashamed. Two hours passed in silence. Henriette’s hand lay in mine; she pressed it but could not speak. Once she rose on her elbow and whispered, “Unhappy man!—ah! if you did but know—” then sank back. The memory of past sufferings and the present shock threw her into convulsions I calmed by an instinctive magnetism. I smoothed her hair—the first time I touched it—and sat looking at the simple room, its chintz curtains, its crucifix, her aunt’s portrait, the children’s drawings with locks of hair. What renunciations of luxury for such a woman—the chamber of a married nun, filled with holy resignation. Painful as the sight was, it exposed hidden, irreparable woe.When the children and Manette came in I left. The count waited for me; he seemed to seek me as mediator. He caught my hands, exclaiming, “Stay, stay with us, Félix!”“Unfortunately,” I said, “Monsieur de Chessel has a party, and my absence would cause remark. But after dinner I will return.”He left the house with me and took me to the lower gate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As I read the letter, I felt the maternal heart beneath my fingers, a warmth sharply at odds with my own mother's coldness. I understood why the countess had forbidden me to open it in Touraine; she likely feared I would break down in tears.I met my brother Charles, who until then had been almost a stranger. His haughtiness blocked any brotherly affection. He lectured me on social matters I already knew, and his mistrust made me awkward. If not for my deep love, his condescension would have left me feeling foolish. He presented me in society, expecting my dullness to highlight his superiority. Remembering childhood sorrows, I recognized his vanity for what it was, not brotherly love. My solitude sharpened my perception of the affections around me; since returning from Clochegourde, I could compare experiences and realized that happiness clarifies understanding.I often visited the Duchesse de Lenoncourt, where Henriette's name was seldom mentioned. Only the old duke, who welcomed me warmly, seemed to know of our connection. Just as I began to overcome my shyness and enjoy social pleasures, the events of March 20th unfolded.Following my brother to Ghent, I went there on Henriette's advice, accompanying the Duc de Lenoncourt. The duke's kindness turned into sincere protection as he saw my loyalty to the Bourbons. He introduced me to the king, who valued my devotion during difficult times. A letter from Madame de Mortsauf informed me of Jacques' illness, and I sensed her distress. She needed support while caring for her son, and despite any ambition to rival my brother, I prioritized her sorrow. I decided to leave the court and serve my true sovereign. The king needed a trustworthy messenger, and I gladly accepted the mission, eager to return to Clochegourde.After carrying out the king's orders in France, I was forced to flee Bonapartist authorities. I traveled through Vendée and reached Clochegourde, where I learned Jacques was better. The count welcomed me, and in the salon I was introduced to Henriette, who was surprised and joyful to see me. We exchanged heartfelt greetings; she prepared my room while the count provided food.At dinner I learned of the disasters at Waterloo and Napoleon's flight, but my primary concern was Henriette's well-being. She had built an ice-house for me; her efforts to provide iced water testified to her affection. Over the following week she regained health and spirits, and I marveled at her beauty, realizing that absence had only intensified my love.Henriette described nights by Jacques' bedside and prayers for his life; her devotion was plain. Yet the count's harshness interrupted her reflections and caused her pain. She wondered why her words never found favor with him, revealing the emotional toll of their relationship. Despite her struggles, she maintained duty toward her family, urging her children to respect their father.As we discussed the estate, Henriette told me of their agricultural success, and I explained my mission for the king. She had a remarkable faculty for foreseeing events concerning her children, which she attributed to divine intervention. I admired her wisdom and felt grateful for her guidance.Henriette encouraged me to pursue ambitions in Paris, stressing the importance of being present for the king's needs. Our bond deepened as we shared thoughts and dreams, and I felt a growing sense of belonging in her life. Yet the count's presence loomed, and I sensed tension in their marriage.One day she confided in me about her struggles with the count's behavior, fearing for her children's well-being. She felt trapped where duty to family conflicted with personal happiness. I urged her to assert herself, but she hesitated, fearful of the consequences.As the count's illness worsened, Henriette and I took turns caring for him. Our shared duty brought us closer; I took joy in supporting her. The count's recovery was slow, and though it marked a turning point, it also resurrected his erratic behavior. Henriette's patience was tested as she struggled to maintain authority; I witnessed the toll it took on her.When the count regained strength he became more difficult. Henriette remained determined to care for her family, often putting her children's needs above her own. I admired her selflessness, yet felt the weight of my feelings for her and the complexity added by the count's presence.We found moments of happiness: intimate conversations, shared vulnerabilities, mutual support. These strengthened our connection, though always shadowed by the count's influence. I reassured Henriette of my unwavering support when she expressed fears about the future, sharing a deep sense of connection even as her reality weighed on us both.Eventually I returned to Paris, consumed by responsibilities but still committed to Henriette. Our correspondence sustained us; her letters revealed the depth of her love and the burden she bore. News later reached me of Jacques' relapse—the toll on her was palpable—and I felt helpless to ease it. Despite my own struggles, I remained devoted, hoping to offer support.Throughout, Henriette's resilience and maternal devotion shone. She navigated obligations with courage, often sacrificing personal happiness for her children and household duties. My loyalty to her only grew; our love was a guiding light amid adversity, a rare and precious bond I cherished and remained hopeful would endure.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################During a deeply emotional period in my life, I encountered a remarkable woman at the Élysée-Bourbon salons. Immensely wealthy, of noble lineage and married to a distinguished British peer, her beauty, grace, and wit made her the idol of Parisian society, where she ruled with a firm yet charming demeanor.The English, distant and reserved, inhabit a world of strict laws and rigid virtues. This upbringing produces a particular Englishwoman who, though delicate and charming, is trapped in hypocrisy and compelled to maintain appearances; her passion, like Shakespeare’s, is intense but constrained.You may envy these women, but they are complex, convinced that love alone suffices. My liaison with the Marchioness of Dudley became notorious. At a time when my better feelings were subdued, I was drawn to her; my fidelity to Madame de Mortsauf only increased her desire. Lady Dudley craved the chase, and my resistance only inflamed her passion. Her remark about my relationship with Madame de Mortsauf—“I am wearied to death of these turtle-dove sighings”—should have warned me.Men have fewer means to resist women than women have to escape men. Social codes keep men from appearing harsh; women are expected to be coy. Though I loved Madame de Mortsauf, I was not immune to Lady Dudley’s beauty and rank. Her attention in society stirred me, and she often hinted she would be my mistress. After one particularly beguiling evening she made her intentions clear, provoking a scandal that shocked the British aristocracy.Seemingly fragile, this woman possessed a strong will. Her physical courage was remarkable—she could outpace any horse—and she pursued her desires relentlessly. By contrast, Madame de Mortsauf was nurturing and expansive, drawing others into her warmth; Lady Dudley was intense and consuming.English customs reflect a materialist bent, valuing comfort and order often at the expense of spiritual depth. Lady Dudley embodied that materialism: compelling physically but leaving an emptiness I did not feel with Madame de Mortsauf, whose love was spiritual and boundless.Lady Dudley’s jealousy was masked by charm; she sought to erase Madame de Mortsauf from my heart. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf, but my letters went unanswered, filling me with dread. When I finally returned to Clochegourde, a coldness revealed the harm my infidelity had caused.Madame de Mortsauf’s demeanor had altered; she was distant and sorrowful, her beauty dimmed by grief. I felt the weight of my betrayal as I watched her withdraw, pride and pain evident. The family gathered around me, but I perceived her retreat. The count, unaware of the turmoil, welcomed me warmly, while Madame de Mortsauf remained aloof.Witnessing her, I realized the depth of my wrongdoing. I had brought her suffering, and my heart ached for the love I had jeopardized. The count, whose own health was fragile, expressed concern for his wife and confessed his fears, sharing the emotional toll he bore.Madame de Mortsauf’s health seemed delicate, and I was tormented by the thought that I was its cause. I longed to make amends, but she remained guarded, her heart closed. Our conversations were strained, full of unspoken words and lingering pain.The count’s condition declined, and I felt helpless as I watched its effect on Madame de Mortsauf. Devoted to her children, she sought solace in their well-being, yet shadows of despair crept into her life. I was torn between love for her and my entanglement with Lady Dudley, whose presence still loomed.In desperation I sought Madame de Mortsauf’s forgiveness, but she rebuffed me, insisting I had caused her too much pain. Left to grapple with guilt, I felt the weight of my actions pressing down on me.As I spent more time with the family, I observed further changes in Madame de Mortsauf. Her once-vibrant spirit was dulled; I feared for her health and resolved to seek atonement, though the path seemed fraught.The tension between us grew and the distance widened. I was caught in a web of my own making, torn between two women who represented different aspects of love: Lady Dudley’s passionate, material allure and Madame de Mortsauf’s nurturing devotion. I was left to navigate the complexities of my heart.In the end I understood that choices have consequences. I was forced to confront the true nature of love and sacrifice; my desires had duties attached, and the harm I caused those I cared for weighed heavily on me.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I know nothing more destructive than the wit of an Englishwoman; it carries the pompous conviction with which the British mask their prejudices. French wit is like lace, adorning life’s joys and quarrels; English wit is corrosive, stripping flesh until only bone remains. Arabella wielded her power like a sultan, cutting down rivals with her words.“My angel,” she said, “I can speak morality too. I’ve questioned whether my love for you is a crime, and I find it both natural and pious. God created beauty to inspire adoration. The real crime would be not to love you. That other woman insults you by comparing you to men; the laws of morality do not apply to you, for you are above them. Am I not drawing closer to divine love by loving you? Will God punish a woman for seeking the divine? My love is a weakness, while that woman’s is strength. Don’t frown; I do not wish her ill. I adore the morality that has freed you for me—because you are mine forever, aren’t you?”“Yes.”“Forever and ever?”“Yes.”“Ah! I have found favor in my lord! I alone understand his worth! You say she knows how to cultivate her estate. I leave that to farmers; I cultivate your heart.”I try to recall this intoxicating babble to convey Arabella and what followed. How to describe the passion mixed with her words? She sought to obliterate the impression left by Henriette’s dignified love with impetuous affection. Arabella’s fierce attack revealed fear and admiration of her rival. The next morning she was tearful, lamenting a sleepless night.“What troubles you?” I asked.“I fear my love will ruin me,” she replied. “I have given all. That woman possesses something you still desire. If you prefer her, forget me; I will not trouble you. I will go far and die, like a plant deprived of sunlight.”She extracted reluctant promises and was filled with joy.“Ah!” she exclaimed, drying her eyes. “I am happy. Go back to her; I do not want to owe you to the force of my love, but to your own will. If you return, I shall know you love me as much as I love you, which I always doubted.”She persuaded me to return to Clochegourde. Under her influence I did not feel the false position I had placed myself in. Had I refused to return, I would have given Lady Dudley a triumph over Henriette. Arabella planned to take me to Paris; to go to Clochegourde now would insult Madame de Mortsauf—Arabella was sure of me. Would any loving woman forgive crimes against love? Unless she were an angel, a loving woman would rather see her lover die than know him happy with another. Thus, leaving Clochegourde for the Grenadiere was fatal to my true love and beneficial to the transient affection that held me. Lady Dudley had calculated skillfully.When I met the countess that morning, she was pale and depressed, like one who had not slept. The perception still given to generous hearts revealed the nature of actions the world ignores but lofty spirits call criminal. Like a child playing near a precipice, I realized she and I were separated by a universe. A wail rose within us, echoing the woeful “Consummatum est” of Good Friday. Henriette’s illusions were shattered; her heart had endured its passion. She refused to look at me, denying me the light that had shone on my life for six years. I felt the wrong of bringing into this roof, where pleasure was unknown, a face that had known joy. If I had let Lady Dudley depart alone and returned to Clochegourde, perhaps Henriette would not have resolved to treat me as a brother. But now she paid me ostentatious attentions, humiliating, like caring for a sick man.“You were out walking early,” said the count. “I hope you have brought back a good appetite, you whose stomach is not yet destroyed.”That remark, which made Henriette smile, completed my sense of ridicule. Day and night I could not be at Clochegourde and Saint-Cyr. By day it was difficult to befriend again the woman I had long loved; the transition, though possible in time, felt like an illness. I was ashamed; I cursed the pleasure Lady Dudley gave me; I wished Henriette would demand my blood. I could not tear her rival apart before her, for she avoided speaking of Arabella. Had I mentioned her, Henriette would have despised my infamy. After five years of delightful intercourse, we had nothing to say; our words had no connection with our thoughts; we hid intolerable pain from each other.Henriette feigned cheerfulness, but she was sad. She called herself my sister, yet we had no common ground and remained in constrained silence. She increased my misery by pretending to be the only victim.“I suffer more than you,” I said at one point.“How so?” she asked haughtily.“Because I am the one to blame.”Eventually her manner grew so cold that I resolved to leave Clochegourde. That evening on the terrace I bade farewell. As I took my horse, she said, “Let us walk down the avenue together, alone.” We walked slowly. At the grove marking the boundary, she stopped.“Farewell, my friend,” she said, resting her head on my chest and wrapping her arms around me. “Farewell, we shall never meet again. God has given me the sad power to see the future. Do you remember the terror when you first returned, so young, so handsome? I saw you turn your back on me as you do now. Once again, I have seen into the future. Friend, we speak for the last time. I can hardly say a few words; part of me speaks. Death has seized something in me. You have taken the mother from her children; I ask you to take her place. You can; Jacques and Madeleine love you, as if you had always made them suffer.”“Die!” I cried, frightened at the fire in her eyes. “Henriette, I command you to live. Swear to me you will send for Origet and obey him in everything.”“Would you oppose God’s mercy?” she cried, misreading me.“You do not love me enough to obey blindly, as that miserable Lady Dudley does?”“Yes, yes, I will do all you ask,” she cried, goaded by jealousy.“Then I stay,” I said, kissing her eyelids.Frightened, she escaped my arms and walked home without looking back. I followed her; she was weeping and praying. We reached the lawn; I took and respectfully kissed her hand. This submission touched her.“I am yours—forever, and as you will,” I said; “for I love you as your aunt loved you.”She trembled and wrung my hand.“One look,” I said, “one last of our old looks! The woman who gives herself wholly,” I cried, “gives less of life and soul than I have now received. Henriette, thou art my best-beloved—my only love.”“I shall live!” she said; “but cure yourself as well.”The look erased Arabella’s memory. Thus I was the plaything of two irreconcilable passions; I was influenced alternately. I loved an angel and a demon; two women equally beautiful—one adorned with virtues we decry through hatred of our imperfections, the other with vices we deify through selfishness. Returning down the avenue, seeing Madame de Mortsauf surrounded by her children waving handkerchiefs, I felt pride in being arbiter of two such destinies; proud of inspiring such great passions that death must come to whichever I abandoned. That passing conceit was doubly punished.I do not know what demon prompted me to remain with Arabella and await the moment the count’s death might give me Henriette, for she would ever love me. Her harshness, tears, remorse, and Christian resignation testified to a feeling that could not be erased. Walking down that avenue, I was no longer twenty-five; I was fifty. A man passes quickly from youth to middle age. Perhaps these evil thoughts came from the Tuileries, from the king’s cabinet. Who could resist the polluting spirit of Louis XVIII?When I reached the end of the avenue, I rushed back—Henriette was still there, alone. I went to bid her a last farewell, bathed in repentant tears, given, though unconsciously, to noble loves forever lost, to virgin emotions that cannot bloom again. Later, a man gives nothing; he receives; he loves himself in his mistress; but in youth he loves his mistress in himself. Woe to the man who has not had his Henriette. Woe to him who has never known a Lady Dudley. Joy to him who can find the two women in one.After my return to Paris, Arabella and I became more intimate. We soon abandoned the conventional restrictions I had tried to keep—the external comedies and concealments that renew desire and protect the heart. Young passions, like youth, raze their forests instead of cutting timber. Arabella adopted none of these bourgeois strategies; she wished to exhibit me to all Paris as her “sposo.” She used her powers of seduction to keep me under her roof, not content with rumored scandal whispered behind fans. Seeing her happy in an imprudence that frankly admitted her position, how could I not believe her love?But once plunged into the comforts of our illegal union, despair seized me; I saw my life bound to a course defying Henriette’s ideas and advice. From then on I lived in a rage like consumptives who, knowing death near, cannot bear scrutiny. There was no corner of my heart where I could escape suffering; an avenging spirit filled me with thoughts I dared not dwell on. My letters to Henriette depicted this moral malady and did her infinite harm. “At the cost of so many treasures lost, I wished you to be at least happy,” she wrote in the only answer I received. But I was not happy. Happiness is absolute; it admits no comparisons. Once first ardor fades, comparisons arise. All great passions first check character’s asperities; later, when lovers are accustomed, moral physiognomies reappear and natural antipathies surface. This period began with me. Less blinded, I undertook a critical examination of Lady Dudley that harmed her.First, she lacked the qualities of mind that distinguish Frenchwomen. When a Frenchwoman loves, she metamorphoses: coquetry adorns love; she abandons dangerous vanity and claims no merit but loving well. She espouses her lover’s interests and acquires experience in a day; heedless and prodigal by nature, she manages with grace. She becomes mother, adviser, doctor, giving transformations a grace that reveals infinite love. She combines other nations’ special qualities and unifies them with wit that enlivens and varies love, relieving monotony. The Frenchwoman loves always, in public or solitude; in public she speaks by silence or looks; if speech and look fail, she finds other means. The Englishwoman, by contrast, makes her love bend to the world. Educated to icy manners, she opens and shuts her heart with a British mechanism. Passionate when no eye sees her, she is cold before the world. A lover doubts his empire seeing her immobility, her aloof countenance, the calm voice with which she leaves her boudoir. Hypocrisy becomes indifference; she has forgotten all.A woman who can lay aside her love like a garment may be thought capable of changing it. What tempests in a man’s heart when he sees a woman take and drop her love like embroidery. These women, too completely mistress of themselves, never wholly belong to you; society’s influence prevents you reigning supreme. Where a Frenchwoman comforts with a look, an Englishwoman’s silence irritates and frets. They are so on their dignity that fashion often reigns over pleasures. An Englishwoman forces everything into form; Protestantism’s doubt and reasoning kill belief; it is the death of art and love. Passionate hearts flee worldliness.You can understand my shock when I found Lady Dudley could not live without the world, and the English system of two lives was natural to her. When she loved, she loved madly—no woman could compare; but when the curtain fell, she banished memory of it. In public she never answered a look or smile; she was ambassadress-like, folding her phrases and elbows; her composure outraged my heart. She degraded love to a need rather than raising it to an ideal. She expressed neither fear nor regret nor desire; then, at a given hour, tenderness reappeared like a sudden fire.Which of the two should I believe? By a thousand pin-pricks I felt the infinite differences between Henriette and Arabella. Madame de Mortsauf’s slightest departures reminded me of her: the sound of her gown, her lowered eyelids, her musical voice—a continual caress—her consistent thought. She did not halve her soul for two atmospheres. Arabella’s mind existed for the world, spent in sarcasm; she loved to rend and bite to satisfy craving. Madame de Mortsauf would have hidden her happiness; Lady Dudley chose to exhibit hers to all Paris yet kept an impenetrable English mask in public. This mixture of ostentation and dignity, love and coldness, wounded me. When I complained, she turned her tongue with triple sting, mingling boasts of love with cutting English sarcasms. In opposition she made amusement of hurting me, kneading me like dough. To any remark about keeping a medium, she caricatured my ideas. She asked if I wished her to kiss me at the opera before all Paris, seriously enough that I trembled she might. Despite passion, she was never meditative, self-contained, or reverent like Henriette; she was insatiable for excitement, noise, and celebrity; everything concerned herself, not me.Henriette’s letter you have read—proof of how this virtuous woman obeyed the genius of a Frenchwoman—shows how she had studied my prospects, political relations, moral conquests, and with what ardor she took hold of my life in permissible directions. Lady Dudley affected the reticence of an acquaintance. She never informed herself about my affairs or likings as a man. Prodigal for herself without being generous, she separated self-interest and love too decidedly. I knew, without proving it, that to save me a pang, Henriette would have sought for me what she would never seek for herself. In any great misfortune I would have gone to Henriette for counsel; I would have been dragged to prison sooner than say a word to Lady Dudley.The contrast extended to outward things. In France luxury expresses the man; it reproduces his ideas and gives attention value between lovers. English luxury, which at first allured by choiceness, proved mechanical. The thousand attentions at Clochegourde Arabella considered servants’ business; each had a duty as if horses. She never attached to servants; the death of the best would not affect her; money could replace him. Her selfishness was candid and created laughter. Crimson draperies covered an iron nature. The siren of amorous folly could make a young man forget the hard Englishwoman; only gradually I discovered the stony rock where my seeds were wasted. Madame de Mortsauf had seen this at a glance. She was right; Arabella’s love became intolerable to me. I have since remarked that many women who ride well have little tenderness. Like Amazons, they lack a breast; their hearts are hard in some direction.When weariness took possession of me, when I comprehended the sanctity true feeling imparts to love, when memories of Clochegourde brought the fragrance of roses, the warmth of the terrace, and the warble of nightingales, I received a blow whose echo still wakes in my heart.I was working in the king’s cabinet when the Duc de Lenoncourt entered. The king asked news of the countess. I raised my head hastily; the king, offended, gave me the look that precedes harsh words.“Sire, my poor daughter is dying,” replied the duke.“Will the king deign to grant me leave of absence?” I cried, tears in my eyes.“Go, my lord,” he answered, withholding reprimand in satire.More courtier than father, the duke asked no leave but got into the carriage with the king. I left without bidding Lady Dudley goodbye, leaving a note that I was sent on a mission by the king. At the Croix de Berny, I met his Majesty returning. He threw me an ironic look seeming to say: “If you mean to be anything in politics, come back; don’t parley with the dead.” The duke waved sadly. The pompous equipages rolled away to cries of “Vive le Roi!” It seemed the court had driven over the dead body of Madame de Mortsauf with utter insensibility. Though the duke was an excellent man, he would no doubt play whist with Monsieur after the king retired. The duchess had long ago given her daughter the first stab by writing of Lady Dudley.My hurried journey was like a ruined gambler’s dream; I despaired at receiving no news. Had the confessor excluded me? I accused everyone. Beyond Tours, on the poplar-bordered road to Poncher, I met Monsieur Origet. He guessed I was going to Clochegourde and told me: “I doubt if you find her living. She is dying a frightful death—of inanition. When she called me last June, no medical power could control the disease; she had the symptoms Monsieur de Mortsauf knows, for he thinks he has them. Madame la comtesse was not in a transient condition; her disease had reached a point where science is useless; it is the incurable result of grief. Her physical condition is produced by inertia of an organ as necessary as the heart. Grief has done the work of a dagger. Don’t deceive yourself; she is dying of hidden grief.”“Hidden!” I exclaimed.“No,” he said, looking at me, “and since she was so seriously attacked, Monsieur de Mortsauf ceased to torment her. I am no longer needed; Monsieur Deslandes is all-sufficient; nothing can be done; her sufferings are dreadful. Young, beautiful, and rich, to die emaciated—for she dies of hunger! During the last forty days the stomach has rejected all nourishment.”Origet pressed my hand with respect. “Courage, monsieur,” he said, lifting his eyes.These words were like poisoned arrows. I sprang into the carriage and promised a reward if I arrived in time.Despite impatience, the distance seemed short as bitter reflections crowded my soul. Dying of grief, yet her children were well? Then she died through me! My conscience arraigned me. What weakness in human justice, which avenges open deeds but forgives murderers of the soul! How many victims—Madame de Beauseant, the Duchesse de Langeais, Lady Brandon—our period teems with such events. Who does not remember the young woman who poisoned herself from jealousy? Society and science are accomplices in crimes without assizes. The world declares no one dies of grief; our scientific nomenclature provides labels that serve as passports to the coffin. Is there some law we do not know? Must the centenary pitilessly strew earth with corpses? My God! do I belong to the race of tigers?Remorse gripped me as I entered Clochegourde’s avenue on a damp October morning. Jacques, Madeleine, and the Abbe Dominis knelt at the foot of a wooden cross. I sprang from the carriage toward them, my heart aching. The old huntsman stood apart.I kissed Jacques and Madeleine, who gave a cold look. The abbe rose; I took him by the arm.“Is she still alive?” I implored. He bowed sadly and said it might be better to wait; she was greatly changed. “It is necessary to prepare her for this interview, or it might increase her suffering—death would be a blessed release.”He told me how for six months the countess had suffered more despite Origet’s care. The doctor had come every evening for two months. “To heal the body, the heart must first be healed,” the doctor had said. As the illness increased, the countess’s words grew bitter. She called on earth to keep her, then repented. Such alternations rend the heart. “She falls, but each time she stumbles she rises higher toward heaven,” he said.I cried, “Surely this pure lily will flower in heaven?”“You left her still a flower,” he answered. “You will find her consumed, purified, pure as a diamond buried in ashes.”The count, white-headed, hurried out, declaring, “She was right! He is here! ‘Felix, Felix, Felix has come!’ she kept crying. My dear friend, death is here. Why did it not take a poor madman like me?”I walked toward the house but the Abbe Birotteau stopped me. “Madame la comtesse begs you will not enter yet.” Inside, servants moved, all dumb with grief. Manette searched for Madeleine; we paced the lawn. I saw the dear “mignonne” gathering autumn flowers. Overcome, I sat, broken.“Poor Felix,” said the count, “she forbade me to write to you. She knew how much you loved her.”All was gloom. The servants wept; work was left undone; the place seemed uninhabited. The count, speaking in bursts, blamed and then wept for his wife, mixing despair with accusations. Madeleine told me her mother was ready; the Abbe Birotteau accompanied me. He warned, “I have done all to prevent this meeting. The salvation of this saint required it. I shall be present to protect you against yourself and perhaps against her. Respect her weakness. She is dying of hunger and thirst. Since morning she suffers feverish irritation. Pray God she die not in a moment of doubt.”We reached the chamber. Henriette sat in white, before the fireplace, flowers about her. The abbe’s amazement at the room’s restored tenderness showed she had prepared to receive me. Her shrunken face had the greenish pallor of a magnolia; hollow eyes shone; her forehead kept proportion; inward fires issued despite waxy features. The smile on her whitened lips resembled death’s grin. Her robe showed emaciation. She knew she was changed; thought filled her with bitterness. No longer the sublime Madame de Mortsauf, she was a nameless thing struggling against annihilation. I took her dry, burning hand to kiss. Her discolored lips tried a smile—the forced smile of one seeking to hide irony, expectation, or fury.“Ah, my poor Felix, this is death,” she said, “and you do not like death; odious death. This is the end of love; I knew it would be so. Lady Dudley will never see you thus surprised. Ah! why have I so longed for you, Felix? You have come at last, and I reward your devotion by the same horrible sight that made the Comte de Rance a Trappist. I, who hoped to remain beautiful and noble in your memory, destroy your illusions! True love cannot calculate. But stay; do not go, stay. Monsieur Origet said I was better this morning; I shall recover. Your looks will bring me back to life. When I regain strength and nourishment, I shall be beautiful again. I am scarcely thirty-five; there are many years of happiness before me—we will leave Clochegourde and go to Italy.”Tears filled my eyes; I turned to the window. She put her burning lips to my ear: “My anguish has been in not seeing thee! Did you not bid me live? I will live; I will ride on horseback; I will know life, Paris, fêtes, pleasures!”Those tones pictured a lifetime’s battles, the anguish of true love lost. The countess rose with the motion of a child. The confessor fell on his knees and prayed. She flung an arm around my neck, kissed me violently, and said, “You shall not escape me now!” She fainted; I laid her on the bed. “You carried me thus before,” she murmured.Deslandes entered, surprised at the decorations. “We must suffer much to die,” she said faintly. The doctor felt a pulse and murmured that it was the forty-second day since she had eaten or drunk. The Abbe Birotteau took me to the garden. “Let us leave her to the doctor,” he said; “they will wrap her in opium. Well, you have heard her—if indeed it is she.”“No,” I said, “it is not she.”Stupefied, I hid in a punt to be alone. I tried to detach myself from my life—torture like that which afflicts adulterers. My life was a failure. Despair suggested to die beside her or bury myself at Meilleraye among Trappists. I looked at the windows where Henriette was dying, fancying I saw the light burning the night I betrothed my soul to hers. Ought I not to have followed the simple life she created, keeping faithful while I worked in the world? Had she not bidden me become great to be saved from shameful passions? Chastity! Love as Arabella understood it disgusted me.Madeleine walked slowly and sat on a bench. When I approached, she rose and returned to the house, hating me; she fled from her mother’s murderer. At the portico Jacques sat, expressionless. Young persons carrying death are often unmoved at funerals. I longed to question Jacques.“You know, Jacques,” I said, “that in me you have a most devoted brother.”“Your friendship is useless; I shall follow my mother,” he replied with a sullen look.He showed me his handkerchief stained with blood.They each had a fatal secret. The brother and sister avoided each other. Henriette lay low; all was ruins at Clochegourde.“Madame is asleep,” Manette said, happy that the countess was out of pain. Small joys matter then. “Monsieur Deslandes ordered the flowers taken away; they excited Madame’s nerves,” Manette added. She thought the flowers caused delirium.I returned as the sunset gilded Azay’s roofs. All was calm and pure. A soft light lit the bed where my Henriette lay wrapped in opium. The body seemed annihilated; the soul reigned. Blanche and Henriette, two sublime faces of one woman, reappeared. The abbes sat at the bed. The count stood stupefied. I took a seat on the sofa. We exchanged looks of admiration mingled with tears. The Abbe Dominis and I communicated in signs: yes, angels were watching. Her face cleared; the green of suffering gave way to pure white pallor; she seemed to embrace the divine.Madeleine knelt, adoring, crying “My mother!” Jacques smiled; he would follow her. “She is entering the haven,” said the Abbe Birotteau. The Angelus rang; Nature’s poetry joined the sacred hour. We burst into tears. Even servants knelt and prayed. The doctor signaled that this sleep was the hour without pain; it was time for the last sacraments. At nine o’clock she awoke quietly with gentle eyes, and we beheld her in former beauty.“Mother! you are too beautiful to die—life and health are coming back to you!” cried Madeleine.“Dear daughter, I shall live—in thee,” she answered, smiling.They embraced. The count kissed his wife. She colored when she saw me.“Dear Felix,” she said, “this is, I think, the only grief I have ever caused you. Forget all I may have said—I, a poor creature beside myself.” She held out her hand; I took and kissed it. “As in the old days, Felix?”We left the room during the last confession. I approached Madeleine. In the presence of others she could not escape civility; but, like her mother, she looked at no one.“Dear Madeleine,” I said softly, “what have you against me? Why such coldness before death, which should reconcile us?”“I hear in my heart what my mother says,” she replied, with a look like Ingres’ “Mother of God.”“And you condemn me when your mother absolves me—if indeed I am guilty.”“You, you—always yourself!” Her voice revealed determined hatred, implacable as Corsican judgment.An hour passed in silence. The Abbe Birotteau returned after receiving the countess’s confession. We found her sitting up, dressed in the long white garment to be her shroud. She had had her letters burned—sacrifice she had delayed until this hour. She smiled with eyes moist and inward lucidity; she saw the promised land.“Dear Felix,” she said, pressing my hand, “stay with us. You must be present at my last scene; be not absent from the least painful among many things in which you are concerned.”She made a sign and the door closed. She knelt before the astonished count, then laid her head on his knees.“Though I have been a faithful wife to you,” she said faintly, “I have sometimes failed in my duty. I have given a friendship outside my family more affectionate care than to you. Perhaps I have irritated you by comparisons. I have had a deep friendship which no one—not even he who has been its object—has fully known. Though virtuous by human laws and an irreproachable wife, other thoughts, voluntary or involuntary, have crossed my mind, and in this hour I fear I welcomed them too warmly. But having loved you and continued submissive, I now pray for your blessing with a clean heart. I shall die without a bitter thought if I can hear a tender word for your Blanche, for the mother of your children—if you forgive her those things she did not forgive herself till reassured by the great tribunal which pardons all.”“Blanche, Blanche!” cried the broken man, shedding tears on her head. “Would you kill me?” He raised and kissed her solemnly. “Have I no forgiveness to ask? Have I never been harsh?”“Perhaps,” she said. “But dear friend, indulge a dying woman’s weakness; tranquillize me. Will you grant that I leave to our friend here that pledge of my affection?” She showed a letter on the mantel. “He is now my adopted son, and that is all. The heart makes bequests; my last wishes impose a duty on that dear Felix. If you do not wish to marry Madeleine, at least seek my soul’s repose by making Monsieur de Mortsauf as happy as he can be. Farewell, long farewell like that I gave last night to our valley, where I shall rest and where you will often—will you not?—return.Henriette.”I fell into abyssal reflections as the depths of her life were lighted by this expiring flame. My clouds of egotism rolled away. She had suffered as much as I—more, for she was dead. She believed others would be kind to her friend; she was blind to her daughter’s enmity. That last proof of tenderness pained me. Poor Henriette wished to give me Clochegourde and her daughter.From that dread day when I first followed my noble Henriette to the grave, the sun has been less warm, nights more gloomy, movement less agile, thought more dull. Some departed we bury in earth; others are buried within us, their memory mingling with our heartbeats; they are in us by a metempsychosis of love. A soul is within my soul. When some good thing is done by me, that soul acts and speaks. All that is good within me issues from that grave; sarcasm and bitterness are mine. Natalie, when next my eyes are darkened or my lips silent, do not ask, “Of what are you thinking?”I ceased writing for days; these memories were too bitter. I owe you an account of events that followed; they need few words. Henriette’s letter set a star of hope before me. To live at Clochegourde with Madeleine, consecrating my life to her, seemed a rescue. But it was necessary to know her true feelings. As I bade the count farewell, we walked the terrace. He spoke of his loss but soon of the future; he feared his daughter, who lacked her mother’s gentleness. Madeleine’s firm character alarmed him; he foresaw a struggle between father and daughter that would end his days in bitterness. His son was to leave; his daughter marry; what son-in-law might she have? Though talking of dying, he feared being alone without sympathy.Madeleine could see me from the terrace but would not come down. I seized a moment to beg the count to call her, saying I had a last wish of her mother to convey. The count fetched her and left us on the terrace alone.“Dear Madeleine,” I said, “if I speak to you, it should be here where your mother listened to me. I know your thoughts; but are you not condemning me without knowledge of facts? My life and happiness are bound up in this place; you know that, yet you seek to banish me by coldness instead of brotherly affection that death should have strengthened. If my mother’s name still has power, in her name I beg you never to return me to Clochegourde so long as you are in it. The sight of me causes a repugnance I cannot express, which I shall never overcome.”She stopped me.“Monsieur,” she said, voice trembling, “I know all your thoughts; but I shall not change my feelings. I would rather fling myself into the Indre than ally myself to you. If my mother’s name has power, in her name I beg you never to return while I am here. The sight of you causes repugnance I cannot overcome.”She bowed with dignity and returned to the house without looking back. The searching eye of that young girl had discovered her mother’s secrets; her hatred of the man she fancied fatal to her mother may have been increased by a sense of complicity.All before me was chaos. Madeleine hated me without considering cause or victim. The edifice of my happiness fell in ruins. I alone knew the life of that unknown woman; none had fathomed her heart. Strange truth: how many families have their Henriette! How many noble feelings leave earth with no historian to measure their depths! Mothers know their children little; so with husbands, lovers, brothers. When Madeleine disappeared, I left with a broken heart. Bidding farewell to my host at Sache, I started for Paris, following the right bank of the Indre.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############################################################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At the story's outset, the Stanhope press and ink-distributing roller were not yet common in small provincial printing houses. In Angoulême, linked to Parisian typography by its paper mills, only wooden presses were used. “The press groans” was literal: leather ink-balls were still employed and the pressman inked by hand. This old mechanism, cherished by Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, is central to the tale.Séchard, formerly a journeyman pressman, survived the Requisition of 1793 and became the sole worker in a printing house after the master’s death. Illiterate, he received a master printer’s license from a Representative of the People, letting him buy the business cheaply. He partnered with a nobleman, M. le Comte de Maucombe, who discreetly helped print decrees while avoiding attention from the authorities.By 1795, after the Terror, Séchard needed a compositor and hired an Abbé who later became a Bishop. Despite his lack of education, Séchard learned to run the business, developing a keen sense of costs and profits. His avarice grew as he saw potential wealth: he bought paper when prices fell and became his own landlord, acquiring the old printing house.Séchard had one son, David, whom he sent to grammar school in hopes of a capable successor. David excelled academically, and Séchard—who scorned book learning—insisted he economize while studying in Paris. David completed his education without costing his father anything.The printing house held a monopoly on official printing in the region, but the Cointet Brothers sought a second printer’s license, threatening Séchard’s position. Confident in David’s abilities, Séchard looked forward to retirement, believing his son would face the competition.Now in his seventies, Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard had developed a taste for drink, which his late wife had once kept in check. His appearance reflected the habit: a bloated face, cunning gaze, three-cornered hat, and old-fashioned clothing suited to his character.When David returned home, Séchard welcomed him with feigned warmth, eager to discuss business. Despite David’s plea to postpone serious matters, Séchard pressed on, intent on negotiating the sale of the business—viewing his son not only as offspring but as a potential rival in commercial dealings.The printing house, established since Louis XIV’s reign, consisted of a large room with a glass door opening to the workshop, where customers often stumbled through, distracted by the printing process. The premises included a yard with vines and a kitchen where inky runoff mixed with household waste, giving the place a notorious reputation.The living quarters above the office were simple and cluttered, reflecting Séchard’s greed. The dining room was sparsely furnished and the bedroom held old furniture unchanged since the predecessor’s time. The state of the house mirrored the old printer’s character and relentless pursuit of profit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The sitting-room had been partly modernized by the late Mme. Séchard, with powder-blue wainscoting and Oriental wallpaper. The furniture was sparse—lyre-backed chairs with blue leather cushions. The arched windows overlooking the Place du Mûrier were bare, and the room lacked a clock, candle sconces, or a mirror, for Mme. Séchard had died before finishing her plans. Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, unable to see value in improvements that didn’t yield financial returns, left it as it was.Jérôme-Nicolas brought his son David into the room and pointed to a valuation of the printing plant on the table. “Read that, my boy,” he said, watching him. David read the inventory aloud, noting the old presses and their valuation. “These presses are old and not worth much; they’re only fit for firewood,” he exclaimed, and dropped the paper.“Old tools?” Séchard retorted. “Let’s go downstairs and you’ll see how they work. You’ll regret calling them sabots. They’ve served me twenty years and will last you a lifetime.”He descended the stairs and, to David’s surprise, demonstrated the presses’ functionality with agility. “Where’s the English press that can match this?” he challenged, boasting the presses could generate good income without a foreman and dismissing David’s interest in newer machinery as ruinous to the type. David was skeptical about the valuation of the type, deeming it overpriced.“After twelve years of wear, that type is as good as new,” Séchard insisted, defending the quality from M. Vaflard’s foundry. David noted the price was excessive compared to competitors, but Séchard remained adamant about his equipment’s value.The inventory itemized every tool and amounted to thirty thousand francs, including license and goodwill. David questioned whether he could afford it. Séchard grew anxious over his son’s silence—he preferred a heated argument to quiet—and continued to extol the virtues of old tools, claiming they were worth more than new ones.Feeling cornered, David realized he had to accept the deal or walk away. He knew his father had bought the business for ten thousand francs, making the current asking price seem exorbitant. “Father, you’re cutting my throat!” he exclaimed.Séchard argued that the license alone was worth a fortune, citing income from advertisements and other work. He insisted David was foolish to haggle. Attached to the valuation was a partnership deed: Séchard would rent the premises to David for twelve hundred francs a year and reserve a room for himself.David recalculated the license and goodwill’s worth and thought he might manage to pay off the debt. He accepted the terms, surprising Séchard, who wondered whether his son had savings. The next day, Séchard had the apprentice move David’s belongings to the attic, leaving him with three empty rooms and no funds for wages. When David asked for financial help, Séchard refused, claiming he had already paid his share.David felt humiliated by his father’s refusal. He realized he was alone and that his father sought to profit from him. Desperate, he mentioned his mother’s fortune, but Séchard dismissed it as merely her beauty and intelligence. David understood any claim to his mother’s inheritance would require a long, costly legal battle. Accepting his situation, he resolved to work hard, reminding himself he was ultimately building his own future.Séchard then mentioned a “treasure” he was leaving David—Marion, a capable country girl who managed many tasks in the printing house. Séchard set off for the countryside, uneasy about payment for the business, trusting David’s education would ensure he fulfilled his obligations.He had bought a vineyard in Marsac, where he spent his days. He fretted over the thirty thousand francs and often visited David’s workshop to check business, suspecting his son was not managing well, especially as the Cointet Brothers gained ground in the printing trade.David, indifferent to politics, struggled to secure work. The Cointets, aligning themselves with the local clergy, began monopolizing the market while David’s business dwindled. They slandered him as a Liberal and untrustworthy because of his father’s reputation.As David’s business faltered, the Cointets offered to buy the printing house, prompting Séchard’s intervention. He insisted David keep the paper, arguing it was essential for their connection with the Liberal clientele. Séchard negotiated a price of sixty thousand francs for the business to protect David’s interests.Ultimately David was forced to sell the paper to the Cointets, marking the decline of the Séchard establishment. Séchard, now detached, visited less frequently though he still felt a connection to the old printing house.David’s situation worsened when he met Lucien Chardon, an old school friend who had fallen into poverty after his father’s death. Lucien struggled to find his place; David offered him a position as a printer’s reader in an effort to help.Their friendship deepened as they shared dreams: Lucien aspired to be a poet, David to pursue natural science. They discussed Lucien’s father’s ideas for refining sugar and making paper, which sparked David’s interest in potential ventures.Their ambitions, however, distracted them from the printing house’s pressing needs. While the Cointet Brothers thrived, Séchard & Son scraped by, earning only a fraction of what was needed. The Cointets, recognizing David’s lack of business acumen, allowed his establishment to continue operating at a loss, ensuring no serious competition.The condition of the printing house reflected Séchard’s avarice: repairs were neglected and maintenance ignored. The building was dilapidated, a testament to years of disregard, and the workshop was cluttered and grimy, mirroring the struggles of its occupants.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day in May 1821, David and Lucien stood at a window overlooking the yard, waiting for the men to leave for dinner. Uneasy with the office air, David led Lucien outside to sit under the vines, where sunlight revealed their contrasting looks and characters.David had a fighter’s build: broad shoulders, a swarthy face marked by struggle, thick black hair and deep-set eyes that suggested wisdom tinged with melancholy and an awareness of social limits born of humble origins. Beside him, Lucien was graceful and beautiful, with a refined profile and striking blue eyes that held youthful innocence. His delicate features and slender frame lent an almost feminine appearance, but his ambition and sharp intellect showed. He examined society pragmatically and could contemplate questionable means to succeed.Both were low in social standing yet aspired to higher ideals, which made them bitter against reality. Lucien, with a Gascon temperament, was bold and adventurous; David, physically strong, was more introspective and cautious. Their friendship mixed admiration and imbalance: David often yielded to Lucien’s charm, viewing himself as the more ordinary.For three years they shared a bond nourished by literature and art, immersing themselves in great writers and thinkers and working tirelessly, dreaming of fame as an escape from harsh circumstances. One day David brought a new volume of André de Chénier’s poetry, which moved them both. The verses offered solace and kinship with the poet’s struggles; as they read, the yard seemed to fill with the promise of their aspirations and the hope of transcending their circumstances.Their reverie was broken by Cérizet, an apprentice who brought a stranger wanting to print a monograph. Lucien, eager to impress, engaged the author, who mentioned Mme. de Bargeton, a woman Lucien admired. He blushed and thanked the author for her interest, revealing his admiration.After the author left, David questioned Lucien about Mme. de Bargeton; Lucien confessed his passionate love. David warned him of social barriers, but Lucien hoped love could surmount them. He declared he would not visit Mme. de Bargeton again unless David was welcomed too, showing loyalty to his friend.As Lucien walked through Angoulême, he reflected on the distance between his class and Mme. de Bargeton’s, feeling both hope and anxiety about her reaction. The city, perched on a crag, was split between the upper town, where nobility lived, and the lower town, where trade thrived. That division bred tension: the upper class looked down on merchants and tradespeople, resisting change and viewing outsiders with suspicion, creating deep animosity between classes.Despite government efforts to modernize Angoulême, the upper town remained stagnant, clinging to old values. The social hierarchy was rigid: the nobility kept their exclusivity, and the bourgeoisie resented their disdain. This antagonism would later fuel social upheaval as the rising middle class sought recognition and respect.In this climate, Lucien’s desire to gain acceptance in Mme. de Bargeton’s world was a major challenge. His origins as a druggist’s son made him an outsider in her circle, yet his literary ambitions and charm set him apart. Mme. de Bargeton, a woman of artistic inclination, had a history that shaped her character and influenced Lucien’s path.As Lucien navigated love and ambition, societal barriers loomed, but his determination to bridge the gap between his world and hers drove him. David’s mixture of admiration and concern reflected their friendship’s complexity: he understood Lucien’s depth of feeling but saw the obstacles clearly. Lucien’s pledge to include David in any future visits underscored both loyalty and hope that friendship might soften social distance.Their story would unfold against a society grappling with divisions and shifting fortunes—an Angoulême where tradition and modernity, nobility and bourgeoisie, ambition and constraint met. Lucien’s struggle for acceptance and the qualities that set him and David apart would shape what followed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Bargeton was the great-grandson of a Bordeaux alderman ennobled under Louis XIII; his branch had declined when his grandfather, “the Waster,” squandered the family fortune. By 1789 the current Bargeton possessed entailed lands and a house in Angoulême but had lost most seignorial rights and kept only modest rents. In 1805 he married Mlle. Marie‑Louise‑Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, a noblewoman from a once-prominent family. Her father, M. de Nègrepelisse, lived modestly on his wife’s estate, farming and distilling brandy.During the Revolution Abbé Niollant taught Naïs languages and music, cultivating her love of literature and independence while also encouraging a haughty sense of superiority. Her father’s thrift and provincial isolation reinforced that pride; she resisted marriage, valuing autonomy over submission. Wanting a husband who would not challenge her intellect, her father chose M. de Bargeton: a man of breeding but limited mind. Naïs accepted, intending to pursue social ambitions within the match despite the considerable age gap—thirty‑six to fifty‑eight—and the unequal advantages it implied.Settled in Angoulême, the couple hoped Naïs’s inheritance would enable a move to Paris, but modest income and provincial life stifled her intellectual yearnings and produced absurdities in her character. She grew theatrical, expressing emotion with grand gestures; passions that might once have been elevated were trivialized by small‑town stagnation. Her life became a cycle of unfulfilled hopes and isolation, which intensified her longing for love. The rise of the Empire and the fame of military heroes rekindled romantic dreams; she fell for a young officer whose death in battle left her heartbroken, amplifying loss and regret.For years she lived in solitude, dignity hardening into affectation—until M. du Châtelet arrived: charming, ambitious, and disruptive. He ingratiated himself into her circle, using music to win her favor and stirring a renewed sense of youth. Recognizing her potential as a wealthy widow, he hoped to advance his own aims through the connection. His presence shocked the local elite, who regarded her salon as a bastion of high society; his outsider status challenged that order. Despite the scandal she welcomed him, drawn to his charm and the excitement he brought. His entrance altered the dynamics of her social world, exposing tensions among ambition, love, and provincial expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion was verbose and exaggerated; the Quotidienne tepidly loyal, Louis XVIII likened to a Jacobin. Women in society appeared awkward and poorly dressed, lacking polish in appearance and conversation. Châtelet, however, found solace in company because of his interest in Mme. de Bargeton. The countryside’s noble spirit and breeding masked many deficiencies; their loyalty to the House of Bourbon was an honor that elevated them, like cowrie shells for the Bambara Negroes.Somewomen, flattered by Châtelet, saw in him qualities absent in their husbands, which soothed their vanity. All aspired to be near the Imperial Highness. Châtelet bore much insolence but kept his position by currying favor with the clergy and indulging the queen of Angoulême in local vanities, sharing new literature and poetry with her. Mme. de Bargeton, enchanted by the Bourbon revival, longed for Parisian genius, prompting Châtelet to tell her of a promising young poet in Angoulême.Lucien, the poet, did not foresee the attention he would receive. Praised by his headmaster, he thrilled at being welcomed into the Hôtel de Bargeton. His sister Eve, secretly supportive, saved to buy him new shoes and clothes, preparing him for this moment. She coached him on aristocratic comportment and watched him leave with mixed pride and anxiety.At the Hôtel de Bargeton Lucien found the house less grand than expected, but he was captivated by Mme. de Bargeton, who embodied the poetry he admired. She was impressed by his beauty and diffidence; their meeting brimmed with mutual admiration. Lucien, enchanted, overlooked her faded appearance and sensed a connection that promised further encounters.Châtelet, sensing a rival, tried to influence the young poet and advised him about Angoulême’s social dynamics. He declared his feelings for Mme. de Bargeton and claimed she would soon be his. Despite Châtelet's attempts to intimidate him, Lucien persisted in visiting Mme. de Bargeton, growing used to noble condescension that at first flattered him and later bred resentment.Lucien’s infatuation with Naïs (Mme. de Bargeton) deepened as she flattered him and treated him as a project to cultivate. Their relationship combined familiarity and pride: Naïs sometimes acted as patroness, at other times showed tenderness. Lucien felt the tumult of first love; as their bond grew, Naïs addressed him more intimately, further cementing their connection.One evening Lucien found Naïs gazing at a portrait of a deceased lover, which stirred jealousy. He nevertheless boldly declared his feelings, and their intimacy progressed. Naïs encouraged Lucien's literary ambitions, introduced him to music and books, and by nurturing him revealed her own vulnerabilities.When their liaison became public, the town responded with shock and gossip, creating an uproar. Châtelet, threatened, sought to undermine Lucien. Mme. de Bargeton defended her protégé, arguing that genius could spring from humble origins and using her wit to counter the nobles' disdain.Mme. de Bargeton hosted gatherings to showcase Lucien’s talent and urged him to adopt the noble name Rubempré, promising to help him gain court favor. The idea thrilled Lucien; he began abandoning earlier ideals of equality in favor of social ambition.Under Naïs's influence his aspirations widened; he resolved to seek fame and success even at the cost of some principles. He wrote a heartfelt letter to Naïs, asking her to help his friend David and revealing his emotional turmoil and ambitions. Torn between love and recognition, Lucien navigated complex relationships as he sought to reconcile artistic aims with the realities of social advancement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Lucien descended from Angoulême, he feared Mme. de Bargeton’s anger and whether she would receive David. He perceived a vast gulf between a queen and her favorite and knew David could not easily navigate that social terrain. Unaware that receiving David might ruin Mme. de Bargeton—mingling with a commoner would bring ostracism—Lucien nevertheless felt the danger: many sins are pardoned for the powerful, but breaching social law by associating with lower classes was unforgivable.Lucien’s aristocratic instinct warned him. He recognized that noble bearing did not always match noble feeling; many notable men lacked the aristocratic mold. David did not have the social graces Lucien possessed, and Lucien feared the ridicule his friend would face. After a poetic reverie he shifted to worldly calculation, regretting his impulsive letter to Mme. de Bargeton and foreseeing the sacrifices success would demand. He longed for simpler pleasures and felt it nobler to win by his own efforts rather than through a woman’s favor. Inspired by Napoleon’s rise from humble origins, Lucien resolved to pursue ambitions without patronage, though he felt ashamed of his father’s shop as an obstacle.At home, his father, M. Postel, greeted him and recalled Lucien’s grandfather’s past achievements, a reminder that stung. Lucien searched eagerly among the shop’s items for Mme. de Bargeton’s reply and found a letter offering to help David while revealing prejudice against lower social standing. Lucien read this as a victory, sure David would shine in her company.Eve, his sister, noticed his elation and hoped for his happiness. Absorbed in ambition, Lucien reassured her they would be happy together, though Eve sensed his priorities shifting. David, uncomfortable with the social elevation Lucien sought, decided to decline Mme. de Bargeton’s invitation. He affirmed his commitment to his own path as a printer and emphasized the distance between their stations.David’s humility and loyalty moved Lucien, who felt a deep bond and reflected on the sacrifices of those who supported him. Yet ambition began to eclipse gratitude, focusing him increasingly on advancement.Preparing for his first public reading, Lucien planned a biblical piece, but David worried it would not please the audience and suggested a different poem; eager to impress, Lucien agreed. Meanwhile David and Eve shared a quiet moment that hinted at mutual affection.At the reading, Lucien arrived early and met M. de Bargeton, socially awkward and ineffectual—relying on superficial pleasantries and little intellectual substance. Devoted to his wife, he let her manage their social life and followed her lead unquestioningly.Lucien’s exchanges with M. de Bargeton exposed the challenges of navigating high society. The evening, marked by Lucien’s ambition and the complex loyalties around him, set the stage for the conflicts and aspirations that would shape his future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of nominating the mute gentleman for a deputy, but Lucien had not yet fathomed this enigmatic figure, having visited the house only briefly. M. de Bargeton, reclining, observed everything with a calm silence that awed Lucien. Imaginative, Lucien saw him not as a mere man but as a sphinx, and treated him with deference.“I am the first comer,” Lucien said, bowing more than usual.“That is natural enough,” replied M. de Bargeton.Lucien took the remark as a sly jab, imagining a jealous husband and becoming self-conscious about his appearance. M. de Bargeton continued, “You live in L’Houmeau, and people who live far away always come earlier than those nearby.”“What is the reason for that?” Lucien asked politely.“I don’t know,” M. de Bargeton answered, then returned to silence.Lucien tried to restart the conversation, noting that Mme. de Bargeton was likely dressing. M. de Bargeton confirmed this, and Lucien, awkward, noticed the room’s preparations and fretted about his attire. He turned to a Japanese jar on a console table, seeking common ground.“You seldom leave the city, monsieur?” Lucien ventured.“Very seldom,” came the curt reply.Silence fell again, with M. de Bargeton watching Lucien closely and making him uneasy. Then the old servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered confidently, greeting M. de Bargeton and nodding at Lucien, who felt overshadowed by the Baron’s ease.Sixte du Châtelet wore fashionable white trousers and a black coat, the faded charm of a once-stylish beau. He inspected Lucien’s attire, deepening Lucien’s discomfort. M. de Bargeton, sensing tension, asked Châtelet if there was any news.“The latest is M. Chardon,” Châtelet said with malice. “Have you brought a charming poet for us?” he added, arranging his hair.“I should have asked you whether I succeeded,” Lucien replied, feeling outclassed by Châtelet.“Pshaw! Just a few vaudevilles and songs,” Châtelet dismissed, minimizing his work.Mme. de Bargeton then appeared, elaborately dressed, captivating Lucien with theatrical style. Châtelet showered her with compliments, pleasing her, while she barely acknowledged Lucien, favoring Châtelet instead.Guests arrived: the Bishop and his Vicar-General, both dignified yet contrasting. Mme. de Chandour and her husband followed—almost fictional in eccentricity.
