Amélie de Chandour posed like a rival queen; her husband, M. de Chandour, drew attention with exaggerated dress.M. de Saintot, known as Astolphe, came with his solemn wife, Élisa. Astolphe pretended to be scientific, though his knowledge was superficial. M. de Bartas presented himself as a musical expert and hoped for praise for his singing.M. Alexandre de Brébian, an artist, and his wife came with M. de Bartas, each showing quirks. The women, Lolotte and Fifine, were obsessed with fashion, while their husbands appeared dowdy.Also present were M. le Comte de Senonches, haughty, and his wife Zéphirine, tall and striking despite ill health. Francis, a family friend who managed their household, was devoted to Zéphirine, prompting speculation about their relationship.The wealthiest couple, the Marquis de Pimentel and his wife, arrived with the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac. Their presence provoked jealousy, especially as Mme. de Bargeton paid them special attention, highlighting social dynamics.The Prefect and the General arrived last, along with a nervous country gentleman who brought a treatise on silkworms. He struggled to fit in, eager to discuss his work but met with indifference.Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard, noble yet impoverished, were present, their attempts to make connections marked by desperation. M. de Séverac, a childless widower, engaged them about his silkworm nurseries, while Mme. du Brossard promoted her daughter’s talents.As the evening progressed, Lucien felt increasingly scrutinized. Most called him M. Chardon, not M. de Rubempré, which stung his pride. The audience seemed more absorbed in its own chatter than in his poetry, leaving him isolated.When it came time to read, Lucien’s excitement mixed with anxiety. He announced he would read works of a recently discovered poet, André de Chénier, which the audience misinterpreted as a pretext to spare his feelings.He began with Le Malade, which drew mild applause, but his reading of L’Aveugle fell flat as the audience failed to engage. The lack of connection made the experience painful; he sensed their disinterest and impatience.Despite his efforts, Lucien felt discouraged, sweating from the pressure. A supportive glance from Louise gave him courage, and he continued, reading Chénier’s Iambes, which finally captured some attention, though many still missed the meaning.During a break, Zéphirine sent Francis to investigate the poetry, prompting discussion among the women about Lucien’s background and the nature of his work. Amélie’s remarks about Lucien’s noble name and printing background sparked gossip, complicating perceptions of his status.As the evening unfolded, Lucien remained largely oblivious to the mounting tension and misunderstandings around his poetry, lost in the beauty of his art while the audience remained mostly indifferent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien attempted to immortalize his love for Mme. de Bargeton with an ode he fancied rivaled Chénier. Proud as he presented it, he felt secure under her protective gaze while she secretly worried for him. The audience, however, was unimpressed: the Baron dismissed the poem as youthful verse, full of grandiose imagery but lacking substance. The women feigned admiration; their indifference was palpable, and they exchanged sarcastic remarks about Lucien's ambitions.Amid the mockery, Francis, a cunning observer, sought to undermine Lucien by spreading rumors about his mother’s influence on his poetry. The Bishop, unwittingly complicit, praised Lucien, but his words only deepened the aristocrats’ scorn. Lucien, feeling out of place and overwhelmed, struggled to engage with the crowd, often misidentifying people and sensing the weight of their disdain.As the evening wore on, social dynamics shifted: Lucien’s poetry was eclipsed by card games and music. Mme. de Bargeton, stung by her peers’ contempt for Lucien, retreated to her boudoir and offered him solace, encouraging him to endure for the sake of future greatness. She romanticized suffering as a precursor to artistic immortality and urged him to channel his pain into profound work.Meanwhile Lucien’s sister Eve and her lover David discussed his ambitions and the risks of his relationship with Mme. de Bargeton. David worried Lucien’s aspirations might lead to ruin; he was ill-equipped for high society’s demands. Eve, however, remained optimistic, believing they could support Lucien until he succeeded.Their conversation turned to their own romance when David proposed. Eve hesitated over their differing social standings but ultimately agreed, motivated by love and a desire to help Lucien. David planned to provide for Lucien so he wouldn’t struggle alone.On the way home, Lucien shared his frustrations about the evening, declaring his determination to win Mme. de Bargeton’s love and rise above the aristocrats’ scorn. News of Eve’s engagement to David felt like a setback; he feared their marriage would complicate his ambitions.David then sought his father’s approval, only to meet skepticism. His father, focused on finances, dismissed Eve’s worth and urged a more advantageous match. David stood firm, asserting his love for Eve and commitment to their future despite his father’s objections.Ultimately David left his father’s house disheartened, realizing he could not rely on paternal support. He resolved to build a life with Eve, determined to make their plans real even if it meant facing hardships alone. Lucien remained caught between his lofty aspirations and the harsh realities of his social standing, torn between dreams and the contempt of those around him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Angoulême, the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s rejoinder set tongues wagging and elevated Lucien to local celebrity. Walking through Beaulieu he was envied by young men and overheard flattering remarks that inflated his ego. He sought a private moment with Louise to tell her of his sister Eve’s impending marriage, hoping her kindness would bring him happiness. Louise greeted him with passionate affection, praised his learning, and promised to fashion a poetic oasis for him despite the surrounding envy.Buoyed by family and friends, Lucien’s self-regard grew. He playfully asked Louise if she would be his Beatrice; she teased him with a promise of future love and urged him toward greatness. Lucien confided in her about David and Eve’s attachment, and Louise reassured him their bond transcended family ties. When M. de Bargeton arrived, Lucien hesitated, but Louise invited him to dinner and a reading, reinforcing his sense of belonging.As Lucien settled into life as M. de Rubempré, he enjoyed the comforts of his relationship with Louise and M. de Bargeton’s patronage. His confidence mounted; he believed his literary ambitions were imminent. Eve and David postponed their wedding to support him, enchanted by his charm and persuasion, though David feared Lucien might change with his new status.Their intimacy attracted Châtelet’s attention, who aimed to expose them. He watched closely, hoping to catch a compromising moment. Despite innocent interactions, provincial gossip and speculation produced misunderstandings. The constraints of small-town life made open declarations of love risky, and Louise felt the burden of social expectation.Determined to declare his feelings, Lucien faced the barrier of their environment. Louise, pressured by circumstance, contemplated escaping provincial restraints. Châtelet continued manipulating perception, sowing doubt about their innocence.One day Lucien’s emotional outburst produced a vulnerable scene witnessed by Stanislas, who misread it. Gossip swelled into exaggerated tales through Angoulême. Châtelet seized the opportunity to advance his agenda, while Stanislas relished his role in the drama.Mme. de Bargeton, alarmed by the rumors, felt compelled to defend her honor. She confided in M. de Bargeton, urging him to confront Stanislas for slander. Motivated by his wife’s distress, M. de Bargeton resolved to challenge Stanislas despite his insecurity.Approaching Stanislas’s house, M. de Bargeton struggled to assert himself without seeming ridiculous. His quiet demeanor and careful approach ultimately won respect as he navigated the tense gathering. The stage was set for a confrontation that would further entangle their lives, exposing the complexities of love, honor, and social expectation in their small town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet grasped the implications of a late-night visit, especially with M. de Bargeton already in bed. It was clear Naïs had instigated it. With enough standing in the household to intervene, Châtelet guided M. de Bargeton to ask if he wished to speak with Stanislas. The old gentleman, relieved to have a mediator, went to Amélie’s room, anticipating a confrontation that could leave Mme. de Bargeton a widow and widen the breach between her and Lucien, the source of the trouble.Châtelet warned Stanislas that Bargeton would likely accuse him concerning Naïs and advised him to stay decorous. When they returned, Bargeton demanded Stanislas retract his statements about Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré. Feeling cornered, Stanislas agreed to a duel and chose pistols.The atmosphere shifted as word of the duel spread among the guests; opinions divided on who was right. Châtelet remained composed while Stanislas looked shaken. Amélie, sensing the tension, felt unwell and was escorted to her room. The men debated the duel’s implications, some recalling Bargeton’s past skill as a marksman.The next morning, Lucien had breakfast with David when Mme. Chardon arrived, alarmed by the duel. She reported Bargeton had nearly killed Stanislas, who had accused him of compromising behavior with Mme. de Bargeton. Lucien vehemently defended her innocence. The duel’s details were grim: Bargeton had shot Stanislas in the neck, leaving him injured.As Lucien absorbed the news, he received a note from Louise warning him to stay away from her home because of the duel’s fallout. She suggested he spend the day with her, hinting at the emotional turmoil it had caused. David, preparing for his upcoming marriage, noted Lucien’s frequent visits to Mme. de Bargeton might need curtailing.Compelled to see Mme. de Bargeton, Lucien found her at breakfast with her husband and M. de Nègrepelisse, who scrutinized him, trying to gauge his relationship with his daughter. Afterwards, Louise confided she planned to go to Paris to secure a political position for Bargeton; their leaving Angoulême was meant to mitigate the duel’s consequences.Louise urged Lucien to join her in Paris, emphasizing opportunities in the capital and its vibrant intellectual life compared with provincial stagnation. Lucien felt drawn to this new life and to Louise, but his sister’s upcoming wedding weighed on him. Louise dismissed his concern, questioning why he would prioritize a family event over their future together. Torn between family loyalty and prospects with Louise, Lucien felt overwhelmed.After a heated exchange, Louise fainted; Lucien apologized and expressed desperation. She insisted he be discreet and prepare to leave with her the next night. Grappling with his decision, he returned to David’s home, feeling the weight of his family’s sacrifices.Discussing plans with Eve and their mother, they expressed sorrow at his impending departure, especially with Eve’s wedding approaching. Lucien considered borrowing money to support himself in Paris, but Eve and their mother revealed their own financial strain from wedding expenses.David, overhearing, offered to help financially despite the strain on his marriage. He agreed to endorse a loan for Lucien, demonstrating loyalty and friendship. Lucien felt guilty for the burden but remained determined to pursue his ambitions.As Lucien prepared to leave, he felt the emotional weight of severing ties with his family. David and he devised a plan to discreetly send Lucien’s belongings to Paris, while Châtelet, suspicious of Mme. de Bargeton’s departure, sent someone to follow her.Lucien set out early the next morning with David, who pretended to be on business. They waited for Mme. de Bargeton’s carriage; as it approached, Lucien felt a mix of excitement and dread about the journey. David, sensing the gravity, wished him well, fearing for Lucien’s future in Paris.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY — the narrative's central section; core events and developments occur here.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################
</story>
